nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator (d. 2026)

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2008–2020); Former chief nuclear negotiator (d. 2026)
data.bio: Ali Larijani was the longest-serving speaker in the history of the Islamic Republic, holding the position from 2008 to 2020. Before entering politics, he served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) (1994–2004), appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) as a hardline counterweight to the reformist press during the Khatami era. He subsequently served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2005–2007) and secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council). He was disqualified from the 2021 presidential election by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). His brother [Sadegh Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) served as head of the judiciary. He was killed on 17 March 2026 in an [Israeli airstrike on Tehran](/events/larijani-assassination-2026), along with his son Morteza Larijani.

nodes.label: Ali M. Ansari
nodes.role: Professor of Iranian History at University of St Andrews; founding director, Institute for Iranian Studies; President, British Institute of Persian Studies

data.role: Professor of Iranian History at University of St Andrews; founding director, Institute for Iranian Studies; President, British Institute of Persian Studies
data.bio:
Ali Massoud Ansari is a British-Iranian historian and one of the leading academic voices on modern Iran in the English-speaking world. Born in Rome in 1967, he is a member of the extended Pahlavi family — his father, Mohammad Ali Massoud Ansari, is a cousin of [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi), the last Empress of Iran.

Ansari was educated at University College London (BA), King's College London (MA), and the School of Oriental and African Studies (PhD, 1998), where his doctoral dissertation examined 'Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi and the Myth of Imperial Authority.' He is Professor of Modern History with reference to the Middle East at the University of St Andrews, where he founded and directs the [Institute for Iranian Studies](/orgs/institute-for-iranian-studies-st-andrews). He also serves as President of the [British Institute of Persian Studies](/orgs/british-institute-of-persian-studies), Senior Associate Fellow at [RUSI](/orgs/royal-united-services-institute), and Associate Fellow at [Chatham House](/orgs/chatham-house). In 2016 he was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society of Edinburgh.

His extensive bibliography includes *Modern Iran Since 1921: The Pahlavis and After* (2003), *Confronting Iran* (2006), *The Politics of Nationalism in Modern Iran* (2012), *Iran: A Very Short Introduction* (2014), and *Iran, Islam and Democracy* (3rd ed., 2019). He is a regular commentator on Iranian affairs for the BBC, The Guardian, and The Independent.

His brother, [Ahmad Ali Masoud Ansari](/people/ahmad-ali-ansari), was a confidant of the Pahlavi court who later published controversial memoirs alleging fraud and feuds within the royal family, including claims that [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) was funded by Saudi Arabia and the CIA.

nodes.label: Ali Meshkini
nodes.role: Chairman of the Assembly of Experts for Leadership (1983–2007)

data.role: Chairman of the Assembly of Experts for Leadership (1983–2007)
data.bio: Ayatollah Ali Meshkini was a senior cleric who served as the first and longest-serving chairman of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) for Leadership from its establishment in 1983 until his death in 2007. He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing East Azerbaijan Province. A loyal supporter of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) and later [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), he was based in the Qom seminary and was a member of the [Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom](/orgs/society-of-seminary-teachers-of-qom). His long chairmanship of the Assembly of Experts gave him a pivotal role in the supervision of the Supreme Leader's office, though the assembly rarely exercised its oversight powers during his tenure.

nodes.label: Ali Mohammad Naini
nodes.role: Second Brigadier General; Official IRGC Spokesperson (d. 20 March 2026)

data.role: Second Brigadier General; Official IRGC Spokesperson (d. 20 March 2026)
data.bio: Ali Mohammad Naini served as Second Brigadier General and the official spokesperson of the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). He was killed on 20 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Ali Motahari
nodes.role: Second Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 10th Majlis (2016–2019); MP for Tehran (2008–2020)

data.role: Second Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 10th Majlis (2016–2019); MP for Tehran (2008–2020)
data.bio: Ali Motahari is the son of the martyred Islamic scholar [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) and the brother-in-law of former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani). He represented Tehran for three consecutive terms (8th–10th Majlis, 2008–2020), serving as Second Deputy Speaker in the 10th Majlis (2016–2019). A principlist by formal affiliation, he was widely regarded as a maverick who publicly challenged [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad), criticised [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) political interference, and called for the release of [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) leaders. He was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from the 2020 elections. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran.

nodes.label: Ali Nikzad
nodes.role: Second Deputy Speaker, 11th and 12th Majlis (2020–present)

data.role: Second Deputy Speaker, 11th and 12th Majlis (2020–present)
data.bio: Ali Nikzad is a conservative civil engineer and politician from Ardabil Province who has served as Second Deputy Speaker of the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under Ghalibaf across two consecutive sessions (2020–present). He previously served as Minister of Roads and Urban Development under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2011–2013) and has been a member of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council).

nodes.label: Ali Reza Pahlavi
nodes.role: Prince of Iran; second in line of Pahlavi succession until his death

data.role: Prince of Iran; second in line of Pahlavi succession until his death
data.bio:
Ali Reza Pahlavi was the second son of [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) and [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi), and the younger brother of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi). He was second in line to the Pahlavi succession.

After the 1979 Islamic Revolution, the family went into exile. Ali Reza studied at Princeton University and later pursued graduate studies at Harvard and Columbia, focusing on ancient Iranian history and philology.

He died by suicide on 4 January 2011 in Boston, Massachusetts. His death was a significant personal blow to the Pahlavi family and the Iranian exile community.

nodes.label: Ali Reza Shahvaroghi Farahani
nodes.role: Former Iranian diplomat at Iran's UN Mission, New York; expelled April 2026

data.role: Former Iranian diplomat at Iran's UN Mission, New York; expelled April 2026
data.bio: Ali Reza Shahvaroghi Farahani was an Iranian diplomat stationed at Iran's Permanent Mission to the United Nations in New York. In early April 2026, the US State Department expelled him over unspecified 'national security concerns', as reported by Axios. The expulsion was part of broader US actions to limit Iranian intelligence activities operating under UN mission diplomatic cover in the United States.

sources.title: US expels Iranian diplomat from UN mission over national security concerns

nodes.label: Ali Shadmani
nodes.role: Commander, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters (interim, June 2025)

data.role: Commander, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters (interim, June 2025)
data.bio: Major General Ali Shadmani was a senior [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) commander who briefly held command of the [Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters](/orgs/khatam-al-anbiya-central-headquarters) following the killing of [Gholam Ali Rashid](/people/gholam-ali-rashid) on 13 June 2025. Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) appointed Shadmani as Rashid's successor on the same day. The IDF described Shadmani as Khamenei's closest military adviser and the de facto commander of both the Revolutionary Guards and regular armed forces. He was a veteran of the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), commanding the 32nd Corps of Ansar al-Hussein during that conflict. He joined the IRGC in 1979 following the [Islamic Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Shadmani was killed in an Israeli airstrike on a command facility in Tehran on 17 June 2025, just four days after his appointment, as part of the [Israeli strikes on Iran](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-june-2025). He was succeeded by [Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi](/people/ali-abdollahi-aliabadi).

sources.title: Senior IRGC commander dies of injuries from Israeli attacks, Iran confirms
Israeli airstrikes eliminate senior Iranian military officials

nodes.label: Ali Shamkhani
nodes.role: Former SNSC Secretary (2013–2023); former Defense Minister (1997–2005)

data.role: Former SNSC Secretary (2013–2023); former Defense Minister (1997–2005)
data.bio: Ali Shamkhani is an ethnic Arab from Khuzestan who rose through the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) Navy to become one of Iran's most senior security officials. He commanded the IRGC Navy during the Iran–Iraq War and later served as combined IRGC/Regular Navy commander. [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) appointed him Defense Minister in 1997 — a moderate appointment widely seen as signalling detente — and he served for the full eight years. [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) appointed him Secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) in 2013, where he served until 2023. He brokered the 2023 China-mediated Iran–Saudi Arabia normalisation agreement. He was replaced as SNSC Secretary by Ali Akbar Ahmadian in 2023.

nodes.label: Ali Shariati
nodes.role: Sociologist; revolutionary Islamic thinker

data.role: Sociologist; revolutionary Islamic thinker
data.bio: Influential sociologist and Islamic thinker who helped organize the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) from France. His lectures at the [Hussainiyyeh Ershad](/orgs/hussainiyyeh-ershad) in Tehran in the early 1970s shaped a generation of revolutionary youth. Advocated for a progressive, anti-imperialist Islam. Imprisoned twice under the Pahlavi regime (3.3 years total). Died under suspicious circumstances in Southampton, England on 18 June 1977 — many believe he was killed by [SAVAK](/orgs/savak).

nodes.label: Ali Vaez
nodes.role: Iran Project Director, International Crisis Group

data.role: Iran Project Director, International Crisis Group
data.bio: Ali Vaez is the Iran project director at the [International Crisis Group](/orgs/international-crisis-group) and a protégé of [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley). He was identified as a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023). Leaked emails showed he shared a draft nuclear policy article with [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani) before publication; the article was subsequently published in the National Interest in June 2014 under a modified title. He attended the inaugural IEI meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg in May 2014 and advised Malley's Iran negotiating team.

nodes.label: Ali Younessi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1999–2005)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1999–2005)
data.bio: Ali Younessi served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence for most of [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s presidency (1999–2005), replacing [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) who resigned over the 1998 chain murders of dissidents. A cleric and jurist, Younessi was seen as a reformist appointment and took steps to investigate intelligence operations that had targeted journalists and intellectuals. He was later appointed as [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s special assistant for ethnic minorities and religious affairs (2014–2021). In 2020 he was arrested on charges of espionage but released.

nodes.label: Alireza Arafi
nodes.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Guardian Council member (2019–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Alireza Arafi is a senior cleric and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) member appointed by Khamenei in 2019. He has served as president of the Islamic Seminaries of Iran (Hawza). Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he was appointed as the Guardian Council's representative on the three-member interim leadership council, alongside [President Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) and Chief Justice Mohseni-Ejei, pursuant to Article 111 of the constitution. He has been mentioned as a potential future Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Alireza Bayat
nodes.role: Deputy for Internal Security, Supreme National Security Council (d. 17 March 2026)

data.role: Deputy for Internal Security, Supreme National Security Council (d. 17 March 2026)
data.bio: Alireza Bayat served as Deputy for Internal Security at the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council). He was killed on 17 March 2026 in the same Israeli airstrike that killed [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani), as part of the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave). The SNSC strike also killed [Morteza Larijani](/people/morteza-larijani) and [Ali Bateni](/people/ali-bateni).

sources.title: Iran's wartime leader isn't a dealmaker

nodes.label: Alireza Bi-Azar
nodes.role: Intelligence Officer, Quds Force Lebanon Corps (d. 2026)

data.role: Intelligence Officer, Quds Force Lebanon Corps (d. 2026)
data.bio: Alireza Bi-Azar served as an intelligence officer in the [IRGC Quds Force Lebanon Corps](/orgs/irgc-quds-force-lebanon-corps), the unit responsible for managing IRGC operations in Lebanon and coordinating with [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah). He was killed during the 2026 war as part of the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave) and associated operations targeting the Lebanon Corps.

sources.title: Quds Force Lebanon Corps Intelligence Officer Killed

nodes.label: Alireza Bigdeli
nodes.role: Former Iranian Ambassador to Azerbaijan (2016–2021) and Ukraine

data.role: Former Iranian Ambassador to Azerbaijan (2016–2021) and Ukraine
data.bio: Alireza Bigdeli served as Iranian Ambassador to Azerbaijan from 2016 to 2021 and subsequently to Ukraine. Azerbaijani intelligence sources and Iranian opposition outlets allege that Bigdeli had an IRGC intelligence background and that his posting in Baku was used to coordinate IRGC intelligence operations targeting Israeli interests in Azerbaijan. These allegations have not been confirmed by independent reporting and are categorized as alleged.

nodes.label: Alireza Tangsiri
nodes.role: Former Commander, IRGC Navy (2018–2026); killed in Israeli airstrike March 2026

data.role: Former Commander, IRGC Navy (2018–2026); killed in Israeli airstrike March 2026
data.bio: Alireza Tangsiri commanded the IRGC Navy from August 2018, personally appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) to replace [Ali Fadavi](/people/ali-fadavi). Born in a Persian Gulf fishing community, he built the IRGC Navy's 'swarm tactics' doctrine — coordinated attacks by hundreds of fast-attack craft and missiles designed to overwhelm US carrier groups. He was the public face of Iran's de facto blockade of the Strait of Hormuz during the 2026 Iran war. He was killed on 26 March 2026 in an Israeli airstrike at the port of Bandar Abbas, alongside other senior IRGC Navy commanders. The US Treasury had designated him a Specially Designated Global Terrorist in 2019.

nodes.label: Amin Hossein Rahimi
nodes.role: Minister of Justice (2021–present)

data.role: Minister of Justice (2021–present)
data.bio: Amin Hossein Rahimi has served as Iran's Minister of Justice since [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi)'s cabinet in 2021, retained by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in 2024. He is a career jurist who began working in Iran's judiciary at age 18. He served as Chief of the General and Revolutionary Courts in Tehran and as Political and Security Deputy of the Prosecutor General before his ministerial appointment. He is regarded as a loyalist of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and a product of the hardliner judicial network associated with [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Amir Abbas Hoveyda
nodes.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1965–1977)

data.role: Prime Minister of Iran (1965–1977)
data.bio:
Amir Abbas Hoveyda served as Prime Minister of Iran for twelve years (1965–1977), making him the longest-serving prime minister in Iranian history. A technocrat and loyal servant of [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi), he oversaw a period of rapid modernisation and economic growth fuelled by oil revenues, implementing the Shah's White Revolution reforms. He was also a founding member of the Iran-e Novin (New Iran) Party.

Following the [1979 Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Hoveyda was arrested in the early weeks of February 1979. Despite having been dismissed from office two years earlier and having made no attempt to flee, he was held for months before being brought before the Islamic Revolutionary Courts. On 7 April 1979 he was summarily tried and executed by [Sadegh Khalkhali](/people/sadegh-khalkhali)'s revolutionary tribunal — the most prominent victim of the [post-revolution executions of 1979](/events/post-revolution-executions-1979). He was 59 years old. His trial lasted a matter of hours and denied him adequate legal representation.

nodes.label: Amir Ali Hajizadeh
nodes.role: Former Commander, IRGC Aerospace Force (2009–2025); killed in Israeli airstrike June 2025

data.role: Former Commander, IRGC Aerospace Force (2009–2025); killed in Israeli airstrike June 2025
data.bio: Amir Ali Hajizadeh commanded the [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force) from 2009 until his death in an Israeli airstrike on 13 June 2025, as part of the large-scale Israeli operation that also killed [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) and other senior commanders. He joined the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) in 1980 as a sniper during the Iran–Iraq War and was mentored by Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam — the 'Father of Iran's Missile Program' — who brought him into the nascent missile programme. He organised Iran's first missile unit 'Hadid' and rose to command Iran's entire ballistic missile and UAV arsenal. He directed the January 2020 missile strikes against US bases in Iraq following [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani)'s assassination, and oversaw Iran's April and October 2024 direct missile and drone strikes against Israel. He was in command when IRGC missiles shot down Ukrainian flight PS752 on 8 January 2020, killing all 176 aboard — he admitted 'full responsibility' three days later. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) awarded him the Order of Fath in October 2024 for the strikes on Israel.

nodes.label: Amir Hossein Faghihi
nodes.role: Head, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute (AEOI); former AEOI vice president; faculty, Shahid Beheshti University; killed June 2025

data.role: Head, Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute (AEOI); former AEOI vice president; faculty, Shahid Beheshti University; killed June 2025
data.bio:
Amir Hossein Faghihi was a senior nuclear official who served as head of the [Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute](/orgs/nuclear-science-and-technology-research-institute), the principal research arm of the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran). He previously served as vice president of the AEOI and held a faculty position at [Shahid Beheshti University](/orgs/shahid-beheshti-university). He was killed on 13 June 2025 in Israeli airstrikes — the opening strikes of [Operation Rising Lion](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-june-2025).

Note on spelling: His name appears in some sources as 'Amir Hassan Fakhahi' — these refer to the same individual; the canonical romanisation is Amir Hossein Faghihi.

nodes.label: Amir-Hossein Ghazizadeh Hashemi
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker, 11th Majlis (2020–2024); 2021 presidential candidate

data.role: First Deputy Speaker, 11th Majlis (2020–2024); 2021 presidential candidate
data.bio: Amir-Hossein Ghazizadeh Hashemi is a hardline politician from Mashhad who served as First Deputy Speaker of the 11th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under Ghalibaf (2020–2024). He was one of only seven candidates approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from 585 applicants in the 2021 presidential election, finishing fourth. A physician by training, he represents the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned principlist networks that swept the 11th Majlis.

nodes.label: Amir-Hossein Sabeti
nodes.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); former state television host

data.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); former state television host
data.bio: Amir-Hossein Sabeti is a young conservative activist and state television anchor who placed second in the Tehran ballot in the 2024 parliamentary election, making him one of the most prominent new faces in the 12th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). Born in 1989, he represents the post-war generation of [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned conservatives who entered politics via media rather than clerical routes. He is a member of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability). In 2012 he received a suspended sentence from a Revolutionary Court for protesting [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani)'s parliamentary policies on universities.

nodes.label: Amir Parviz Pouyan
nodes.role: Co-founder of the Ahmadzadeh-Pouyan group and theorist of armed struggle

data.role: Co-founder of the Ahmadzadeh-Pouyan group and theorist of armed struggle
data.bio: Amir Parviz Pouyan (1946–1971) was an Iranian Marxist revolutionary and co-founder of the underground cell known as the Ahmadzadeh-Pouyan group, which merged with [Bijan Jazani](/people/bijan-jazani)'s organisation in 1971 to establish the [Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas). Alongside [Massoud Ahmadzadeh](/people/massoud-ahmadzadeh), Pouyan developed the theoretical justification for armed struggle against the Shah's regime. His essay *The Necessity of Armed Struggle and the Refutation of the Theory of Survival* (1970) became one of the foundational texts of the Iranian guerrilla left, arguing that passive politics was both morally and strategically bankrupt under a dictatorship. Pouyan participated in the [Siahkal incident](/events/siahkal-incident-1971) and was killed in a confrontation with [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) forces in May 1971, just months after the founding operation, becoming one of the movement's first martyrs.

nodes.label: Arang Keshavarzian
nodes.role: Associate Professor of Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies, New York University

data.role: Associate Professor of Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies, New York University
data.bio:
Arang Keshavarzian is an associate professor of Middle Eastern and Islamic Studies at New York University (NYU), where he focuses on the political economy of Iran and the Persian Gulf. He received his doctorate from Princeton University.

His book *Bazaar and State in Iran: The Politics of the Tehran Marketplace* (2007, Cambridge University Press) is a landmark study of the political economy of Iran's commercial sector, examining how the Tehran bazaar — a dense network of merchants, guilds, and credit relationships — both shaped and was shaped by state power across the twentieth century and into the Islamic Republic era. The book argues that the bazaar's social organization enabled collective action and that its relationship with the state explains much about Iran's political trajectory.

Keshavarzian also co-edited *Illiberal Politics in Neoliberal Times: Culture, Power, and Meaning in the Arab World* and has written extensively on labor, class, and political mobilization in the Gulf. His broader research agenda encompasses the political economy of Gulf states, labor migration, and the intersection of urban space and political power in the Middle East. He takes a political-economy and sociological approach to Iranian and Gulf politics, situated within a critical social science framework.

sources.title: Bazaar and State in Iran — Cambridge University Press

nodes.label: Arash Azizi
nodes.role: Historian and journalist; Lecturer, Clemson University

data.role: Historian and journalist; Lecturer, Clemson University
data.bio: Arash Azizi is an Iranian historian and journalist, currently a lecturer at Clemson University. He is the author of *The Shadow Commander: Soleimani, the US, and Iran's Global Ambitions* (2020), a biography of [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) that examines the Quds Force commander's operational career and the strategic logic of Iran's regional expansion. He is also the author of *What Iranians Want: Women, Life, Freedom* (2023), an account of the 2022 [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022) and the generational shift inside Iran. Azizi is a regular commentator on Iranian politics, the Woman Life Freedom movement, and Iranian diaspora politics, contributing to publications including The Atlantic, Foreign Affairs, and the New York Times.

nodes.label: Ariane Tabatabai
nodes.role: Pentagon Chief of Staff for Asst. Secretary of Defense for Special Operations

data.role: Pentagon Chief of Staff for Asst. Secretary of Defense for Special Operations
data.bio: Ariane Tabatabai is a German-Iranian political scientist who served as chief of staff to the Assistant Secretary of Defense for Special Operations and Low-Intensity Conflict at the Pentagon. She was recruited in 2014 by [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh) as a founding member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert [Iranian Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) influence operation coordinated through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis). Leaked emails showed she consulted IPIS head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani) about conference attendance — including an Israeli conference invitation — and informed him of opportunities to testify before Congress. In February 2021, [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley) hired her to join his Iran team at the State Department, and she subsequently moved to the Pentagon. After the [IEI leak](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) in September 2023, the Pentagon launched a security review of her clearance; she was ultimately retained and her top-secret SCI clearance maintained.

nodes.label: Asadollah Badfar
nodes.role: Head, Basij Forces (d. March 2026)

data.role: Head, Basij Forces (d. March 2026)
data.bio: Asadollah Badfar served as the head of the [Basij Forces](/orgs/basij), the paramilitary volunteer militia under the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). He was killed in an Israeli airstrike in early March 2026, with his funeral reported around 9 March 2026 as part of the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Ashraf Rabiei
nodes.role: Early MEK leader; first wife of Massoud Rajavi

data.role: Early MEK leader; first wife of Massoud Rajavi
data.bio: Ashraf Rabiei studied physics at Aryamehr University (now Sharif University) and joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) in the early 1970s. She married [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) in 1980. In the 1980 parliamentary election, she received over 300,000 votes but did not win a seat. She was killed alongside [Moussa Khiabani](/people/moussa-khiabani) on 8 February 1982 when the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) raided their safehouse in Tehran. Camp Ashraf in Iraq was named in her honor.

nodes.label: Assadollah Assadi
nodes.role: MOIS Department 312 officer; former Third Secretary, Iranian Embassy Vienna

data.role: MOIS Department 312 officer; former Third Secretary, Iranian Embassy Vienna
data.bio: Assadollah Assadi is a senior officer of Iran's [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) assigned to [MOIS Department 312](/orgs/mois-department-312), the directorate responsible for external operations in Europe. He served under diplomatic cover as Third Secretary at the Iranian Embassy in Vienna. In June 2018, he orchestrated the [2018 Villepinte bomb plot](/events/villepinte-bomb-plot-2018) — a plan to bomb the annual Free Iran rally of the National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI/MEK) at Villepinte, France, which drew tens of thousands of attendees and prominent Western politicians. Assadi recruited a Belgium-based Iranian couple to plant the device and personally delivered 500 grams of TATP explosives and a detonator to them in Luxembourg. He was arrested in Germany on his Iranian diplomatic passport while attempting to travel back to Austria. In February 2021, an Antwerp court convicted him to 20 years in prison — marking the first conviction in Europe of an Iranian diplomat-intelligence officer for directing a terrorist attack on European soil. In May 2023 he was exchanged for Belgian aid worker Olivier Vandecasteele.

sources.title: Belgium sentences Iranian diplomat to 20 years for bomb plot

nodes.label: Ataollah Mohajerani
nodes.role: Former Minister of Culture and Islamic Guidance (1997–2000); writer; reformist politician

data.role: Former Minister of Culture and Islamic Guidance (1997–2000); writer; reformist politician
data.bio: Ataollah Mohajerani served as [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s Minister of Culture and Islamic Guidance from 1997 to 2000, presiding over the most open period for the Iranian press since the revolution. Under his stewardship, hundreds of new newspapers and magazines were licensed, including the reformist dailies that employed journalists like [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) and [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin). He survived a parliamentary impeachment vote in 1999 with Khatami's backing but resigned in 2000 under mounting pressure from hardliners. He co-authored the 2010 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) manifesto with [Abdolkarim Soroush](/people/abdolkarim-soroush), [Mohsen Kadivar](/people/mohsen-kadivar), and [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji). He has lived in exile in London since 2009.

nodes.label: Azim Esmaili Khosrowabadi
nodes.role: Deputy Commander, Basij Forces (d. 17 March 2026)

data.role: Deputy Commander, Basij Forces (d. 17 March 2026)
data.bio: Azim Esmaili Khosrowabadi served as Deputy Commander of the [Basij Forces](/orgs/basij) under the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). He was killed on 17 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave) targeting senior Iranian military leadership.

sources.title: Senior Basij Deputies Killed in Israeli Strikes

nodes.label: Aziz Nasirzadeh
nodes.role: Minister of Defense (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Defense (2024–present)
data.bio: Aziz Nasirzadeh is Iran's Minister of Defense under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian), appointed in August 2024. A senior air force commander, he previously served as commander of the Iranian Air Force and as deputy chief of staff of the armed forces. His appointment to the defense portfolio followed the tenure of [Mohammad Reza Ashtiani](/people/mohammad-reza-ashtiani) under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Babak Zanjani
nodes.role: Convicted sanctions-evasion tycoon; founder of Sorinet Group; orchestrated billions in illicit Iranian oil sales

data.role: Convicted sanctions-evasion tycoon; founder of Sorinet Group; orchestrated billions in illicit Iranian oil sales
data.bio:
Babak Zanjani is an Iranian billionaire businessman convicted of large-scale embezzlement and sanctions evasion. Between 2010 and 2013, operating through his UAE-based Sorinet Group and a network of over 60 shell companies, he sold millions of barrels of Iranian crude oil on behalf of the [Ministry of Petroleum](/orgs/ministry-of-petroleum) in violation of international sanctions, generating an estimated $17.5 billion in revenue for the Iranian government.

His operations were authorised by senior officials during [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s second presidential term, including Oil Minister [Rostam Ghasemi](/people/rostam-ghasemi), formerly the head of [Khatam al-Anbiya](/orgs/khatam-al-anbiya). Zanjani was accused of withholding $2.7 billion owed to the state.

The European Union sanctioned Zanjani in December 2012 as a key facilitator of Iranian oil deals, and the United States designated him in April 2013 under Executive Order 13382. He was arrested on 30 December 2013 and sentenced to death on 6 March 2016 for 'spreading corruption on earth.' The Supreme Court confirmed the sentence in December 2016.

In April 2024, his death sentence was commuted to 20 years' imprisonment after he returned approximately $2.1 billion in assets, with clemency approved by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). By early 2025, Zanjani was conditionally released and resumed business activity through the newly registered Avan Group (DotOne Group), which quickly expanded into rail transport, crypto trading, and other sectors. In 2025, the United States and United Kingdom imposed fresh sanctions on Zanjani and his crypto exchanges for continued [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) financing.

nodes.label: Bahram Hosseini Motlagh
nodes.role: Brigadier General; Armed Forces Planning and Operations (d. 2 March 2026)

data.role: Brigadier General; Armed Forces Planning and Operations (d. 2 March 2026)
data.bio: Bahram Hosseini Motlagh served as a Brigadier General in Armed Forces Planning and Operations. He was killed on 2 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave), in the same strike that also killed [Hassan-Ali Tajik](/people/hassan-ali-tajik) and [Mohsen Darrebaghi](/people/mohsen-darrebaghi).

sources.title: Senior Armed Forces Officers Killed in March 2 Strike

nodes.label: Behnam Ben Taleblu
nodes.role: Senior Director, Iran Program, Foundation for Defense of Democracies

data.role: Senior Director, Iran Program, Foundation for Defense of Democracies
data.bio: Behnam Ben Taleblu is senior director of the Iran Program at the [Foundation for Defense of Democracies](/orgs/foundation-for-defense-of-democracies) in Washington. An Iranian-American analyst, he specialises in Iranian ballistic missiles, nuclear weapons-related activities, the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and its proxy networks, and US sanctions policy. He is a frequent witness before the US Congress, providing expert testimony on Iran's military capabilities and the effectiveness of maximum-pressure sanctions. His analysis is broadly hawkish, advocating for robust sanctions enforcement and firmness on the nuclear file. He contributes to FDD's Long War Journal and is widely cited in mainstream US media.

sources.title: Behnam Ben Taleblu — Foundation for Defense of Democracies

nodes.label: Behnam Rezaei
nodes.role: Director, IRGC Navy Intelligence (d. 26 March 2026)

data.role: Director, IRGC Navy Intelligence (d. 26 March 2026)
data.bio: Behnam Rezaei served as Intelligence Director of the [IRGC Navy](/orgs/irgc-navy) under the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). He was killed on 26 March 2026 in the same Israeli airstrike at Bandar Abbas that killed [IRGC Navy Commander Alireza Tangsiri](/people/alireza-tangsiri), as part of the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

sources.title: IRGC Navy Leadership Killed in Bandar Abbas Strike

nodes.label: Behzad Nabavi
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (alternating 2000–2004); Co-founder, OMIIR

data.role: First Deputy Speaker of Parliament, 6th Majlis (alternating 2000–2004); Co-founder, OMIIR
data.bio: Behzad Nabavi is a senior figure of the Islamic left turned democratic reformist. An electrical engineer, he fought against the Shah and was imprisoned before the revolution. He co-founded the Islamic Revolution Committees (komiteh) and a forerunner to the Ministry of Intelligence. Under PM Mousavi he served as minister for executive affairs and minister of heavy industry. He was also Iran's chief negotiator in the 1981 [Algiers Accords](/events/algiers-accords-1981) that ended the hostage crisis. In the 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) he alternated as First Deputy Speaker with [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami). He was arrested after the 2009 elections and sentenced to six years.

nodes.label: Bijan Jazani
nodes.role: Intellectual founder of the Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas

data.role: Intellectual founder of the Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas
data.bio: Bijan Jazani (1938–1975) was an Iranian Marxist theorist and revolutionary who is regarded as the intellectual father of the [Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas). Born into a family with ties to the [Tudeh Party](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran), he studied political science and became politically active in the 1950s. In 1963 he founded the underground group that would later merge with the Ahmadzadeh-Pouyan group to form the Fadaiyan-e Khalq in 1971. Arrested by [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) in 1968, Jazani continued his theoretical work from prison, writing influential pamphlets on armed struggle and mass mobilisation. Unlike some contemporaries, he argued that armed propaganda should serve as a catalyst for broader working-class political organisation rather than an end in itself. He was executed on 19 April 1975 in Evin prison, along with eight other political prisoners, under circumstances widely regarded as extrajudicial killing.

nodes.label: Bijan Namdar Zanganeh
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (1997–2005, 2013–2021); longest-serving Oil Minister in the Islamic Republic

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (1997–2005, 2013–2021); longest-serving Oil Minister in the Islamic Republic
data.bio: Bijan Namdar Zanganeh served as Iran's Minister of Petroleum under both [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) (1997–2005) and [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013–2021) — twelve years in total, the longest oil ministerial tenure in the Islamic Republic's history. He is closely associated with the technocratic and pragmatist camp, and with Iran's efforts to attract foreign investment and maintain OPEC standing. His nomination by Rouhani was criticised by hardline newspapers. He is regarded as one of the most capable technocrats in Iranian economic history, having overseen Iran's oil expansion during the Khatami years and the post-JCPOA production recovery. He was replaced by [Javad Owji](/people/javad-owji) under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

nodes.label: Catherine Perez-Shakdam
nodes.role: Middle East analyst and commentator; former Director of Programs at Shafaqna Institute; Research Fellow at Henry Jackson Society

data.role: Middle East analyst and commentator; former Director of Programs at Shafaqna Institute; Research Fellow at Henry Jackson Society
data.bio:
Catherine Perez-Shakdam is a French-born analyst of Middle Eastern affairs whose career spans two sharply distinct phases, making her one of the more unusual figures to have intersected with the Islamic Republic's media ecosystem. Born in 1982 in France into a secular Jewish family, she later studied at the University of London, earning degrees in finance and communications. At university she married a Yemeni Sunni Muslim man and presented herself publicly as a convert to Shia Islam — an identity she maintained for nearly a decade as she built a career as an Iran-sympathetic commentator.

During the period roughly 2013–2021, Perez-Shakdam contributed extensively to outlets aligned with or operated by the Iranian state, including [Mehr News Agency](/orgs/mehr-news-agency), [Tasnim News Agency](/orgs/tasnim-news-agency), and [Mashregh News](/orgs/mashregh-news). Content she contributed was also published on Khamenei.ir, the official website of the Supreme Leader. From 2015 to 2017 she served as Director of Programs and Lead Analyst at the [Shafaqna Institute for Middle East Studies](/orgs/shafaqna-institute-for-middle-east-studies), a UK-based think tank with a Shia-oriented, Tehran-sympathetic editorial position. She also consulted for the United Nations Security Council Panel of Experts on Yemen in 2016–2017, specializing in war economy and sanctions evasion — access that gave her significant institutional credibility. During Iran's 2017 presidential election she traveled to Iran and conducted an interview with then-candidate [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi), obtaining access to elite political circles.

In November 2021 she published a disclosure on the Times of Israel blog platform, revealing that she was Jewish and had never genuinely converted to Islam, framing her prior activities as an effort to gain access to and document the Iranian regime from within. Iranian state media immediately deleted all her published content and regime officials publicly denied any association with her.

Following her disclosure she pivoted to explicitly anti-regime advocacy. In June 2022 she joined the [Henry Jackson Society](/orgs/henry-jackson-society) as a Research Fellow focused on countering Iranian influence. She subsequently became Director of [We Believe in Israel](/orgs/we-believe-in-israel), a British Israeli advocacy organization.

The Iranian regime's response to her story has been characterized by mutually contradictory disinformation. Some regime-aligned media and politicians — most notably former reformist MP Mostafa Kavakebian, who in July 2025 claimed on state television that she had maintained sexual relationships with 120 senior officials — pushed a sensationalized espionage narrative. However, Iran's own security and judicial bodies publicly contradicted these claims, stating that no documents indicated espionage or illegal conduct during her time in Iran, and Tehran's prosecutor charged Kavakebian himself for making unsupported public allegations. Neither Israel nor any Western intelligence service has confirmed any operational relationship with her. The "Mossad superspy" framing is best understood as a regime face-saving narrative — reframing the embarrassment of having published a Jewish analyst on state platforms, including the Supreme Leader's own website, as evidence of a sophisticated infiltration operation rather than a failure of due diligence.

nodes.label: Cristina Fernández de Kirchner
nodes.role: President of Argentina (2007–2015); accused by prosecutor Alberto Nisman of treason for the 2013 MOU with Iran

data.role: President of Argentina (2007–2015); accused by prosecutor Alberto Nisman of treason for the 2013 MOU with Iran
data.bio: Cristina Fernández de Kirchner served as President of Argentina from 2007 to 2015, succeeding her husband Néstor Kirchner. During her second term, her government signed the [Iran–Argentina MOU](/events/iran-argentina-mou-2013) on 27 January 2013 — a memorandum of understanding with the Iranian government that established a joint truth commission to reinvestigate the [AMIA bombing](/events/amia-bombing-1994) of 1994. In January 2015, AMIA special prosecutor [Alberto Nisman](/people/alberto-nisman) filed a 289-page charging document accusing Kirchner and Foreign Minister [Jorge Taiana](/people/jorge-taiana) of treason and cover-up, alleging that the MOU was designed to shield Iranian suspects from Interpol red notices and Argentine prosecution in exchange for oil and trade concessions. On 18 January 2015 — the night before Nisman was scheduled to testify before Congress — he was found dead in his apartment. His death, initially ruled a suicide, was determined to be homicide by a federal court in 2019. The investigation into who ordered the killing and the validity of the treason charges against Kirchner remain contested and politically charged issues in Argentina.

sources.title: Argentine prosecutor Alberto Nisman found dead

nodes.label: Danny Postel
nodes.role: Journalist, editor, and Iran human-rights activist

data.role: Journalist, editor, and Iran human-rights activist
data.bio:
Danny Postel is an American journalist, editor, and activist specializing in Iran, human rights, and Middle East politics. He co-edited *The People Reloaded: The Green Movement and the Struggle for Iran's Future* (2010, Melville House) with [Nader Hashemi](/people/nader-hashemi), a volume that gathered analysis and commentary on the 2009 Green Movement protests and the IRI's violent repression of the democratic opposition.

Postel is a contributing editor at Dissent magazine and has written extensively on Iranian civil society, political prisoners, and the human rights dimensions of US-Iran policy. He advocates for solidarity with Iranian democratic and labor movements, situating his work within a left internationalist framework that is sharply critical of the Islamic Republic while also opposing US military intervention in Iran.

He has contributed to The Nation, In These Times, and various academic publications, and is affiliated with several human rights advocacy networks focused on Iran. His collaboration with Nader Hashemi on The People Reloaded placed him in the center of English-language left discourse on the 2009 Green Movement.

nodes.label: Dariush Forouhar
nodes.role: Secretary-general of Nation of Iran Party; former Minister of Labour; assassinated 1998

data.role: Secretary-general of Nation of Iran Party; former Minister of Labour; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Dariush Forouhar was the leader of the [Nation of Iran Party](/orgs/nation-of-iran-party) (Hezb-e Mellat-e Iran), one of the oldest nationalist-democratic parties in Iran. A veteran of the 1953 oil nationalisation movement, he briefly served as Minister of Labour in the provisional government after the 1979 revolution. He became one of the most outspoken critics of the Islamic Republic, openly calling for free elections and the rule of law from his Tehran home, which served as an informal political salon. On the night of 22 November 1998, agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) entered his home and stabbed him 26 times. His wife [Parvaneh Forouhar](/people/parvaneh-forouhar) was stabbed 25 times the same night. Their murders, along with the killings of Mohammad Mokhtari and Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh weeks later, became the catalyst for the public exposure of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). The home had been under 24-hour MOIS surveillance. Lawyer [Shirin Ebadi](/people/shirin-ebadi) represented the Forouhar family in the subsequent proceedings. Forouhar's killers were sentenced to death but the sentences were commuted at the family's request.

nodes.label: Davoud Alizadeh
nodes.role: IRGC Quds Force Lebanon Corps Commander (d. 2026)

data.role: IRGC Quds Force Lebanon Corps Commander (d. 2026)
data.bio: Davoud Alizadeh served as commander of the [IRGC Quds Force Lebanon Corps](/orgs/irgc-quds-force-lebanon-corps), the primary operational unit managing Iran's relationship with [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah). He assumed effective command of Lebanon Corps operations following the Israeli killing of Ibrahim Aqil in September 2024, inheriting responsibility for coordinating weapons transfers, financial conduits, and Hezbollah military liaison. Alizadeh was killed in an Israeli airstrike on 12 March 2026 during the 2026 Iran war.

sources.title: IDF says it killed senior IRGC Quds Force commander in strike on Tehran

nodes.label: Dina Esfandiary
nodes.role: Senior Advisor, International Crisis Group

data.role: Senior Advisor, International Crisis Group
data.bio: Dina Esfandiary is a political analyst who served as a senior advisor at the [International Crisis Group](/orgs/international-crisis-group). In March 2014, she was recruited alongside [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai) by [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh) at a meeting in Prague to form the core group of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert [Iranian Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) influence operation. Khatibzadeh reported to [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani) that 'we three agreed to be the core group of the IEI.' She attended the inaugural IEI meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg hotel in May 2014. She was hired by ICG during [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley)'s presidency and advised his Iran negotiating efforts.

nodes.label: Djavad Salehi-Isfahani
nodes.role: Professor of Economics, Virginia Tech; specialist on Iranian political economy

data.role: Professor of Economics, Virginia Tech; specialist on Iranian political economy
data.bio:
Djavad Salehi-Isfahani is a professor of economics at Virginia Tech and one of the leading specialists on Iranian political economy, poverty, inequality, and the economic impact of sanctions. He is a nonresident fellow at the Brookings Institution.

His research focuses on labor markets, household welfare, and the distributional effects of economic policy in Iran and the wider Middle East. He has written extensively on how sanctions affect Iranian households and the broader economy, arguing that sanctions disproportionately harm ordinary Iranians while strengthening the regime's control over remaining resources — a position that aligns with the Iranian government's preferred framing on sanctions but that Salehi-Isfahani grounds in empirical household-level data.

He is co-author of *How Sanctions Work: Iran and the Impact of Economic Warfare* (2023, Stanford University Press) with [Vali Nasr](/people/vali-nasr), [Narges Bajoghli](/people/narges-bajoghli), and [Esfandyar Batmanghelidj](/people/esfandyar-batmanghelidj). The book's central argument — that maximum-pressure sanctions are counterproductive and harm civilians without achieving regime change — became a significant intervention in the US sanctions policy debate. One of the original co-authors listed earlier in the project, [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez), was subsequently identified as a participant in the Iran Experts Initiative (IEI), a covert Iranian Foreign Ministry influence operation, adding contested context to the project's analytical framing.

Salehi-Isfahani maintains a blog, *Iran Economics*, that provides regular quantitative analysis of Iranian economic data and is widely cited by Iran analysts.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Mortazavi-Nasb
nodes.role: Basij Commander, Shiraz (d. 22 March 2026)

data.role: Basij Commander, Shiraz (d. 22 March 2026)
data.bio: Ebrahim Mortazavi-Nasb served as the [Basij Forces](/orgs/basij) commander in Shiraz. He was killed on 22 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Ebrahim Raisi
nodes.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)

data.role: 8th President of Iran (2021–2024)
data.bio: Ebrahim Raisi was a hardline cleric who served as head of the judiciary from 2019 before being elected president in 2021 in an election with record-low turnout. He is alleged to have been a member of the 'Death Commission' that ordered the mass execution of political prisoners in 1988. He was subject to US Treasury sanctions since 2019. His presidency was dominated by the Mahsa Amini protests of 2022. He died on 19 May 2024 when his helicopter crashed in foggy conditions near the Azerbaijan border.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Yazdi
nodes.role: Secretary-general of Freedom Movement of Iran (1995–2017); former Foreign Minister

data.role: Secretary-general of Freedom Movement of Iran (1995–2017); former Foreign Minister
data.bio: Pharmacologist and political activist. Organized [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) activities in the United States during exile. Served as foreign minister in [Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan)'s provisional government (1979). Second secretary-general of the Freedom Movement (1995–2017). Repeatedly arrested and imprisoned by the Islamic Republic. Participated in the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Died 27 August 2017.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Zalzadeh
nodes.role: Editor; publisher; owner of Ebtekar publishing house; assassinated 1997

data.role: Editor; publisher; owner of Ebtekar publishing house; assassinated 1997
data.bio: Ebrahim Zalzadeh was the editor of the literary magazine *Me'yar* and owner of Ebtekar publishing house. On 24 February 1997, he was arrested by agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). His body was found half-buried outside Tehran on 29 March 1997. His murder was part of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) campaign targeting Iranian intellectuals and publishers.

nodes.label: Ebrahim Zolfaghari
nodes.role: Spokesperson, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters

data.role: Spokesperson, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters
data.bio: Brigadier General Ebrahim Zolfaghari is the official spokesperson of the [Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters](/orgs/khatam-al-anbiya-central-headquarters), the joint operational command of Iran's armed forces. An IRGC-affiliated officer, Zolfaghari gained international prominence as Iran's primary military spokesperson during periods of heightened tensions with the United States and Israel. He has delivered video addresses to Israeli citizens in broken Hebrew. No individual sanctions designation has been identified.

nodes.label: Ehsan Tabari
nodes.role: Chief ideologue of the Tudeh Party of Iran

data.role: Chief ideologue of the Tudeh Party of Iran
data.bio: Ehsan Tabari (1917–1989) was Iran's most prominent Marxist intellectual and the chief ideologue of the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran). A member of the famous Group of Fifty-Three arrested in 1937, he was among the founders of the Tudeh Party upon his release in 1941. Over four decades he produced an extensive body of Marxist philosophical and literary criticism that profoundly influenced Iranian intellectual life. After the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Tabari was arrested alongside other Tudeh leaders in 1983 and subjected to torture and forced confessions. He appeared on state television renouncing Marxism and praising Islam, confessions widely understood to have been extracted under duress. He died in Evin prison in 1989 from heart failure.

nodes.label: Ellie Geranmayeh
nodes.role: Senior Policy Fellow, European Council on Foreign Relations

data.role: Senior Policy Fellow, European Council on Foreign Relations
data.bio: Ellie Geranmayeh is a senior policy fellow and deputy head of the Middle East and North Africa programme at the [European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR)](/orgs/european-council-on-foreign-relations). She was identified as a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), attending the inaugural meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg in May 2014 alongside other Western-based Iranian academics recruited by the Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions.

nodes.label: Emadeddin Baghi
nodes.role: Investigative journalist; human rights activist; writer for Neshat and Fath on chain murders

data.role: Investigative journalist; human rights activist; writer for Neshat and Fath on chain murders
data.bio: Emadeddin Baghi is an investigative journalist and human rights defender who co-authored, alongside [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), the landmark chain murders investigations in [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper), and [Fath](/orgs/fath-newspaper). He was a key source alongside [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian). His September 1999 article in Neshat criticising capital punishment was the stated pretext for that paper's closure and for Shamsolvaezin's prosecution. Baghi himself was arrested on 29 May 2000 and sentenced to five and a half years in prison for 'undermining national security,' later reduced on appeal to three years. He went on to found the Society for the Defense of Prisoners' Rights in Iran, becoming a leading human rights NGO figure.

nodes.label: Ervand Abrahamian
nodes.role: Distinguished Professor emeritus of history, CUNY; historian of modern Iran

data.role: Distinguished Professor emeritus of history, CUNY; historian of modern Iran
data.bio:
Ervand Abrahamian (born 1940) is widely regarded as the foremost Western historian of modern Iran. Born in Tehran to an Armenian-Iranian family, he received his doctorate from Oxford University and spent the bulk of his career at the City University of New York (CUNY), where he is Distinguished Professor emeritus of history.

His landmark work *Iran Between Two Revolutions* (1982) remains the standard scholarly reference for Iranian social and political history from the Constitutional Revolution through the [1979 Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). The book introduced a rigorous class-analysis framework to understanding Iranian political movements and fundamentally shaped how later scholars approached the subject. His follow-up *The Iranian Mojahedin* (1989) was the first serious academic study of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq).

*Tortured Confessions: Prisons and Public Recantations in Modern Iran* (1999) examined the use of show trials and forced confessions from the Pahlavi era through the Islamic Republic, documenting patterns of coercion that extended into the [mass executions of 1988](/events/mass-executions-1988). *A History of Modern Iran* (2008) synthesised his decades of research into an accessible single-volume overview. His most controversial work, *The Coup* (2013), argued that [the 1953 overthrow](/events/iranian-coup-1953) of Prime Minister [Mohammad Mosaddegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh) was primarily a [CIA](/orgs/central-intelligence-agency) and [British intelligence](/orgs/mi6-sis) operation, challenging interpretations that had minimised foreign involvement.

Abrahamian is a critic of the Islamic Republic and has written extensively on its use of political repression, including executions, torture, and forced confessions across the post-revolutionary period. His work serves as a foundational secondary source for virtually every major event and institution in post-1906 Iranian history.

sources.title: Iran Between Two Revolutions — Princeton University Press

nodes.label: Esfandyar Batmanghelidj
nodes.role: Founder and CEO, Bourse & Bazaar Foundation

data.role: Founder and CEO, Bourse & Bazaar Foundation
data.bio: Esfandyar Batmanghelidj is the founder and CEO of the [Bourse & Bazaar Foundation](/orgs/bourse-and-bazaar-foundation), a London-based think tank focused on Iran's economy, trade, and the impact of international sanctions. He is widely regarded as the leading independent analyst of Iranian political economy and sanctions in the Western policy world. His research challenges the effectiveness of maximum-pressure sanctions as a tool for political change, arguing they harm Iranian civil society while strengthening regime-connected economic actors. He co-authored *How Sanctions Work: Iran and the Impact of Economic Warfare* (2023) with [Narges Bajoghli](/people/narges-bajoghli) and others. He publishes regularly in Foreign Affairs, the Financial Times, and contributes to [Chatham House](/orgs/chatham-house) and other policy forums.

nodes.label: Eskandar Momeni
nodes.role: Minister of Interior (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Interior (2024–present)
data.bio: Eskandar Momeni is Iran's Minister of Interior under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian), appointed in August 2024. A senior security official with a military background, he previously served in senior positions in law enforcement and security agencies. He superseded [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi)'s hardline tenure at the Interior Ministry.

nodes.label: Eskandar Sadeghi-Boroujerdi
nodes.role: Lecturer in Politics, Goldsmiths, University of London

data.role: Lecturer in Politics, Goldsmiths, University of London
data.bio:
Eskandar Sadeghi-Boroujerdi is a lecturer in politics at Goldsmiths, University of London, specializing in Iranian political thought, the Islamic Republic, and critical theory. He is the author of *Revolution and Its Discontents: Political Thought and Reform in Iran* (2019, Cambridge University Press), a study of the reformist movement and the ideological contradictions internal to the Islamic Republic's political culture.

His work engages with the intellectual genealogy of Iranian political Islam, post-revolutionary reformism, and the contested legacies of Islamist thought. Drawing on critical theory and political philosophy, Sadeghi-Boroujerdi examines how reformist intellectuals like Abdolkarim Soroush sought to reconcile democratic values with Shia Islamic governance, and why those projects ultimately failed to transform the IRI system.

He writes for academic journals and policy publications including the Journal of Persianate Studies and MERIP, and contributes commentary on Iranian politics to outlets including Jacobin and The Guardian. He represents a critical-left perspective on Iranian politics that is skeptical of both the Islamic Republic and of Western interventionism, situating Iran within broader postcolonial and authoritarian frameworks.

sources.title: Eskandar Sadeghi-Boroujerdi — Goldsmiths, University of London
Revolution and Its Discontents — Cambridge University Press

nodes.label: Esmaeil Khatib
nodes.role: Minister of Intelligence (2021–present)

data.role: Minister of Intelligence (2021–present)
data.bio: Esmaeil Khatib is Iran's Minister of Intelligence, appointed by [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) in 2021 and retained by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in 2024. He joined the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) intelligence wing in 1980 and built a career spanning the Ministry of Intelligence, the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) intelligence apparatus, and the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). Under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) as Judiciary chief, Khatib headed the Judiciary's Protection and Intelligence Centre. He studied jurisprudence under [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) himself, giving him direct personal ties to the Supreme Leader network. The Intelligence Ministry under Khatib has overseen intensified suppression of protest movements including the [2022 Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022).

nodes.label: Esmail Ahmadi
nodes.role: Brigadier General; Head, Basij Intelligence (d. 17 March 2026)

data.role: Brigadier General; Head, Basij Intelligence (d. 17 March 2026)
data.bio: Esmail Ahmadi served as a Brigadier General and head of intelligence for the [Basij Forces](/orgs/basij) under the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). He was killed on 17 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave) targeting senior Iranian military and intelligence leadership.

sources.title: Senior Basij Deputies Killed in Israeli Strikes

nodes.label: Esmail Dehghan
nodes.role: Senior Commander, IRGC Aerospace Force (d. 10 March 2026)

data.role: Senior Commander, IRGC Aerospace Force (d. 10 March 2026)
data.bio: Esmail Dehghan served as a senior commander in the [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force), representing the successor generation to [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) who was killed in June 2025. He was killed on 10 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave) targeting the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s reconstituted aerospace command.

sources.title: IRGC Aerospace Commander Killed in Strike

nodes.label: Esmail Qaani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (2020–2025)
data.bio: Esmail Qaani succeeded [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) as [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) commander on the day of Soleimani's assassination, appointed immediately by Khamenei. A veteran of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and longtime Quds Force deputy, his tenure was marked by criticism that he lacked Soleimani's strategic flair and public profile. He oversaw Quds Force operations during the Gaza conflict from 2023 and the broader escalation with Israel. Iranian sources reported he was killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Ezzatollah Sahabi
nodes.role: Freedom Movement activist; journalist

data.role: Freedom Movement activist; journalist
data.bio: Son of [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi). Co-founder of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran). He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Tehran Province, where he opposed the concentration of power in the velayat-e faqih provisions. Imprisoned 3 times under the Pahlavi regime (10.8 years total) and twice under the Islamic Republic (1.3 years). Active in nationalist-religious opposition politics. Co-founded the [Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists](/orgs/council-of-nationalist-religious-activists) in 2000.

nodes.label: Ezzatollah Zarghami
nodes.role: Head of IRIB (2004–2014); Minister of Cultural Heritage (2021–2024)

data.role: Head of IRIB (2004–2014); Minister of Cultural Heritage (2021–2024)
data.bio: Ezzatollah Zarghami is a former [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) member who served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) for a decade, appointed by Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in 2004 after [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani)'s departure. His tenure covered the controversial [2009 presidential election](/events/presidential-election-iran-2009) and the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), during which IRIB declared [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) the winner before official certification and broadcast no honest coverage of the massive street protests. Under Zarghami, IRIB expanded its international satellite channels including [Press TV](/orgs/press-tv) and aired forced confessions of detained reformist activists and journalists. He was personally sanctioned by the European Union in 2012 for IRIB's role in broadcasting coerced confessions. After leaving IRIB, he was appointed Minister of Cultural Heritage and Tourism by President [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) in 2021.

nodes.label: Fadel Rasoul
nodes.role: Kurdish-Iraqi academic and mediator; killed in Vienna 1989

data.role: Kurdish-Iraqi academic and mediator; killed in Vienna 1989
data.bio: Fadel Rasoul was a Kurdish-Iraqi academic who served as a mediator in the negotiations between the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan (PDKI)](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) and representatives of the Islamic Republic. On 13 July 1989, Rasoul was shot dead in a Vienna apartment alongside PDKI Secretary-General [Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou](/people/abdul-rahman-ghassemlou) and PDKI senior official [Abdullah Ghaderi-Azar](/people/abdullah-ghaderi-azar), in what Austrian and later German investigations established was an assassination carried out by Iranian intelligence operatives. Rasoul was present in his capacity as a trusted neutral facilitator. He was the third victim of the [Ghassemlou assassination](/events/ghassemlou-assassination-1989), and his killing underscored that the Islamic Republic was willing to murder even neutral intermediaries to eliminate Kurdish opposition leadership.

nodes.label: Farah Pahlavi
nodes.role: Last Empress (Shahbanou) of Iran (1959–1979); exiled opposition figurehead

data.role: Last Empress (Shahbanou) of Iran (1959–1979); exiled opposition figurehead
data.bio:
Farah Pahlavi (née Diba) is the widow of Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi and the last Empress of Iran. Born into a prominent family — her father Captain Sohrab Diba was an army officer, her maternal family the Ghotbis were of Gilak origin — she studied architecture at the École Spéciale d'Architecture in Paris before marrying the Shah in 1959.

As Shahbanou she was a patron of the arts, culture, and education, overseeing numerous museums and cultural institutions. After the 1979 Islamic Revolution, she accompanied the Shah into exile, living in Egypt, Morocco, the Bahamas, Mexico, and Panama before settling in the United States after the Shah's death in Cairo in 1980.

She divides her time between Washington, D.C. and Paris, remaining active in exile politics. During the 2022–2023 Woman, Life, Freedom protests, she publicly supported the movement. Her son [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) is a prominent opposition figure who advocates for a secular democratic Iran. Her cousin, Mohammad Ali Massoud Ansari, was the father of historian [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari).

nodes.label: Farzad Kamangar
nodes.role: Kurdish teacher and political prisoner

data.role: Kurdish teacher and political prisoner
data.bio: Farzad Kamangar (1975–2010) was a Kurdish schoolteacher from Kamyaran who became an international symbol of political repression in Iran. Arrested in 2006, he was charged with moharebeh (enmity against God) for alleged membership in [PJAK](/orgs/pjak), charges he denied. His trial lasted only minutes and was condemned by Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch as lacking due process. He was secretly executed on 9 May 2010 in Evin prison, along with four other political prisoners, without his family or lawyer being notified. His letters from prison gained widespread circulation and he became a symbol of the Kurdish rights struggle in Iran.

nodes.label: Farzin Nadimi
nodes.role: Senior fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy; specialist on Iran and Gulf defense, ballistic missiles, and IRGC military capabilities

data.role: Senior fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy; specialist on Iran and Gulf defense, ballistic missiles, and IRGC military capabilities
data.bio:
Farzin Nadimi is a London-based defense and security analyst who serves as a senior fellow at the [Washington Institute for Near East Policy](/orgs/washington-institute-for-near-east-policy). He specializes in Iran and Gulf security, with particular focus on the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s military capabilities, Iran's ballistic missile and drone programs, and the regional projection of Iranian military power.

Nadimi has produced some of the most technically detailed open-source analysis of Iran's missile tests, including the Shahab-3 and Sejjil programs, and has tracked the IRGC's weapons transfers to regional proxies including Hezbollah and the Houthis. His work is widely cited by policymakers, journalists, and intelligence analysts seeking to understand the operational dimensions of Iran's defense posture. He has testified before the U.S. Congress and briefed European governments on Iran's military programs.

sources.title: Farzin Nadimi — Washington Institute for Near East Policy
sources.publisher: Washington Institute for Near East Policy

nodes.label: Fatemeh Hashemi Rafsanjani
nodes.role: Women's rights activist, head of Charity Foundation for Special Diseases

data.role: Women's rights activist, head of Charity Foundation for Special Diseases
data.bio:
Fatemeh Hashemi Rafsanjani is an Iranian women's rights activist and the daughter of former President [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). She heads the Charity Foundation for Special Diseases and serves as secretary-general of the Women's Solidarity Association of Iran.

She is a senior member of the [Moderation and Development Party](/orgs/moderation-and-development-party). In 1995, she led Iran's delegation to the Organisation of Islamic Countries' symposium on women's role in Islamic society.

She studied under [Sadegh Zibakalam](/people/sadegh-zibakalam) at the University of Tehran.

nodes.label: Fereydoun Abbasi-Davani
nodes.role: Former head, Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (2011–2013); former MP; nuclear physicist; killed June 2025

data.role: Former head, Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (2011–2013); former MP; nuclear physicist; killed June 2025
data.bio:
Fereydoun Abbasi-Davani was Iran's most prominent nuclear scientist and a central figure in the Islamic Republic's nuclear establishment. He headed the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran) from 2011 to 2013 under President Ahmadinejad, overseeing uranium enrichment and nuclear fuel activities during the most confrontational phase of Iran's nuclear standoff with the West. He subsequently served in the Iranian parliament (Majlis) as a member for Tehran.

Abbasi-Davani was the subject of a failed Israeli assassination attempt on 29 November 2010 when a magnetic bomb was attached to his car in Tehran. He survived the attack, though a colleague was killed in a simultaneous strike. Western and Israeli intelligence described him as a key figure in Iran's military nuclear programme.

He was sanctioned by the United Nations Security Council, the European Union, and the United States for his role in Iran's nuclear activities. He was killed on 13 June 2025 in Israeli airstrikes on Iran — the opening strikes of what became known as the Twelve-Day War and [Operation Rising Lion](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-june-2025).

sources.title: Iran nuclear scientists killed in 'motorcycle bomb' attacks

nodes.label: Feridoun Verdinejad
nodes.role: Former head of IRNA; former Iranian Ambassador to China (2004–2009)

data.role: Former head of IRNA; former Iranian Ambassador to China (2004–2009)
data.bio: Feridoun Verdinejad served as editor of Kayhan newspaper — Iran's leading hardline daily — and as head of IRNA (Islamic Republic News Agency), two roles that place him at the centre of state-controlled media. He subsequently served as Iran's Ambassador to China from 2004 to 2009. Opposition sources allege an intelligence background tied to MOIS and/or the IRGC Intelligence Organisation; this connection has not been confirmed by official documentation.

nodes.label: Foad Izadi
nodes.role: Associate Professor, Faculty of World Studies, University of Tehran; state media surrogate

data.role: Associate Professor, Faculty of World Studies, University of Tehran; state media surrogate
data.bio:
Foad Izadi is an associate professor at the Faculty of World Studies at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran). He appears regularly on Western outlets including Channel 4 News and BBC World Service, typically introduced as an Iranian academic analyst. This framing obscures his documented function as a state media surrogate for the Islamic Republic.

Izadi has publicly advocated for **targeting US troops** in the Middle East in comments made during periods of elevated US–Iran tension. He has also been documented in discussions about building an **"Iran lobby" in the United States** — an influence operation strategy aimed at shaping American public and policy opinion.

His public commentary consistently mirrors Islamic Republic official positions on US policy, sanctions, and regional affairs. His appearances on British and international television give these positions a veneer of academic credibility.

nodes.label: Ghadir Azarian
nodes.role: IRGC Commander, East Azerbaijan Province (d. 21 March 2026)

data.role: IRGC Commander, East Azerbaijan Province (d. 21 March 2026)
data.bio: Ghadir Azarian served as the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) provincial commander in East Azerbaijan Province. He was killed on 21 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel
nodes.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.role: Speaker of Parliament (2004–2008); Head of Iranian Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.bio: Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel is a philosopher and politician who served as Speaker of Parliament from 2004 to 2008, the first non-cleric to hold the role since the 1979 revolution. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Tehran and has led the Iranian [Academy of Persian Language and Literature](/orgs/iranian-academy-of-persian-language) since 1995. His daughter Zahra married [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei), son of the Supreme Leader, making Haddad-Adel one of the most personally connected figures to the supreme leadership. He is a senior member of the principlist [Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran).

nodes.label: Gholam Ali Rashid
nodes.role: Commander, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters

data.role: Commander, Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters
data.bio: Major General Gholam Ali Rashid was a senior [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) commander who served as commander of the [Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters](/orgs/khatam-al-anbiya-central-headquarters), the highest joint operational command of Iran's armed forces. He simultaneously held the position of deputy commander-in-chief of the Iranian armed forces. A veteran of the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), Rashid participated in all major IRGC operations during that conflict. He held a master's degree in political geography from the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran) and a doctorate in political geography from Tarbiat Modares University. His political activism began in 1978 against the Pahlavi regime; he was imprisoned twice by [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) before the [1979 Islamic Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Rashid was killed on 13 June 2025 in the [Israeli strikes on Iran](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-june-2025) that opened the Twelve-Day War. He was succeeded briefly by [Ali Shadmani](/people/ali-shadmani).

sources.title: Profile: Gholamali Rashid, Iran's Former War Commander
Israeli airstrikes eliminate senior Iranian military officials

nodes.label: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei
nodes.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council

data.role: Chief Justice of Iran (2021–present); member of post-Khamenei interim leadership council
data.bio: Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei is a hardline jurist appointed Chief Justice by Khamenei in July 2021. He previously served as Minister of Intelligence under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2005–2009), as prosecutor-general, and as first deputy chief justice.
