He is regarded as a reliable enforcer of the regime's security priorities. Following Khamenei's death in February 2026, he became one of three members of the constitutional interim leadership council alongside [President Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) member Arafi.

nodes.label: Gholam-Hossein Sadighi
nodes.role: Co-founder of National Front; sociologist and professor; Minister of Interior under Mossadegh

data.role: Co-founder of National Front; sociologist and professor; Minister of Interior under Mossadegh
data.bio:
Gholam-Hossein Sadighi was an Iranian sociologist, politician, and one of the founding figures of the [National Front of Iran](/orgs/national-front-iran). He studied in France and became a prominent academic, eventually serving as a professor and rector at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran).

In 1949 he joined [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh) and [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi) in co-founding the National Front, Iran's principal secular nationalist coalition. During Mossadegh's government he served as Minister of Interior (1952–1953), a key post during the oil nationalisation crisis and the period of confrontation with British interests.

After the [1953 coup d'état](/events/iranian-coup-1953) backed by the CIA and MI6 overthrew Mossadegh's government, Sadighi was arrested and imprisoned. He continued to be associated with the National Front opposition in subsequent decades, maintaining his academic career alongside political activism.

Sadighi was a close colleague of [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi) within the National Front leadership. He represented the secular, intellectual strand of Iranian nationalism that the Front embodied.

nodes.label: Gholam Reza Aghazadeh
nodes.role: Head of the Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (1997–2011); former Vice President

data.role: Head of the Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (1997–2011); former Vice President
data.bio: Gholam Reza Aghazadeh served as head of the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran) (AEOI) for fourteen years under Presidents [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) and [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (1997–2011), the longest tenure of any AEOI head. He also served as Minister of Petroleum (1985–1997) under Khamenei when the latter was president. During his AEOI tenure he oversaw a major expansion of Iran's uranium enrichment capabilities, including the restart of the Natanz enrichment facility and the installation of first-generation centrifuges that became subjects of IAEA scrutiny. His name appears prominently in IAEA Board of Governors reports from the early 2000s concerning Iran's undisclosed nuclear activities. He resigned abruptly in July 2009 amid the post-election political crisis. He is named in connection with efforts to acquire nuclear-related materials for Iran's programme.

sources.title: IAEA Board of Governors Report GOV/2003/75 — Implementation of the NPT Safeguards Agreement in the Islamic Republic of Iran

nodes.label: Gholam Shakuri
nodes.role: IRGC-Quds Force officer; directed the 2011 Arbabsiar assassination plot against Saudi Ambassador Adel Al-Jubeir

data.role: IRGC-Quds Force officer; directed the 2011 Arbabsiar assassination plot against Saudi Ambassador Adel Al-Jubeir
data.bio: Gholam Shakuri is an [IRGC-Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) officer and the principal operational director of the [2011 plot to assassinate Saudi Ambassador Adel Al-Jubeir](/events/arbabsiar-plot-2011) in Washington DC. A Tehran-based officer, Shakuri recruited his cousin [Manssor Arbabsiar](/people/manssor-arbabsiar) — an Iranian-American with no intelligence background — and directed him to arrange the killing through what both believed was a Mexican drug cartel. US prosecutors identified Shakuri as reporting to Abdul Reza Shahlai, a senior IRGC-QF officer. Shakuri is listed on the FBI's wanted list and subject to US Treasury sanctions. He remains at large in Iran.

nodes.label: Gholamhossein Karbaschi
nodes.role: Mayor of Tehran 1989–1998; founder of Hamshahri and Ham-Mihan; reformist politician

data.role: Mayor of Tehran 1989–1998; founder of Hamshahri and Ham-Mihan; reformist politician
data.bio: Gholamhossein Karbaschi served as Mayor of Tehran from 1989 to 1998, transforming the city's infrastructure and cultural life — and the media landscape. He founded [Hamshahri](/orgs/hamshahri-newspaper) in 1992 as the first colour daily in Iran, which grew to 400,000 circulation within three years and became the reformists' most powerful media platform. A close Khatami ally, he managed the reformist coalition's communications strategy. In 1998–1999 he was prosecuted on graft charges widely regarded as politically motivated — part of the hardliners' strategy of destroying the reformist political machine institution by institution. He was sentenced to 18 months in prison (later suspended) and five years' ban from public office. He subsequently ran the newspaper [Ham-Mihan](/orgs/ham-mihan-newspaper). He was also the founder of the Executives of Construction Party (Hezb-e Kargozaran-e Sazandegi), one of the main reformist parties.

nodes.label: Gholamhossein Mohammadnia
nodes.role: Former Senior MOIS Deputy; former Iranian Ambassador to Albania (2016–2018)

data.role: Former Senior MOIS Deputy; former Iranian Ambassador to Albania (2016–2018)
data.bio: Gholamhossein Mohammadnia is a senior deputy within Iran's [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) who was posted as Iran's Ambassador to Albania starting in 2016 — assessed by Western analysts as using the diplomatic position as intelligence cover. In December 2018, the Albanian government declared him persona non grata and expelled him for activities 'damaging to national security'. Specifically, Albanian authorities alleged a plot against members of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK) based at Camp Ashraf-3 near Manez, Albania. His expulsion was the first in a series of actions by Tirana against Iranian intelligence operatives — part of the [Albania expulsions of Iranian diplomats](/events/albania-expulsions-iranian-diplomats-2018) that culminated in the full severing of diplomatic relations in September 2022.

sources.title: Albania Expels Iranian Ambassador Over 'National Security' Concerns

nodes.label: Gholamreza Mehrabi
nodes.role: Deputy Head of Iran's Military Intelligence Directorate

data.role: Deputy Head of Iran's Military Intelligence Directorate
data.bio: Gholamreza Mehrabi served as the Deputy Head of Iran's military intelligence directorate under the [General Staff of the Armed Forces](/orgs/general-staff-of-the-armed-forces). He was killed on 14 June 2025 in the early phase of the [Israeli strikes on Iran](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-june-2025) that initiated the Twelve-Day War.

nodes.label: Gholamreza Molabeygi
nodes.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council

data.role: Jurist member of the Guardian Council
data.bio: Gholamreza Molabeygi is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council), nominated by the Chief Justice and confirmed by the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He is one of six constitutional law experts on the Council.

nodes.label: Gholamreza Rezaian
nodes.role: Major General, FARAJA Intelligence Head (d. 2026)

data.role: Major General, FARAJA Intelligence Head (d. 2026)
data.bio: Gholamreza Rezaian held the rank of Major General and served as head of intelligence for [FARAJA](/orgs/faraja) (Law Enforcement Command of the Islamic Republic of Iran), Iran's national police force. As FARAJA Intelligence Head he oversaw the domestic surveillance and intelligence apparatus of the police, including the monitoring of dissidents, journalists, and suspected opponents of the regime. He was killed on 2 March 2026 in an Israeli airstrike during the 2026 Iran war.

sources.title: Israeli airstrikes kill senior Iranian security officials
Iran's FARAJA intelligence chief killed in Israeli strike

nodes.label: Gholamreza Soleimani
nodes.role: Commander of the Basij Resistance Force (d. 2026)

data.role: Commander of the Basij Resistance Force (d. 2026)
data.bio: Gholamreza Soleimani served as commander of the [Basij Resistance Force](/orgs/basij), the paramilitary volunteer militia subordinate to the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). Under his command, Basij forces played a central role in the violent suppression of the [2025-2026 protests](/events/january-2026-massacres), using live ammunition and other lethal force against civilian demonstrators. He was killed on 17 March 2026 in an Israeli airstrike during the [assassination of Ali Larijani](/events/larijani-assassination-2026), along with other senior officials.

nodes.label: Goli Ameri
nodes.role: Iranian-American businessperson; reported principal beneficiary of Volant Media

data.role: Iranian-American businessperson; reported principal beneficiary of Volant Media
data.bio: Goli Ameri is an Iranian-American businessperson and former US diplomat who served as Assistant Secretary of State for Educational and Cultural Affairs under President George W. Bush (2006–2009). She has been reported as the principal beneficiary of Volant Media Ltd, the UK-registered company that founded and owns [Iran International](/orgs/iran-international), the London-based Persian-language satellite news channel. The ownership structure of Volant Media and its alleged links to Saudi funding have made Goli Ameri a significant figure in debates about the editorial independence and funding of diaspora Persian media.

nodes.label: Habibollah Elghanian
nodes.role: Industrialist and leader of the Iranian Jewish community

data.role: Industrialist and leader of the Iranian Jewish community
data.bio:
Habibollah Elghanian was one of Iran's most prominent industrialists and the informal leader of the Iranian Jewish community. He founded PLASCO, a major plastics and manufacturing conglomerate that became one of Iran's largest private enterprises. He was widely respected as a philanthropist and community leader who built schools, hospitals, and synagogues.

Following the [1979 Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Elghanian was arrested in early 1979. On 9 May 1979, he was tried before [Sadegh Khalkhali](/people/sadegh-khalkhali)'s Islamic Revolutionary Court on charges of "corruption on earth" (Mofsed-e-filarz), espionage for Israel, and contact with Zionists. The trial lasted approximately twenty minutes. He was found guilty and executed the same day as part of the [post-revolution executions of 1979](/events/post-revolution-executions-1979).

His execution sent shockwaves through Iran's 80,000-strong Jewish community, triggering a mass emigration. Within two years, the vast majority of Iranian Jews had fled the country. His case became a landmark moment in the history of Iranian Jews and drew international condemnation.

nodes.label: Habibollah Sayyari
nodes.role: Commander of the Islamic Republic of Iran Navy (2007–2017); Deputy Chief of Staff for Coordination

data.role: Commander of the Islamic Republic of Iran Navy (2007–2017); Deputy Chief of Staff for Coordination
data.bio: Vice Admiral Habibollah Sayyari served as Commander of the [Islamic Republic of Iran Navy](/orgs/islamic-republic-of-iran-navy) (NEDAJA) from 2007 to 2017, making him one of the longest-serving NEDAJA commanders. During his decade-long tenure he oversaw a significant expansion of Iran's naval ambitions: the deployment of Iranian naval flotillas to the Gulf of Aden for anti-piracy operations (Iran's first sustained presence in international waters), port calls in China and Russia, and a substantial domestic shipbuilding and retrofit programme. He commanded the navy during the 2011–2012 period when Iran sent warships through the Suez Canal for the first time since the Islamic Revolution — a deliberate political signal. Following his replacement by Hossein Khanzadi in 2017, Sayyari was appointed Deputy Chief of Staff of the [Artesh](/orgs/artesh) for Coordination, a senior joint-forces role reflecting his continued institutional standing.

nodes.label: Hadi Soleimanpour
nodes.role: Former Iranian Ambassador to Argentina (1994)

data.role: Former Iranian Ambassador to Argentina (1994)
data.bio: Hadi Soleimanpour served as Iran's Ambassador to Argentina at the time of the [AMIA bombing](/events/amia-bombing-1994) in July 1994. Argentine prosecutors named him as a participant in the conspiracy that led to the attack. In 2003, he was arrested in the United Kingdom on an Argentine extradition request. However, a UK court ruled there was insufficient evidence to extradite him and he was released. He subsequently returned to Iran. In 2024, an Argentine court concluded a trial-in-absentia process and issued sentencing orders against him.

nodes.label: Hadi Tahan Nazif
nodes.role: Spokesman of the Guardian Council; jurist member

data.role: Spokesman of the Guardian Council; jurist member
data.bio: Hadi Tahan Nazif is the current spokesman of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and a jurist member. He serves as the Council's primary public communicator, issuing formal statements on candidate vetting decisions and electoral results. He confirmed [Siamak Rahpeyk](/people/siamak-rahpeyk)'s reappointment as deputy secretary in July 2023.

nodes.label: Hamed Esmaeilion
nodes.role: Diaspora activist; author; co-founder of Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims

data.role: Diaspora activist; author; co-founder of Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims
data.bio:
Hamed Esmaeilion (born 12 March 1977) is an Iranian-Canadian dentist, author, and activist who became one of the most prominent voices of the Iranian diaspora opposition after losing his wife Parisa Eghbalian and nine-year-old daughter Reera in the [Ukraine International Airlines Flight 752 shootdown](/events/ukraine-airlines-flight-752-shootdown-2020) on 8 January 2020. The [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force) shot down the passenger aircraft with two surface-to-air missiles shortly after takeoff from Tehran, killing all 176 people aboard.

Esmaeilion co-founded the [Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims](/orgs/association-of-families-of-flight-ps752-victims) and served as its president and spokesperson, leading legal and advocacy campaigns for accountability. He resigned from the association on 7 March 2023 to focus on broader opposition work.

During the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022), Esmaeilion emerged as a key organiser of diaspora solidarity rallies, coordinating demonstrations on 1 October 2022 in over 150 cities worldwide and a major Berlin rally on 22 October 2022 that drew an estimated 80,000–100,000 participants. He joined a loose coalition with [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) and [Masih Alinejad](/people/masih-alinejad) to amplify the Woman, Life, Freedom movement internationally.

Before his activism, Esmaeilion was a published novelist. His works include *Thyme is not Fair* (2008) and *Dr. Datis* (2013), both recognised by the Hooshang Golshiri Literary Awards. He emigrated from Iran to Canada in 2010 and practises dentistry in Richmond Hill, Ontario.

nodes.label: Hamid Ashraf
nodes.role: Operational leader of the Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas, 1971–1976

data.role: Operational leader of the Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas, 1971–1976
data.bio: Hamid Ashraf (1946–1976) was the most celebrated operational commander of the [Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas). After the founding generation of the Fadaiyan was decimated — [Amir Parviz Pouyan](/people/amir-parviz-pouyan) killed in 1971, [Bijan Jazani](/people/bijan-jazani) executed in 1975, and [Massoud Ahmadzadeh](/people/massoud-ahmadzadeh) executed in 1972 — Ashraf kept the organisation's underground network operational against sustained [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) pressure for five years. His ability to evade capture despite massive counter-insurgency operations gave him a near-legendary status on the Iranian left. On 29 June 1976, SAVAK forces located a safe house in Tehran; Ashraf and eight comrades were killed in the ensuing raid. His death marked the effective end of the OIPFG's armed campaign before the revolution, though the organisation reconstituted itself politically after 1979.

nodes.label: Hamid Dabashi
nodes.role: Hagop Kevorkian Professor of Iranian Studies and Comparative Literature, Columbia University

data.role: Hagop Kevorkian Professor of Iranian Studies and Comparative Literature, Columbia University
data.bio:
Hamid Dabashi is the Hagop Kevorkian Professor of Iranian Studies and Comparative Literature at Columbia University, one of the most prominent and prolific Iranian studies scholars in the anglophone world. Born in 1951 in Ahvaz, he received his doctorate from the University of Pennsylvania.

His scholarly output spans theology, philosophy, cinema, and postcolonial theory. Major works include *Theology of Discontent: The Ideological Foundation of the Islamic Revolution in Iran* (1993), *Iran: A People Interrupted* (2007), *Iran, the Green Movement and the USA* (2010), *The Arab Spring: The End of Postcolonialism* (2012), and *Can Non-Europeans Think?* (2015). His *Theology of Discontent* remains a foundational text for understanding the intellectual history of the Islamic Revolution.

Dabashi writes from an explicitly anti-imperialist and postcolonial framework, critiquing both the Islamic Republic's authoritarianism and what he characterizes as US imperialism and Israeli settler-colonialism in the same breath. He has been a vocal critic of US military intervention in Iran and a consistent opponent of Western-sponsored regime change, while also criticizing the IRI's human rights record. His op-ed work appears frequently in Al Jazeera English.

His ideological positioning — simultaneously critical of the IRI and of Western interventionism — makes him a distinctive voice in Iranian studies, though his anti-imperialist framing and opposition to sanctions align with certain IRI foreign policy preferences even when he is personally critical of the regime.

nodes.label: Hamid Hajizadeh
nodes.role: Poet; scholar; high school teacher; assassinated with his son 1998

data.role: Poet; scholar; high school teacher; assassinated with his son 1998
data.bio: Hamid Hajizadeh was a poet, scholar, and high school teacher in Kerman. On the night of 22 September 1998, [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) agents entered his home and stabbed him 38 times in his bed. His nine-year-old son Karoon (Karun) was sleeping in his father's arms and was also stabbed to death. The murder of Hajizadeh and his child — one of the most horrifying acts of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) — occurred two months before the more widely publicised assassinations of the Forouhars in November 1998.

nodes.label: Hamid Naghashan
nodes.role: Alleged MOIS officer; operated from Iranian Embassy Vienna

data.role: Alleged MOIS officer; operated from Iranian Embassy Vienna
data.bio: Hamid Naghashan is alleged in Belgian and Austrian intelligence reporting to have operated as a Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS) officer from the Iranian Embassy in Vienna. He has been named in connection with the Assadollah Assadi case — a network of Iranian intelligence activity in Europe. He maintains a low public profile and details remain limited to intelligence and law-enforcement reporting. All connections are alleged.

nodes.label: Hamid Rasaee
nodes.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); publisher, Noh Dey Weekly

data.role: MP for Tehran, 12th Majlis (2024–present); publisher, Noh Dey Weekly
data.bio: Hamid Rasaee is a hardline cleric representing Tehran in the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) (12th Majlis). He is a senior figure of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) and publisher of the *Noh Dey Weekly*, shut down multiple times during the [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) presidency. He publicly clashed in a famous 2015 parliamentary confrontation with an aide of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) over the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). He was previously disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from one election cycle. He served in the 8th and 9th Majlis before returning in the 12th.

nodes.label: Hamid-Reza Haji Babaee
nodes.role: First Deputy Speaker, 12th Majlis (2024–present)

data.role: First Deputy Speaker, 12th Majlis (2024–present)
data.bio: Hamid-Reza Haji Babaee is a conservative politician from Hamadan Province serving as First Deputy Speaker of the 12th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) under [Speaker Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf). He previously served as Minister of Education under [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2009–2013). A long-serving principlist MP, he represents the established traditional conservative wing of the Majlis.

nodes.label: Hamid Reza Jalalipour
nodes.role: Co-director, Jame'eh, Tous, Neshat; reformist political analyst

data.role: Co-director, Jame'eh, Tous, Neshat; reformist political analyst
data.bio: Hamid Reza Jalalipour was the co-director and publishing head of the company behind [Jame'eh](/orgs/jameeh-newspaper), [Tous](/orgs/tous-newspaper), and [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper), working alongside [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin). He and Shamsolvaezin were jailed together without charges for 35 days after Jame'eh's closure in 1998. Jalalipour is also a sociologist and political analyst associated with Tehran University, one of the academic-reformist intellectuals who staffed the Khatami-era press.

nodes.label: Hamid Taqvaee
nodes.role: Leader of the Worker-communist Party of Iran (WPI); Marxist politician

data.role: Leader of the Worker-communist Party of Iran (WPI); Marxist politician
data.bio: Hamid Taqvaee is the longtime leader of the [Worker-communist Party of Iran (WPI)](/orgs/worker-communist-party-of-iran), the principal organisation of the Iranian worker-communist movement founded by [Mansoor Hekmat](/people/mansoor-hekmat). After Hekmat's death in 2002, Taqvaee emerged as the dominant figure in the WPI, steering it through the 2004 factional split that produced the rival [Hekmatist Party](/orgs/hekmatist-party) led by [Koorosh Modarresi](/people/koorosh-modarresi). Under Taqvaee's leadership the WPI has maintained a strong secularist and internationalist stance, opposing both the Islamic Republic and imperialist intervention. He is one of the most prominent voices of the Iranian Marxist left in exile.

nodes.label: Hassan Ahmadian
nodes.role: Assistant Professor, Department of West Asia and North Africa, University of Tehran

data.role: Assistant Professor, Department of West Asia and North Africa, University of Tehran
data.bio:
Hassan Ahmadian is an assistant professor in the Department of West Asia and North Africa at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran). He appears on BBC World Service Newshour and other international outlets commenting on Middle East affairs, Iranian regional policy, and the Islamic Republic's relationships with non-state actors.

Ahmadian is based in Tehran and his institutional position gives him access to regime interlocutors on regional affairs. His research focus on West Asia and North Africa aligns closely with the Islamic Republic's areas of strategic interest — Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Yemen, and Palestine — and his commentary tracks closely with the regime's positions on these theatres.

He should be understood as an academic whose institutional position is inside the Islamic Republic's educational system, with access shaped by that context.

nodes.label: Hassan-Ali Tajik
nodes.role: Brigadier General; Armed Forces Logistics Department (d. 2 March 2026)

data.role: Brigadier General; Armed Forces Logistics Department (d. 2 March 2026)
data.bio: Hassan-Ali Tajik served as a Brigadier General in the Armed Forces Logistics Department. He was killed on 2 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave), in the same strike that also killed [Bahram Hosseini Motlagh](/people/bahram-hosseini-motlagh) and [Mohsen Darrebaghi](/people/mohsen-darrebaghi).

sources.title: Senior Armed Forces Officers Killed in March 2 Strike

nodes.label: Hassan Ayat
nodes.role: Secretary of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution; IRP central committee member

data.role: Secretary of the Assembly of Experts for the Constitution; IRP central committee member
data.bio: Hassan Ayat was a lawyer and political activist who served as secretary of the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979. A member of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) central committee, he played a key role in inserting the doctrine of velayat-e faqih into the constitution, arguing forcefully for broad powers for the Supreme Leader. He was elected to the first [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) (Majles) in 1980. He was assassinated on 5 August 1981, an attack attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq).

nodes.label: Hassan Hassanzadeh
nodes.role: Brigadier General; IRGC Tehran Province Commander (d. 29 March 2026)

data.role: Brigadier General; IRGC Tehran Province Commander (d. 29 March 2026)
data.bio: Hassan Hassanzadeh served as a Brigadier General and Commander of the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) in Tehran Province. He was killed on 29 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Hassan Karoubi
nodes.role: Iranian cleric; intermediary in the Iran-Contra affair

data.role: Iranian cleric; intermediary in the Iran-Contra affair
data.bio: Hassan Karoubi is a Shia cleric and brother of [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). He served as an intermediary in the early stages of the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985). In late October 1985, he met with [Manucher Ghorbanifar](/people/manucher-ghorbanifar) and U.S. consultant Michael Ledeen in Geneva, presenting himself as a member of a 'moderate' faction within the Iranian establishment and proposing a staggered exchange of American hostages for Hawk, Sidewinder, and Phoenix missiles. He had previously been reported in contact with U.S. intelligence figures as early as 1980. His exact rank within the Iranian government was uncertain to American officials at the time.

nodes.label: Hassan Rouhani
nodes.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)

data.role: 7th President of Iran (2013–2021)
data.bio: Hassan Rouhani was elected in 2013 on a centrist platform promising to ease sanctions. He oversaw negotiations that led to the 2015 [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) nuclear deal, the signature achievement of his presidency. After the United States withdrew from the JCPOA in 2018, Iran's economy deteriorated sharply. Prior to the presidency he served as Iran's chief nuclear negotiator (2003–2005) and as secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) for sixteen years.

nodes.label: Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam
nodes.role: Brigadier General, IRGC; founder of Iran's ballistic missile programme

data.role: Brigadier General, IRGC; founder of Iran's ballistic missile programme
data.bio: Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam (1959–2011) was an [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) Brigadier General widely regarded as the principal architect of Iran's ballistic missile programme. He joined the IRGC at the outbreak of the Iran–Iraq War in 1980 and by 1982 had established the IRGC's first artillery corps. In October 1983 he founded the IRGC missile command centre and in March 1985 directed Iran's first indigenous missile strike, against Kirkuk, Iraq. He subsequently served as Missile Commander of the [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force) from 1985 onward, overseeing development of every major Iranian ballistic missile system including the Oghab, Naze'at, Zelzal, Shahab-1, Shahab-2, Shahab-3, Ghadr-110, and Sejjil solid-fuelled missile. In 1986–87 he travelled to Lebanon to establish [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah)'s first missile unit, extending Iran's deterrence architecture into the region. He was killed on 12 November 2011 in the [Bid Kaneh explosion](/events/bid-kaneh-explosion-2011) at the Modarres missile garrison near Shahriar, along with sixteen other IRGC members. Iranian authorities attributed the blast to an accident during munitions handling; Western officials and Israeli signals suggested deliberate sabotage. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) attended his funeral personally — an exceptional honour reflecting Moghaddam's strategic importance to the regime.

nodes.label: Heydar Moslehi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2009–2013)

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (2009–2013)
data.bio: Heydar Moslehi served as Iran's Minister of Intelligence during [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s second term (2009–2013). His appointment became the epicentre of the Ahmadinejad–Khamenei power struggle: Ahmadinejad attempted to dismiss Moslehi in 2011, but [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) publicly overruled the dismissal — prompting Ahmadinejad to boycott cabinet meetings for eleven days. This was the most visible public rupture in the Ahmadinejad–Khamenei relationship. A mid-ranking cleric, Moslehi had previously been head of the Organisation of Islamic Endowments and was an Ahmadinejad loyalist until the falling out.

nodes.label: Holly Dagres
nodes.role: Libitzky Family Senior Fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy; specialist on Iranian social media, youth politics, and US-Iran relations

data.role: Libitzky Family Senior Fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy; specialist on Iranian social media, youth politics, and US-Iran relations
data.bio:
Holly Dagres is an Iranian-American analyst and journalist who specializes in Iranian social media, youth culture, and U.S.-Iran relations. She holds the Libitzky Family Senior Fellowship in the Viterbi Program on Iran and U.S.-Iran Relations at the [Washington Institute for Near East Policy](/orgs/washington-institute-for-near-east-policy). Previously she was a senior fellow at the Atlantic Council's Middle East Programs.

Dagres is the founder and editor of The Iranist, a newsletter tracking Iranian social and political trends. She is known for her close attention to the Iranian public sphere, including Persian-language social media, youth movements, and diaspora dynamics. Her work bridges analytical commentary and accessible journalism aimed at policy audiences, and she has appeared frequently in international media as a commentator on Iranian affairs.

sources.title: Holly Dagres — Washington Institute for Near East Policy
sources.publisher: Washington Institute for Near East Policy

nodes.label: Hooman Majd
nodes.role: Journalist and author; Iran commentator

data.role: Journalist and author; Iran commentator
data.bio:
Hooman Majd is an Iranian-American journalist and author based in the United States. He was born in Tehran and is the grandson of a senior Shia ayatollah and the nephew of [Sadegh Kharrazi](/people/sadegh-kharrazi), a former Iranian diplomat who served as Iran's ambassador to France and held senior positions in the Iranian Foreign Ministry. This family connection gives Majd rare inside access to the upper reaches of the Islamic Republic's elite that few diaspora writers possess.

His most well-known book, *The Ayatollah Begs to Differ: The Paradox of Modern Iran* (2008), offered a nuanced insider-outsider portrait of Iranian society and the Islamic Republic's governing class, drawing on his personal access to both reformist and conservative IRI figures. His subsequent books include *The Ayatollahs' Democracy: An Iranian Challenge* (2010) and *The Ministry of Guidance Invites You to Not Stay: An American Family in Iran* (2013), the latter based on a year spent living in Tehran with his wife.

Majd's framing is consistently moderate-to-dovish: he tends to contextualize IRI behavior as more pragmatic and internally divided than Western caricatures suggest, argues against military action or maximum-pressure strategies, and portrays the Islamic Republic's governing elite as rational actors. His family connections to the IRI establishment make his perspective genuinely distinctive, while also making it important to note that his analysis reflects access granted partly by those connections.

He writes for The New Yorker, the Guardian, and other publications and appears regularly as a commentator on Iran in American media.

sources.title: The Ayatollah Begs to Differ — Penguin Random House

nodes.label: Hooshang Amirahmadi
nodes.role: Professor at Rutgers Bloustein School; founder and president, American Iranian Council

data.role: Professor at Rutgers Bloustein School; founder and president, American Iranian Council
data.bio:
Hooshang Amirahmadi is a professor at Rutgers University's Edward J. Bloustein School of Planning and Public Policy, where he has taught urban planning, development policy, and Middle East studies for decades. He is the founder and president of the American Iranian Council (AIC), a Washington-area advocacy organization he established in 1990 that promotes engagement between the United States and the Islamic Republic of Iran.

Amirahmadi is a perennial figure in Iranian-American political life, having attempted to register as a candidate in multiple Iranian presidential elections — 2005, 2013, and subsequent cycles — only to be disqualified each time by the Guardian Council. His persistent candidacy attempts, though unsuccessful, have made him a notable symbol of the diaspora's ambiguous relationship with IRI political participation.

His policy positions consistently favor engagement, sanctions relief, and diplomatic accommodation with the Islamic Republic. While Amirahmadi is not a regime official or affiliated with the IRI, his public stances — opposing maximum-pressure sanctions, advocating sustained diplomatic dialogue, and framing the AIC as a bridge between the two governments — have consistently aligned with positions the Islamic Republic finds convenient. His analysis and advocacy should be understood in this context: not as a regime agent, but as a consistent accommodation-oriented voice whose preferred policy outcomes overlap substantially with IRI preferences.

His academic work focuses on Iranian political economy, development policy in the Middle East, and US-Iran relations. He appears regularly in American and international media as a commentator on Iran affairs.

nodes.label: Hosein Fardust
nodes.role: Intelligence official; first head of SAVAMA (1979–1985); former Deputy Head of SAVAK

data.role: Intelligence official; first head of SAVAMA (1979–1985); former Deputy Head of SAVAK
data.bio:
Hosein Fardust (1917–1987) was an Iranian intelligence official who occupied a uniquely paradoxical position in modern Iranian history: the Shah's closest childhood friend who became a pillar of the Islamic Republic's security apparatus. Fardust and Crown Prince Mohammad Reza Pahlavi attended the same palace-grounds private school together as children and later the prestigious Le Rosey Institute in Switzerland, forging a lifelong personal bond. This intimacy gave Fardust unparalleled access throughout the Pahlavi era. He ran the **Special Intelligence Bureau** (Daftar-e Vijeh-ye Ettelaat) — a private intelligence channel serving the Shah directly, operating parallel to and separate from [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) — for roughly twenty years, and served concurrently as Deputy Head of [SAVAK](/orgs/savak), effectively managing its day-to-day operations.

When the Islamic Revolution came in 1979, Fardust did not flee with the Shah or the bulk of the Pahlavi establishment. He remained in Iran and cooperated with the Islamic Republic, reportedly bringing [SAVAK](/orgs/savak)'s institutional files, personnel networks, and methods intact into the post-revolution state. He was appointed the first head of [SAVAMA](/orgs/savama), the post-revolution successor intelligence service established in 1979, a position he held until approximately 1985. Whether his collaboration with the Islamic Republic was a deliberate long-term defection, pragmatic accommodation, or evidence of prior ties to Soviet intelligence — as alleged by some sources — remains disputed. He was arrested in December 1985 on charges of KGB cooperation. In April 1987 he appeared in a staged state television interview in which he denounced the Shah and the Pahlavi court. He died in May 1987, reportedly of a heart attack while still imprisoned.

nodes.label: Hossein Ahmadlou
nodes.role: Israel Desk Officer, Quds Force 'Zionist File' (d. 2026)

data.role: Israel Desk Officer, Quds Force 'Zionist File' (d. 2026)
data.bio: Hossein Ahmadlou served as the Israel Desk Officer within the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), running what was internally called the 'Zionist File' — the directorate responsible for intelligence and operations targeting Israel. He was killed during the 2026 war as part of the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave) and associated Israeli operations.

sources.title: Quds Force Israel Desk Officer Killed

nodes.label: Hossein-Ali Montazeri
nodes.role: Grand Ayatollah; designated successor to Khomeini (1985–1989); leading dissident cleric

data.role: Grand Ayatollah; designated successor to Khomeini (1985–1989); leading dissident cleric
data.bio:
Hossein-Ali Montazeri was a Grand Ayatollah and one of the architects of Iran's Islamic Republic. A student of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), he was imprisoned for years under the Shah and played a central role in drafting Iran's theocratic constitution. In 1979, he was elected chairman of the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution), the 73-member body that drafted the Islamic Republic's constitution, with [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti) as deputy chairman. The assembly embedded the concept of velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the jurist) into the new constitution. In 1985, the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) designated him as Khomeini's successor.

Montazeri's path to [dismissal](/events/montazeri-dismissal-1989) began with the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985): his protégé [Mehdi Hashemi](/people/mehdi-hashemi), an IRGC official, leaked details of the secret U.S. arms deals in 1986. Hashemi was arrested and executed in September 1987, weakening Montazeri's political base. Then came the [1988 mass executions of political prisoners](/events/mass-executions-1988). Montazeri wrote at least three letters to Khomeini protesting the killings and confronted the Death Committee members directly, telling them: 'the greatest crime committed during the reign of the Islamic Republic has been committed by you.' He argued that prisoners — including [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) sympathisers who had already been tried and sentenced — deserved fair treatment under Islamic law. [Ahmad Khomeini](/people/ahmad-khomeini), who controlled access to his father, and [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), then Speaker of Parliament, both worked to isolate Montazeri. On 28 March 1989, Khomeini formally stripped him of the succession. The subsequent [1989 constitutional revision](/events/constitutional-revision-1989) removed the requirement that the Supreme Leader be a marja, clearing the path for [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) — who was only a Hojatoleslam — to succeed Khomeini.

After Khomeini's death, Montazeri continued to challenge Khamenei's religious credentials. In November 1997 he was placed under house arrest in Qom, where he remained until approximately 2003. He maintained ties to the reformist movement, sharing common ground with [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) on civil liberties and with the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) on democratic governance. During the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), he issued a fatwa declaring the disputed election illegitimate and became the movement's most authoritative clerical voice, aligning with [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). He [died on 19 December 2009](/events/death-of-montazeri-2009); his funeral and mourning ceremonies became major protest gatherings that sustained the Green Movement through its most intense period.

nodes.label: Hossein-Ali Nayyeri
nodes.role: Head, Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges (2013–present); former Sharia judge, Death Committee (1988)

data.role: Head, Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges (2013–present); former Sharia judge, Death Committee (1988)
data.bio: Hossein-Ali Nayyeri presided over the Tehran 'Death Committee' that in summer 1988 ordered mass executions of political prisoners pursuant to [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa — an event the UN has classified as crimes against humanity and genocide. He is explicitly named in Khomeini's written decree as the Sharia judge responsible for the Tehran operations. He personally told Ayatollah Montazeri on 15 August 1988 that 750 prisoners had already been executed in Tehran. The committee he chaired — alongside [Morteza Eshraqi](/people/morteza-eshraqi), [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (deputy), and [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (Intelligence Ministry representative) — interrogated and executed prisoners in minutes. He was subsequently appointed Deputy Chief Justice of Iran's Supreme Court (1989–2013) and has headed the Supreme Disciplinary Court for Judges since 2013, facing no legal consequences.

nodes.label: Hossein Amir-Abdollahian
nodes.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2021–2024); died in helicopter crash

data.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (2021–2024); died in helicopter crash
data.bio: Hossein Amir-Abdollahian was Iran's Foreign Minister under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from August 2021 until his death in May 2024. He died alongside Raisi in the helicopter crash on 19 May 2024 in East Azerbaijan. A career diplomat who specialised in Arab and African affairs, he served as Deputy Foreign Minister for Arab and African Affairs under [Manouchehr Mottaki](/people/manouchehr-mottaki) (2011–2016) and was known for his close ties to Hezbollah, Hamas, and the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). He was removed from his deputy role by [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) in 2016, then became a senior adviser to [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf) at the Majlis. His foreign policy prioritised the 'resistance axis' and East-facing engagement with Russia and China.

nodes.label: Hossein Dehghan
nodes.role: Former Minister of Defense (2013–2017); IRGC Aerospace Force veteran

data.role: Former Minister of Defense (2013–2017); IRGC Aerospace Force veteran
data.bio: Hossein Dehghan is an IRGC brigadier general who served as Defense Minister in [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s first cabinet (2013–2017). He joined the IRGC at its founding and commanded contingents in Lebanon and Syria in the early 1980s. He was Deputy Defense Minister under [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). Under Rouhani's second term he was replaced by Amir Hatami. He ran as a presidential candidate in 2021. He is subject to US and EU sanctions. He was a military adviser to [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) before his cabinet appointment.

nodes.label: Hossein Jabal Amelian
nodes.role: Head of SPND (Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research)

data.role: Head of SPND (Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research)
data.bio: Brigadier General Hossein Jabal Amelian was an [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) officer who served as director of [SPND](/orgs/spnd) — the Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research — the classified Iranian body assessed by Western intelligence services and the IAEA as the successor to [Mohsen Fakhrizadeh](/people/mohsen-fakhrizadeh)'s Amad Project for nuclear weapons research. He succeeded [Reza Mozaffari Nia](/people/reza-mozaffari-nia) as SPND director. Under his leadership, SPND was assessed to have continued research relevant to nuclear weapons development, contributing to accelerating tensions with Israel and the United States that preceded the 2026 conflict. Jabal Amelian was killed on 28 February 2026 in Israeli airstrikes. His killing, alongside that of Mozaffari Nia, effectively decapitated SPND's institutional leadership.

sources.title: Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research — Wikipedia

nodes.label: Hossein Khanzadi
nodes.role: Commander of the Islamic Republic of Iran Navy (2017–2021)

data.role: Commander of the Islamic Republic of Iran Navy (2017–2021)
data.bio: Rear Admiral Hossein Khanzadi served as Commander of the [Islamic Republic of Iran Navy](/orgs/islamic-republic-of-iran-navy) (NEDAJA, the Artesh Navy) from 2017 to 2021, succeeding Habibollah Sayyari and being replaced by Shahram Irani. Despite having an IRGC background, he commanded the conventional Artesh Navy — an arrangement reflecting the Supreme Leader's practice of cross-posting trusted officers. During his tenure, Khanzadi oversaw continued expansion of Iranian naval presence in international waters, including the Gulf of Aden deployments, and presided over the January 2020 confrontation following the US killing of Qasem Soleimani, during which Iranian naval forces raised a red flag over the Imam Ali mosque in Bushehr. He also oversaw the catastrophic accidental sinking of the IRIS Konarak support vessel by friendly fire during an exercise in May 2020, which killed 19 sailors.

nodes.label: Hossein Makki
nodes.role: Co-founder of National Front; nationalist politician and historian

data.role: Co-founder of National Front; nationalist politician and historian
data.bio:
Hossein Makki was an Iranian nationalist politician, historian, and parliamentarian who played a central role in the oil nationalisation movement. In 1949 he co-founded the [National Front of Iran](/orgs/national-front-iran) alongside [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh), [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi), and other nationalist figures.

Makki was among the most active campaigners for the nationalisation of the Anglo-Iranian Oil Company and served as a member of the [National Consultative Assembly](/orgs/national-consultative-assembly) (Majlis), where he was a vocal advocate for Iranian sovereignty over its oil resources. He was part of the National Front's initial core leadership and worked closely with Mossadegh during the oil crisis of 1951–1953.

After the [1953 coup d'état](/events/iranian-coup-1953) that overthrew Mossadegh's government, Makki's relationship with the National Front became more complex. He later drifted away from the mainstream of the Front and at times took positions at variance with the Mossadeghist line, leading to tensions with figures like [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi). He was also a prolific historian and writer on modern Iranian political history.

He died in January 1999.

nodes.label: Hossein Mousavian
nodes.role: Research Scholar at Princeton University

data.role: Research Scholar at Princeton University
data.bio: Seyed Hossein Mousavian is a former Iranian diplomat and nuclear policy scholar. He served as Iran's Ambassador to Germany (1990–1997) and as head of the Foreign Relations Committee of the Supreme National Security Council. His ambassadorship to Germany spanned the September 1992 [Mykonos restaurant assassination](/events/mykonos-restaurant-assassination-1992) in Berlin — in which four Kurdish Iranian opposition leaders were killed on orders traced to Iran's top leadership — and the April 1997 Berlin court verdict that directly implicated senior Iranian officials, leading to the expulsion of four Iranian diplomats from Germany, an event that directly affected Mousavian's diplomatic mission. From 2003 to 2005, he was spokesman and member of Iran's nuclear negotiating team under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani). In 2007, he was briefly arrested on espionage charges widely viewed as politically motivated by hardliners. He subsequently relocated to the United States and became a research scholar at Princeton University's School of Public and International Affairs. He was named in the leaked [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) emails published by Semafor and Iran International in September 2023 as a participant in the network coordinated by the [Iranian Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis).

nodes.label: Hossein Salami
nodes.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)

data.role: IRGC Commander-in-Chief (2019–2025)
data.bio: Hossein Salami served as Commander-in-Chief of the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) from April 2019, appointed by Khamenei following the removal of [Mohammad Ali Jafari](/people/mohammad-ali-jafari). He joined the IRGC in 1980 at the outbreak of the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and rose through its Aerospace Force before appointment to the top post. He oversaw the IRGC during the Mahsa Amini crackdown of 2022, the expansion of Iran's ballistic missile and drone programmes, and the direct Iranian strikes against Israel in April 2024. He was reportedly killed in Israeli strikes on Iran in June 2025.

nodes.label: Hossein Shariatmadari
nodes.role: Managing editor of Kayhan since 1993; Khamenei appointee; former MOIS interrogator

data.role: Managing editor of Kayhan since 1993; Khamenei appointee; former MOIS interrogator
data.bio: Hossein Shariatmadari has been the managing editor of [Kayhan](/orgs/kayhan-newspaper) since 1993, appointed directly by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), whose views Kayhan consistently reflects. He was a student activist in the 1970s who printed leaflets denouncing the Shah and was arrested by SAVAK, tortured (including having his nails pulled out and receiving electric shocks), and sentenced to life imprisonment — freed when the Shah fell. After the revolution he worked as an interrogator for the Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS), where he is reported to have tortured dissidents to extract false confessions and to have produced state television programmes broadcasting those confessions. Under his editorship Kayhan has called for the prosecution of reformist presidents, denied the Holocaust (he stated in 2020 that the Holocaust 'was falsely claimed by Zionist and Western governments'), called for taking EU nationals hostage, incited violence against Baha'is, and attacked journalists. Shamsolvaezin considers him personally responsible for Neshat's closure.

nodes.label: Hossein Sheikholeslam
nodes.role: Former Foreign Ministry Deputy for Political Affairs; 1979 embassy hostage-taker

data.role: Former Foreign Ministry Deputy for Political Affairs; 1979 embassy hostage-taker
data.bio: Hossein Sheikholeslam was one of the student leaders who seized the US Embassy in Tehran on November 4, 1979, participating in the [Iran hostage crisis](/events/iran-hostage-crisis-1979). Following the revolution, he rose through the Foreign Ministry to serve 17 years as Deputy Foreign Minister for Political Affairs — one of the most influential career positions in Iranian diplomacy. He was ambassador to Syria from 1998 to 2003, a period during which [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) expanded its operations under Iranian direction. Sheikholeslam is credited by researchers as one of the figures involved in the co-founding of Lebanese Hezbollah in the early 1980s, coordinating between the IRGC and Lebanese Shia militias in the Bekaa Valley. He was an ally of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and a consistent advocate for the resistance axis. He died of COVID-19 on March 26, 2020.

nodes.label: Hossein Taeb
nodes.role: Former head, IRGC Intelligence Organisation (2009–2022)

data.role: Former head, IRGC Intelligence Organisation (2009–2022)
data.bio: Hossein Taeb headed the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s Intelligence Organisation for over thirteen years (2009–2022), making him one of the most feared figures in Iran's security apparatus. A student of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and wartime associate of [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) in the Habib Battalion, he combined IRGC and MOIS experience spanning from the [1988 massacre of political prisoners](/events/mass-executions-1988) through the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) to the suppression of the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) and [2022 Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022). He was dismissed in June 2022 following a series of Israeli intelligence successes inside Iran, including the assassination of nuclear scientists, and a foiled plot against Israeli tourists in Turkey that exposed IRGC-IO networks. He was replaced by General Mohammad Kazemi.

nodes.label: Ibrahim Alizadeh
nodes.role: First Secretary of the Communist Party of Iran

data.role: First Secretary of the Communist Party of Iran
data.bio: Ibrahim Alizadeh (born c. 1950) is the First Secretary of the [Communist Party of Iran](/orgs/communist-party-of-iran) and leader of the CPI-affiliated [Komala](/orgs/komala-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) faction. After the 2000 split in which [Abdullah Mohtadi](/people/abdullah-mohtadi) led one wing of Komala out of the CPI, Alizadeh maintained the party's communist ideological orientation and its affiliation with the CPI. He has led the organisation from exile in Iraqi Kurdistan, continuing to advocate for Kurdish rights and working-class politics within the framework of Marxism-Leninism.

nodes.label: Ibrahim Aqil
nodes.role: IRGC Quds Force Lebanon Corps commander; Hezbollah senior commander

data.role: IRGC Quds Force Lebanon Corps commander; Hezbollah senior commander
data.bio: Ibrahim Aqil was a senior Lebanese Hezbollah commander who served as head of the [IRGC Quds Force Lebanon Corps](/orgs/irgc-quds-force-lebanon-corps) and commanded Hezbollah's elite Radwan Force. A veteran of decades of operations against Israel, Aqil had been designated as a terrorist by the United States since the 1980s for his alleged role in the 1983 Beirut barracks bombings and other attacks. He was killed on 20 September 2024 in an Israeli airstrike on a building in the Dahieh (southern suburbs of Beirut), along with other senior Hezbollah commanders. His killing was part of a major Israeli decapitation campaign against Hezbollah leadership in the run-up to the October 2024 invasion of southern Lebanon. Following his death, [Davoud Alizadeh](/people/davoud-alizadeh) was appointed as the new commander of the Quds Force Lebanon Corps.

sources.title: Ibrahim Aqil, Hezbollah commander, killed in Beirut Israeli strike

nodes.label: Imad Mughniyeh
nodes.role: Hezbollah military commander; accused of co-directing the 1994 AMIA bombing operation; assassinated 2008

data.role: Hezbollah military commander; accused of co-directing the 1994 AMIA bombing operation; assassinated 2008
data.bio: Imad Mughniyeh was [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah)'s chief military and intelligence commander, widely regarded as one of the most operationally capable terrorist figures of the late twentieth century. Operating under the alias Hajj Radwan, he is held responsible by US and Argentine prosecutors for a series of mass-casualty attacks including the 1983 Beirut barracks bombings, the 1985 TWA Flight 847 hijacking, and — critically for the OIFI network — the [AMIA bombing](/events/amia-bombing-1994) of July 1994 in Buenos Aires. Argentine investigators, led by [Alberto Nisman](/people/alberto-nisman), concluded that Mughniyeh worked alongside [Mohsen Rabbani](/people/mohsen-rabbani) as the Hezbollah operational director for the AMIA attack, providing the trained bomber and coordinating with the IRGC-QF command that authorised the strike. Mughniyeh maintained extremely close operational ties with the [IRGC-Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), functioning effectively as a joint Hezbollah-IRGC asset. He was assassinated on 12 February 2008 in the Kafr Sousa district of Damascus by a car bomb widely attributed to the CIA and Mossad.

sources.title: CIA and Mossad killed senior Hezbollah figure in car bombing

nodes.label: Iraj Eskandari
nodes.role: First Secretary of the Tudeh Party of Iran (1969–1979)

data.role: First Secretary of the Tudeh Party of Iran (1969–1979)
data.bio: Iraj Eskandari (1907–1985) was an Iranian communist politician and a Qajar prince who became one of Iran's most prominent Marxist leaders. Educated in France, he was among the founding members of the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) in 1941. He served as Minister of Commerce and Industry in 1946 under Prime Minister Ahmad Qavam's coalition cabinet. After the 1953 coup and the banning of the Tudeh Party, Eskandari went into exile. He returned to the party leadership in 1969, serving as First Secretary until 1979. On the eve of the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), he was dismissed from the leadership after opposing the decision to support [Ayatollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), and was replaced by [Nureddin Kianouri](/people/nureddin-kianouri). Eskandari died in exile in 1985.

nodes.label: Jafar Panahi
nodes.role: Filmmaker and political dissident

data.role: Filmmaker and political dissident
data.bio: Jafar Panahi is an internationally acclaimed Iranian filmmaker and one of the Islamic Republic's most prominent cultural dissidents. Winner of the Golden Lion at the Venice Film Festival and multiple prizes at Cannes and Berlin, he was arrested in 2010 and sentenced to six years in prison and a 20-year ban on filmmaking, travel, and public speaking. He continued to make films clandestinely under the ban. He was arrested again in July 2022 during the lead-up to the Mahsa Amini protests. On 28 January 2026, he co-signed a joint statement with other prominent political prisoners and civil society figures condemning the [January 2026 massacres](/events/january-2026-massacres).

nodes.label: Grand Ayatollah Jafar Sobhani
nodes.role: Senior Shia marja; head of the Imam Sadiq Institute, Qom

data.role: Senior Shia marja; head of the Imam Sadiq Institute, Qom
data.bio:
Grand Ayatollah Jafar Sobhani (born 1930, Tabriz) is one of the senior Shia marjas (sources of emulation) based in Qom. He is the founder and head of the Imam Sadiq Institute (Muassasat al-Imam al-Sadiq) in Qom, a major theological research and publication center that has produced numerous books on Islamic theology, jurisprudence, and Shia doctrinal history.

Sobhani is a prolific author in Arabic and Persian on Islamic theology (kalam), jurisprudence (fiqh), Islamic history, and the history of Shia doctrine. His multi-volume theological encyclopedias are widely used in Qom's hawza (seminary) curriculum. He studied under senior Qom marjas of the mid-twentieth century and has taught at the Qom seminary for decades, training generations of clerics.

He is regarded as a traditional clerical figure within the Qom establishment, aligned with the Islamic Republic's religious framework without being a political hardliner in the factional sense. His institute publishes extensively in Arabic, reaching Shia audiences across Iraq, Lebanon, and the Gulf. He is a recognized authority on matters of Shia doctrine and has issued fatwas on contemporary issues including technology, bioethics, and interfaith relations.

nodes.label: Jamal Abdi
nodes.role: President, NIAC & Executive Director, NIAC Action

data.role: President, NIAC & Executive Director, NIAC Action
data.bio: Jamal Abdi is the president of the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac) and executive director of [NIAC Action](/orgs/niac-action), succeeding [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi) as the organization's leader. He publicly applauded the appointment of [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley) as Biden's Iran envoy in January 2021, calling it 'a major step in putting U.S. diplomacy back in the hands of genuine professionals.' Under his leadership, NIAC Action has continued lobbying for diplomatic engagement with Iran and against sanctions.

nodes.label: Jamshid Eshaghi
nodes.role: Brigadier General; Budget and Finance Chief, Armed Forces (d. 31 March 2026)

data.role: Brigadier General; Budget and Finance Chief, Armed Forces (d. 31 March 2026)
data.bio: Jamshid Eshaghi served as a Brigadier General and chief of the Budget and Finance department of the Iranian Armed Forces. He was killed on 31 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Jason Rezaian
nodes.role: Journalist; former Washington Post Tehran bureau chief; Iranian-American hostage held in Evin Prison 2014–2016

data.role: Journalist; former Washington Post Tehran bureau chief; Iranian-American hostage held in Evin Prison 2014–2016
data.bio:
Jason Rezaian (born 20 November 1976) is an Iranian-American journalist who served as Tehran bureau chief for [The Washington Post](/orgs/washington-post). On 22 July 2014, he and his wife Yeganeh Salehi were arrested by Iranian authorities at their Tehran home. Salehi was released after two months; Rezaian was held in Evin Prison for 544 days — one of the longest detentions of a journalist in Iranian history. He was charged with espionage, propaganda against the state, collecting classified information, and providing it to hostile governments, charges he and the Post denied.

On 16 January 2016, Rezaian was released as part of a prisoner exchange negotiated alongside the implementation of the [JCPOA nuclear deal](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). The exchange involved four Iranian-Americans held by Iran and seven Iranians held or sought by the United States. His case drew widespread international condemnation and focused global attention on Iran's systematic use of dual-national detentions as diplomatic leverage.

After his release, Rezaian returned to writing for the Post and published a memoir, *Prisoner: My 544 Days in an Iranian Prison* (2019), recounting his detention. He has become an outspoken advocate on behalf of other dual-national detainees and a prominent commentator on US-Iran relations.

nodes.label: Javad Owji
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2021–2024)

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2021–2024)
data.bio: Javad Owji served as Iran's Minister of Petroleum under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) from 2021 to 2024. A longtime executive in the oil and gas sector, he previously served as Deputy Oil Minister and managing director of the National Iranian Gas Company (NIGC). His appointment was influenced by First Vice President [Mohammad Mokhber](/people/mohammad-mokhber), given Owji's connections to Setad subsidiaries. He is sanctioned by the US Treasury. His predecessor [Bijan Namdar Zanganeh](/people/bijan-namdar-zanganeh) is regarded as one of Iran's most capable oil technocrats, a standard Owji found difficult to match. He was replaced by [Mohsen Paknejad](/people/mohsen-paknejad) under [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian).

nodes.label: Mohammad Javad Zarif
nodes.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013–2021); Vice President (2024–2025)

data.role: Minister of Foreign Affairs (2013–2021); Vice President (2024–2025)
data.bio: Mohammad Javad Zarif served as Iran's Foreign Minister throughout [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani)'s presidency, leading the Iranian negotiating team that concluded the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) nuclear deal in 2015. A US-educated career diplomat, he was Iran's ambassador to the United Nations before becoming foreign minister. He endorsed [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in the 2024 presidential election and briefly served as vice president for strategic affairs before resigning. He was subject to US sanctions from 2019. In 2023, leaked emails revealed that his Foreign Ministry had overseen the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert influence operation run through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) that recruited Western-based Iranian academics to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions.

nodes.label: Jon Stewart
nodes.role: American comedian, television host, and filmmaker

data.role: American comedian, television host, and filmmaker
data.bio: Jon Stewart is an American comedian and television personality best known as the longtime host of The Daily Show. His connection to Iranian political history comes through his directorial debut, Rosewater (2014), a biographical film about [Maziar Bahari](/people/maziar-bahari), the Iranian-Canadian journalist who was imprisoned for 118 days by the Islamic Republic in 2009. Bahari had been interviewed on The Daily Show shortly before his arrest, and a clip of that interview was used as evidence against him during his interrogation. Stewart was motivated to direct the [Rosewater film](/events/rosewater-film-2014) in part by his sense of responsibility for that use of his show's content. The film brought international attention to the Islamic Republic's treatment of journalists and its use of forced confessions.

nodes.label: Jorge Taiana
nodes.role: Argentine Foreign Minister (2005–2010); named in Alberto Nisman's 2015 treason charging document alongside President Kirchner

data.role: Argentine Foreign Minister (2005–2010); named in Alberto Nisman's 2015 treason charging document alongside President Kirchner
data.bio: Jorge Taiana served as Argentina's Foreign Minister under President Cristina Fernández de Kirchner from 2005 to 2010, and was later appointed Defence Minister under President Alberto Fernández in 2021. In January 2015, AMIA special prosecutor [Alberto Nisman](/people/alberto-nisman) filed a charging document accusing both [Cristina Fernández de Kirchner](/people/cristina-fernandez-de-kirchner) and Taiana of treason, alleging they had conspired to shield Iranian suspects in the [AMIA bombing](/events/amia-bombing-1994) case from prosecution through the [Iran–Argentina MOU](/events/iran-argentina-mou-2013) of January 2013. The charges alleged the MOU was designed to lift Interpol red notices against Iranian officials in exchange for trade concessions. Nisman was found dead on 18 January 2015, the night before his planned congressional testimony.

nodes.label: Kamal Kharrazi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (1997–2005); Strategic Council for Foreign Relations chairman

data.role: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs (1997–2005); Strategic Council for Foreign Relations chairman
data.bio: Kamal Kharrazi served as Iran's Foreign Minister for the full eight years of [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s presidency (1997–2005). Previously Iran's Ambassador to the United Nations (1989–1997), he was a calm and relatively moderate voice on international affairs during the reform era, presiding over the diplomatic opening with Europe and the Middle East. He replaced [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati) when Khatami took office. Since 2005 he has headed the Strategic Council for Foreign Relations, an advisory body to the Supreme Leader. His brother Mohsen Kharazi is connected by marriage to the Khamenei family.

nodes.label: Karim Sadjadpour
nodes.role: Senior fellow, Carnegie Endowment for International Peace; Iranian-American Iran analyst

data.role: Senior fellow, Carnegie Endowment for International Peace; Iranian-American Iran analyst
data.bio:
Karim Sadjadpour is a senior fellow at the [Carnegie Endowment for International Peace](/orgs/carnegie-endowment-for-international-peace), where he focuses on Iran and US foreign policy in the Middle East. He is widely regarded as one of the most influential Iranian-American foreign policy analysts in Washington.

Before joining Carnegie, Sadjadpour was based in Tehran as the [International Crisis Group](/orgs/international-crisis-group)'s chief Iran analyst, a posting that gave him rare direct access to senior Iranian officials, reformist politicians, and civil society figures inside the country. That experience underpinned his widely circulated 2008 monograph *Reading Khamenei: The World View of Iran's Most Powerful Leader*, which provided a systematic analysis of Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei)'s political thought drawn from decades of public speeches.

Sadjadpour is an adjunct professor at Georgetown University and a contributing writer to *The Atlantic*. He has testified before the US Congress numerous times and is regularly cited in American media on Iranian politics. He has been particularly outspoken on the nature of the Islamic Republic's ruling ideology — arguing that the system is held together less by popular legitimacy than by coercion and the personal power of the Supreme Leader — and on the geopolitical dimensions of Iran's regional policy.

nodes.label: Karim Sanjabi
nodes.role: Leader of the National Front; Foreign Minister in provisional government (1979)

data.role: Leader of the National Front; Foreign Minister in provisional government (1979)
data.bio:
Karim Sanjabi was a Kurdish-Iranian lawyer and politician from the Sanjabi tribe of Kermanshah. He studied law at the University of Paris and became a professor of law at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran).

In 1949 he co-founded the [National Front of Iran](/orgs/national-front-iran) alongside [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh) and became one of its most prominent leaders. He served as Minister of Education in Mossadegh's cabinet during the oil nationalisation period (1951–1953). After the CIA/MI6-backed coup of August 1953, he was arrested and imprisoned alongside other National Front figures.

During the buildup to the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Sanjabi travelled to Paris in October 1978 to meet [Ayatollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) at Neauphle-le-Château. On 4 November 1978 they issued a joint Three-Point Declaration calling for the overthrow of [Mohammad Reza Shah](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi), the establishment of a government based on Islamic and democratic principles, and a national referendum. The declaration helped unify secular nationalists and Islamists behind a common revolutionary front.

When [Shapour Bakhtiar](/people/shapour-bakhtiar) accepted the Shah's appointment as Prime Minister in January 1979, Sanjabi and the National Front expelled him from the party.

After the revolution, Sanjabi served as Minister of Foreign Affairs in [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan)'s provisional government from February 1979, succeeded by [Ebrahim Yazdi](/people/ebrahim-yazdi) when Sanjabi resigned around April 1979 in protest against revolutionary tribunals, summary executions, and the growing dominance of the clerical faction.

He was also politically close to [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar), a fellow National Front ally. In 1981 Sanjabi left Iran for exile in France, where he died in Paris on 4 July 1995.

nodes.label: Kaveh Afrasiabi
nodes.role: Iranian political scientist; convicted unregistered foreign agent

data.role: Iranian political scientist; convicted unregistered foreign agent
data.bio: Kaveh Afrasiabi is an Iranian political scientist and US permanent resident who was arrested in Watertown, Massachusetts in January 2021 for acting as an unregistered agent of the Iranian government in violation of the Foreign Agents Registration Act (FARA). He was paid approximately $265,000 by Iran's Permanent Mission to the United Nations to promote Iranian government positions while presenting himself as an independent expert in American media, including the New York Times, Washington Post, and Huffington Post. His case is closely related to the broader pattern of Iranian influence operations exposed by the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) leak. He was released to Iran in a prisoner swap in September 2023.

nodes.label: Kayhan Barzegar
nodes.role: Assistant Professor, Islamic Azad University; Senior Research Fellow, Center for Middle East Strategic Studies, Tehran

data.role: Assistant Professor, Islamic Azad University; Senior Research Fellow, Center for Middle East Strategic Studies, Tehran
data.bio:
Kayhan Barzegar is an assistant professor at [Islamic Azad University](/orgs/islamic-azad-university) and a senior research fellow at the Center for Middle East Strategic Studies in Tehran — an institution that articulates and disseminates the Islamic Republic's strategic worldview for international audiences. He has also held a fellowship at the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs at Harvard University.

Barzegar's work packages regime foreign policy rationales in the language of academic international relations. His output on Iranian strategic interests, nuclear policy, and regional security consistently reflects and legitimises Islamic Republic positions while gaining credibility through association with Western academic institutions.

His Belfer Center fellowship allowed him to engage with US policy circles directly. This combination — Tehran-based institutional affiliation with the Centre for Middle East Strategic Studies, plus Harvard access — makes him an effective articulator of regime positions in Western policy and academic contexts.

nodes.label: Kayvan Abbassi
nodes.role: Media entrepreneur and co-founder of Manoto TV

data.role: Media entrepreneur and co-founder of Manoto TV
data.bio: Kayvan Abbassi is an Iranian-British media entrepreneur and the co-founder of [Manoto TV](/orgs/manoto-tv), the Persian-language satellite television channel launched in 2010. Together with his wife Marjan Abbassi, he built Manoto into one of the most-watched Persian-language channels globally, combining entertainment with news coverage and maintaining a secular, non-partisan editorial stance. The channel operated from London and reached audiences inside Iran despite regime jamming efforts. Manoto ceased live broadcasting in 2024 and transitioned to on-demand digital content.

nodes.label: Kazem Darabi
nodes.role: Iranian intelligence operative; convicted perpetrator of the Mykonos assassination

data.role: Iranian intelligence operative; convicted perpetrator of the Mykonos assassination
data.bio: Kazem Darabi is an Iranian national convicted by a German court for his role as the principal organiser of the [Mykonos restaurant assassination](/events/mykonos-restaurant-assassination-1992) in Berlin on 17 September 1992. Working as a greengrocer and Iranian intelligence asset in Germany, Darabi coordinated the attack in which PDKI Secretary-General [Sadegh Sharafkandi](/people/sadegh-sharafkandi) and three associates were shot dead. He was convicted by the Berlin Court of Appeals in April 1997 and sentenced to life imprisonment. The court found that the killings were ordered by Iran's Committee for Special Operations — a body that included [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian), and [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati). Darabi was released and deported to Iran in December 2007 after serving ten years.

nodes.label: Kazem Gharib-Abadi
nodes.role: Former Iranian Permanent Representative to the IAEA; Deputy FM for International Affairs

data.role: Former Iranian Permanent Representative to the IAEA; Deputy FM for International Affairs
data.bio: Kazem Gharib-Abadi served as Iran's permanent representative to the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) in Vienna from 2019 to 2022, a post at the intersection of nuclear diplomacy and intelligence coordination. He subsequently became Deputy Foreign Minister for International Affairs and Secretary of Iran's High Council for Human Rights — despite Iran's ongoing human rights record being subject to intense international criticism. In this latter role he regularly engaged with UN human rights bodies, often deflecting scrutiny of Iran's domestic repression. He has been a vocal critic of Western sanctions and IAEA demands for enhanced access to Iranian nuclear sites.

nodes.label: Kazem Rajavi
nodes.role: Human rights advocate; Iran's first post-revolution ambassador to UN in Geneva

data.role: Human rights advocate; Iran's first post-revolution ambassador to UN in Geneva
data.bio: Kazem Rajavi was a professor of political science at the University of Geneva and elder brother of [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi). He played a crucial role in saving Massoud's life by lobbying internationally to commute his death sentence under the Shah. After the 1979 revolution, he briefly served as Iran's ambassador to the UN in Geneva before breaking with the regime. He became a leading human rights advocate, documenting abuses by the Islamic Republic. On 24 April 1990, he was [assassinated](/events/assassination-of-kazem-rajavi-1990) near his home in Coppet, Switzerland by Iranian intelligence agents who had entered the country on diplomatic passports. [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) was charged by a Swiss court with masterminding the operation.

nodes.label: Kazem Sami
nodes.role: Physician; former Minister of Health; Islamic nationalist; assassinated 1988

data.role: Physician; former Minister of Health; Islamic nationalist; assassinated 1988
data.bio: Kazem Sami was a physician, member of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran), and Islamic nationalist political figure who served as Iran's Minister of Health in the early post-revolution provisional government. A follower of [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh)'s nationalist tradition, he became increasingly critical of the consolidation of clerical power. On 23 November 1988, Sami was attacked with an axe in his Tehran medical clinic and killed. His murder is considered likely the first victim of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) — the decade-long campaign of assassinations carried out by rogue units within the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). The connection was only established years later when the 1998 murders brought the entire chain to light.

nodes.label: Kazem Shariatmadari
nodes.role: Grand Ayatollah; critic of Velayat-e Faqih

data.role: Grand Ayatollah; critic of Velayat-e Faqih
data.bio: Grand Ayatollah Kazem Shariatmadari was one of Iran's most senior Shia clerics and a major religious authority. He initially supported the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) but became one of the most prominent critics of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist). He advocated for a more democratic interpretation of Islamic governance. In 1982, he was implicated in an alleged coup plot involving [Sadegh Ghotbzadeh](/people/sadegh-ghotbzadeh) and was stripped of his Grand Ayatollah title — an unprecedented act in Shia history.
