data.bio: Third secretary-general of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) since 2017, succeeding [Ebrahim Yazdi](/people/ebrahim-yazdi). Imprisoned once under the Pahlavi regime (1 year) and five times under the Islamic Republic (4.3 years total).

nodes.label: Mohammad Yazdi
nodes.role: Chief Justice of Iran (1989–1999); chairman of the Assembly of Experts (2015–2016)

data.role: Chief Justice of Iran (1989–1999); chairman of the Assembly of Experts (2015–2016)
data.bio: Ayatollah Mohammad Yazdi was a senior hardline cleric who served as Chief Justice of Iran from 1989 to 1999, appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Kermanshah Province. A member of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party), he later served multiple terms on the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and in the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts), briefly chairing the latter from 2015 to 2016. As head of the [Judiciary](/orgs/judiciary-of-iran) during the 1990s, he oversaw a period of political repression including crackdowns on the press and reformist activists. He was known as one of the most conservative figures in the clerical establishment.

nodes.label: Mohammadreza Saqafifar
nodes.role: Special Assistant to the Supreme Leader (d. 8 March 2026)

data.role: Special Assistant to the Supreme Leader (d. 8 March 2026)
data.bio: Mohammadreza Saqafifar served as a Special Assistant to Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), working out of the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). He was killed on 8 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Mohsen Armin
nodes.role: Reformist politician and journalist; member of the 6th Majlis

data.role: Reformist politician and journalist; member of the 6th Majlis
data.bio: Mohsen Armin is a reformist politician, journalist, and key figure in the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization). He served as a member of parliament in the reformist 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). He has been active in political journalism and the reform movement, advocating for political openness and democratic governance within the Islamic Republic framework.

nodes.label: Mohsen Darrebaghi
nodes.role: Brigadier General; Logistics and Support Deputy (d. 2 March 2026)

data.role: Brigadier General; Logistics and Support Deputy (d. 2 March 2026)
data.bio: Mohsen Darrebaghi served as a Brigadier General and Deputy for Logistics and Support in the Iranian Armed Forces. He was killed on 2 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave), in the same strike that killed [Bahram Hosseini Motlagh](/people/bahram-hosseini-motlagh) and [Hassan-Ali Tajik](/people/hassan-ali-tajik).

sources.title: Senior Armed Forces Officers Killed in March 2 Strike

nodes.label: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh
nodes.role: Head, IRGC Amad Project (nuclear weapons programme); IRGC Brigadier General; assassinated 2020

data.role: Head, IRGC Amad Project (nuclear weapons programme); IRGC Brigadier General; assassinated 2020
data.bio: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh was Iran's foremost nuclear weapons scientist and the head of the Amad Project — the Islamic Republic's clandestine nuclear weapons research programme, which the IAEA documented as pursuing warhead miniaturisation, implosion tests, and delivery system integration. He held the rank of Brigadier General in the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and served simultaneously as Deputy Defence Minister for Research and Innovation. The IAEA identified him as the scientist 'orchestrating' Iran's military nuclear work in classified annex documents leaked in 2011. He was the target of [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh)'s aerospace programme's delivery systems. He was assassinated on 27 November 2020 near Absard, east of Tehran, in a sophisticated remote-controlled ambush widely attributed to Israeli intelligence. His killing triggered calls in the Iranian Majlis to accelerate uranium enrichment and contributed to further JCPOA collapse. [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi), then head of the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran), described him as the 'shahid of science'.

nodes.label: Mohsen Kadivar
nodes.role: Dissident Shia cleric; philosopher of religion; professor at Duke University

data.role: Dissident Shia cleric; philosopher of religion; professor at Duke University
data.bio: Mohsen Kadivar is a Shia cleric and philosopher who became one of the most prominent religious critics of velayat-e faqih (guardianship of the jurist). Trained in the Qom seminaries, he studied under Grand Ayatollah Hossein-Ali Montazeri and earned a doctorate in philosophy from Tarbiat Modares University. In February 1999 he was sentenced to 18 months in prison by the Special Clergy Court for 'disseminating propaganda against the Islamic Republic' and 'disturbing public opinion' — his real offence being published critiques arguing that absolute clerical rule has no basis in Islamic jurisprudence. After his release he continued teaching at Tarbiat Modares University until 2008, when he left Iran for a visiting position at Duke University, where he remains as a research professor of Islamic studies. He co-authored the 2010 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) manifesto with [Abdolkarim Soroush](/people/abdolkarim-soroush), [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), and others, calling for the resignation of Ahmadinejad and free elections.

nodes.label: Mohsen Kangarloo
nodes.role: Security Adviser to Prime Minister Mousavi (1982–1989); key Iranian negotiator in the Iran-Contra affair

data.role: Security Adviser to Prime Minister Mousavi (1982–1989); key Iranian negotiator in the Iran-Contra affair
data.bio: Mohsen Kangarloo served as security adviser to Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) from 1982 to 1989 and was one of the key Iranian negotiators in the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985). In February 1986, he met with Oliver North, [Manucher Ghorbanifar](/people/manucher-ghorbanifar), and Albert Hakim in Germany to discuss weapons transfers and hostage releases. Kangarloo pushed for the United States to provide Iran advanced air-to-air missiles for its fleet of F-14 fighters needed in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). He later stated in a 2018 interview that [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) took hostages 'because it can be useful for us,' confirming Iran's strategic leverage over the hostage situation. He died on 25 March 2020.

nodes.label: Mohsen Mahdavi Kalateh
nodes.role: Deputy Minister of Intelligence

data.role: Deputy Minister of Intelligence
data.bio: Mohsen Mahdavi Kalateh served as a Deputy Minister within Iran's [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) (MOIS). He was killed on 3 March 2026 in the continuing wave of targeted strikes on Iranian intelligence leadership that followed the [February 28, 2026 decapitation strike](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-feb-2026).

nodes.label: Mohsen Milani
nodes.role: Professor of Political Science, University of South Florida; Iran analyst

data.role: Professor of Political Science, University of South Florida; Iran analyst
data.bio:
Mohsen Milani is a professor of political science at the University of South Florida (USF), where he has taught for decades and served as executive director of USF's Center for Strategic and Diplomatic Studies. He is a widely cited specialist on the Islamic Republic's foreign policy, regional strategy, and US-Iran relations.

Milani is the author of *The Making of Iran's Islamic Revolution* (1988, 1994), one of the standard English-language accounts of the 1979 revolutionary process. He contributes frequently to Foreign Affairs, the Wilson Center, and other policy outlets on Iran-US tensions, Iran-Gulf dynamics, and nuclear diplomacy. He is a regular commentator for American and international broadcast media on Iranian foreign policy and often frames Iran's behavior as strategically rational within its own logic, arguing for sustained diplomatic engagement over coercive pressure.

His academic and policy commentary consistently situates Iran's regional posture in a realist framework, analyzing IRGC activities, proxy networks, and sanctions policy from a perspective that emphasizes Iran's security calculations rather than ideological drivers.

sources.title: Mohsen Milani — University of South Florida

nodes.label: Mohsen Pak-Aeen
nodes.role: Former Iranian Ambassador to Azerbaijan, Thailand, and Zambia

data.role: Former Iranian Ambassador to Azerbaijan, Thailand, and Zambia
data.bio: Mohsen Pak-Aeen is a veteran Iranian diplomat who served as ambassador to Azerbaijan, Thailand, and Zambia. He is associated with the Office of the Supreme Leader's international affairs apparatus. Opposition and diaspora sources allege that his postings involved liaison functions for the Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS), though these claims have not been independently verified and are categorized as alleged.

nodes.label: Mohsen Paknejad
nodes.role: Minister of Petroleum (2024–present)

data.role: Minister of Petroleum (2024–present)
data.bio: Mohsen Paknejad is Iran's current Minister of Petroleum, appointed by [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in August 2024. A petroleum engineer, he previously served in various management roles within the national oil sector, including at the National Iranian Oil Company (NIOC). His appointment was seen as a technocratic choice aimed at improving production efficiency and attracting investment to the oil sector.

nodes.label: Mohsen Rabbani
nodes.role: Former Iranian cultural attaché in Buenos Aires; MOIS/Quds Force asset

data.role: Former Iranian cultural attaché in Buenos Aires; MOIS/Quds Force asset
data.bio: Mohsen Rabbani served as Iran's cultural attaché in Buenos Aires from 1994 and operated out of the [Islamic Cultural Institute of Buenos Aires](/orgs/islamic-cultural-institute-buenos-aires) (At-Tauhid Mosque). Argentine prosecutors identify him as the on-the-ground coordinator of the [AMIA bombing](/events/amia-bombing-1994) of July 18, 1994, which killed 85 people at the Jewish community centre in Buenos Aires. He is assessed by investigators to be both a [MOIS](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) asset and affiliated with the [IRGC-Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). He is subject to an Interpol red notice and Argentine arrest warrant. After leaving Argentina, Rabbani remained active in Latin America as a recruiter of Shia converts and builder of Hezbollah-linked networks across the region.

nodes.label: Mohsen Rezaei
nodes.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (1981–1997); Secretary of the Expediency Discernment Council (1997–2021); Vice President for Economic Affairs (2021–2024)

data.role: Commander-in-Chief of the IRGC (1981–1997); Secretary of the Expediency Discernment Council (1997–2021); Vice President for Economic Affairs (2021–2024)
data.bio: Mohsen Rezaei was the longest-serving commander of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), leading it from 1981 to 1997 — a period spanning the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), the [Iran-Contra affair](/events/iran-contra-affair-1985), and the IRGC's transformation from a revolutionary militia into a major military-industrial force. Appointed by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) at age 27, he oversaw the IRGC's wartime operations and its expansion into intelligence and economic activities. After leaving the IRGC, he served as Secretary of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) under [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). He ran unsuccessfully for president in 2005, 2009, and 2013. In 2021, he was appointed Vice President for Economic Affairs under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi). An Argentine court issued an international arrest warrant for him in 2006 in connection with the 1994 AMIA bombing in Buenos Aires. In March 2026, [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) appointed him as military adviser to the Supreme Leader.

nodes.label: Mojtaba Khamenei
nodes.role: 3rd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (2026–present)

data.role: 3rd Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran (2026–present)
data.bio: Mojtaba Khamenei is the second son of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and has been a shadowy but powerful figure in the Islamic Republic for decades. He long headed an intelligence bureau within the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), functioning as a key conduit between his father's office and the IRGC's security apparatus. His wife is Zahra Haddad-Adel, daughter of [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel), former Speaker of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly). Following Ali Khamenei's death on 28 February 2026, the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) elected Mojtaba as the third Supreme Leader on 8 March 2026 — the second succession in the republic's history.

nodes.label: Monireh Gorji
nodes.role: Quran scholar; only woman elected to the 1979 Assembly of Experts for the Constitution

data.role: Quran scholar; only woman elected to the 1979 Assembly of Experts for the Constitution
data.bio: Monireh Gorji was an Islamic scholar and the only woman among the 73 members of the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979. Elected from Tehran Province on the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) ticket with over 1.3 million votes, she was a Quran scholar and mujtahid. Despite her IRP affiliation, she raised objections during the constitutional debates about restrictions being imposed on women in the name of Islam. She continued her scholarly career in Quranic studies and Islamic jurisprudence.

nodes.label: Morteza Agha-Tehrani
nodes.role: MP; Chairman, Majlis Cultural Committee; former Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability

data.role: MP; Chairman, Majlis Cultural Committee; former Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability
data.bio: Morteza Agha-Tehrani is a senior figure of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) who served as the party's founding Secretary-General before handing the role to [Sadegh Mahsouli](/people/sadegh-mahsouli) in late 2020. He chairs the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly)'s Cultural Committee — a key position that made him the primary legislative driver of the controversial mandatory hijab enforcement bill. He was also described as [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s personal ethics teacher, cementing the Paydari network's ties to the Ahmadinejad presidency.

nodes.label: Morteza Eshraqi
nodes.role: Former Tehran Prosecutor General; member, Death Committee (1988)

data.role: Former Tehran Prosecutor General; member, Death Committee (1988)
data.bio: Morteza Eshraqi was Tehran's Prosecutor General in 1988 and is one of two officials explicitly named in [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa as responsible for implementing the mass execution of political prisoners in Tehran — an event the UN classifies as crimes against humanity. His deputy, [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi), also sat on the committee. His presence at the committee's 15 August 1988 meeting with Ayatollah Montazeri is captured on audio recordings published in 2016. He subsequently served as a judge on the Supreme Court from 1989 to 1998.

nodes.label: Morteza Larijani
nodes.role: SNSC Assistant for Internal Security; son of Ali Larijani (d. 17 March 2026)

data.role: SNSC Assistant for Internal Security; son of Ali Larijani (d. 17 March 2026)
data.bio: Morteza Larijani served as Assistant for Internal Security at the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council), and was the son of [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani), the former Speaker of Parliament. He was killed on 17 March 2026 alongside his father in an Israeli airstrike, as part of the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave). The strike also killed [Alireza Bayat](/people/alireza-bayat) and [Ali Bateni](/people/ali-bateni).

sources.title: Iran's wartime leader isn't a dealmaker

nodes.label: Morteza Motahhari
nodes.role: Co-founder of Combatant Clergy Association; Council of Islamic Revolution chairman

data.role: Co-founder of Combatant Clergy Association; Council of Islamic Revolution chairman
data.bio: Morteza Motahhari was one of the most influential clerical scholars of the Islamic Revolution. A devoted student of [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in Qom, he co-founded the Hussainiyyah Ershad and the [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) (JRM). He served as chairman of Khomeini's Council of the Islamic Revolution in early 1979. He was assassinated on 1 May 1979 by the [Forqan Group](/orgs/forqan-group). His intellectual legacy underpins much of the Islamic Republic's ideological framework. He is the father-in-law of former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and the father of MP [Ali Motahari](/people/ali-motahari).

nodes.label: Mostafa Chamran
nodes.role: First Minister of Defence of Islamic Republic; co-founder of Amal Movement in Lebanon

data.role: First Minister of Defence of Islamic Republic; co-founder of Amal Movement in Lebanon
data.bio: Engineer, guerrilla fighter, and political figure. Helped organize the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) from the United States. In 1971 he left the US for Lebanon, where he became the close associate and military right-hand man of [Musa al-Sadr](/people/musa-al-sadr). Together they co-founded the [Amal Movement](/orgs/amal-movement) in 1975 as the military wing of the Movement of the Deprived. Chamran established training camps in southern Lebanon near the Israeli border, where he trained hundreds of Shia fighters — and, by his own account, Iranian volunteers as well. His training infrastructure, fighter networks, and organizational methods in Lebanon laid critical groundwork for the later emergence of [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) after the 1982 Israeli invasion. After the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Chamran returned to Iran and served as the first post-revolution Minister of Defence. He fought on the front lines in the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and was killed in action on 21 June 1981 in Dehlavieh, Khuzestan.

nodes.label: Mostafa Izadi
nodes.role: Senior IRGC commander; close associate of Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam

data.role: Senior IRGC commander; close associate of Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam
data.bio: Mostafa Izadi is a senior commander in the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) who served alongside [Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam](/people/hassan-tehrani-moghaddam) in the early years of Iran's ballistic missile programme. Izadi testified that Moghaddam pioneered the IRGC's ground-to-ground missile systems from 1984, beginning with the Oghab rocket during the Iran–Iraq War. He has been cited as one of Moghaddam's closest colleagues and provided first-hand accounts of the foundational period of Iran's indigenous missile development effort.

nodes.label: Mostafa Kavakebian
nodes.role: Reformist politician; founder and leader of the Democracy Party of Iran; former MP; editor-in-chief of Mardomsalari newspaper

data.role: Reformist politician; founder and leader of the Democracy Party of Iran; former MP; editor-in-chief of Mardomsalari newspaper
data.bio:
Mostafa Kavakebian (born 18 March 1963) is an Iranian reformist politician with a Ph.D. in political science from Tarbiat Modarres University. He founded the [Democracy Party of Iran](/orgs/democracy-party-iran) (Hezb-e Mardomsalari) in 2000 and has served as its secretary-general. He was twice elected to the Islamic Consultative Assembly: representing Semnan and Mehdishahr from 2008 to 2012, and representing Tehran from 2016 to 2020. The Guardian Council disqualified him from presidential candidacies in 2005, 2013, 2017, 2021, and 2024. He also serves as editor-in-chief of Mardomsalari newspaper, affiliated with his party.

Kavakebian is associated with the reformist camp and has at various points aligned with the broader reform movement while maintaining his own party structure. Despite his reformist profile, he attracted significant controversy in July 2025 when he appeared on Iranian state television and claimed that [Catherine Perez-Shakdam](/people/catherine-perez-shakdam) had maintained sexual relationships with 120 senior Iranian officials. Iran's state broadcaster publicly dismissed the claims as "obscene and false," and Iran's security and judicial bodies stated that no documents indicated any such conduct. Tehran's prosecutor subsequently charged Kavakebian for making unsupported public allegations that disturbed public opinion. He was convicted and sentenced to 14 months in prison, suspended for four years, and banned from all media activity for two years. The episode illustrates the disinformation dynamics surrounding the Perez-Shakdam case, in which regime-aligned figures fabricated sensationalized narratives to reframe an institutional embarrassment as a sophisticated foreign intelligence operation.

nodes.label: Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar
nodes.role: Former Minister of Defense (2005–2009); former Minister of Interior (2009–2013)

data.role: Former Minister of Defense (2005–2009); former Minister of Interior (2009–2013)
data.bio: Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar is an IRGC brigadier general who served as Defense Minister in [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s first term (2005–2009) and then transferred to the Interior Ministry in the second term (2009–2013). As Defense Minister he presided over the rapid advancement of Iran's missile programme. As Interior Minister he supervised the disputed 2009 presidential election that triggered the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). He is subject to US and EU sanctions.

nodes.label: Mostafa Pourmohammadi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Justice (2013–2017); former Minister of Interior (2005–2008)

data.role: Former Minister of Justice (2013–2017); former Minister of Interior (2005–2008)
data.bio: Mostafa Pourmohammadi is a cleric who served as Interior Minister under [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) (2005–2008) and later as Justice Minister under [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (2013–2017). His appointment by Rouhani was controversial and drew protests from human rights organisations, as Pourmohammadi had served as a member of the 'Death Committee' — the three-person panel in Tehran that approved the mass execution of thousands of political prisoners in 1988 following [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s fatwa. He later explicitly defended the executions. He ran in the 2024 presidential election.

nodes.label: Mostafa Roudaki
nodes.role: Alleged MOIS officer; linked to UK-based surveillance of Iranian dissidents

data.role: Alleged MOIS officer; linked to UK-based surveillance of Iranian dissidents
data.bio: Mostafa Roudaki is alleged to be a Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS) officer who conducted UK-based surveillance and targeting operations against journalists at Iran International and Iranian dissidents in Britain. Unlike formal diplomats, he is reported to have operated through Iranian state infrastructure in the UK without a formal diplomatic posting. Details are drawn from UK security reporting and the investigations surrounding Iran International's decision to temporarily relocate operations due to death threats. All intelligence connections are alleged; evidence remains partial.

nodes.label: Mostafa Tajzadeh
nodes.role: Reformist politician; former Deputy Interior Minister

data.role: Reformist politician; former Deputy Interior Minister
data.bio: Mostafa Tajzadeh is a prominent reformist politician and member of the [Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization](/orgs/mojahedin-of-islamic-revolution-organization). He served as deputy interior minister under [President Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). He was arrested after the 2009 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests and sentenced to six years in prison. He attempted to run for president in 2021 but was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). He was re-arrested in 2022 during the Mahsa Amini protests.

nodes.label: Mostafa Zahrani
nodes.role: Director of IPIS (Institute for Political and International Studies)

data.role: Director of IPIS (Institute for Political and International Studies)
data.bio: Mostafa Zahrani served as head of the [Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS)](/orgs/ipis), the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs)' in-house think tank. He oversaw the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), serving as the main liaison between the network of Western-based academics and [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif)'s Foreign Ministry. Leaked emails showed that IEI members such as [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez) shared article drafts with Zahrani before publication, and that [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai) consulted him on conference attendance and engagements with Israeli and Saudi counterparts.

nodes.label: Moussa Khiabani
nodes.role: Deputy leader of MEK; commander of MEK armed wing

data.role: Deputy leader of MEK; commander of MEK armed wing
data.bio: Moussa Khiabani studied physics at the University of Tehran and joined the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq). He trained in guerrilla warfare in Lebanon and was sentenced to life imprisonment in 1972 for MEK activities. Released in January 1979 during the revolution, he became [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi)'s deputy and commanded the MEK's armed wing inside Iran. On 8 February 1982, the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) raided his safehouse in northern Tehran. After a three-hour firefight, Khiabani was killed along with [Ashraf Rabiei](/people/ashraf-rabiei).

nodes.label: Mozaffar Baghai
nodes.role: Co-founder of National Front; leader of the Toilers' Party of Iran

data.role: Co-founder of National Front; leader of the Toilers' Party of Iran
data.bio:
Mozaffar Baghai was an Iranian politician from Kerman who co-founded the [National Front of Iran](/orgs/national-front-iran) in 1949 with [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh), [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi), and [Hossein Makki](/people/hossein-makki). He simultaneously led the Toilers' Party of Iran (Hezb-e Zahmatkeshan), a left-leaning nationalist party that was a constituent member of the National Front.

Baghai was initially a close ally of Mossadegh and a prominent voice in the oil nationalisation movement. However, his relationship with Mossadegh deteriorated sharply before the [1953 coup d'état](/events/iranian-coup-1953). He broke with the National Front and became a fierce critic of Mossadegh, to the point where he was accused of collaborating with the Shah's regime and possibly with [SAVAK](/orgs/savak), the Iranian intelligence service, as well as with CIA-connected networks in the lead-up to the coup. Some historians consider him to have played a role in weakening Mossadegh's government from within.

After the coup, Baghai initially benefited from his improved standing with the Shah's government but was later imprisoned under the Pahlavi regime. He died in 1987.

nodes.label: Murray Bookchin
nodes.role: Political philosopher; founder of social ecology and libertarian municipalism

data.role: Political philosopher; founder of social ecology and libertarian municipalism
data.bio:
Murray Bookchin (1921–2006) was an American political philosopher and social theorist who developed the theory of **social ecology** — the thesis that ecological crises are rooted in social hierarchies and can only be resolved through fundamentally democratic social reorganisation — and from it derived **libertarian municipalism**: the concept of directly democratic neighbourhood assemblies confederated across cities as an alternative to both capitalism and the centralised nation-state. Born to Russian Jewish immigrants in the Bronx, Bookchin moved through Communist youth organisations and Trotskyism before charting his own course. In 1999 he broke formally with anarchism and named his philosophy **communalism**.

Bookchin's most significant indirect impact on Iranian politics came through [Abdullah Öcalan](/people/abdullah-ocalan), the imprisoned founder of the [PKK](/orgs/pkk). Imprisoned on Imrali island after 1999, Öcalan encountered Bookchin's writings and adapted libertarian municipalism into **democratic confederalism** — a bottom-up, feminist, ecologist, and pluralist self-governance model that explicitly rejects the nation-state as a goal. Öcalan described himself as Bookchin's student and attempted to arrange a meeting before Bookchin died in July 2006. The PKK formally adopted democratic confederalism around 2005. Through the PKK-affiliated [PJAK](/orgs/pjak), this ideology became the foundational framework for Iranian Kurdish armed resistance after 2004, giving Bookchin an indirect but documented influence on the Iranian-Kurdish political landscape.

nodes.label: Musa al-Sadr
nodes.role: Lebanese-Iranian Shia cleric; founder of the Amal Movement

data.role: Lebanese-Iranian Shia cleric; founder of the Amal Movement
data.bio: Musa al-Sadr was an Iranian-born Lebanese Shia cleric who became the most prominent leader of Lebanon's Shia community in the 1960s and 1970s. He founded the Movement of the Deprived in 1974 and co-founded its military wing, the [Amal Movement](/orgs/amal-movement), with [Mostafa Chamran](/people/mostafa-chamran). He worked closely with Chamran to establish training facilities for Shia fighters in southern Lebanon. His political mobilization of the Shia community laid the social and organizational groundwork from which [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) later emerged. He disappeared during a visit to Libya on 31 August 1978 under mysterious circumstances widely attributed to Muammar Gaddafi, and is presumed dead.

nodes.label: Mustafa Barzani
nodes.role: Founder and President of the Kurdistan Democratic Party (KDP); military commander

data.role: Founder and President of the Kurdistan Democratic Party (KDP); military commander
data.bio: Mustafa Barzani (1903–1979) was the towering figure of twentieth-century Kurdish nationalism. A tribal chieftain and military commander from the Barzan region of northern Iraq, he led Iraqi Kurdish forces in a series of revolts against successive Baghdad governments. In 1946, Barzani crossed into Iranian Kurdistan and played a critical military role in the [Republic of Mahabad](/events/republic-of-mahabad-1946), commanding its forces alongside President [Qazi Muhammad](/people/qazi-muhammad). When the republic collapsed in December 1946 after the withdrawal of Soviet support, Barzani led his fighters on an epic march to the Soviet Union, where they lived in exile until 1958. He returned after the Iraqi revolution and led the Kurdistan Democratic Party (KDP), which he had co-founded in 1946. He spent the 1960s–70s waging a sustained insurgency against the Baghdad government, becoming the pre-eminent symbol of Kurdish resistance. After the collapse of the Kurdish revolt following the Algiers Accord of 1975, Barzani went into exile in the United States, where he died in 1979. He is the father of Masoud Barzani, who later led the KDP and served as President of the Kurdistan Region of Iraq.

nodes.label: Mustafa Hijri
nodes.role: Secretary-General of the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan

data.role: Secretary-General of the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan
data.bio: Mustafa Hijri (born 1948) is the current Secretary-General of the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan), a position he has held since 2006. A lifelong party member, he joined the PDKI during the Kurdish armed struggle in the 1970s and rose through its political and military ranks. Operating from Iraqi Kurdistan, he has continued to advocate for Kurdish self-determination within a federal democratic Iran. During the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022) of 2022, he called for a united front among Iranian opposition groups and the PDKI's Kurdistan branch saw significant mobilisation.

nodes.label: Nader Hashemi
nodes.role: Director, Alwaleed Center for Muslim-Christian Understanding, Georgetown University

data.role: Director, Alwaleed Center for Muslim-Christian Understanding, Georgetown University
data.bio:
Nader Hashemi is the director of the Alwaleed Center for Muslim-Christian Understanding at Georgetown University. He previously directed the Center for Middle East Studies at the University of Denver's Josef Korbel School of International Studies. He received his doctorate from the University of Toronto.

Hashemi is a scholar of Islam and democracy, comparative politics, and Iranian political thought. His books include *Islam, Secularism, and Liberal Democracy: Toward a Democratic Theory for Muslim Societies* (2009, Oxford University Press) and *The People Reloaded: The Green Movement and the Struggle for Iran's Future* (co-edited with [Danny Postel](/people/danny-postel), 2010). He has also co-edited volumes on the Syrian civil war and the Arab Spring.

Hashemi is a consistent and vocal critic of the Islamic Republic's human rights record. He has spoken and written extensively on behalf of political prisoners in Iran, including during the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) crackdown and after the 2022 Woman Life Freedom uprising. He supports political change inside Iran and advocates for Western governments to prioritize human rights in their Iran policy, which puts him in a more confrontational posture toward the IRI than most Iran scholars.

He has been associated with the [National Iranian American Council](/orgs/niac) on some advocacy efforts but is more clearly aligned with a liberal democratic normative framework that prioritizes human rights over diplomatic accommodation.

sources.title: Nader Hashemi — University of Denver Korbel School

nodes.label: Naqi Mohaddesnia
nodes.role: IRGC Clergy Affairs Head, East Azerbaijan Province (d. 12 March 2026)

data.role: IRGC Clergy Affairs Head, East Azerbaijan Province (d. 12 March 2026)
data.bio: Naqi Mohaddesnia served as head of Clergy Affairs for the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) in East Azerbaijan Province. He was a clerical commissar figure within the IRGC — part of the ideological-political officer corps responsible for religious oversight and indoctrination. He was killed on 12 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Narges Bajoghli
nodes.role: Associate Professor, Johns Hopkins SAIS

data.role: Associate Professor, Johns Hopkins SAIS
data.bio:
Narges Bajoghli is an associate professor at Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies (SAIS). She is a political anthropologist whose fieldwork inside Iran examined the cultural and media production of [Basij](/orgs/basij) and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) loyalists. Her book *Iran Reframed: Anxieties of Power in the Islamic Republic* (2019) documents how the regime's core supporters understand and project their own legitimacy, based on embedded access to IRGC and Basij cultural producers — an unusual level of institutional access that is itself a documented fact of her methodology.

She is co-author of *How Sanctions Work: Iran and the Impact of Economic Warfare* (2023, Stanford University Press) with [Vali Nasr](/people/vali-nasr), Djavad Salehi-Isfahani, and [Esfandyar Batmanghelidj](/people/esfandyar-batmanghelidj). One of the original co-authors listed in earlier versions of the project was [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez) — subsequently identified by Semafor (September 2023) and Iran International as a participant in the Iran Experts Initiative (IEI), a covert Iranian Foreign Ministry influence operation. The book's central argument — that maximum-pressure sanctions strengthen the regime and harm the civilian population — aligns with the Iranian government's preferred framing on sanctions. The co-authorship with an IEI-documented figure on this specific topic is editorially relevant context, even absent evidence of direct coordination on Bajoghli's part.

nodes.label: Narges Mohammadi
nodes.role: Human rights activist; Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2023); imprisoned in Evin Prison

data.role: Human rights activist; Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2023); imprisoned in Evin Prison
data.bio:
Narges Mohammadi is an Iranian human rights activist and the vice-president of the Defenders of Human Rights Center, founded by [Shirin Ebadi](/people/shirin-ebadi). She has been arrested and imprisoned multiple times by the Islamic Republic for her activism, particularly her campaigns against the death penalty and mandatory hijab.

She was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2023 for her fight against the oppression of women in Iran and her efforts to promote human rights and freedom for all. She received the prize while imprisoned in Evin Prison.

During the 2025–2026 protests, she characterized [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi)'s supporters as "the opposition against opposition," suggesting that his polarizing role alienated other opposition factions.

nodes.label: Naser Makarem Shirazi
nodes.role: Grand Ayatollah; Shia marja' (source of emulation)

data.role: Grand Ayatollah; Shia marja' (source of emulation)
data.bio: Grand Ayatollah Naser Makarem Shirazi is one of the most prominent Shia scholars and marjas (sources of emulation) in Iran. He was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979, representing Fars Province with over 84% of the vote. Based in Qom, he is the author of the influential Tafsir Nemooneh (Exemplary Commentary), a multi-volume Quranic exegesis. He has served as a member of the [Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom](/orgs/society-of-seminary-teachers-of-qom) and is known for issuing fatwas on social and economic matters. While broadly supportive of the Islamic Republic's system, he has occasionally criticized economic policies and corruption.

nodes.label: Nasrin Sotoudeh
nodes.role: Human rights lawyer and political prisoner

data.role: Human rights lawyer and political prisoner
data.bio: Nasrin Sotoudeh is an Iranian human rights lawyer known for defending political prisoners, women's rights activists, and minors facing the death penalty. She has been imprisoned multiple times by the [judiciary](/orgs/judiciary-of-iran) for her legal advocacy. In 2019, she was sentenced to 38 years in prison and 148 lashes on charges including espionage and insulting the Supreme Leader. She has defended clients involved in the 2009 Green Movement and the 2022 Mahsa Amini protests. On 28 January 2026, she co-signed a joint statement with other prominent political prisoners and civil society figures condemning the [January 2026 massacres](/events/january-2026-massacres). She received the European Parliament's Sakharov Prize for Freedom of Thought in 2012.

nodes.label: Nasrollah Pejmanfar
nodes.role: MP; Chairman, Article 90 Committee, 11th and 12th Majlis

data.role: MP; Chairman, Article 90 Committee, 11th and 12th Majlis
data.bio: Nasrollah Pejmanfar is a hardline MP and member of the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) who chairs the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly)'s Article 90 Committee — the parliamentary oversight body that investigates complaints against the judiciary, executive, and Majlis itself. His committee chairmanship gives the Paydari Front an institutional oversight lever in disputes with other state bodies.

nodes.label: Nasser Hadian
nodes.role: Professor of Political Science, University of Tehran

data.role: Professor of Political Science, University of Tehran
data.bio:
Nasser Hadian is a professor of political science at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran). He is a regular interlocutor with Western think tanks, having engaged with institutions including the Atlantic Council and the [International Crisis Group](/orgs/international-crisis-group). He is often characterised as reformist-adjacent in his political outlook.

Hadian represents a category of Iranian academics who operate within regime-permissible bounds: able to engage with Western institutions and articulate positions that differ at the margins from hardline orthodoxy, but dependent on the continued tolerance of the state for their platform and position. His academic tenure and ability to travel for international engagements indicates he has not crossed lines the Islamic Republic regards as unacceptable.

His commentary on nuclear negotiations, US–Iran relations, and Iranian domestic politics is informed by genuine academic analysis but is constrained by the institutional reality that severe dissent would end his career or worse. He should not be read as a fully independent voice, though he is a meaningfully different type of figure from overt propagandists embedded in the regime's formal apparatus.

nodes.label: Nasser Moghadam
nodes.role: Director of SAVAK (1978–1979)

data.role: Director of SAVAK (1978–1979)
data.bio:
Nasser Moghadam was the last director of [SAVAK](/orgs/savak), the Pahlavi-era intelligence and security service, serving from 1978 until the organisation's dissolution following the [1979 Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). He was appointed to the position by [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) in a late attempt to reform SAVAK's brutal image during the revolutionary crisis of 1977–1979. Moghadam released some political prisoners and attempted to reduce SAVAK's most overt repressive activities, but was unable to stem the revolutionary tide.

After the revolution, Moghadam was arrested along with other senior Pahlavi-era military and security officials. On 11 April 1979, he was tried before [Sadegh Khalkhali](/people/sadegh-khalkhali)'s revolutionary tribunal and executed alongside other senior security figures as part of the [post-revolution executions of 1979](/events/post-revolution-executions-1979).

nodes.label: Nasser Zarafshan
nodes.role: Human rights lawyer; represented chain murders victims' families; political prisoner

data.role: Human rights lawyer; represented chain murders victims' families; political prisoner
data.bio: Nasser Zarafshan is a human rights lawyer who represented the families of [Mohammad Mokhtari](/people/mohammad-mokhtari) and [Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh](/people/mohammad-jafar-pouyandeh), two writers murdered in the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). He publicly challenged the official narrative that the killings were the work of 'rogue agents,' arguing that the fatwa authorising the murders came from senior clerical figures. He stated: 'The problem is not to which faction the perpetrators belong, but to which faction the issuer of the fatwa belongs.' On 19 March 2002, Zarafshan was sentenced to five years in prison — two years for 'disseminating state secrets' and three years for allegedly possessing firearms — plus 70 lashes for possessing alcohol. His bail was set at $50,000, four times the bail of some of the accused murderers. His imprisonment was widely condemned by international human rights organisations and PEN International.

nodes.label: Nazila Fathi
nodes.role: Former New York Times Tehran bureau correspondent; author of The Lonely War; journalist forced into exile in 2009

data.role: Former New York Times Tehran bureau correspondent; author of The Lonely War; journalist forced into exile in 2009
data.bio:
Nazila Fathi is an Iranian journalist who served as Tehran correspondent and bureau chief for the New York Times from the late 1990s through 2009. She covered the [presidential election of 2009](/events/presidential-election-iran-2009) and the subsequent [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), providing some of the most detailed on-the-ground reporting from inside Iran during the uprising. Following threats to her safety as the crackdown intensified, Fathi was forced to flee Iran in 2009.

She is the author of *The Lonely War: One Woman's Account of the Struggle for Modern Iran* (2014), a memoir combining personal experience with political history, tracing the arc of the Islamic Republic from revolutionary promise to authoritarian consolidation. Since leaving Iran, Fathi has continued to write about Iranian affairs from exile and has taught journalism and contributed to policy discussions on Iran.

nodes.label: Negar Mortazavi
nodes.role: Journalist; host of The Iran Podcast; senior fellow at the Center for International Policy

data.role: Journalist; host of The Iran Podcast; senior fellow at the Center for International Policy
data.bio:
Negar Mortazavi is an Iranian journalist and media commentator who has been based in the United States since going into exile following the [Green Movement protests of 2009](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). She is the host and producer of The Iran Podcast, which covers Iranian politics, society, and U.S.-Iran relations with a focus on diplomatic engagement. She serves as a senior fellow at the [Center for International Policy](/orgs/center-for-international-policy), where she works on Iran policy issues.

Mortazavi worked as a media and communications assistant at the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac) in 2014, an organization whose founder [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi) was described by Iranian Foreign Minister [Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) in 2013 parliamentary testimony as his "main partner" in the United States, and which a US federal court found it "reasonable to assert" was "lobbying on behalf of the Iranian government" (Hassan Dai v. NIAC, 2015). In September 2024, Mortazavi met with Iranian President [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) at his hotel during his visit to New York for the UN General Assembly — an unusual level of direct access to an Iranian head of state for a diaspora journalist.

Mortazavi is consistently dovish on Iran, advocating for diplomacy over sanctions. She is not an opposition figure in any political sense — she has not aligned with dissident movements, exile political organizations, or the protest waves inside Iran. Her institutional positioning at NIAC and CIP, and her access to Iranian officials, place her in a pro-engagement lane that should be understood when her analysis is cited.

sources.title: Negar Mortazavi — Center for International Policy
Mortazavi meets Iranian President Pezeshkian in New York

nodes.label: Nematollah Nassiri
nodes.role: Director of SAVAK (1965–1978)

data.role: Director of SAVAK (1965–1978)
data.bio:
Nematollah Nassiri was the longest-serving director of [SAVAK](/orgs/savak), Iran's secret police, holding the position from 1965 to 1978 under [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi). During his tenure, SAVAK became notorious for the systematic torture and imprisonment of political dissidents, and for its role in suppressing all forms of organised opposition to the Shah's regime. He was a key pillar of Pahlavi security architecture.

Nassiri was removed from his position in 1978 as the Shah attempted to distance himself from SAVAK's repressive reputation in the face of the growing revolutionary movement. He was subsequently appointed ambassador to Pakistan.

After the [1979 Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Nassiri was among the first senior Pahlavi officials to be arrested. He was tried and executed on 15–16 February 1979 — among the very first victims of the [post-revolution executions of 1979](/events/post-revolution-executions-1979) — by [Sadegh Khalkhali](/people/sadegh-khalkhali)'s revolutionary court, alongside three other senior military officials.

nodes.label: Noor Pahlavi
nodes.role: Eldest daughter of Reza Pahlavi; declared heir to Pahlavi succession (2019)

data.role: Eldest daughter of Reza Pahlavi; declared heir to Pahlavi succession (2019)
data.bio: Noor Pahlavi is the eldest daughter of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) and [Yasmine Etemad-Amini](/people/yasmine-etemad-amini). In 2019, her father declared her heir to the Pahlavi succession, emphasizing equal rights between men and women — a break from traditional male-line primogeniture.

nodes.label: Nureddin Kianouri
nodes.role: First Secretary of the Tudeh Party of Iran (1979–1984)

data.role: First Secretary of the Tudeh Party of Iran (1979–1984)
data.bio: Nureddin Kianouri (1915–1999) was an Iranian engineer, professor, and communist political leader who served as First Secretary of the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) during the post-revolutionary period. He joined the Tudeh Party in 1942 and rose through its ranks, becoming a central committee member by 1945. After the 1953 coup, he and his wife [Maryam Firouz](/people/maryam-firouz) fled to East Germany, where they lived in exile for over two decades. They returned to Iran after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Kianouri replaced [Iraj Eskandari](/people/iraj-eskandari) as party leader in 1979 and pursued a controversial strategy of supporting [Ayatollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s government as an anti-imperialist ally. In 1983, the regime arrested the entire Tudeh leadership. Kianouri was tortured and forced to deliver a televised confession denouncing the party. He was released and placed under house arrest, where he remained until his death on 5 November 1999.

nodes.label: Parvaneh Forouhar
nodes.role: Political activist; co-leader of Nation of Iran Party; assassinated 1998

data.role: Political activist; co-leader of Nation of Iran Party; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Parvaneh Eskandari Forouhar was a political activist and co-leader of the [Nation of Iran Party](/orgs/nation-of-iran-party) alongside her husband [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar). Active in Iranian politics since the 1950s, she was a fierce advocate for women's rights and democratic governance. On the night of 22 November 1998, [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) agents entered the Forouhar home and murdered both husband and wife — she was stabbed 25 times. Their double assassination was one of the most shocking acts of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) and galvanised the reformist press and civil society into demanding accountability from the intelligence services.

nodes.label: Peyman Jebelli
nodes.role: Head of IRIB (2021–present)

data.role: Head of IRIB (2021–present)
data.bio: Peyman Jebelli has served as head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) since 2021, appointed by Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). He previously headed IRIB's World Service, overseeing international broadcasting operations. His tenure has coincided with the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022), during which IRIB broadcast forced confessions of detained protesters and framed demonstrators as rioters backed by foreign powers. The European Union imposed fresh sanctions on IRIB leadership in October 2022 in response to this coverage.

nodes.label: Pirouz Davani
nodes.role: Leftist activist; former political prisoner; editor; assassinated 1998

data.role: Leftist activist; former political prisoner; editor; assassinated 1998
data.bio: Pirouz Davani was a leftist political activist, former political prisoner, and editor of the newspaper *Pirooz*. He disappeared on 29 August 1998. His body was never recovered, but he is believed to have been murdered around 20 September 1998 by agents of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). His disappearance was one of the earliest of the 1998 wave of [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998), preceding the more widely publicised killings of the Forouhars, Mokhtari, and Pouyandeh.

nodes.label: Qasem Soleimani
nodes.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)

data.role: Commander of the IRGC Quds Force (1998–2020)
data.bio: Qasem Soleimani rose from a working-class background in Kerman to become the architect of Iran's network of regional proxies — [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah), Shia militias in Iraq, the Houthis, and forces in Syria. As [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) commander from 1998 until his assassination, he was widely described as the most powerful military operative in the Middle East. He directed Iranian intervention in Syria from 2012 and was the primary coordinator of Iran's Axis of Resistance. He was killed on 3 January 2020 in a US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport, authorised by President Trump.

nodes.label: Qassem Qoreishi
nodes.role: Deputy Head, Basij Forces (d. 17 March 2026)

data.role: Deputy Head, Basij Forces (d. 17 March 2026)
data.bio: Qassem Qoreishi served as Deputy Head of the [Basij Forces](/orgs/basij), the paramilitary volunteer militia under the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). He was killed on 17 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave) targeting senior Iranian military leadership.

sources.title: Senior Basij Deputies Killed in Israeli Strikes

nodes.label: Qazi Muhammad
nodes.role: President of the Republic of Mahabad

data.role: President of the Republic of Mahabad
data.bio: Qazi Muhammad (1893–1947) was a Kurdish judge, religious leader, and politician who served as president of the short-lived [Republic of Mahabad](/events/republic-of-mahabad-1946) in 1946. He founded the Kurdistan Democratic Party (later the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan)) in August 1945. When Soviet forces withdrew from northwestern Iran under international pressure in May 1946, the republic lost its military backing and collapsed in December 1946. Qazi Muhammad was arrested, tried by a military tribunal, and hanged in Mahabad's Chia Chira Square on 31 March 1947. He remains an iconic figure in Kurdish nationalism.

nodes.label: Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1997–1999); former Prosecutor General

data.role: Former Minister of Intelligence (1997–1999); former Prosecutor General
data.bio: Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi was [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami)'s first Intelligence Minister, appointed in 1997 as a compromise candidate after hardliners blocked Khatami's preferred choice. His tenure coincided with the 1998 'chain murders' — the serial killing of reformist intellectuals, writers, and political activists by rogue elements of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). When evidence implicated the ministry itself, Dorri-Najafabadi resigned in 1999. He later served as Iran's Prosecutor General (2008–2009) and as a member of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). He was a member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) before his intelligence appointment.

nodes.label: Ray Takeyh
nodes.role: Senior fellow for Middle East studies, Council on Foreign Relations; former State Department adviser on Iran

data.role: Senior fellow for Middle East studies, Council on Foreign Relations; former State Department adviser on Iran
data.bio:
Ray Takeyh is a senior fellow for Middle East studies at the [Council on Foreign Relations](/orgs/council-on-foreign-relations), where he focuses on Iran, the Persian Gulf, and US policy in the region. He is one of the most prominent hawkish voices among mainstream American Iran analysts.

In 2009, Takeyh served as a senior adviser on Iran in the State Department under Secretary of State Hillary Clinton during the early Obama administration. His tenure coincided with initial US attempts at diplomatic outreach toward Iran and the eruption of the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) following the [disputed presidential election](/events/presidential-election-iran-2009). He later became publicly critical of the Obama administration's Iran policy, arguing that the nuclear negotiations and the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) insufficiently addressed Iran's regional behaviour and ballistic missile programme.

Takeyh's major scholarly works include *Guardians of the Revolution: Iran and the World in the Age of the Ayatollahs* (2009), which examined Iran's foreign policy doctrine since 1979, and *The Last Shah: America, Iran, and the Fall of the Pahlavi Dynasty* (2021), which offered a revisionist account of the [1979 Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) that emphasised [Mohammad Reza Shah](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi)'s own political failures and agency. An earlier work, *Hidden Iran: Paradox and Power in the Islamic Republic* (2006), analysed the internal factional dynamics of the Islamic Republic.

Takeyh is known for arguing that the Islamic Republic is primarily a revolutionary state driven by ideology rather than strategic calculation — a position that leads him to advocate for more confrontational US policies toward Iran, including tougher sanctions and support for opposition movements.

sources.title: Ray Takeyh — Council on Foreign Relations

nodes.label: Reza Amiri Moghadam
nodes.role: Iranian Ambassador to Pakistan (2024–present); former senior MOIS officer

data.role: Iranian Ambassador to Pakistan (2024–present); former senior MOIS officer
data.bio: Reza Amiri Moghadam is a senior Iranian intelligence officer-turned-diplomat, appointed Iran's Ambassador to Pakistan in 2024. According to the US Federal Bureau of Investigation and the US Treasury, he previously headed the operations unit of the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) (MOIS), where he oversaw MOIS agents operating in Europe. The Iranian foreign ministry's own biography lists him as a former deputy of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) for foreign and international policy affairs. In March 2025, the US Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control sanctioned him for his alleged role in the 2007 abduction, prolonged detention, and probable death of former FBI agent Robert Levinson on Kish Island, as well as the subsequent Iranian cover-up. The FBI added him to its 'Seeking Information' list the same year, identifying him by his operational alias Ahmad Amirinia. Despite the sanctions and FBI listing, Pakistan's Foreign Office publicly defended him as 'widely respected' and entitled to full diplomatic privileges. He is an active regime spokesperson on Iran-Pakistan relations and US–Iran negotiations via the Iranian embassy in Islamabad.

sources.title: Reza Amiri Moghadam — FBI Seeking Information
Treasury Sanctions Additional Iranian Intelligence Officers Involved in the Probable Death and Cover-Up of Robert Levinson
Iran's envoy to Pakistan 'widely respected', says FO after FBI adds him to most wanted list

nodes.label: Reza Aslan
nodes.role: Author and religion scholar; Professor, UC Riverside

data.role: Author and religion scholar; Professor, UC Riverside
data.bio:
Reza Aslan is an Iranian-American author, religion scholar, and public intellectual. He was born in Tehran in 1972 and emigrated with his family to the United States after the [1979 Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). He holds a doctorate in the sociology of religion from the University of California, Santa Barbara, and is a professor of creative writing at the University of California, Riverside.

His bestselling books include *No god but God: The Origins, Evolution, and Future of Islam* (2005), which became one of the most widely read popular accounts of Islamic history and theology; and *Zealot: The Life and Times of Jesus of Nazareth* (2013), which was a major commercial success. He is also the author of *Beyond Fundamentalism: Confronting Religious Extremism in the Age of Globalization* (2010) and *God: A Human History* (2017).

Aslan is primarily a religion scholar and public communicator rather than a specialist on contemporary Iranian politics. His commentary on Iran is typically framed from a moderate-to-dovish perspective, opposing military intervention and sanctions escalation and contextualizing IRI behavior within broader patterns of religious nationalism. He produced the CNN docuseries *Believer* (2017) exploring religious traditions around the world.

He was a board member of the National Iranian American Council ([NIAC](/orgs/niac)) for several years, which is relevant context for understanding his Iran policy positioning.

nodes.label: Reza Khazaei
nodes.role: Official, IRGC Quds Force (d. 3 March 2026)

data.role: Official, IRGC Quds Force (d. 3 March 2026)
data.bio: Reza Khazaei was an official within the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). He was killed on 3 March 2026 during the [March 2026 Strike Wave](/events/march-2026-strike-wave).

nodes.label: Reza Marashi
nodes.role: Former Research Director, NIAC

data.role: Former Research Director, NIAC
data.bio: Reza Marashi served as the first Research Director of the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac), joining in 2010. Before NIAC, he spent four years at the U.S. State Department's Office of Iranian Affairs and worked at the Institute for National Strategic Studies on China-Middle East issues. He was also a Tehran-based private strategic consultant connected to the Atieh Bahar consulting ecosystem. His career path — from the State Department's Iran desk to NIAC — exemplifies the revolving door between government Iran policy positions and the NIAC network that critics have highlighted.

nodes.label: Reza Mozaffari Nia
nodes.role: Former Head of SPND (Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research)

data.role: Former Head of SPND (Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research)
data.bio: Brigadier General Reza Mozaffari Nia was an [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) officer who served as a director of [SPND](/orgs/spnd) — the Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research — the classified Iranian body widely assessed as continuing [Mohsen Fakhrizadeh](/people/mohsen-fakhrizadeh)'s covert nuclear weapons research programme. He was among the most senior figures in Iran's clandestine nuclear weapons infrastructure, overseeing SPND during a period of significant activity flagged by the IAEA. He was succeeded as SPND director by [Hossein Jabal Amelian](/people/hossein-jabal-amelian). Mozaffari Nia was killed on 28 February 2026 in Israeli airstrikes targeting Iran's nuclear and military leadership.

sources.title: Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research — Wikipedia
Organization of Defensive Innovation and Research (SPND)
sources.publisher: Wisconsin Project on Nuclear Arms Control

nodes.label: Reza Naji
nodes.role: Military Governor of Isfahan (1978–1979)

data.role: Military Governor of Isfahan (1978–1979)
data.bio:
Reza Naji was a major general in the Imperial Iranian Army who served as Military Governor of Isfahan during the final phase of [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi)'s reign. In this role he was responsible for enforcing martial law in Isfahan during the revolutionary unrest of 1978–1979.

Following the [1979 Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Naji was arrested and tried before [Sadegh Khalkhali](/people/sadegh-khalkhali)'s Islamic Revolutionary Court. On the night of 15–16 February 1979, he was executed by firing squad on the rooftop of the Refah School in Tehran as part of the inaugural round of the [post-revolution executions of 1979](/events/post-revolution-executions-1979), alongside [Nematollah Nassiri](/people/nematollah-nassiri), [Mehdi Rahimi](/people/mehdi-rahimi), and [Manouchehr Khosrodad](/people/manouchehr-khosrodad).

nodes.label: Reza Pahlavi
nodes.role: Crown Prince of Iran (in exile); opposition leader advocating secular democratic transition

data.role: Crown Prince of Iran (in exile); opposition leader advocating secular democratic transition
data.bio:
Reza Pahlavi is the eldest son of the late [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) and [Farah Pahlavi](/people/farah-pahlavi). He was designated Crown Prince in 1967 during his father's coronation. In August 1978, he left Iran to train as a fighter pilot at Reese Air Force Base in Texas and never returned after the [1979 Islamic Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). He enrolled at Williams College in September 1979 but dropped out in 1980, later obtaining a B.S. in political science from the University of Southern California by correspondence in 1985.

Following his father's death in Cairo on 27 July 1980, he declared himself Shah of Iran (Reza Shah II) on his 20th birthday at Koubbeh Palace in Cairo — a claim the U.S. State Department immediately disassociated itself from. He later shifted his public stance toward advocacy for a secular democratic republic, positioning himself as a unifying figure for the Iranian opposition rather than claiming the throne.

In 1982, he was involved in a coup d'état scheme alongside Israeli businessman Yaakov Nimrodi, Saudi businessman Adnan Khashoggi, and General Said Razvani. The plan reportedly had CIA and Israeli cabinet approval but was abandoned in 1983 when Israeli Prime Minister Menachem Begin resigned. He announced formation of a government-in-exile in May 1986.

He married [Yasmine Etemad-Amini](/people/yasmine-etemad-amini) in 1986. They have three daughters, including [Noor Pahlavi](/people/noor-pahlavi), whom he declared heir to the Pahlavi succession in 2019 — a break from traditional male-line primogeniture. His siblings include [Ali Reza Pahlavi](/people/ali-reza-pahlavi) (died 2011) and [Leila Pahlavi](/people/leila-pahlavi) (died 2001).

He co-founded the [Iran National Council](/orgs/iran-national-council) in 2013 as a platform for coordinating opposition activities, and launched the [Phoenix Project of Iran](/orgs/phoenix-project-of-iran) think tank in Washington, D.C. in February 2019, focused on post-regime transition planning.

During the [2022–2023 Woman, Life, Freedom protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022), he emerged as one of the most visible exile voices, calling for civil disobedience and international support. He organized a rally on 11 February 2023 that drew 80,000+ attendees in Los Angeles. A Change.org petition for Pahlavi as representative reached 460,000+ signatures. In March 2023, he spoke at the Oxford Union, and in April 2023 he visited Israel, meeting with President Isaac Herzog and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.

His father's former confidant, [Ahmad Ali Masoud Ansari](/people/ahmad-ali-ansari) (brother of historian [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari)), has publicly alleged that Reza Pahlavi and his aides were funded by Saudi Arabia and the CIA — claims that Reza Pahlavi has not publicly addressed. Nobel Peace Prize laureate [Narges Mohammadi](/people/narges-mohammadi) has characterized his supporters as "the opposition against opposition," suggesting his role is polarizing within the broader movement. He resides in Potomac, Maryland.

nodes.label: Reza Shah Pahlavi
nodes.role: Shah of Iran (1925–1941); Founder of the Pahlavi dynasty

data.role: Shah of Iran (1925–1941); Founder of the Pahlavi dynasty
data.bio:
Reza Shah Pahlavi was the founder of the Pahlavi dynasty and Shah of Iran from 1925 until his forced abdication in 1941. Born Reza Khan in a military family, he rose from a Cossack officer to seize power through the 1921 coup d'état, becoming prime minister and then deposing the last Qajar ruler to declare himself Shah in 1925.

His reign was marked by rapid secular modernisation — building railways, founding Tehran University, reforming the civil service, introducing conscription, and banning the veil — but also by brutal authoritarian rule, suppression of tribal autonomy, and elimination of political opposition. In 1937 his security services arrested 53 prominent Marxist intellectuals in the crackdown later known as the [Group of Fifty-Three arrests (1937)](/events/group-of-fifty-three-arrests-1937), effectively ending the Communist Party of Iran and setting the stage for the later founding of the Tudeh Party.

Following the Anglo-Soviet invasion of Iran in August 1941, the Allied powers forced Reza Shah to abdicate in favour of his eldest son, [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi). He died in exile in Johannesburg, South Africa on 26 July 1944.

nodes.label: Richard Holbrooke
nodes.role: US Special Representative for Afghanistan and Pakistan (2009–2010); veteran diplomat

data.role: US Special Representative for Afghanistan and Pakistan (2009–2010); veteran diplomat
data.bio:
Richard Holbrooke (April 24, 1941 – December 13, 2010) was one of the most consequential American diplomats of the late twentieth and early twenty-first centuries. He served as US Ambassador to Germany, US Ambassador to the United Nations, and as the chief architect of the 1995 Dayton Peace Accords that ended the Bosnian War.

In 2009, President Barack Obama appointed Holbrooke as US Special Representative for Afghanistan and Pakistan (SRAP), a newly created position tasked with coordinating American strategy in the Af-Pak theater. [Vali Nasr](/people/vali-nasr) served as his senior adviser in this role from 2009 to 2011. Nasr later described Holbrooke as a champion of diplomatic engagement over military escalation in Afghanistan, and wrote critically in *The Dispensable Nation* (2013) about what he portrayed as the Obama White House's sidelining of Holbrooke's diplomatic strategy.

Holbrooke died on December 13, 2010, of a torn aorta, while in office. He is a key node in the US-Iran diplomacy network through his influence on a generation of American diplomats and his direct management of Vali Nasr, one of the most prominent pro-engagement Iran analysts in US policy circles.

sources.title: Richard Holbrooke — US Department of State

nodes.label: Robert Malley
nodes.role: US Special Envoy for Iran (2021–2023); President of International Crisis Group (2018–2021)

data.role: US Special Envoy for Iran (2021–2023); President of International Crisis Group (2018–2021)
data.bio: Robert Malley is an American lawyer and diplomat who served as president and CEO of the [International Crisis Group](/orgs/international-crisis-group) from 2018 to 2021, and as the Biden administration's special envoy for Iran from January 2021 until his suspension in June 2023. While at ICG and the State Department, he employed or worked with at least three members of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) — [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai), [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez), and [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary). He recruited Tabatabai to join his Iran team at the State Department in February 2021. In June 2023, his security clearance was suspended and he was placed on unpaid leave amid an FBI investigation into his handling of classified information. The [IEI email leak](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) in September 2023 intensified congressional scrutiny of his Iran negotiating team.

nodes.label: Rostam Ghasemi
nodes.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2011–2013); former Minister of Roads (2021–2023)

data.role: Former Minister of Petroleum (2011–2013); former Minister of Roads (2021–2023)
data.bio: Rostam Ghasemi is an IRGC brigadier general and oil sector executive who served as Minister of Petroleum in [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s second term (2011–2013) and then as Minister of Roads and Urban Development under [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (2021–2023). He was previously managing director of the IRGC engineering arm Khatam al-Anbiya — the massive construction and contracting conglomerate that controls much of Iran's infrastructure market. He was replaced as Roads Minister in 2023. He is subject to US, EU, and UN sanctions.

nodes.label: Rouzbeh Parsi
nodes.role: Head of MENA Programme, Swedish Institute of International Affairs

data.role: Head of MENA Programme, Swedish Institute of International Affairs
data.bio: Rouzbeh Parsi is a Swedish-Iranian academic who headed the Middle East and North Africa programme at the Swedish Institute of International Affairs (UI). He was identified as a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), attending the inaugural meeting at Vienna's Palais Coburg in May 2014 alongside other Western-based Iranian academics recruited by the Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) through [IPIS](/orgs/ipis) to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions in Western policy circles.

nodes.label: Roya Hakakian
nodes.role: Iranian-American-Jewish author and journalist; founding board member of the Iran Human Rights Documentation Center

data.role: Iranian-American-Jewish author and journalist; founding board member of the Iran Human Rights Documentation Center
data.bio:
Roya Hakakian (born 1966) is an Iranian-American Jewish author, poet, and journalist who fled Iran in 1985. She is the author of *Journey from the Land of No* (2004), a memoir of growing up Jewish in post-revolutionary Iran, and *Assassins of the Turquoise Palace* (2011), a detailed account of the [Mykonos restaurant assassination](/events/mykonos-restaurant-assassination-1992) in which [Sadegh Sharafkandi](/people/sadegh-sharafkandi) and three other Kurdish opposition leaders were killed by Iranian agents in Berlin in 1992.

Hakakian is a founding board member of the [Iran Human Rights Documentation Center](/orgs/iran-human-rights-documentation-center), which she helped establish in 2004 to document abuses by the Islamic Republic. She has contributed to major American publications including the New York Times, Time, and NPR, and has testified before the U.S. Congress on Iranian human rights issues. Her work as a documentarian and journalist has placed her at the intersection of the Iranian diaspora's literary, human rights, and political advocacy communities.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Hosseinian
nodes.role: Hardline cleric; former MP; MOIS-linked; publicly justified chain murders

data.role: Hardline cleric; former MP; MOIS-linked; publicly justified chain murders
data.bio: Ruhollah Hosseinian is a hardline cleric who worked within the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) apparatus and later served as a member of parliament. He became notorious for publicly justifying the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) on television, declaring: 'We have been a murderer ourselves! Murder of dissidents is not carried out the way that it has been described!' He attended Saeed Emami's memorial service. Hosseinian's open defence of the killings, at a time when the regime was attempting to portray them as the work of 'rogue elements,' was seen as revealing the true sentiment of the hardline establishment toward the assassination campaign.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Khomeini
nodes.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran

data.role: Founder and first Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic of Iran
data.bio: Ruhollah Khomeini was the Shia cleric and marja who led the 1979 [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), overthrowing the Pahlavi monarchy and establishing the Islamic Republic. He served as Supreme Leader from 1979 until his death in June 1989. He developed the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist), which forms the constitutional basis of clerical rule in Iran.

nodes.label: Ruhollah Zam
nodes.role: Dissident journalist; founder of Amad News

data.role: Dissident journalist; founder of Amad News
data.bio: Ruhollah Zam was an Iranian dissident journalist and the founder of the Amad News Telegram channel, which became one of the most influential opposition media outlets during the December 2017–January 2018 protests in Iran, with over one million followers. The son of reformist cleric [Mohammad-Ali Zam](/people/mohammad-ali-zam), he had fled Iran and was living in exile in France. In October 2019, he was lured to Iraq by [IRGC Intelligence](/orgs/irgc-intelligence-organisation) operatives and captured. He was returned to Iran, where [IRIB](/orgs/irib) broadcast his forced confession. He was charged with corruption on earth, sentenced to death by a revolutionary court, and [executed](/events/execution-of-ruhollah-zam-2020) on 12 December 2020. His execution was widely condemned by France, the European Union, and international human rights organisations.

nodes.label: Sadegh Ghotbzadeh
nodes.role: First head of IRIB (1979–1980); Foreign minister (1979–1980)

data.role: First head of IRIB (1979–1980); Foreign minister (1979–1980)
data.bio: Sadegh Ghotbzadeh was a member of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) and a close associate of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) during the exile years in Paris. He played a key role in the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). He briefly served as the first head of [IRIB](/orgs/irib) in 1979, overseeing the transformation of the Shah-era National Iranian Radio and Television into the revolutionary broadcasting apparatus. He was subsequently appointed foreign minister, serving during the [Iran hostage crisis](/events/iran-hostage-crisis-1979) and the early months of the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). Ghotbzadeh grew disillusioned with the clerical monopoly on power and was arrested in 1982 on charges of plotting a coup against [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) with the alleged involvement of [Kazem Shariatmadari](/people/kazem-shariatmadari). He was executed by firing squad on 15 September 1982.

nodes.label: Sadegh Khalkhali
nodes.role: Chief Judge of the Islamic Revolutionary Courts (1979–1981)

data.role: Chief Judge of the Islamic Revolutionary Courts (1979–1981)
data.bio:
Sadegh Khalkhali was an Iranian Shia cleric who served as the first chief judge of the Islamic Revolutionary Courts after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Appointed by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), he presided over summary trials and executions of former regime officials, political prisoners, and alleged drug traffickers, earning him the epithet "the Hanging Judge."

In the weeks following the revolution, Khalkhali oversaw the rapid trial and execution of senior figures from the Pahlavi era, including former Prime Minister Amir Abbas Hoveyda, [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) chief Nasser Moghadam, and Jewish-Iranian businessman Habibollah Elghanian. Many of these trials lasted only minutes and denied defendants legal representation.
