In August 1979, he was dispatched to Kurdistan to suppress autonomy movements, where he held summary courts and ordered the execution of political prisoners affiliated with the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) and [Komala](/orgs/komala-party-of-iranian-kurdistan). In 1980, he was appointed head of the Anti-Narcotics Revolutionary Courts, where he sentenced hundreds more to death before being removed from this role amid criticism.

Khalkhali served as a member of the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) (1980–1984) representing Tehran, and as a member of the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) (1982–1990). He published his memoirs in 2000, which were briefly banned. He died on 26 November 2003 in Tehran.

nodes.label: Sadegh Kharrazi
nodes.role: Former Iranian Ambassador to France; former Deputy Foreign Minister

data.role: Former Iranian Ambassador to France; former Deputy Foreign Minister
data.bio: Sadegh Kharrazi is an Iranian diplomat who served as Ambassador to France and as Deputy Foreign Minister. He is the nephew of [Kamal Kharrazi](/people/kamal-kharrazi), who served as Foreign Minister under President Khatami, making him part of one of the Islamic Republic's most prominent diplomatic families. He is also the uncle of journalist Hooman Majd. Kharrazi was associated with reformist currents within the Iranian diplomatic establishment and was at times seen as a potential reformist candidate for the presidency. He served as Iran's permanent representative at the UN in New York before his posting in Paris, accumulating extensive experience in multilateral diplomacy.

nodes.label: Sadegh Larijani
nodes.role: Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (2018–present); former Chief Justice (2009–2019); former Guardian Council member (2001–2021)

data.role: Chairman of Expediency Discernment Council (2018–present); former Chief Justice (2009–2019); former Guardian Council member (2001–2021)
data.bio: Sadegh Amoli Larijani is the younger brother of former Majlis Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and one of the most powerful figures in Iranian jurisprudence. He served as Chief Justice for a decade (2009–2019) and simultaneously sat on the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) from 2001. His tenure as Chief Justice was characterised by mass prosecutions of Green Movement figures, executions of political prisoners and juveniles, and widespread corruption allegations. He was removed from the judiciary in 2019 and replaced by [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi). He resigned from the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) in September 2021 in a public protest after the Council disqualified his brother [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) from the presidential race. He was appointed chairman of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) in December 2018, replacing the late Mahmoud Hashemi Shahroudi.

nodes.label: Sadegh Mahsouli
nodes.role: Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; former Interior Minister (2008–2009)

data.role: Secretary-General, Front of Islamic Revolution Stability; former Interior Minister (2008–2009)
data.bio: Sadegh Mahsouli is a former [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) commander and the organisational kingmaker within the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability), described by Iranian media as the 'Godfather of Paydari'. He became Secretary-General in 2020, replacing [Morteza Agha-Tehrani](/people/morteza-agha-tehrani). A political associate of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) from their time as officials in Azerbaijan Province, he served as Interior Minister (2008–2009) and Welfare Minister (2009–2010) under Ahmadinejad. He commands significant IRGC-adjacent organisational networks across provincial Iran.

nodes.label: Sadegh Sharafkandi
nodes.role: Secretary-General of the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan

data.role: Secretary-General of the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan
data.bio: Sadegh Sharafkandi (1938–1992) was a Kurdish physician and political leader who served as Secretary-General of the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) from 1989 until his assassination. He assumed the leadership after the [assassination of Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou](/events/ghassemlou-assassination-1989) in Vienna. On 17 September 1992, Sharafkandi and three companions were shot dead at the Mykonos restaurant in Berlin by agents linked to the Iranian government. The subsequent German trial (1993–1997) produced a landmark verdict directly implicating senior Iranian leaders — including [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), and [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) — in ordering the killings, triggering the recall of all EU ambassadors from Tehran.

nodes.label: Sadegh Zibakalam
nodes.role: Political scientist, former professor at University of Tehran

data.role: Political scientist, former professor at University of Tehran
data.bio:
Sadegh Zibakalam is an Iranian political scientist and commentator known for publicly challenging the state line on sensitive topics including Iran's nuclear programme, relations with Israel, and political freedoms. He earned his PhD in political science from the [University of Bradford](/orgs/university-of-bradford) in 1989.

Zibakalam served as professor at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran) and held faculty positions at [Islamic Azad University](/orgs/islamic-azad-university). He played a role in the [Iranian Cultural Revolution](/events/iranian-cultural-revolution-1980) in the early 1980s, for which he later publicly apologised and expressed regret.

He registered as a parliamentary candidate from Zanjan Province in the [2000 legislative election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2000) but was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). In January 2014, he wrote an open letter criticising President [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) for failing to fulfil campaign promises, including freeing political prisoners [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi), [Zahra Rahnavard](/people/zahra-rahnavard), and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). In February 2014, he made headlines for publicly recognising the State of Israel based on its UN membership.

In March 2018, an Islamic Revolutionary Court sentenced him to one year in prison and a two-year ban from political and social media activity for critical remarks in an interview with Deutsche Welle. He received the Deutsche Welle Freedom of Speech Award in 2018.

His notable students include [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian) and [Fatemeh Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/fatemeh-hashemi-rafsanjani). His bestselling books include *How Did We Become What We Are?* and *An Introduction to Islamic Revolution*. His brother [Saeid Zibakalam](/people/saeid-zibakalam) is also an academic at the University of Tehran, and [Ahmad Tavakkoli](/people/ahmad-tavakkoli) is his co-father-in-law.

nodes.label: Saeed Borji
nodes.role: Iranian nuclear scientist

data.role: Iranian nuclear scientist
data.bio: Saeed Borji was an Iranian nuclear scientist killed in June 2025 in Israeli airstrikes targeting Iran's nuclear programme. He was among the 11 scientists the IDF claimed to have eliminated during the large-scale Israeli military campaign against Iran's nuclear infrastructure.

sources.title: Iranian nuclear scientists killed in Israeli strikes

nodes.label: Saeed Emami
nodes.role: Deputy Minister of Intelligence; ringleader of chain murders; died in custody 1999

data.role: Deputy Minister of Intelligence; ringleader of chain murders; died in custody 1999
data.bio: Saeed Emami (also known as Saeed Eslami) was a senior official in the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) who served as Deputy Minister for Security Affairs under [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian). He masterminded and directed the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) — a decade-long campaign of assassinations targeting Iranian intellectuals, writers, and dissidents from 1988 to 1998. Emami led a unit of operatives who carried out the killings of Dariush and Parvaneh Forouhar, Mohammad Mokhtari, Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh, Ali Akbar Saidi Sirjani, and dozens of other victims. He also orchestrated the 1996 attempt to kill 21 writers by driving their bus off a cliff at Heyran Pass. He remained an advisor to Intelligence Minister [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) at the time of his arrest in early 1999. On 20 June 1999, his death was announced — officially by suicide after drinking depilatory cream on 16 June. The claim was widely disbelieved; Shirin Ebadi stated it was 'impossible' for him to have died that way. No public photographs of his body were ever released, and no grave was registered in his name. He is widely believed to have been murdered to prevent him from revealing who gave the orders for the chain murders.

nodes.label: Saeed Hajjarian
nodes.role: Publisher, Sobh-e Emroz; chief political strategist for Khatami; former deputy Minister of Intelligence

data.role: Publisher, Sobh-e Emroz; chief political strategist for Khatami; former deputy Minister of Intelligence
data.bio: Saeed Hajjarian is the most consequential reformist strategist of the Khatami era and the publisher of the investigative newspaper [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper). He had an extraordinary trajectory: from revolutionary intelligence officer who helped storm the US Embassy in 1979, to deputy Minister of Intelligence (1984–1989), to reformist political architect who theorised 'pressure from below, negotiation from above' as the strategy for incremental democratisation. When [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) was elected president in 1997 Hajjarian became his chief political advisor; he was also elected to Tehran's City Council in 1999. His newspaper [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper) published [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi)'s landmark investigations into the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998), forcing the government to admit Ministry of Intelligence responsibility. On 12 March 2000, he was shot in the face outside the Tehran City Council building by a Basij gunman (Saeed Asgar). The bullet lodged in his neck and left him permanently disabled — largely wheelchair-bound, with severe neurological damage. After the [2009 Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) he was arrested despite his disability and held in deteriorating health in Evin Prison.

nodes.label: Saeed Jalili
nodes.role: Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council (2007–2013); presidential candidate

data.role: Secretary of the Supreme National Security Council (2007–2013); presidential candidate
data.bio: Saeed Jalili is an Iranian hardline politician who served as secretary of the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) from 2007 to 2013, appointed by [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad). He was Iran's chief nuclear negotiator during that period, taking a confrontational stance toward Western powers. He ran unsuccessfully in the 2013 presidential election and was a leading candidate in the [2024 snap presidential election](/events/presidential-election-iran-2024-snap), finishing second to [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) in the runoff. He is closely aligned with the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) and was endorsed by [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah-Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi). He lost part of his right leg during the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war).

nodes.label: Saeed Khatibzadeh
nodes.role: IPIS Director (2025–present); Former MFA Spokesman (2020–2021)

data.role: IPIS Director (2025–present); Former MFA Spokesman (2020–2021)
data.bio: Saeed Khatibzadeh is an Iranian diplomat who served as spokesman for the [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) from 2020 to 2021 and was appointed head of the [Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS)](/orgs/ipis) in January 2025. While stationed in Berlin in 2014, Khatibzadeh conceived and organised the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert Foreign Ministry influence operation that recruited Western-based Iranian academics to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions. In a March 2014 email to IPIS head [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani), he proposed building a network of 'second-generation Iranians' affiliated with Western think tanks and offered them 'political support.' He recruited [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai) and [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary) as the core group following a meeting in Prague. In April 2015, he reported the IEI's media metrics to [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) and the negotiating team. The operation was exposed when leaked emails were published by Iran International and Semafor in September 2023.

nodes.label: Saeed Mohsen
nodes.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)

data.role: Co-founder of Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK)
data.bio: Saeed Mohsen was an engineer and one of the three co-founders of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq), established on 6 September 1965 alongside [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad) and [Ali-Asghar Badizadegan](/people/ali-asghar-badizadegan). A former member of the Freedom Movement, he helped build the MEK's early organizational structure. He was arrested by SAVAK in 1971 and executed on 25 May 1972.

nodes.label: Saeed Mortazavi
nodes.role: Press Court judge; 'Press Murderer'; later Tehran Prosecutor-General; imprisoned 2018 for Kahrizak deaths

data.role: Press Court judge; 'Press Murderer'; later Tehran Prosecutor-General; imprisoned 2018 for Kahrizak deaths
data.bio: Saeed Mortazavi was the Press Court judge who became the primary instrument of the hardline judicial assault on the reformist press, earning the nickname 'Press Murderer' (ghattale matbooat). Beginning in April–May 2000, following Khamenei's 'enemy bases' speech, Mortazavi issued an order closing 16–21 reformist newspapers overnight — including [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), [Mosharekat](/orgs/mosharekat-newspaper), and [Ham-Mihan](/orgs/ham-mihan-newspaper). By February 2004 he had personally overseen the closure or license revocation of approximately 90–120 publications. He was also the judge who closed [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper) in September 1999. Appointed Tehran Prosecutor-General in May 2003 — the first to hold that post since prosecutors were abolished in 1995 — Mortazavi was involved in the torture and death in custody of Canadian-Iranian photographer Zahra Kazemi in 2003. He was a leading figure in the suppression of the [2009 Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), signing arrest warrants for hundreds of reformist figures including [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian). In 2018 he was himself imprisoned for his role in the deaths of three detainees at the Kahrizak detention centre during the 2009 crackdown.

nodes.label: Saeid Golkar
nodes.role: Associate Professor, University of Tennessee at Chattanooga

data.role: Associate Professor, University of Tennessee at Chattanooga
data.bio: Saeid Golkar is an associate professor at the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga. He is a leading scholar of the regime's coercive and mobilisation infrastructure, with particular expertise on the [Basij](/orgs/basij) militia. His book *Captive Society: The Basij Militia and Social Control in Iran* (2015) is the definitive study of how the Basij functions not merely as a paramilitary force but as a comprehensive system of societal penetration — monitoring workplaces, universities, mosques, and neighbourhoods to enforce ideological conformity and suppress dissent. Golkar draws on original field research to document the Basij's role in the Islamic Republic's coercive apparatus and its relationship with the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). He has also written on Iranian civil society, the revolutionary ideology of the regime, and the sustainability of authoritarian control.

sources.title: Saeid Golkar — University of Tennessee at Chattanooga

nodes.label: Saeid Hassanzadeh
nodes.role: Iranian diplomat expelled from Albania (2020); alleged MOIS operative

data.role: Iranian diplomat expelled from Albania (2020); alleged MOIS operative
data.bio: Saeid Hassanzadeh was an Iranian diplomat stationed in Albania who was expelled in January 2020 in the second wave of Albanian expulsions of Iranian intelligence personnel — the first having been the expulsion of Gholamhossein Mohammadnia in 2018. Albanian authorities alleged that Hassanzadeh operated as a Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS) officer tasked with surveillance and targeting operations against members of the Mojahedin-e Khalq ([MEK](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq)) at their Ashraf-3 compound near Manez.

nodes.label: Saeid Zibakalam
nodes.role: Academic, faculty member at University of Tehran

data.role: Academic, faculty member at University of Tehran
data.bio:
Saeid Zibakalam is an Iranian academic and conservative political activist. He holds a PhD in philosophy from the University of Leeds (1990), where his dissertation focused on the social epistemology of the Edinburgh School. He is a faculty member at the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran), specialising in philosophy of science and political philosophy.

Zibakalam is a staunch supporter of [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) and has publicly criticised [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) and the [JCPOA nuclear deal](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). In 2014, he was summoned to court for criticising nuclear negotiators. He attempted to run in the [2020 legislative election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2020) but was disqualified by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council).

He received Iran's Book of the Year Award in 2007 for his work *Social Epistemology*. His brother [Sadegh Zibakalam](/people/sadegh-zibakalam) is a prominent reformist commentator.

nodes.label: Said Amir Arjomand
nodes.role: Distinguished Service Professor of Sociology, Stony Brook University

data.role: Distinguished Service Professor of Sociology, Stony Brook University
data.bio:
Said Amir Arjomand is a distinguished service professor of sociology at Stony Brook University (State University of New York), where he has been a central figure in the comparative sociology of religion, law, and the state, with a particular focus on Shia Islam and Iran.

His most influential works are *The Shadow of God and the Hidden Imam: Religion, Political Order, and Societal Change in Shi'ite Iran from the Beginning to 1890* (1984) and *The Turban for the Crown: The Islamic Revolution in Iran* (1988, Oxford University Press), widely regarded as one of the finest sociological analyses of the 1979 Islamic Revolution. His later *After Khomeini: Iran Under His Successors* (2009, Oxford University Press) traced the evolution of the Islamic Republic's political and constitutional order under Khamenei and Rafsanjani.

Arjomand is a founder of the journal *International Sociology* and has written extensively on constitutional law and legitimacy in Muslim-majority states, comparative democratization, and the sociology of Shia clericalism. His analytical framework is rooted in Weberian sociology of religion and political sociology, treating the Islamic Revolution and IRI institutions as subjects of rigorous comparative-historical sociology rather than ideological commentary.

He is regarded as one of the most rigorous and systematic scholars in the field of Iranian studies and comparative Islamic sociology.

sources.title: Said Amir Arjomand — Stony Brook University
The Turban for the Crown — Oxford University Press

nodes.label: Saleh Asadi
nodes.role: Brigadier General; Deputy Head of Armed Forces Intelligence

data.role: Brigadier General; Deputy Head of Armed Forces Intelligence
data.bio: Saleh Asadi was a Brigadier General serving as Deputy Head of Armed Forces Intelligence under the [General Staff of the Armed Forces](/orgs/general-staff-of-the-armed-forces). He was killed on 28 February 2026 in the combined US-Israel decapitation strikes that also killed Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and multiple senior military commanders. His death was part of a systematic effort to destroy Iran's intelligence and military command infrastructure during the [February 28, 2026 strikes](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-feb-2026).

nodes.label: Sanam Vakil
nodes.role: Director of the Middle East and North Africa Programme at Chatham House; British-Iranian scholar on Iranian politics

data.role: Director of the Middle East and North Africa Programme at Chatham House; British-Iranian scholar on Iranian politics
data.bio:
Sanam Vakil is a British-Iranian academic and policy analyst who serves as Director of the Middle East and North Africa Programme at [Chatham House](/orgs/chatham-house) (the Royal Institute of International Affairs) in London. She is one of the most prominent Iran scholars at a major European think tank. She is the author of *Women and Politics in the Islamic Republic of Iran: Action and Reaction* (2011), a study of Iranian women's political participation and their negotiation of space within the Islamic Republic's structures.

Vakil regularly advises governments and international organisations on Iranian politics, US-Iran negotiations, and regional security. She has testified before the European Parliament and the UK Parliament on Iran-related policy questions. Her analysis tends toward understanding Iranian political actors within their structural context rather than advocacy positions, and she has commented extensively on nuclear negotiations, internal Iranian factional dynamics, and the impact of sanctions on Iranian society.

nodes.label: Seyed Ali Marandi
nodes.role: Physician; Personal Doctor to Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei

data.role: Physician; Personal Doctor to Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei
data.bio: Dr. Seyed Ali Marandi is a prominent Iranian physician who has served as the personal doctor of Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). A specialist in pediatrics and nutrition, he served as Minister of Health of Iran from 1989 to 1993 under President [Ali Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ali-Akbar_Hashemi_Rafsanjani) and has remained a trusted figure within the conservative establishment. His long-standing personal access to Khamenei as his physician has given the Marandi family exceptional proximity to the regime's inner circle. He is the father of [Mohammad Marandi](/people/mohammad-marandi), the University of Tehran professor, literary critic, and prominent English-language defender of the Islamic Republic, whose regime access is substantially explained by this family connection.

nodes.label: Shahram Chubin
nodes.role: Veteran strategic studies scholar; former director of research, Geneva Centre for Security Policy

data.role: Veteran strategic studies scholar; former director of research, Geneva Centre for Security Policy
data.bio:
Shahram Chubin is a veteran strategic studies scholar and one of the foremost English-language analysts of Iranian security and nuclear policy. He served for many years as director of research at the Geneva Centre for Security Policy (GCSP), a Swiss research institute focused on arms control and international security. He was also a long-serving researcher at the International Institute for Strategic Studies (IISS) in London.

Chubin began writing on Iranian security and foreign policy in the 1970s, making him among the earliest Western academics to systematically analyze the Islamic Republic's strategic posture. His major works include *Iran's Nuclear Ambitions* (2006, Carnegie Endowment) and *Whither Iran? Reform, Domestic Politics and National Security* (2002, IISS), as well as numerous reports for Carnegie and IISS on Iran's nuclear program, deterrence calculations, and the interaction between domestic politics and foreign policy.

His analytical framework is realist and security-focused: he treats Iranian nuclear decision-making as driven primarily by security calculations, regime legitimacy imperatives, and deterrence logic rather than purely ideological motivations. He has consistently argued that understanding Iran's strategic incentives is a prerequisite for effective diplomacy. His career represents the foundation of the English-language academic literature on Iran's nuclear and security policy.

nodes.label: Shahram Irani
nodes.role: Commander of the Islamic Republic of Iran Navy (2021–present)

data.role: Commander of the Islamic Republic of Iran Navy (2021–present)
data.bio: Rear Admiral Shahram Irani has served as Commander of the [Islamic Republic of Iran Navy](/orgs/islamic-republic-of-iran-navy) since August 2021, appointed by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) to replace Hossein Khanzadi. A career Artesh (regular armed forces) officer with a background in naval operations and electronic warfare, he is widely reported in Iranian state media as the first Sunni Kurd to command a branch of Iran's military — a point the regime has emphasised for domestic political signalling. Under his command, NEDAJA completed its first world circumnavigation voyage with the 86th Flotilla in 2023 and expanded joint naval exercises with the Russian and Chinese navies in the Indian Ocean. He has overseen the induction of the Deilaman and Shahid Soleimani-class destroyers and the continued presence of Iranian naval convoys escorting commercial traffic in the Gulf of Aden.

nodes.label: Shapour Bakhtiar
nodes.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran under the Shah (1979); opposition leader in exile

data.role: Last Prime Minister of Iran under the Shah (1979); opposition leader in exile
data.bio: Appointed as Iran's last Prime Minister by [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) in January 1979 in a last-ditch effort to quell the revolution. A member of the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran), his acceptance of the position led to his expulsion from the party. He fled to France after the [revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) and was assassinated by Iranian government agents in his home near Paris on 6 August 1991.

nodes.label: Shirin Ebadi
nodes.role: Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2003); human rights lawyer; represented Forouhar family

data.role: Nobel Peace Prize laureate (2003); human rights lawyer; represented Forouhar family
data.bio: Shirin Ebadi is an Iranian lawyer and human rights activist who was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 2003 for her efforts to promote democracy and the rights of women and children. She was Iran's first female judge, serving on the Tehran city court from 1975 until she was forced to resign after the 1979 revolution. As a lawyer, she represented the family of [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar) and Parvaneh Forouhar in the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) case. During the investigation she discovered that she herself had been on the murder list — her assassination had been postponed until after Ramadan, and the unit was arrested in the interim. She stated unequivocally that Saeed Emami's claimed suicide in prison was impossible. She also represented the family of Canadian-Iranian photographer Zahra Kazemi, who was killed in Evin Prison in 2003. Ebadi left Iran in 2009 and lives in exile in the UK.

nodes.label: Shohreh Aghdashloo
nodes.role: Actress and political activist; vocal supporter of Reza Pahlavi and Iranian opposition

data.role: Actress and political activist; vocal supporter of Reza Pahlavi and Iranian opposition
data.bio:
Shohreh Aghdashloo is an Iranian-American actress and political activist. She received an Academy Award nomination for her role in *House of Sand and Fog* (2003) and has had a long career in both Iranian and Hollywood cinema.

She is a vocal supporter of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) and the Iranian opposition movement, using her public platform to advocate for secular democracy in Iran. During the 2022–2023 Woman, Life, Freedom protests, she was among prominent Iranian-Americans who publicly backed the protest movement.

nodes.label: Siamak Namazi
nodes.role: Former managing director, Atieh Bahar Consulting; former political prisoner

data.role: Former managing director, Atieh Bahar Consulting; former political prisoner
data.bio:
Siamak Namazi is an Iranian-American businessman who managed Atieh Bahar Consulting, a Tehran-based firm founded by his mother Pari Namazi and Bijan Khajehpour that provided business advisory services and access to Iranian regime contacts for foreign enterprises. In 1999, Namazi and [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi) co-authored a paper at a Cyprus conference proposing the creation of an Iranian-American lobby to influence U.S.-Iran policy — the conceptual blueprint for what became [NIAC](/orgs/niac). While Parsi led NIAC, he simultaneously worked as an analyst for Atieh Bahar writing newsletters, a dual role not publicly disclosed.

In October 2015, Namazi was arrested by the IRGC while visiting Iran and sentenced to 10 years in Evin Prison on espionage charges. His father, Baquer Namazi, was also arrested in February 2016. Both were held as de facto hostages and released in September 2023 as part of a U.S.-Iran prisoner exchange deal. Their imprisonment was widely seen as a hostage-taking by IRGC hardliners targeting individuals with Western connections, including those in the pragmatist/reformist network.

nodes.label: Siamak Rahpeyk
nodes.role: Vice Secretary of the Guardian Council; jurist member

data.role: Vice Secretary of the Guardian Council; jurist member
data.bio: Siamak Rahpeyk is a jurist member of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and its Vice Secretary, responsible for overseeing the Council's executive and electoral affairs. Appointed to the position by Secretary [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), he has been the operational manager of Iran's candidate-vetting process. He publicly justified the mass disqualification of reformist candidates in the lead-up to the [2020 parliamentary election](/events/parliamentary-election-iran-2020). He was sanctioned by the US Treasury in February 2020 alongside [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati), [Abbas-Ali Kadkhodaei](/people/abbas-ali-kadkhodaei), and others for denying Iranians free and fair elections.

nodes.label: Sina Toossi
nodes.role: Senior nonresident fellow at the Center for International Policy; former NIAC research analyst; commentator on US-Iran diplomacy

data.role: Senior nonresident fellow at the Center for International Policy; former NIAC research analyst; commentator on US-Iran diplomacy
data.bio:
Sina Toossi is an Iranian-American policy analyst who focuses on U.S.-Iran relations, sanctions, and diplomacy. He serves as a senior nonresident fellow at the [Center for International Policy](/orgs/center-for-international-policy) and previously worked as a research analyst at the [National Iranian American Council](/orgs/niac). He is a consistent advocate for diplomatic engagement between Washington and Tehran, opposing sanctions-heavy approaches and military escalation.

Toossi writes and comments regularly on Iran nuclear negotiations, JCPOA diplomacy, and the impact of U.S. sanctions policy on Iranian civil society and the diplomatic track. He has contributed to outlets including Foreign Policy, The Nation, and Al-Monitor, and has appeared as an analyst on major broadcast and online media.

sources.title: Sina Toossi — Center for International Policy

nodes.label: Soleiman Mohsen Eskandari
nodes.role: First President of the Tudeh Party of Iran (1941–1944)

data.role: First President of the Tudeh Party of Iran (1941–1944)
data.bio: Soleiman Mohsen Eskandari (1863–1944) was an Iranian statesman and Qajar prince who served as the first president of the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) from its founding in 1941 until his death in 1944. A prominent progressive politician during the Constitutional Era, he had been a member of Iran's Second Majles (parliament) and a founding figure of the Democratic Party of Iran in 1909. Despite his aristocratic Qajar origins, he became a committed supporter of socialist and democratic causes. His nephew was [Iraj Eskandari](/people/iraj-eskandari), who would go on to serve as First Secretary of the Tudeh Party decades later. Soleiman Mohsen Eskandari's prestige and connections lent the newly founded Tudeh Party significant legitimacy in its early years.

nodes.label: Taghi Arani
nodes.role: Marxist intellectual, physicist, and founder of the Group of Fifty-Three

data.role: Marxist intellectual, physicist, and founder of the Group of Fifty-Three
data.bio: Taghi Arani (1903–1940) was an Iranian physicist, philosopher, and Marxist intellectual regarded as the founding father of organised Iranian communism. Born in Tabriz, he studied in Berlin and completed a doctorate in physics and chemistry, becoming one of Iran's earliest scientifically trained Marxist theorists. In the early 1930s he returned to Iran, where he taught at the University of Tehran and began publishing the journal *Donya* (World), a Marxist theoretical periodical that circulated clandestinely. He organised a network of leftist intellectuals and activists known as the [Group of Fifty-Three](/orgs/group-of-fifty-three), named for the number arrested in 1937 during Reza Shah's crackdown on communism. Arani was tortured in Qasr Prison and died on 4 February 1940 from a combination of torture, typhus, and deliberate medical neglect. Many members of the Group of Fifty-Three who survived their sentences went on to found the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) in 1941, making Arani the intellectual godfather of the Tudeh. His writings on historical materialism, Iranian history, and Marxist philosophy influenced generations of Iranian leftists.

nodes.label: Taghi Shahram
nodes.role: Leader of the Marxist faction of MEK that became the Peykar Organization

data.role: Leader of the Marxist faction of MEK that became the Peykar Organization
data.bio: Taghi Shahram was the leader of the Marxist faction within the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) that carried out a dramatic ideological coup in 1975, declaring that Marxism-Leninism had superseded the MEK's original Islamic-leftist ideology. Shahram's faction orchestrated a brutal internal purge of MEK members who refused to accept the ideological shift, including the murder of several who resisted. The most notorious killing attributed to his faction was that of Majid Sharif-Vaghefi, an MEK member executed for his opposition to the Marxist turn. The Marxist faction formally constituted itself as the [Peykar Organization](/orgs/peykar-organization) in 1978. After the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Shahram was captured by the Islamic Republic. He was executed by the Islamic Republic in 1982.

nodes.label: Trita Parsi
nodes.role: Co-founder & Executive Vice President, Quincy Institute; founder, NIAC

data.role: Co-founder & Executive Vice President, Quincy Institute; founder, NIAC
data.bio:
Trita Parsi is an Iranian-Swedish political scientist who founded the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac) in 2002 and served as its president until approximately 2018. Born in Iran and raised in Sweden, he holds a Ph.D. from Johns Hopkins SAIS. In 1997, he founded Iranians for International Cooperation (IIC) in Sweden, which lobbied U.S. Congress members for sanctions removal. In 1999, he and [Siamak Namazi](/people/siamak-namazi) co-authored a paper at a Cyprus conference proposing the creation of an Iranian-American lobby to influence U.S.-Iran policy — the conceptual foundation for NIAC.

During the 2008 Hassan Dai defamation lawsuit, discovery revealed extensive email exchanges between Parsi and [Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) (then Iran's UN Ambassador) from 2002 to 2007, including arranging meetings between Zarif and members of Congress. In April 2006, Zarif provided Parsi with a copy of the alleged 2003 Iranian 'grand bargain' offer, which Parsi publicized. In August 2013, Zarif told the Iranian parliament that Parsi and NIAC had been his 'main partners' in exploiting American political divisions on Iran policy. While leading NIAC, Parsi simultaneously worked as an analyst for Atieh Bahar Consulting — a Tehran-based firm run by the Namazi family — writing newsletters, a dual role he did not publicly disclose.

In 2019, Parsi co-founded the [Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft](/orgs/quincy-institute), where he serves as Executive Vice President. The Hoover Institution's December 2023 report by Abbas Milani placed Parsi and NIAC at the center of a 'diaspora pillar' of Tehran's influence network in the West.

nodes.label: Vahid Haghanian
nodes.role: Executive Deputy of the Office of the Supreme Leader; US-sanctioned member of Khamenei's inner circle

data.role: Executive Deputy of the Office of the Supreme Leader; US-sanctioned member of Khamenei's inner circle
data.bio:
Vahid Haghanian is a senior Iranian security and political operative who served as executive deputy of the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) for over three decades. The US Treasury described him as 'the Supreme Leader's right hand.'

Haghanian's relationship with [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) dates to 1979, when he served as Khamenei's personal driver. He joined the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) in 1984, commanded Tehran security patrols under the Sarallah Corps, and was wounded in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) in 1987. He subsequently served as a commander in the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) for approximately four years, focusing on Eastern Bloc operations.

As Khamenei's chief political fixer, Haghanian relayed the Supreme Leader's directives across military, economic, and political spheres. During the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), he served as the formal communication channel between [Mir Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi)'s campaign and Khamenei's office. He was reportedly involved in the detention of [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) in 2011. In the 2021 presidential election, he openly orchestrated candidate withdrawals to favour [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi).

Under Raisi's government, Haghanian held a dual role — simultaneously serving in the president's administration at a rank higher than minister while maintaining his position in Khamenei's office. In June 2024, he registered as a presidential candidate following Raisi's death but was disqualified by the Guardian Council.

The United States sanctioned Haghanian on 4 November 2019 alongside eight other members of Khamenei's inner circle, including [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei).

nodes.label: Vali Nasr
nodes.role: Professor of international affairs and Middle East studies, Johns Hopkins SAIS; former dean of SAIS (2012–2019)

data.role: Professor of international affairs and Middle East studies, Johns Hopkins SAIS; former dean of SAIS (2012–2019)
data.bio:
Vali Nasr (born 1960 in Tehran) is a professor of international affairs and Middle East studies at the Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies (SAIS), where he served as dean from 2012 to 2019. He is one of the foremost scholars of Shia Islam and its political dimensions in the Muslim world.

Nasr left Iran after the [1979 Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) and was educated in the United States, receiving his doctorate from MIT. His breakthrough scholarly work *The Shia Revival: How Conflicts within Islam Will Shape the Future* (2006) argued that the US invasion of Iraq had inadvertently empowered Shia Muslim communities across the Middle East, reshaping regional geopolitics in ways that benefited Iran. The book became required reading in Washington policy circles and helped mainstream the concept of a 'Shia crescent.'

From 2009 to 2011, Nasr served as senior adviser to Richard Holbrooke, the US Special Representative for Afghanistan and Pakistan. In that role he was deeply involved in US strategy toward Af-Pak policy during the Obama administration's first term, giving him unusual insider experience of US diplomatic policymaking. He described his disillusionment with the Obama administration's policymaking process in *The Dispensable Nation: American Foreign Policy in Retreat* (2013).

Nasr is a consistent advocate for engagement with Iran over sanctions and military pressure. His positions on nuclear diplomacy, the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015), and sanctions relief have consistently aligned with Iranian government preferences on these issues. He co-authored *How Sanctions Work: Iran and the Impact of Economic Warfare* (2023, Stanford University Press) with [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez) — who was subsequently identified by [Semafor](/orgs/semafor) and [Iran International](/orgs/iran-international) as a participant in the [Iran Experts Initiative](/orgs/iran-experts-initiative) (IEI), a covert Iranian Foreign Ministry influence operation targeting Western-based analysts. Nasr himself has not been named in the IEI leaked cables; the co-authorship with a documented IEI participant on a topic where both authors' conclusions align with Iranian government preferences is a relevant editorial fact. His work has been translated and amplified by irdiplomacy.ir, a website affiliated with the Iranian Foreign Ministry.

Nasr is a pro-engagement, sanctions-skeptic scholar whose policy positions do not constitute opposition to the Islamic Republic. He should be understood in that context when his analysis is cited in Western media.

sources.title: Vali Nasr — Council on Foreign Relations

nodes.label: Yadollah Sahabi
nodes.role: Co-founder of Freedom Movement of Iran

data.role: Co-founder of Freedom Movement of Iran
data.bio: Co-founder of the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) alongside [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan) and [Mahmoud Taleghani](/people/mahmoud-taleghani) in 1961. Served in the post-revolution provisional government and the [Islamic Revolutionary Council](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-council). Father of [Ezzatollah Sahabi](/people/ezzatollah-sahabi).

nodes.label: Yahya Hosseini Panjaki
nodes.role: Deputy Minister of Intelligence for Israel Affairs

data.role: Deputy Minister of Intelligence for Israel Affairs
data.bio: Yahya Hosseini Panjaki served as Deputy Minister of Intelligence responsible for Israel Affairs within Iran's [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) (MOIS). His portfolio overseeing intelligence operations targeting Israel made him a prime target in the combined US-Israel campaign. He was killed on 28 February 2026 as part of the [February 28, 2026 strikes](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-feb-2026).

nodes.label: Yasmine Etemad-Amini
nodes.role: Wife of Reza Pahlavi; Iranian exile community figure

data.role: Wife of Reza Pahlavi; Iranian exile community figure
data.bio:
Yasmine Etemad-Amini is the wife of [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi), whom she married in 1986. They have three daughters, including [Noor Pahlavi](/people/noor-pahlavi), whom Reza declared heir to the Pahlavi succession in 2019.

She accompanied Reza Pahlavi on his visit to Israel in April 2023, where they met with President Isaac Herzog and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu. She resides with her family in Potomac, Maryland.

nodes.label: Yazdan Mir
nodes.role: Commander, IRGC Quds Force Unit 840 (d. 2026)

data.role: Commander, IRGC Quds Force Unit 840 (d. 2026)
data.bio: Yazdan Mir commanded [IRGC Quds Force Unit 840](/orgs/irgc-quds-force-unit-840), the Quds Force's external covert operations unit specialising in kidnapping, assassination planning, and irregular operations against foreign nationals. Mir and Unit 840 were sanctioned by the US Treasury Department in September 2022 for documented plots against American officials and Israeli nationals abroad, including plots targeting Israeli tourists and Iranian dissidents in foreign countries. He was killed in an Israeli airstrike on 6 April 2026 in Tehran during the 2026 Iran war, killed the same day as IRGC Intelligence chief Majid Khademi.

sources.title: Treasury Sanctions IRGC Officials Responsible for Plots Against Americans and Israelis
U.S. sanctions Iranian unit tied to plots against Americans, Israelis

nodes.label: Zahra Rahnavard
nodes.role: Political activist, academic, first female university chancellor post-revolution

data.role: Political activist, academic, first female university chancellor post-revolution
data.bio:
Zahra Rahnavard is an Iranian political activist, academic, and sculptor. She holds a PhD in political science from [Islamic Azad University](/orgs/islamic-azad-university) and degrees in art and architecture from the [University of Tehran](/orgs/university-of-tehran).

She served as chancellor of [Alzahra University](/orgs/alzahra-university) from 1998 to 2006, making her the first woman appointed as a university chancellor in Iran since the 1979 revolution. She also served as political adviser to President [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) and headed the Women's Social and Cultural Council.

Rahnavard is the wife of former Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and was active in his 2009 presidential campaign. Following the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), she was placed under house arrest in February 2011, which reportedly lasted until May 2018.

Foreign Policy magazine named her one of the world's most distinguished thinkers in 2009. She is the author of 15 books and a proponent of Islamic feminism.

data.description: The Ahmadzadeh-Pouyan Group was a clandestine Marxist-Leninist guerrilla cell co-founded by [Massoud Ahmadzadeh](/people/massoud-ahmadzadeh) and [Amir Parviz Pouyan](/people/amir-parviz-pouyan) in the late 1960s. The group developed a distinct theoretical framework for armed struggle under conditions of dictatorship, articulated most clearly in Pouyan's pamphlet 'The Necessity of Armed Struggle and the Refutation of the Theory of Survival' (1970). In 1971, the Ahmadzadeh-Pouyan Group merged with the urban guerrilla network organised by [Bijan Jazani](/people/bijan-jazani) to form the [Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas) (OIPFG), the main Marxist guerrilla organization of the 1970s. The founding act of the merged organization was the [Siahkal incident](/events/siahkal-incident-1971) in February 1971.

Al-Alam is [IRIB](/orgs/irib)'s Arabic-language 24-hour news channel, launched in 2003 to project Iranian soft power and the regime's perspective to Arab audiences across the Middle East. The channel promotes pro-resistance-axis narratives and covers events in Iraq, Syria, Lebanon, Yemen, and Palestine from an Iranian-aligned viewpoint.

The Alavi School is a Tehran institution built and funded by the bazaari merchant class. On 1 February 1979, when [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) returned from exile, the Alavi School complex (together with the adjacent Refah School) became his Tehran headquarters and the operational nerve centre of the revolution. Khomeini resided at the Alavi School while the nearby Refah School was used for press operations and the Islamic Revolutionary Court. It was on the rooftop of this complex that the first four revolutionary executions took place on the night of 15–16 February 1979, when [Nematollah Nassiri](/people/nematollah-nassiri), [Mehdi Rahimi](/people/mehdi-rahimi), [Manouchehr Khosrodad](/people/manouchehr-khosrodad), and [Reza Naji](/people/reza-naji) were shot by firing squad following summary trials presided over by [Sadegh Khalkhali](/people/sadegh-khalkhali).

nodes.label: Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran

data.name_en: Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran
data.description: The Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran (Abadgaran) was a conservative-principlist political coalition formed in 2003. Described as 'Iran's neocons', it was composed mainly of [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and [Basij](/orgs/basij) veterans who had been marginalised under [Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and [Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). It swept the 2003 municipal and 2004 parliamentary elections — helped enormously by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s mass disqualification of reformist candidates — and backed [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad)'s successful 2005 presidential run. [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel), the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker from 2004–2008, was a key figure.

Alzahra University is a public women's university in Tehran, Iran. Founded in 1964, it is one of the few single-sex universities in the country. [Zahra Rahnavard](/people/zahra-rahnavard) served as its chancellor from 1998 to 2006, becoming the first woman to head a university in post-revolution Iran.

The Amad Project (Project 111) was Iran's covert nuclear weapons research and development programme, led by [Mohsen Fakhrizadeh](/people/mohsen-fakhrizadeh) under the [Ministry of Defense](/orgs/ministry-of-defense). The programme pursued nuclear warhead miniaturisation, implosion-type weapon design, high-explosives testing, and integration of a nuclear payload into a ballistic missile delivery system. The IAEA documented the project extensively through analysis of a 'possible military dimensions' dossier. Iran officially claimed to have shut down the programme in 2003 following the US invasion of Iraq, though Western intelligence agencies assessed that key work continued under the successor organisation [SPND](/orgs/spnd) (Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research), which absorbed Amad's personnel and institutional knowledge.

sources.title: IAEA Report: Possible Military Dimensions in Iran — November 2011
Amad Project — Wisconsin Project on Nuclear Arms Control
sources.publisher: Wisconsin Project on Nuclear Arms Control

data.description: The Amal Movement (Afwaj al-Muqawama al-Lubnaniyya — Lebanese Resistance Regiments) is a Lebanese Shia political party and militia founded in 1975 by [Musa al-Sadr](/people/musa-al-sadr) and [Mostafa Chamran](/people/mostafa-chamran) as the military wing of the Movement of the Deprived. Chamran served as the movement's military commander, establishing training camps in southern Lebanon with support from Palestinian Fatah. In 1982, a faction led by Husayn al-Musawi split from Amal to form Islamic Amal, which later merged into [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah). The movement remains a major political force in Lebanon, distinct from and at times a rival to Hezbollah.

Washington, D.C.-based conservative-leaning think tank covering economic, domestic, and foreign policy. On Iran, AEI occupies the hawkish end of the US policy debate alongside the Foundation for Defense of Democracies and the Washington Institute for Near East Policy, generally advocating maximum-pressure strategies and skepticism of diplomatic engagement.

Rutgers University-affiliated policy NGO founded by Hooshang Amirahmadi that advocates for US-Iran engagement and normalization. The AIC has historically positioned itself as a bridge between American and Iranian political and business communities, organizing dialogues and producing policy analysis oriented toward diplomacy rather than confrontation.

The Anglo-Iranian Oil Company (AIOC) was a British oil company with a monopoly over Iran's petroleum resources. Formed in 1909 as the Anglo-Persian Oil Company (renamed in 1935), the AIOC was the central grievance of Iran's oil nationalisation movement. Prime Minister Mohammad Mossadegh and the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran) led the drive to nationalise the company in 1951, ending British control. The AIOC appealed to the British government, triggering a British oil embargo against Iran and ultimately co-sponsoring with the CIA the [1953 Iranian coup d'état](/events/iranian-coup-1953) that overthrew Mossadegh. After the coup the AIOC was restructured into the British Petroleum Company (BP) and its Iranian assets were returned under a new consortium agreement. [Hossein Makki](/people/hossein-makki) was one of the leading nationalist figures who championed nationalisation in the Majlis.

Ansar-e Hezbollah (Helpers of the Party of God) is an Iranian vigilante paramilitary group with links to hardline factions within the state. Founded in the mid-1990s, the group is known for violently disrupting reformist gatherings, student protests, and intellectual lectures. It was behind a series of physical assaults on religious intellectual [Abdolkarim Soroush](/people/abdolkarim-soroush) and his audiences in the mid-1990s, and was instrumental in attacking student dormitories during the [July 1999 student uprising](/events/iran-student-uprising-july-1999). The group operates with impunity, widely understood to enjoy protection from elements within the security establishment and the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader).

nodes.label: Arab Gulf States Institute in Washington

data.name_en: Arab Gulf States Institute in Washington
data.description: Washington-based research institution focused on the Arab Gulf states and their relationship with the United States. Hosts fellows studying Gulf politics, security, and economy, including senior fellow [Ali Alfoneh](/people/ali-alfoneh) whose work on the IRGC has been influential in policy circles.

sources.title: About AGSIW
sources.publisher: Arab Gulf States Institute in Washington

data.description: The Artesh (Persian: ارتش, 'Army') is Iran's conventional armed forces, formally the Islamic Republic of Iran Army (IRIA). It comprises four branches: Ground Forces (Niru-ye Zamini), Air Force (Niru-ye Havapeyma), Navy (NEDAJA), and Air Defense Force. The Artesh exists in a parallel structure alongside the IRGC, which has historically received preferential budget allocations and political trust from the regime. The Artesh is responsible for conventional territorial defense, while the IRGC handles internal security, missile forces, and extraterritorial operations. Key Artesh Navy (NEDAJA) commanders include [Habibollah Sayyari](/people/habibollah-sayyari) (2007-2017), [Hossein Khanzadi](/people/hossein-khanzadi) (2017-2021), and [Shahram Irani](/people/shahram-irani) (2021-present).

sources.title: Islamic Republic of Iran Army — Wikipedia

data.description: Asr-e Azadegan (عصر آزادگان, 'Age of the Free People') was the fourth and final iteration of [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin)'s serial newspaper chain, assembling staff from all three predecessors. [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) wrote landmark investigative columns on the chain murders of 1998 in its pages. Closed in the April 2000 crackdown. It was succeeded by the weekly *Gunagoun* under editor Fatemeh Farahmandpour — which the Press Court explicitly closed for 'closely resembling Jame'eh, Tous, Neshat, and Asr-e Azadegan.'

nodes.label: Assembly of Experts for the Constitution

data.name_en: Assembly of Experts for the Constitution
data.description: The Assembly of Experts for the Constitution (Majles-e Khobregan-e Qanun-e Asasi) was a 73-member body convened in 1979 to draft the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Iran. Elected on 3 August 1979, the assembly was dominated by clerics aligned with the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party), who won approximately 55 of 73 seats. Chaired by [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri) with [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti) as deputy speaker, the assembly met from 18 August to 15 November 1979 at the former Senate building in Tehran. Its most consequential act was embedding the doctrine of velayat-e faqih (Guardianship of the Islamic Jurist) into the constitution, granting sweeping powers to a Supreme Leader — a provision that went far beyond the original draft prepared by the provisional government. Opposition members, including figures from the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) and secular nationalists, were outnumbered and unable to block the clerical majority. The resulting constitution was approved in a national referendum on 2–3 December 1979 with 99.5% approval on 75% turnout, though the referendum was boycotted by leftists, Kurdish groups, and followers of Ayatollah Shariatmadari. This body is distinct from the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) (Majles-e Khobregan-e Rahbari), the permanent body established in 1982 to select and supervise the Supreme Leader.

The Assembly of Experts (Majles-e Khobregan-e Rahbari) is an 88-member body of Islamic scholars directly elected to eight-year terms by popular vote, with all candidates vetted by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). It holds the sole constitutional authority to appoint, supervise, and if necessary dismiss the Supreme Leader. Sessions are held at least twice per year and all records are confidential. It has convened twice to appoint a Supreme Leader: in June 1989 choosing [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) after [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s death, and on 8 March 2026 choosing [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei). [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati) served as its chairman from 2016 to 2024.

The Association of Combatant Clerics (Majma'-e Rowhaniyoun-e Mobarez, MRM) broke away from the conservative [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) in 1988. It is the reformist counterpart in clerical politics, associated with the Islamic left and later the reform movement. Key figures include [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) and [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) (the president). It supports political liberalisation within an Islamic framework.

nodes.label: Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims

data.name_en: Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims
data.description: Advocacy organisation established by families of the 176 victims of Ukraine International Airlines Flight 752, shot down by the IRGC Aerospace Force on 8 January 2020. The association campaigns for accountability, transparency, and justice through legal action and international advocacy. It has pursued cases in Canadian and international courts and has called for an independent international investigation into the shootdown.

Washington, D.C.-based non-partisan think tank focused on transatlantic security, international relations, and global policy. Its Middle East Programs host Iran-focused analysts including former senior fellow [Holly Dagres](/people/holly-dagres). The Council convenes policymakers, scholars, and practitioners across the political spectrum and maintains programs on energy, economics, and geopolitics relevant to Iran.

The Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran (AEOI, Sazman-e Enerji-ye Atomi-ye Iran) is the state body overseeing Iran's civil nuclear programme, including uranium enrichment, fuel fabrication, and the Bushehr nuclear power plant. Founded under the Shah in 1974, it continued after the revolution and became the primary institutional interface with the IAEA for inspections and safeguards. Its head is a Vice President of Iran. Key heads include Gholam Reza Aghazadeh (1997–2011), [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) (2009–2013, 2015–2021), [Fereydoun Abbasi-Davani](/people/fereydoun-abbasi-davani) (2011–2013), and Mohammad Eslami (2021–present). The AEOI administers the enrichment facilities at Natanz and Fordow, the heavy water reactor at Arak, and the uranium conversion facility at Isfahan. It was the formal counterpart in [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015) negotiations and continues enriching uranium to 60% purity under the collapsed JCPOA framework. The Amad Project — the clandestine nuclear weapons research programme led by [Mohsen Fakhrizadeh](/people/mohsen-fakhrizadeh) — operated in parallel to and largely hidden from the AEOI's formal structures.

Ayandeh Bank was a private Iranian bank formed in 2012 through the merger of Tat Bank, Salehin Credit Institution, and ATI Credit Institution, orchestrated by founder [Ali Ansari](/people/ali-ansari-banker). Operations began in March 2015 and the bank grew to 270 branches across Iran.

The bank's lending was heavily concentrated in [Iran Mall](/orgs/iran-mall), with approximately 70 percent of its loans directed to entities linked to the mall's development. By 2024, the bank had accumulated $5.1 billion in losses, $2.9 billion in overdrafts from the [Central Bank of Iran](/orgs/central-bank-of-iran), and a capital adequacy ratio of negative 600 percent. Iranian authorities accused the bank of operating as a de facto Ponzi scheme.

On 23 October 2025, the Central Bank of Iran revoked Ayandeh Bank's operating licence and merged its assets and deposits into state-owned [Bank Melli Iran](/orgs/bank-melli-iran). The collapse contributed to broader public protests over Iran's banking sector. The [United Kingdom](/events/uk-sanctions-ali-ansari-2025) sanctioned founder Ali Ansari one week later for alleged [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) financing.

Bank Melli Iran is Iran's first national bank and one of the largest state-owned commercial banks in the country. Founded in 1927, it operates hundreds of branches domestically and maintains an international presence. In October 2025, it absorbed the assets and deposits of the dissolved [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank) on the order of the [Central Bank of Iran](/orgs/central-bank-of-iran).

The Basij Resistance Force (Niroo-ye Moqavemat-e Basij) is a paramilitary volunteer militia subordinate to the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). Founded by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in 1979, it played a prominent role in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) through human-wave assaults. It functions as an internal security and social control force, operating through mosques, universities, and government offices. It was centrally involved in the violent suppression of the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) in 2009 and the Mahsa Amini protests in 2022.

BBC Persian is the Persian-language service of the BBC World Service, broadcasting radio since 1940 and television since 2009. Funded by the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office, it is one of the most trusted and widely consumed Persian-language media outlets, particularly during periods of political crisis. The Islamic Republic has consistently attempted to disrupt BBC Persian's broadcasts, jammed its satellite signals, and harassed and arrested family members of its staff inside Iran. During the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) and [2022 Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022), BBC Persian was a critical information conduit for Iranians. In 2023 the BBC announced cuts to Persian TV broadcasting, shifting to digital-first content.

nodes.label: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs

data.name_en: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs
data.description: Harvard Kennedy School's flagship research center on international security, nuclear policy, and science diplomacy. Has hosted Iranian diaspora scholars and, at various points, regime-adjacent figures including [Kayhan Barzegar](/people/kayhan-barzegar) on visiting fellowships, making it a node in Iranian attempts to conduct Track II and academic diplomacy with Western institutions.

sources.title: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs

data.description: Bonyan (بنیان, 'Foundation') absorbed journalists displaced by the April 2000 closures. Journalist Ahmad Zeidabadi received a 23-month sentence for an interview in Bonyan — charged as 'insulting the Supreme Leader' for condemning Palestinian suicide bombings. Closed 2002.

Jesuit research university in Chestnut Hill, Massachusetts. Home to [Ali Banuazizi](/people/ali-banuazizi), professor emeritus of political science and psychology, who spent his career there and helped institutionalize Iranian studies in North America through his role in founding the International Society for Iranian Studies.

London-based independent think tank focused on Iran's economy, business environment, and the impact of international sanctions. Founded by [Esfandyar Batmanghelidj](/people/esfandyar-batmanghelidj). Publishes research and hosts events bridging Western policymakers with Iranian civil society and business voices.

The British Institute of Persian Studies (BIPS) promotes scholarship and research on all aspects of Iran and the wider Persianate world — including Afghanistan, Central Asia, and the Caucasus. Founded in 1961 during a British state visit to Iran, it opened a purpose-built institute in northern Tehran in 1976, which remarkably remained open after the 1979 Revolution. Funded by the British Academy, it publishes the journal *IRAN* (since 1963) and awards research and travel grants. [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari) was elected President in 2023.

The Broadcasting Board of Governors (BBG) was the US government agency responsible for supervising and funding non-military, international broadcasting by the United States from 1994 to 2018. It oversaw VOA Persian, Radio Farda (via Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty), and other international broadcasting services targeting audiences in authoritarian states. In 2018, Congress reorganised the BBG into the [US Agency for Global Media (USAGM)](/orgs/us-agency-for-global-media), which assumed all its functions and responsibilities.

Washington, DC-based nonpartisan think tank dedicated to the advancement of international peace and active international engagement by the United States. One of the oldest international affairs think tanks in the world, with offices in Washington, Moscow, Beijing, Brussels, Beirut, and New Delhi. Known for independent policy research on issues including nuclear policy, democracy, and Middle East affairs.

sources.title: About Carnegie Endowment for International Peace

nodes.label: CARPO (Center for Applied Research in Partnership with the Orient)

data.name_en: CARPO (Center for Applied Research in Partnership with the Orient)
data.description: Bonn-based independent research centre focused on the Middle East, Gulf, and Iran. Co-founded by [Adnan Tabatabai](/people/adnan-tabatabai), a German-Iranian analyst specializing in Iranian domestic politics and foreign policy. CARPO produces policy-oriented research and organizes Track II dialogues between European and Iranian stakeholders.

The Center for International Policy (CIP) is a Washington D.C.-based progressive think tank founded in 1975 focused on U.S. foreign policy, military spending, and diplomacy. Its Iran program publishes analysis advocating for diplomatic engagement and sanctions relief. CIP fellows have been prominent voices for reviving the JCPOA nuclear deal and opposing military confrontation with Iran.

nodes.label: Center for Middle East Strategic Studies (CMESS)

data.name_en: Center for Middle East Strategic Studies (CMESS)
data.description: Tehran-based think tank with ties to the Iranian foreign-policy establishment. Institutional home of [Kayhan Barzegar](/people/kayhan-barzegar), who has served as director. CMESS produces analysis on regional geopolitics, Iran's nuclear program, and Persian Gulf security broadly aligned with regime foreign-policy perspectives.

sources.title: Center for Middle East Strategic Studies

data.description: The Central Bank of the Islamic Republic of Iran (Bank Markazi) is Iran's central bank and monetary authority, responsible for maintaining monetary and financial stability, regulating the banking sector, and managing foreign exchange reserves. In October 2025, it revoked the operating licence of [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank) and ordered its merger into [Bank Melli Iran](/orgs/bank-melli-iran).

The Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) is the primary foreign intelligence service of the United States government. In the context of Iranian history, the CIA played a decisive role in the [1953 Iranian coup d'état](/events/iranian-coup-1953), orchestrating Operation Ajax (alongside Britain's MI6) to overthrow the democratically-elected government of Prime Minister Mohammad Mossadegh and restore [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) to full power. The agency subsequently assisted in the creation and training of [SAVAK](/orgs/savak), Iran's secret police, in 1957. The CIA's role in the coup was officially acknowledged by the US government in 2013 with the declassification of relevant documents.

Chatham House, formally the Royal Institute of International Affairs, is a London-based independent policy institute. Founded in 1920, it is one of the world's leading think tanks for international affairs, with extensive programmes on the Middle East and North Africa, including regular analysis of Iranian politics, nuclear negotiations, and sanctions policy. Its Iran-related work has involved numerous scholars and analysts connected to Iranian affairs.

Iranian studies program at Columbia University in New York City, home to the Hagop Kevorkian Chair in Middle Eastern Languages and Cultures held by Hamid Dabashi. The program also hosted Kian Tajbakhsh, an urban planner and social scientist arrested by the Iranian government in 2007 and 2009 on espionage charges. Columbia's Iranian Studies constellation has been a significant node in academic engagement with Iran.

sources.title: Middle Eastern, South Asian, and African Studies — Columbia University

data.description: The Combatant Clergy Association (Jame'e-ye Rowhaniyat-e Mobarez, JRM) is a conservative clerical political organisation founded in 1977 by a group of clerics including [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari). It is one of the oldest and most powerful political bodies in the Islamic Republic, consistently aligning with the conservative and principlist factions. Key affiliated figures include [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and [Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri](/people/ali-akbar-nategh-nouri). It split in 1988 when its left wing broke away to form the [Association of Combatant Clerics](/orgs/association-of-combatant-clerics) (MRM).

The Committee for Special Operations was a covert Iranian state body identified by the Berlin District Court in its landmark April 1997 verdict in the [Mykonos restaurant assassination](/events/mykonos-restaurant-assassination-1992) trial. The court found that the committee ordered the September 1992 assassination of [Sadegh Sharafkandi](/people/sadegh-sharafkandi) and three other Kurdish opposition figures at the Mykonos restaurant in Berlin. According to the verdict, the committee comprised the highest levels of the Islamic Republic, including [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian), and [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati). The ruling was the first time a European court officially attributed state-sponsored assassination to the highest echelons of the Iranian government, and triggered the recall of all EU ambassadors from Tehran. It remains a foundational legal document establishing the command structure of Iran's extraterritorial assassination programme.

The Communist Party of Iran (CPI) was founded on 2 September 1983 through the merger of the [Komala Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/komala-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) with several Iranian leftist organizations, including the Union of Communist Militants and a faction of the [Peykar Organization](/orgs/peykar-organization). [Mansoor Hekmat](/people/mansoor-hekmat) was a co-founder and leading theoretician of the party. The CPI opposed both the Islamic Republic and the Soviet model of socialism, advocating worker-council communism. In 1991, Hekmat and a significant portion of the membership left to form the [Worker-communist Party of Iran](/orgs/worker-communist-party-of-iran). In 2000, the [Komala](/orgs/komala-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) wing under [Abdullah Mohtadi](/people/abdullah-mohtadi) also split, further weakening the party. The CPI continues to operate in exile.

data.name_fa: کنفدراسیون جهانی محصلین و دانشجویان ایرانی (اتحادیه ملی)
data.description: The Confederation of Iranian Students, National Union (CISNU) was the first universal Iranian student organization, established in 1960 in Heidelberg, Germany. Initially influenced by [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran) and [Tudeh Party](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) sympathizers, the Confederation progressively radicalized throughout the 1960s and 1970s, eventually embracing Marxist, Maoist, and pro-guerrilla positions. CISNU played a prominent role in the international student protests of 1967-1969, particularly demonstrations against the Shah's state visits to Western Europe. The Iranian government declared CISNU illegal in 1971. By 1975, the Confederation had fragmented into competing Maoist and pro-guerrilla factions. CISNU was a key incubator of the Iranian left abroad, with many of its members joining organizations like the [Fadaiyan-e Khalq](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas) and other revolutionary groups. The organization dissolved after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) as members returned to Iran.

nodes.label: Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists of Iran

data.name_en: Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists of Iran
data.description: The Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists (Melli-Mazhabi) is a political organization in Iran established in 2000 by members closely associated with the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran). It carries forward the nationalist-religious political tradition that blends Islamic values with democratic nationalism. The group faced a government crackdown shortly after its founding, with dozens of members arrested and tried.

Nonpartisan American think tank and publisher specialising in US foreign policy and international affairs, headquartered in New York with an office in Washington, DC. Publishes Foreign Affairs magazine and hosts an independent Task Force program producing influential policy reports.

The Democracy Party of Iran (Hezb-e Mardomsalari, حزب مردم‌سالاری) is a reformist political party founded in 2000 by [Mostafa Kavakebian](/people/mostafa-kavakebian), who has served as its secretary-general. The party publishes the Mardomsalari newspaper, which Kavakebian edits. It has participated in reformist electoral coalitions and contested parliamentary elections in 2004, 2008, 2016, and subsequent cycles. The party operates within the reformist camp of the Islamic Republic's political system, advocating for greater civil liberties and democratic governance within the existing constitutional framework.

The Democratic Party of Iran was a progressive, constitutionalist political party founded in 1909 during the Constitutional Revolution period, distinct from the later Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan or the 1946 Azerbaijan Democratic Party. It was co-founded by Soleiman Mohsen Eskandari, a Qajar prince who would later become the first president of the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran), and other constitutionalist reformers. The party advocated for parliamentary government, secularism, and social reform, representing the liberal and democratic aspirations of the constitutional movement. It was the earliest significant antecedent in the genealogy of Iranian left and democratic politics, linking the constitutionalist era to the later emergence of the Tudeh Party.

The Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan (PDKI) is a Kurdish political party founded in 1945 in Mahabad by [Qazi Muhammad](/people/qazi-muhammad). It played a central role in the short-lived [Republic of Mahabad](/events/republic-of-mahabad-1946) and has been a leading voice for Kurdish autonomy within Iran ever since. After the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), the party initially participated in the new political process — its leader [Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou](/people/abdul-rahman-ghassemlou) was elected to the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in August 1979 with over 80% of the vote in West Azerbaijan — but the Islamic Republic banned the party, rejected Ghassemlou's credentials, and launched a military campaign against Kurdish regions. Ghassemlou was [assassinated in Vienna in 1989](/events/ghassemlou-assassination-1989), and his successor [Sadegh Sharafkandi](/people/sadegh-sharafkandi) was assassinated in the [Mykonos restaurant attack](/events/mykonos-restaurant-assassination-1992) in Berlin in 1992. The party has operated in exile since the early 1980s and is currently led by [Mustafa Hijri](/people/mustafa-hijri).

Donya (دنیا, 'The World') was a clandestine Marxist theoretical journal published in Tehran from 1934 to 1937. Founded and edited by [Taghi Arani](/people/taghi-arani), the journal was the central intellectual vehicle through which Arani built the proto-Tudeh network later known as the Group of Fifty-Three. The journal published articles on scientific Marxism, philosophy, and social theory, attracting young intellectuals to the underground movement. SAVAK and its predecessors monitored the journal closely. The publication was shut down when Reza Shah's government arrested Arani and 52 associates in 1937. The surviving members of this group, released after the Allied invasion of Iran in 1941, went on to found the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran).

The Dutch Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Ministerie van Buitenlandse Zaken) is the Netherlands government department responsible for foreign policy and international relations. In the context of Iranian diaspora media, the Dutch Ministry of Foreign Affairs provided initial funding for the launch of [Radio Zamaneh](/orgs/radio-zamaneh) in Amsterdam in 2006, making the Netherlands government a key early funder of independent Persian-language diaspora media. This support was part of a broader Dutch policy of promoting freedom of information and press freedom in authoritarian states.

Deutsche Welle Persian is the Persian-language service of Deutsche Welle, Germany's international public broadcaster. Operating since 1962, DW Persian provides radio, television, and digital content funded by the German federal government. Like other international Persian-language outlets, DW Persian staff and their families in Iran have faced harassment by Iranian authorities, and the regime has banned DW correspondents from operating inside the country.

Research and advisory initiative founded by [Afshin Molavi](/people/afshin-molavi), a senior fellow at the Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies (SAIS) and fellow at the Arab Gulf States Institute.
