Emerging World focuses on political economy, energy, and strategic analysis of the Persian Gulf and broader Middle East, reflecting Molavi's expertise in Gulf economic diversification and Iran's regional role.

Etemaad (اعتماد, 'Trust') was founded in 2002 and managed by former MP Elias Hazrati, who also co-founded [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi)'s National Trust Party in 2005. Banned after the 2009 Green Movement, it resumed in 2011 and remains one of the few reformist papers still operating.

Ettelaat (اطلاعات, 'Information') is the oldest continuously-published Persian-language daily newspaper in the world, founded in 1926. Under the Shah it was the newspaper of record. On 6 January 1978 it published an article attacking [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) as a British agent — triggering the Qom protests that opened the revolutionary cycle. After the revolution it was seized and placed under the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader).

The European Council on Foreign Relations (ECFR) is a pan-European think tank focused on foreign and security policy, headquartered in Berlin. [Ellie Geranmayeh](/people/ellie-geranmayeh), a member of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), served as deputy head of its Middle East and North Africa programme and senior policy fellow.

Evin Prison (Zendan-e Evin) is Tehran's most notorious detention facility, located in the Evin district of northwestern Tehran. Built under the Shah in 1972, it has under both regimes held political prisoners, dissidents, journalists, dual nationals, and activists. Under the Islamic Republic it became synonymous with forced confessions, torture, solitary confinement, and the mass executions of the [1988 prison massacre](/events/mass-executions-1988). Ward 209, run by the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence), houses intelligence detainees and is a site of systematic psychological and physical abuse documented extensively by human rights organizations. Numerous figures in this database have been detained at Evin.

The Expediency Discernment Council of the System (Majma'-e Tashkhis-e Maslahat-e Nezam) was established by [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in 1988 to resolve legislative disputes between the [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) and [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). It also acts as an advisory body to the Supreme Leader. Membership is appointed by the Supreme Leader and typically includes former presidents, senior clerics, and military figures, making it a powerful gathering point for Iran's political elite.

The Fadaiyan-e Khalq (Majority) split from the [Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas) in 1980, adopting a pro-Soviet line similar to the [Tudeh Party](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran). The Majority faction abandoned armed struggle and initially supported the Islamic Republic, believing in a two-stage revolution theory where the anti-imperialist phase must precede socialist transformation. This collaboration with the regime proved disastrous: when the government cracked down on leftists in 1983, many Majority members were arrested, imprisoned, and executed alongside Tudeh members in the [1988 mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988). Surviving members fled to exile. The organization continues to operate from abroad, having evolved toward social-democratic positions.

The Fadaiyan-e Khalq (Minority) split from the [Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas) in 1980, rejecting the pro-Soviet line adopted by the [Majority faction](/orgs/fadaiyan-e-khalq-majority). The Minority maintained an anti-imperialist stance critical of both the Soviet Union and the Islamic Republic, and continued to advocate armed struggle. The faction opposed the regime from the outset and was among the first leftist groups targeted in the post-revolution crackdown. Many of its members were executed in the early 1980s and in the [1988 mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988). The organization continues in exile.

nodes.label: FARAJA (Law Enforcement Command of the Islamic Republic of Iran)

data.name_en: FARAJA (Law Enforcement Command of the Islamic Republic of Iran)
data.description: FARAJA (Farmandehi-ye Entezami-ye Jomhuri-ye Eslami-ye Iran) is Iran's national police and law enforcement command, established in 1991 by merging the Gendarmerie, national police, and Revolutionary Committees. It is responsible for public order, traffic enforcement, border control, and combating drug trafficking. FARAJA is distinct from both the IRGC and the Artesh; it operates under the Interior Ministry but its commander is appointed by the Supreme Leader. FARAJA was heavily involved in suppressing the Woman, Life, Freedom protests in 2022-2023, including the arrest of Mahsa Amini by its 'guidance patrol' (Gasht-e Ershad) that triggered the uprising.

sources.title: Law Enforcement Command of Islamic Republic of Iran — Wikipedia
Iran Human Rights — FARAJA and the crackdown on protests

data.description: Fars News Agency is a hardline news agency widely considered to be the media arm of the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). Launched in 2003, it rapidly became one of Iran's most prominent wire services, consistently amplifying principlist and [Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader)-aligned narratives. Fars has published forced confessions of political prisoners and served as a platform for information warfare against reformists, dissidents, and Western governments. In 2012 the US Treasury sanctioned Fars as an IRGC front entity, and it was subsequently included in EU sanctions. The agency is known for publishing unverified intelligence-style exposés and targeting diaspora activists and dual nationals.

Fath (فتح, 'Victory') was a short-lived reformist daily on whose editorial board [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi) served. Closed in the April 2000 crackdown; both Ganji (22 April) and Baghi (29 May) were arrested for their Fath and [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper) articles.

Military-affairs publication of the [Foundation for Defense of Democracies](/orgs/foundation-for-defense-of-democracies), tracking IRGC activities, Iranian proxy networks, and regional Islamist militant groups. [Behnam Ben Taleblu](/people/behnam-ben-taleblu) is among contributors who publish IRGC and Iran-related analysis through FDD platforms.

The Forqan Group (Goruh-e Forqan) was a small militant Islamist organisation that carried out a series of assassinations in the chaotic months following the 1979 revolution. It assassinated [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari) on 1 May 1979 and attempted to kill [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). The group opposed clerical rule, arguing for a return to the Quran without clerical intermediaries. Its members were arrested and executed by 1980.

Washington-based hawkish national security think tank founded after 9/11. Known for its Iran sanctions and maximum-pressure advocacy. Hosts the Center on Economic and Financial Power and a dedicated Iran Program. Senior director [Behnam Ben Taleblu](/people/behnam-ben-taleblu) is a frequent congressional witness on Iranian missiles, sanctions, and proxy networks.

France 24 Persian is the Persian-language service of France 24, the French public international broadcaster. Launched in January 2017, it provides round-the-clock news and current affairs programming aimed at Persian-speaking audiences in Iran and the diaspora. Funded by the French government, France 24 Persian expanded its audience significantly during the 2019–2020 protests and the 2022–2023 Woman Life Freedom uprising, when it became a major source of breaking news on events inside Iran. The Islamic Republic jams its satellite signal and has charged its Iran-based contributors with security offences.

France 24 is a French state-funded international news television channel launched in 2006 and operated by France Médias Monde, under the supervision of the French Ministry of Foreign Affairs. Broadcasting in French, English, Arabic, and Spanish, France 24 is part of France's international public broadcasting apparatus. It is the parent broadcaster of [France 24 Persian](/orgs/france-24-persian), the Persian-language service launched in 2017, which has rapidly grown as a news source for Iranian audiences both inside Iran and in the diaspora.

France Médias Monde (FMM) is the French state's international broadcasting holding company, established in 2008 as Audiovisuel Extérieur de la France and renamed France Médias Monde in 2012. It is 100% state-owned, supervised by the French Ministry of Foreign Affairs, and funded through the French public broadcasting budget. Its principal subsidiaries are [France 24](/orgs/france-24), the international television news network broadcasting in French, English, Arabic, and Spanish, and Radio France Internationale (RFI), the world-radio service broadcasting in French and over a dozen other languages. France 24 in turn operates [France 24 Persian](/orgs/france-24-persian), the Persian-language service launched in January 2017, which has grown into a significant news source for Iranian audiences both inside Iran and in the diaspora. The Islamic Republic jams France 24's satellite signal and has prosecuted Iranian journalists working for its Persian service.

Founded 17 May 1961 by [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan), [Mahmoud Taleghani](/people/mahmoud-taleghani), and [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi) as a religiously oriented offshoot of the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran). Oldest political party still active in Iran. Self-describes as "Muslims, Iranians, Constitutionalists and [Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh)ists." Members of the Freedom Movement formed the core of the provisional government after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), with [Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan) as prime minister. Barred from elections since 1980 (with a brief exception in 2003 local elections). Despite being effectively outlawed, continues to exist as a "barely tolerated" opposition force. Splinter groups include the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (1965) and the [Council of Nationalist-Religious Activists](/orgs/council-of-nationalist-religious-activists) (2000).

The Front of Islamic Revolution Stability (Jebhe-ye Paydari-ye Enqelab-e Eslami), known as Paydari or the Steadfast Front, is the most hardline political faction operating within the Islamic Republic's official political space. Founded in 2011 by disciples of [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), it advocates absolute velayat-e faqih and the total rejection of Western diplomacy. Key parliamentary figures include [Mahmoud Nabavian](/people/mahmoud-nabavian), [Hamid Rasaee](/people/hamid-rasaee), and [Amir-Hossein Sabeti](/people/amir-hossein-sabeti). The front outperformed [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf)'s SHANA coalition in Tehran in the 2024 parliamentary election.

nodes.label: General Staff of the Armed Forces of Iran

data.name_en: General Staff of the Armed Forces of Iran
data.description: The General Staff of the Armed Forces (Setad-e Koll-e Niruhaye Mosallah) is the supreme military coordination body of the Islamic Republic of Iran, directly subordinate to the Supreme Leader. It coordinates both the [Artesh](/orgs/artesh) and the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), making it the apex of the unified command structure. The Chief of Staff is appointed by and reports directly to the Supreme Leader. [Mohammad Bagheri](/people/mohammad-bagheri) served as Chief of Staff from 2016 until his death on 13 June 2025 in Israeli airstrikes on Tehran. [Gholam Ali Rashid](/people/gholam-ali-rashid) served as deputy commander and was killed in the same strike.

sources.title: General Staff of the Armed Forces (Iran) — Wikipedia
Israeli airstrikes eliminate senior Iranian military officials

data.description: Swiss international security policy foundation based in Geneva, established under Swiss federal law with support from over 50 states. Long-time research home of Shahram Chubin, a prominent Iranian-Swiss scholar on Iranian nuclear policy, regional security, and Iran's strategic culture. The GCSP runs executive education and policy research programs drawing participants from governments and international organizations worldwide.

sources.title: Geneva Centre for Security Policy — About

data.description: The Group of Fifty-Three (Goruh-e Panjah-o-Se Nafar) was a clandestine Marxist network in Iran organised by physicist and intellectual [Taghi Arani](/people/taghi-arani) in the mid-1930s. The group produced and distributed the Marxist journal *Donya* and sought to build a disciplined underground communist cadre. Reza Shah's security apparatus arrested fifty-three of its members in 1937, and Arani died in prison in 1940. After serving their sentences under the changed conditions following the Allied invasion of Iran in 1941, surviving members — including [Iraj Eskandari](/people/iraj-eskandari) — formed the core founding cadre of the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) on 29 September 1941. The Group of Fifty-Three is thus considered the direct organisational precursor to the Tudeh Party.

The Guardian Council (Shura-ye Negahban) is a 12-member constitutional body with three mandates: vetting legislation for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution; supervising elections; and approving or disqualifying all candidates for parliament, presidency, and [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). Six members are Islamic jurists appointed directly by the Supreme Leader; six are civil lawyers nominated by the Chief Justice (also appointed by the Supreme Leader) and confirmed by parliament. Members serve six-year terms in staggered cohorts.

The Haghani Seminary (Howzeh-ye Elmieh-ye Haqqani) is a hardline Shia seminary in Qom co-founded in the 1960s by [Mohammad-Taqi Mesbah Yazdi](/people/mohammad-taqi-mesbah-yazdi), [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti), and [Ahmad Jannati](/people/ahmad-jannati). Described as 'a kind of École Nationale d'Administration for the Islamic Republic', its graduates form the backbone of the clerical management class in Iran's key political and security institutions. The seminary operates under a strict interpretation of velayat-e faqih and has been the primary incubator of the Haghani Circle ideology.

Ham-Mihan (همیهن, 'Compatriots') was a reformist daily operated by [Gholamhossein Karbaschi](/people/gholamhossein-karbaschi) after he was jailed in 1999 on politically-motivated corruption charges. Closed by Tehran's Press Court on 16 May 2000. Briefly relaunched in 2007.

Hamshahri (همشهری, 'Fellow Citizens') was founded in 1992 by Tehran Mayor [Gholamhossein Karbaschi](/people/gholamhossein-karbaschi) as Iran's first colour daily. Within three years it became Iran's best-selling newspaper at 400,000 copies daily — twice the circulation of [Kayhan](/orgs/kayhan-newspaper) and [Ettelaat](/orgs/ettelaat-newspaper). During the Khatami era it was reformist-aligned. After Karbaschi was jailed in 1999 on politically-motivated charges, Tehran Municipality under conservative control shifted its editorial line. In February 2006 its graphics editor launched the International Holocaust Cartoon Competition, drawing global controversy.
Hayat-e No (حیات نو, 'New Life') emerged from the wreckage of the April 2000 closures and continued through Khatami's second term under repeated suspensions.

The Worker-communist Party of Iran — Hekmatist (WPI-H) is a splinter party that emerged following a leadership dispute after the death of [Mansoor Hekmat](/people/mansoor-hekmat) in 2002. The faction led by Koorosh Modarresi formally split from the [Worker-communist Party of Iran](/orgs/worker-communist-party-of-iran) in 2004, claiming to be the legitimate heir to Hekmat's ideological legacy and accusing the Hamid Taqvaee-led WPI of deviating from Hekmat's line. The party operates in exile and publishes theoretical works positioning itself as the orthodox current of worker-communism. The split reflects personal and political disagreements over party direction following Hekmat's death rather than fundamental ideological divergence.

The Henry Jackson Society is a British foreign policy think tank founded in 2005, named after US Senator Henry 'Scoop' Jackson. It advocates a hawkish, interventionist foreign policy orientation and has been a prominent voice for robust Western responses to Iranian influence operations, Islamist extremism, and authoritarian states. Based in London, it produces policy research, hosts events, and publishes commentary on security and geopolitical affairs. The society has been a platform for analysts and former intelligence practitioners focused on countering Iranian and Chinese state influence in the West.

Hezbollah (Party of God) is a Lebanese Shia Islamist political party and militant group formally established in 1982 during the Israeli invasion of Lebanon. Its organizational roots trace to the Shia military infrastructure built in Lebanon during the 1970s by [Mostafa Chamran](/people/mostafa-chamran) and [Musa al-Sadr](/people/musa-al-sadr) through the [Amal Movement](/orgs/amal-movement). The formal founding was actively supervised by [Ali Akbar Mohtashamipur](/people/ali-akbar-mohtashamipur), Iran's ambassador to Syria, who worked within the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) framework to merge existing radical Shia factions — including the Islamic Amal splinter, the Lebanese Dawa Party, and other groups — into a unified organization. It is the most powerful of Iran's regional proxies and receives extensive financial, military, and logistical support from the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). It operates as both a political party in Lebanese parliament and a military force with an arsenal that surpasses the Lebanese Armed Forces.

HispanTV is [IRIB](/orgs/irib)'s Spanish-language television channel, launched in 2012 to target Latin American audiences. The channel frames content through an anti-US, anti-imperialist lens and seeks to extend Iranian soft power in the Spanish-speaking world. Its YouTube and social media channels have been periodically suspended by platforms.

A research initiative at Stanford University's Hoover Institution focused on promoting democratic governance and civil society in Iran. Co-directed by [Abbas Milani](/people/abbas-milani), the project produces research, policy papers, and public programming on Iran's political development, human rights, and prospects for democratic transition.

Hussainiyyeh Ershad is a religious and cultural institution in Tehran co-founded by [Morteza Motahhari](/people/morteza-motahhari). It became famous in the 1960s and 1970s as a centre for modernist Islamic lectures, notably by Ali Shariati, whose talks attracted large audiences and contributed to the intellectual groundwork of the Islamic Revolution. It was closed by the Shah's government in 1973 but resumed activity after the revolution.

ILNA is an Iranian news agency connected to the Workers' House (Khaneh Kargar), a reformist-leaning labour institution. Founded in 2003, it is notable for covering labour protests, workers' rights, and trade union issues — topics that most Iranian state media avoid or downplay. ILNA occupies a reformist position in the regime media spectrum and has periodically faced pressure from hardline factions.

The Imam Sadiq Institute (Muassasat al-Imam al-Sadiq) is a theological research and publication institute in Qom, Iran, founded and headed by [Grand Ayatollah Jafar Sobhani](/people/jafar-sobhani). It is one of the most prolific Islamic theological publishers in the Shia world, having produced hundreds of volumes in Arabic and Persian on Islamic theology (kalam), jurisprudence (fiqh), Quranic studies, and the history of Shia doctrine. Its publications are widely used in the Qom hawza curriculum and in Shia seminaries across Iraq, Lebanon, and the Gulf. The institute functions as both a research centre and a publishing house, and is associated with Sobhani's teaching and fatwa-issuing activities.

The Institute for Iranian Studies at the University of St Andrews is one of the leading academic centres for the study of Iran in Europe. Founded in 2006 by [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari), it provides expertise on Iran from late antiquity to the modern era. The university library holds over 6,000 Persian books. The institute offers an MLitt in Iranian Studies and hosts a regular seminar series featuring researchers from across Britain and Europe.

The International Crisis Group (ICG) is a Brussels-based transnational NGO that conducts field research on violent conflict and advocates for policy solutions. [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley) served as its president and CEO from 2018 to 2021 before joining the Biden administration as special envoy on Iran. At least two members of the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) — [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez) and [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary) — were employed by ICG, with Vaez serving as Iran project director. After the [IEI email leak](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), ICG characterised the initiative as 'an informal platform' supported by European governments and institutions.

Professional academic association for the field of Iranian Studies, co-founded by [Ali Banuazizi](/people/ali-banuazizi) and other scholars of Iran in 1967. ISAS publishes the journal *Iranian Studies* and organizes biennial international conferences that serve as the primary gathering point for the global community of Iran scholars. It is the main institutional hub connecting diaspora and international academics working on Iranian history, politics, literature, and culture.

nodes.label: Institute for Political and International Studies

data.name_en: Institute for Political and International Studies
data.description: The Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS) is the in-house think tank of Iran's [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs). Founded in 1983, it conducts research on foreign policy, regional affairs, and international relations, and trains diplomats. Under [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani)'s directorship, IPIS oversaw the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert influence operation that recruited Western-based Iranian academics to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions in Western media and policy circles. [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh), the initiative's mastermind, was appointed head of IPIS in January 2025.

The Iran-e Novin (New Iran) Party was the dominant ruling party of Pahlavi Iran from its founding in late 1963 until its dissolution in March 1975. It grew out of the Progressive Circle established by Hassan Ali Mansur, a technocratic faction that won favour with [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) as an instrument for implementing the White Revolution reforms. [Amir Abbas Hoveyda](/people/amir-abbas-hoveyda) was among its founding members and served as Mansur's finance minister. When Mansur was assassinated in January 1965, Hoveyda assumed both the party chairmanship and the premiership, leading Iran-e Novin throughout the twelve years of his record-setting tenure as prime minister.

The party functioned as the Shah's modernisation vehicle, championing land reform, women's suffrage, literacy programmes, and state-led industrialisation under the White Revolution. In March 1975 the Shah dissolved all parties and established the single-party Rastakhiz (Resurgence) Party, with Hoveyda as its secretary-general, effectively absorbing Iran-e Novin.

The Iran Experts Initiative (IEI) was a covert Iranian [Ministry of Foreign Affairs](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) program to cultivate Western-based Iranian-diaspora academics and analysts as back-channels and influence agents during the JCPOA negotiation period. Conceived in early 2014 by [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh), then a Berlin-based diplomat, and operated through the [Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS)](/orgs/ipis), the Foreign Ministry's think tank. Under IPIS director [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani), participants including [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez), [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary), [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai), [Adnan Tabatabai](/people/adnan-tabatabai), [Ellie Geranmayeh](/people/ellie-geranmayeh), and [Rouzbeh Parsi](/people/rouzbeh-parsi) shared draft publications with Iranian officials before release, sought guidance on congressional testimony and conference attendance, and served as conduits between the Iranian Foreign Ministry and US policymakers. The operation was exposed in September 2023 when [Iran International](/orgs/iran-international) and [Semafor](/orgs/semafor) published thousands of leaked Foreign Ministry emails.

The Iran Human Rights Documentation Center (IHRDC) is a New Haven-based nonprofit organization founded in 2004 to document human rights violations in Iran. It has produced detailed reports on the 1988 mass executions, the chain murders of the 1990s, and ongoing political repression. Its founding board included prominent Iranian-American academics and activists.

Iran International is a Persian-language television news network launched in 2017, headquartered in London. Founded by [Volant Media](/orgs/volant-media), the channel quickly became one of the most-watched Persian-language outlets outside Iran, covering Iranian politics, human rights, and diaspora affairs. The Islamic Republic has repeatedly accused the channel of ties to Saudi Arabia and the UK, and Iran's [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) designated it a terrorist organisation. In 2023 the Metropolitan Police warned two Iran International journalists of credible assassination threats from Iranian intelligence, prompting the channel to temporarily relocate operations. In September 2023 Iran International, together with [Semafor](/orgs/semafor), published leaked [Iranian Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) emails that exposed the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023), a covert influence operation targeting Western policy circles.

Iran Mall is the world's largest shopping mall by area, located in northwest Tehran by Chitgar Lake. Conceived by [Ali Ansari](/people/ali-ansari-banker) around 2000, construction began in 2012 and the first phase opened on 1 May 2018 with 267,000 square metres of gross leasable area and 708 retail units.

The project was financed primarily through [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank), which directed approximately 70 percent of its total lending — some 97 thousand billion tomans — to Iran Mall and related entities. The majority of these loans became non-performing. In December 2020, the complex was announced for sale at $3.5 billion. Over 1,200 contractors and 25,000 workers participated in its construction.

The Iran National Council (INC) is an opposition political platform co-founded by [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) in 2013 to coordinate Iranian exile opposition activities. It was conceived as a broad-based coalition aiming to unite disparate opposition factions under a shared commitment to secular democracy and regime change in Iran.

The organization was active from 2013 to approximately 2017, when Pahlavi shifted his focus to other vehicles for opposition coordination.

Iran (ایران) is the primary government-owned daily, published since 1995 by the state news agency IRNA. It supports the presidency. English-language edition: *Iran Daily*. In September 2006 it was banned on the same day [Shargh](/orgs/shargh-newspaper) was closed; the Iran ban was quickly lifted while Shargh remained suspended.

nodes.label: Iran's Permanent Mission to the United Nations (New York)

data.name_en: Iran's Permanent Mission to the United Nations (New York)
data.name_fa: نمایندگی دائمی ایران نزد سازمان ملل متحد
data.description: Iran's Permanent Mission to the United Nations in New York represents the Islamic Republic in the UN General Assembly and Security Council proceedings. It serves as a formal channel for Iran's diplomatic engagement with the international community on issues including sanctions, nuclear negotiations, and regional conflicts. In April 2026, the United States expelled a senior Iranian diplomat — the fourth-ranking official at the mission — on security grounds, citing undisclosed concerns about activities incompatible with diplomatic status, continuing a pattern of US expulsions of Iranian UN mission personnel.

sources.title: Permanent Mission of Iran to the United Nations — Wikipedia

data.description: Independent English-language podcast on US-Iran relations, Iranian politics, and civil society, hosted by journalist [Negar Mortazavi](/people/negar-mortazavi). The show features interviews with scholars, activists, and policy practitioners, and is widely listened to by the Iranian diaspora and Iran-watchers in Washington and beyond.

nodes.label: Academy of Persian Language and Literature

data.name_en: Academy of Persian Language and Literature
data.description: The Academy of Persian Language and Literature (Farhangestan-e Zaban va Adab-e Farsi) is Iran's official language-regulatory body, tasked with preserving and developing the Persian language. It coins Persian equivalents for foreign terms and oversees linguistic standards. [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel) has served as its president since 1995.

Iran's diplomatic mission in Vienna, Austria, accredited to the Austrian government and to international organizations headquartered in Vienna (including IAEA, UNODC, and UNIDO). The embassy has been documented as an operational hub for [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) (MOIS) activities in Central Europe. Most notably, Assadollah Assadi — a senior MOIS officer operating under diplomatic cover as third counselor — used the Vienna embassy as his base and was arrested in Germany in 2018 after Belgian authorities foiled a plot to bomb the Iranian opposition group NCRI's rally in Villepinte, France. Assadi was convicted in Belgium in 2021.

sources.title: Belgium convicts Iranian diplomat for bomb plot

data.description: The Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas (IPFG) is a splinter organisation that broke away from the [Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas) after the 1980 split. Unlike both the [Fadaiyan-e Khalq (Majority)](/orgs/fadaiyan-e-khalq-majority), which moved toward collaboration with the Islamic Republic, and the [Fadaiyan-e Khalq (Minority)](/orgs/fadaiyan-e-khalq-minority), which maintained a militant opposition stance, the IPFG represented a smaller current committed to the original armed struggle line of the Fadaiyan tradition. It operates in exile and maintains the heritage of the pre-revolutionary guerrilla organisation while remaining a marginal force in post-revolutionary Iranian opposition politics.

The Iranian Writers' Association (Kanun-e Nevisandegan-e Iran) is a literary and civil society organisation founded in 1968 to defend freedom of expression and the rights of writers in Iran. In 1994, 134 of its members signed the landmark open letter 'We Are the Writers!' demanding an end to censorship. Several members of its organising committee — including [Mohammad Mokhtari](/people/mohammad-mokhtari), [Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh](/people/mohammad-jafar-pouyandeh), and [Ali Akbar Saidi Sirjani](/people/ali-akbar-saidi-sirjani) — were murdered as part of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). In August 1996, 21 writers travelling to a poetry conference were nearly killed when a [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) operative attempted to drive their bus off a cliff at Heyran Pass. The association has been repeatedly suppressed by Iranian authorities but continues to operate informally.

IranWire is an independent investigative journalism outlet founded in 2014 by [Maziar Bahari](/people/maziar-bahari), the Iranian-Canadian journalist and filmmaker who was imprisoned in Iran for 118 days in 2009 and coerced into a televised confession on [IRIB](/orgs/irib). Headquartered in London and funded through grants, IranWire produces investigative reporting on [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) corruption, human rights abuses, executions, and political repression in Iran.

Iranian foreign-policy news website affiliated with the Iranian Ministry of Foreign Affairs. IRDiplomacy.ir translates and amplifies analysis from Western commentators deemed favorable to regime positions, helping launder regime-aligned narratives through apparent third-party academic or journalistic endorsement.

The 16th Qods Division of Gilan is a regional IRGC ground forces division based in Gilan Province, northern Iran. It was commanded by [Ali Abdollahi Aliabadi](/people/ali-abdollahi-aliabadi) and is representative of the IRGC's provincial command structure established during and after the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war).

The 33rd Al-Mahdi Division is an IRGC ground forces combat division that fought during the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war). It was commanded by [Mohammad Jafar Asadi](/people/mohammad-jafar-asadi) during major operations of that conflict. The division is part of the IRGC Ground Forces (Niru-ye Zamini-ye Sepah) and represents one of the key IRGC formation units that gave the corps its battlefield experience.

The IRGC Aerospace Force (Niruy-e Hava-Faza-ye Sepah) is the branch of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) responsible for Iran's ballistic missile programme, satellite launches, and UAV (drone) operations. Founded in 1985 as the IRGC Air Force, it was expanded from 1989 onward under Supreme Leader [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). Its role was elevated dramatically by the appointment of [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) as commander in 2009. The force operates Iran's arsenal of short-, medium-, and long-range ballistic missiles — including the Shahab, Emad, and Fattah-1 hypersonic series — as well as a large UAV fleet including the Shahed loitering munitions. It directed the January 2020 strikes on US bases, the April and October 2024 direct strikes on Israel, and the Strait of Hormuz drone campaign. It was responsible for the accidental shoot-down of Ukrainian Airlines PS752 in January 2020. [Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) was killed in an Israeli airstrike in June 2025; he was succeeded by Hossein Mousavi Eftekhari.

data.name_fa: نیروی زمینی سپاه پاسداران انقلاب اسلامی
data.description: The IRGC Ground Forces Command is the largest branch of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) by personnel, responsible for conventional ground operations, border security, and internal defence. It controls numerous infantry and armoured divisions deployed across Iran's provinces and along contested borders. [Mohammad Pakpour](/people/mohammad-pakpour) commanded the IRGC Ground Forces from 2014 to 2025 before his appointment as IRGC Commander-in-Chief. The Ground Forces played a major role in operations during the Iran–Iraq War, Kurdish insurgency suppression, and have maintained a forward presence along the western and northwestern borders. During the 2019 Mahabad and border crackdowns it acted as a primary enforcement arm.

sources.title: Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Ground Forces — Wikipedia
Iran's Military Forces and Warfighting Capabilities — CSIS
sources.publisher: Center for Strategic and International Studies

data.description: The IRGC Intelligence Organisation (Sazman-e Ettelaat-e Sepah, IRGC-IO) is a parallel intelligence service operating alongside the civilian [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence). Formally restructured in its current form in 2009 following the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), it handles counter-intelligence within the IRGC, surveillance of political dissidents, arrests of dual nationals and foreign journalists, and covert operations abroad. It is distinct from the IRGC Quds Force's external operations wing but overlaps in function. It was headed by [Hossein Taeb](/people/hossein-taeb) from 2009 to 2022, then by General Mohammad Kazemi. The organisation has been sanctioned by the US, EU, and UK for its role in arresting and torturing activists during the 2009, 2019, and 2022 protest waves.

data.name_fa: نیروی دریایی سپاه پاسداران انقلاب اسلامی
data.description: The IRGC Navy (Niru-ye Daryayi-ye Sepah, NEDSA) is the naval arm of the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), operating separately from the Artesh Navy (NEDAJA). NEDSA focuses on asymmetric warfare in the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz: fast-attack craft, anti-ship missiles, sea mines, and harassment of foreign naval and commercial vessels. It controls a network of island bases in the Persian Gulf and has been responsible for numerous documented seizures and confrontations with US Navy vessels in the Gulf. NEDSA doctrine centers on area-denial and the ability to close the Strait of Hormuz in a conflict scenario.

sources.title: Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps Navy — Wikipedia
Iran's Military Forces and Warfighting Capabilities — CSIS
sources.publisher: Center for Strategic and International Studies

data.description: The Quds Force Iraq Corps is the regional command of the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) responsible for operations in Iraq. It serves as the primary institutional mechanism for Iran's influence over Iraqi Shia militia networks, including the [Popular Mobilisation Forces](/orgs/popular-mobilization-forces) (PMF/Hashd al-Shaabi). The Iraq Corps was central to Iran's strategy following the 2003 US invasion and the empowerment of Shia political parties, providing training, arms, financing, and operational command to Iraqi proxy forces. It became particularly prominent after 2014 in the fight against ISIS, during which Iranian-backed PMF factions played a major role under Quds Force coordination. The Iraq Corps operates alongside and through figures such as [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) and his successors who managed the Iraq portfolio personally from Quds Force HQ.

sources.title: IRGC Quds Force — AEI Critical Threats
Iran's Use of Proxy Forces — CSIS
sources.publisher: Center for Strategic and International Studies

data.description: The Lebanon Corps is the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) regional command responsible for operations in Lebanon, principally the training, arming, financing, and operational coordination of [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah). Established in the early 1980s during the founding period of Hezbollah, the Lebanon Corps has been the primary conduit for Iranian strategic influence in the Levant. It oversees weapons transfers (including precision-guided missiles), financial flows, and liaison with Hezbollah's military and political leadership. Following the Israeli killing of Hezbollah's liaison commander Ibrahim Aqil in September 2024, command of Lebanon Corps operations was assumed by [Davoud Alizadeh](/people/davoud-alizadeh), who was himself killed in an Israeli strike on 12 March 2026. Financial operations for the Lebanon portfolio were run by [Majid Hosseinikandesar](/people/majid-hosseinikandesar), killed 8 March 2026.

sources.title: Who was Ibrahim Aqil, senior Hezbollah commander killed by Israel?

data.description: The Palestine Corps is the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) regional command responsible for operations in Gaza and the Palestinian territories, principally providing training, weapons, and funding to Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad (PIJ). The Palestine Corps coordinates the transfer of rocket technology, anti-tank weapons, and military training that underpinned Hamas's military buildup prior to the October 7, 2023 attack. Commander [Ahmad Rasouli](/people/ahmad-rasouli) was killed in an Israeli airstrike in 2026.

sources.title: Treasury Targets Hamas and Its Financing Network

data.description: Unit 840 is a covert external operations unit of the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force) specialising in kidnapping, assassination, and irregular operations against foreign targets including foreign officials, dissidents, Israeli tourists, and Western nationals. The unit was designated by the US Treasury Department in 2022 for plots against US officials and Israeli nationals. Commanded by [Yazdan Mir](/people/yazdan-mir), who was himself sanctioned by the US Treasury and killed in an Israeli airstrike on 6 April 2026.

sources.title: Treasury Sanctions IRGC Officials Responsible for Plots Against Americans and Israelis
U.S. sanctions Iranian unit tied to plots against Americans, Israelis

data.description: The Quds Force (Jerusalem Force) is the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) branch responsible for extraterritorial operations, unconventional warfare, and support to non-state allies. It reports directly to the Supreme Leader. The Quds Force supports [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) in Lebanon, Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad in Gaza, the Houthis in Yemen, and Shia militias in Iraq and Syria. It has been described by the US military as analogous to a combination of the CIA and JSOC.

sources.title: Treasury Designates the IRGC under Terrorism Authority and Targets IRGC and ESF in Response to Continued Sanctions Evasion

data.description: IRIB (Seda va Sima) holds a constitutional monopoly on all radio and television broadcasting in Iran under Article 175 of the constitution, which grants the [Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) sole authority to appoint and dismiss its director. Founded in 1979 after the [revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) replaced the Shah-era National Iranian Radio and Television (NIRT), its first head was [Sadegh Ghotbzadeh](/people/sadegh-ghotbzadeh), followed by [Mohammad Hashemi](/people/mohammad-hashemi) (1980–1994), who ran IRIB through the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) and expanded it from two channels to a multi-channel network. [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) (1994–2004) was appointed as a hardline counterweight to the reformist press during the Khatami era. [Ezzatollah Zarghami](/people/ezzatollah-zarghami) (2004–2014) oversaw coverage of the disputed [2009 election](/events/presidential-election-iran-2009) and the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), during which IRIB declared [Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) the winner before official certification and ignored mass protests. [Mohammad Sarafraz](/people/mohammad-sarafraz) (2014–2016), [Abdolali Ali-Asgari](/people/abdolali-ali-asgari) (2016–2021), and [Peyman Jebelli](/people/peyman-jebelli) (2021–present) followed. IRIB operates dozens of domestic television and radio channels, provincial stations, and international outlets including [Press TV](/orgs/press-tv) (English), Al-Alam (Arabic), HispanTV (Spanish), and iFilm. It also publishes the daily newspaper Jam-e Jam. IRIB has systematically broadcast forced confessions of political prisoners, journalists, and activists — a practice documented in over 500 cases by human rights organisations. The European Union sanctioned IRIB and director Zarghami in 2012, and Ofcom revoked Press TV's UK licence the same year for airing Newsweek journalist Maziar Bahari's coerced confession. Fresh EU sanctions were imposed in October 2022 over IRIB's role during the [Mahsa Amini protests](/events/mahsa-amini-protests-2022). Dissident journalist [Ruhollah Zam](/people/ruhollah-zam), whose forced confession was broadcast on IRIB, was [executed](/events/execution-of-ruhollah-zam-2020) in December 2020.

IRNA is Iran's official state news agency, originally established in 1934 as the Pars Agency and renamed after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). As a government-owned agency under the executive branch, IRNA's editorial orientation shifts with the sitting president — it leaned pragmatist under Rouhani and hardliner under Raisi. IRNA serves as the primary wire service for official government statements and is one of the oldest news agencies in the Middle East.

Islamic Azad University is the largest university system in Iran, with branches across the country. It was founded in 1982 by [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and operates as a private, non-profit institution. It has played a significant role in expanding higher education access in Iran.

The Islamic Consultative Assembly (Majles-e Shura-ye Eslami), commonly known as the Majlis or Majles, is the unicameral legislature of the Islamic Republic of Iran. It currently has 290 seats, elected for four-year terms. All candidates must be vetted and approved by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). The Majlis can legislate, ratify treaties, and impeach the president, but all legislation must pass Guardian Council review for conformity with Islamic law and the constitution.

nodes.label: Islamic Cultural Institute of Buenos Aires

data.name_en: Islamic Cultural Institute of Buenos Aires
data.description: The Islamic Cultural Institute of Buenos Aires (also known as the At-Tauhid Mosque) was a mosque and Islamic cultural centre from which [Mohsen Rabbani](/people/mohsen-rabbani) operated under cover as Iran's cultural attaché to Argentina in the early 1990s. Argentine prosecutors and international investigators concluded that the institute served as an operational platform for MOIS and IRGC-Quds Force activities in Latin America, including the coordination of the 1994 AMIA bombing. Rabbani used the institution as a base for recruiting Shia converts and building intelligence networks across the region.

The Islamic Ideology Dissemination Organization (IIDO; Persian: Sazman-e Tablighat-e Eslami) is a state body subordinate to the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) responsible for promoting the Islamic Republic's ideological and cultural messaging. Established in 1981, the IIDO oversees a wide range of propaganda and cultural activities including publications, art exhibitions, religious ceremonies, and media. It is the parent organization of [Mehr News Agency](/orgs/mehr-news-agency), Iran's largest official news wire. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) exercises ultimate authority over the IIDO as Supreme Leader.

The Islamic Iran Participation Front (Jebhe-ye Mosharekat-e Iran-e Eslami), abbreviated IIPF, was the largest and most prominent reformist political party in post-revolutionary Iran. Founded in 1998 by [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami), it was the main vehicle for the reformist 2nd Khordad movement that swept the 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) in 2000. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) barred all IIPF candidates from running in the 2004 parliamentary election, effectively eliminating the party from parliament. It was formally suspended by the government in 2010 following the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests. The party advocated political pluralism, civil society, rule of law, and engagement with the West.

The Islamic Propaganda Organization (سازمان تبلیغات اسلامی) is a quasi-state cultural and religious body established after the 1979 revolution, operating under the direct supervision of the Supreme Leader. It manages religious messaging, cultural production, and arts policy across Iran, including oversight of affiliated institutions such as Soura University and the Arts Organization. Senior clerics and cultural officials have held leadership positions within the organization, which is a key instrument of ideological governance within the Islamic Republic.

data.name_fa: نیروی دریایی ارتش جمهوری اسلامی ایران
data.description: The Islamic Republic of Iran Navy (NEDAJA) is the naval branch of the Artesh — Iran's regular armed forces — distinct from the parallel IRGC Navy. NEDAJA is responsible for blue-water operations in the Gulf of Oman, Indian Ocean, and beyond, while the IRGC Navy handles Persian Gulf asymmetric operations. It operates frigates, submarines, and destroyers, and since the 2010s has conducted long-range deployments including circumnavigation voyages and joint exercises with the Russian and Chinese navies. Recent commanders include Habibollah Sayyari, Hossein Khanzadi, and [Shahram Irani](/people/shahram-irani) (2021–present).

The Islamic Republican Party (Hezb-e Jomhuri-ye Eslami, IRP) was the dominant political party of the early Islamic Republic, co-founded in 1979 by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s inner circle including [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti), [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar), and [Abdolkarim Mousavi-Ardabili](/people/abdolkarim-mousavi-ardabili). It dominated the first three [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) sessions and the executive branch until its dissolution in 1987 by Khomeini's order, following irreconcilable internal factional disputes. Its dissolution led directly to the formation of the rival [Combatant Clergy Association](/orgs/combatant-clergy-association) and [Association of Combatant Clerics](/orgs/association-of-combatant-clerics) as successor factions.

The Islamic Revolutionary Council was a governing body established by [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in January 1979 to oversee the transition of power during the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Its members included [Mehdi Bazargan](/people/mehdi-bazargan), [Yadollah Sahabi](/people/yadollah-sahabi), [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr), and others. It operated in parallel with Bazargan's provisional government and was dissolved in 1980 after the first Majlis was elected.

The Islamic Revolutionary Courts (Dadgah-ha-ye Enqelab-e Eslami) were established in February 1979 immediately after the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) to try individuals accused of crimes against the revolution, including former officials of the Pahlavi regime, political dissidents, and those charged with "corruption on earth" (mofsed-e fel-arz) or "enmity against God" (moharebeh). Initially presided over by Sadegh Khalkhali under the authority of [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini), the courts conducted summary trials with minimal due process. The courts remain an active part of Iran's [judiciary](/orgs/judiciary-of-iran), handling cases involving national security, narcotics, and political offences.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC), known domestically as Sepah or Pasdaran, is a multi-service elite military force established by [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) in May 1979. It consists of five branches: Ground Forces, Aerospace Force, Navy, [Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), and [Basij](/orgs/basij). Its commander-in-chief reports directly to the Supreme Leader. Beyond its military role, the IRGC controls a vast economic empire including Khatam al-Anbiya Construction, multiple banks, and extensive holdings in energy, telecommunications, and construction. Estimated at 125,000 personnel.

ISNA is an Iranian news agency established in 1999 under the auspices of the Ministry of Science, Research and Technology, linked to regime-aligned student organizations. Among Iranian state-adjacent media, ISNA has at times published relatively more open reporting, occupying a centrist position in the regime media spectrum. It covers education, science, and general news.

Jame'eh (جامعه, 'Society') was the first of the 'serial newspapers' — the chain of reformist dailies run by [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin) and co-directed by [Hamid Reza Jalalipour](/people/hamid-reza-jalalipour) that were successively closed and immediately relaunched under new names. Founded in early 1998, it ran a headline quoting Khatami's challenge to the IRGC: 'Society cannot be moved forward by instilling fear.' After suspension and permanent closure, Shamsolvaezin and Jalalipour were jailed 35 days without charge. Upon release, Shamsolvaezin immediately relaunched as [Tous](/orgs/tous-newspaper).

British weekly Jewish newspaper founded in 1841, the world's oldest continuously published Jewish periodical. In 2023 the Jewish Chronicle broke the story of [Mohammad Marandi](/people/mohammad-marandi) appearing in an IRGC military uniform at a formal ceremony, providing key photographic evidence of his direct connections to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

nodes.label: Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies (SAIS)

data.name_en: Johns Hopkins School of Advanced International Studies (SAIS)
data.description: Elite graduate school of international relations based in Washington, D.C. and Bologna, Italy. Former institutional home of [Vali Nasr](/people/vali-nasr), who served as dean (2012–2019), and scholars including [Narges Bajoghli](/people/narges-bajoghli) and Afshin Molavi. SAIS is a leading training ground for American foreign-policy practitioners and hosts influential Iran-focused research.

Jomhouri-ye Eslami (جمهوری اسلامی) was founded on 30 May 1979 as the organ of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party). Managing director: [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei). First editor-in-chief: [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi). The paper continued after the IRP's dissolution in 1987 and is classified as close to [Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani)'s circle by the 2010s.

The Judiciary of Iran (Qovveh-ye Qaza'iyeh) is the judicial branch of the Islamic Republic, headed by a Chief Justice appointed directly by the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) for five-year terms. The Chief Justice also nominates the six jurist members of the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). The Judiciary has been a primary instrument of political repression, overseeing prosecutions of reformists, journalists, and human rights defenders. Chief Justices since 1989 include [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) (briefly), Mohammad Yazdi (1989–1999), Mahmoud Hashemi Shahroudi (1999–2009), [Sadegh Amoli Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) (2009–2019), [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) (2019–2021), and [Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei) (2021–present).

Kayhan (کیهان, 'Universe') is Iran's most prominent hardline daily newspaper, founded in 1941 as a private paper. After the revolution its owner Mostafa Mesbahzadeh fled abroad and the paper was seized and placed directly under the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). Since 1993 the managing editor has been [Hossein Shariatmadari](/people/hossein-shariatmadari), a Khamenei appointee and former MOIS interrogator who is regarded as the Supreme Leader's direct mouthpiece in the press. Kayhan regularly calls for the prosecution of reformist politicians and journalists, denies the Holocaust, and attacks critical media. In the early 1990s it published *Kayhan-e Farhangi*, an intellectual magazine hosting Abdolkarim Soroush's circle, until Khatami's 1992 resignation from the Culture Ministry forced that group out to found [Kian](/orgs/kian-magazine).

Khabar Online is an Iranian online news outlet associated with [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) and the pragmatist-conservative wing of the establishment. Founded around 2006, it occupies a relatively moderate position within the regime media spectrum, covering political and parliamentary news.

Khatam al-Anbiya Central Headquarters (KCHQ) is the joint operational military command of Iran's armed forces, distinct from the [Khatam al-Anbiya Construction Headquarters](/orgs/khatam-al-anbiya) which shares a similar name. Established as a permanent standing command in 2016 when it was separated from the General Staff, KCHQ coordinates joint operations between the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and the regular Iranian Army. It has served as Iran's primary joint warfighting command structure and was sanctioned by the United Kingdom and Canada in April 2024 following Operation True Promise.

Khatam al-Anbiya Construction Headquarters is the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)'s engineering and construction arm and one of Iran's largest conglomerates. Originally formed to rebuild infrastructure after the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), it grew into a sprawling enterprise with interests in oil and gas, transportation, water management, and construction. It has been subject to US and EU sanctions for its role in Iran's nuclear and missile programmes and its ties to the IRGC.

Khordad (خرداد) was the flagship newspaper of the '2nd of Khordad' reformist movement, named for the date of Khatami's election. Published by the Islamic Iran Participation Front, editor-in-chief [Abbas Abdi](/people/abbas-abdi). Closed in 1999 as one of the most politically explicit press suppressions of the era.

Kian (کیان) was the most influential intellectual magazine of the pre-Khatami reform era, founded in November 1991 by publisher Mostafa Rokh-Sefat with [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin) as editor. Its circle (*halgheh-ye Kian*) included Abdolkarim Soroush, [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian), [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji), and others who became the intellectual architects of the reform movement. Kian emerged when Khatami's 1992 resignation from the Culture Ministry forced Soroush's circle out of *Kayhan-e Farhangi*. Closed by judicial order in 1998.

Komala (meaning 'association' in Kurdish) was founded in 1969 by Kurdish student leaders and intellectuals in Tehran. Initially Maoist in orientation, it operated underground until the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), after which it became a major force in the Kurdish resistance to the Islamic Republic. Komala fought alongside the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) against the central government's military campaigns in Kurdistan. In 1983, Komala merged with several other leftist groups to form the [Communist Party of Iran](/orgs/communist-party-of-iran), serving as its Kurdish branch. In 2000, the faction led by [Abdullah Mohtadi](/people/abdullah-mohtadi) split from the CPI and reconstituted itself as the independent Komala Party, gradually evolving from Marxism toward social democracy. The organization advocates Kurdish self-determination and federalism in Iran.

The Komala Toilers Party (Zehmetkêshan, meaning 'toilers' or 'labourers' in Kurdish) is the third major faction to emerge from the historic Komala organisation, distinct from both the [Komala Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/komala-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) led by Abdullah Mohtadi and the Communist Party of Iran-Komala faction led by Ibrahim Alizadeh. It represents a current that broke away during the post-2000 reorganisation of the Kurdish left, maintaining a Marxist orientation while charting an independent course from both of the larger Komala factions. Like the other Komala branches, it operates in exile from Iraqi Kurdistan and advocates Kurdish self-determination within a federal, democratic Iran.

The Law Enforcement Command of Iran (FARAJA, formerly NAJA) is the national police force of the Islamic Republic. Subordinate to the [Ministry of Interior](/orgs/ministry-of-interior), it is responsible for public security, border control, and crowd control. Under Commander-in-Chief [Ahmad-Reza Radan](/people/ahmad-reza-radan), police forces participated in the violent suppression of the [2025-2026 protests](/events/january-2026-massacres), using live ammunition and military-grade weapons against civilian demonstrators.

Manoto (Me and You) was a Persian-language satellite television channel founded in 2010 by [Kayvan Abbassi](/people/kayvan-abbassi) and his wife Marjan Abbassi, an Iranian-British couple, and headquartered in London. Privately funded with no confirmed state backing, Manoto became one of the most-watched Persian-language channels globally, combining entertainment programming with news and current affairs coverage. The channel maintained a secular, anti-regime editorial line and attracted a broad audience inside Iran despite satellite jamming. The Iranian regime alleged MI6 and Mossad connections to the channel — claims that remain unsubstantiated. In 2024 Manoto ceased live broadcasting and shifted to on-demand digital content.

Mardomsalari (مردم‌سالاری, meaning 'Democracy') is the official newspaper of the [Democracy Party of Iran](/orgs/democracy-party-iran), founded in 2002. It is one of the few reformist-affiliated newspapers still publishing in Iran. [Mostafa Kavakebian](/people/mostafa-kavakebian), the party's founder and secretary-general, serves as editor-in-chief. The paper covers Iranian domestic politics from a reformist perspective and has survived successive press crackdowns that shuttered many reform-era publications.

Mashregh News is a hardline online news outlet widely attributed to IRGC-linked circles. Launched around 2010, it is known for publishing intelligence-style exposés targeting reformists, dissidents, dual nationals, and diaspora activists — effectively functioning as a doxxing and information-warfare platform. Together with [Fars News Agency](/orgs/fars-news-agency) and [Tasnim News Agency](/orgs/tasnim-news-agency), Mashregh forms an [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned media triangle.

Mehr News Agency is an Iranian news agency affiliated with the [Islamic Ideology Dissemination Organization](/orgs/islamic-ideology-dissemination-organization) (Sazman-e Tablighat-e Eslami), a body under the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). Founded in 2003, Mehr produces multilingual content in English, Arabic, Turkish, and other languages. It occupies a conservative to hardliner position in the Iranian media spectrum, promoting the regime's cultural and ideological messaging.

The Secret Intelligence Service (SIS), commonly known as MI6, is the foreign intelligence service of the United Kingdom. MI6 was a co-architect of the [1953 Iranian coup d'état](/events/iranian-coup-1953), planning Operation Boot alongside the CIA's Operation Ajax to overthrow Prime Minister Mohammad Mossadegh. British involvement was primarily motivated by the nationalisation of the [Anglo-Iranian Oil Company](/orgs/anglo-iranian-oil-company) in 1951, which threatened substantial British economic and strategic interests. The British government's role in the coup was formally acknowledged in 2013.

Washington-based think tank founded in 1946, one of the oldest US institutions focused on Middle East policy. Hosts subject-matter programs including an Iran Program directed by [Alex Vatanka](/people/alex-vatanka). Publishes analysis and convenes policy discussions bridging government, academia, and the private sector.

nodes.label: Ministry of Defense and Armed Forces Logistics

data.name_en: Ministry of Defense and Armed Forces Logistics
data.name_fa: وزارت دفاع و پشتیبانی نیروهای مسلح
data.description: Iran's Defense Ministry (Vezarat-e Defa va Poshtibani-ye Niruhaye Mosallah) manages defense procurement, arms production, and logistics for Iran's armed forces. It does not command operational forces; that function resides with the General Staff and IRGC command. Key ministers: [Ali Shamkhani](/people/ali-shamkhani) (1997–2005), [Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar](/people/mostafa-mohammad-najjar) (2005–2009), [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi) (2009–2013), [Hossein Dehghan](/people/hossein-dehghan) (2013–2017), Amir Hatami (2017–2021), [Mohammad Reza Ashtiani](/people/mohammad-reza-ashtiani) (2021–2024), [Aziz Nasirzadeh](/people/aziz-nasirzadeh) (2024–present).

nodes.label: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance

data.name_en: Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance
data.description: Iran's Ministry of Economic Affairs and Finance (Vezarat-e Omur-e Eqtesadi va Daraii) manages the state budget, tax policy, financial regulation, and oversees state-owned enterprises. Key ministers: Ali Tayyebnia (2013–2017), Masoud Karbasian (2017–2018), Farhad Dejpasand (2018–2021), Ehsan Khandouzi (2021–2024), [Abdolnaser Hemmati](/people/abdolnaser-hemmati) (2024, briefly).

Iran's Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Vezarat-e Omur-e Khareje) is the executive body responsible for conducting the country's foreign policy, managing diplomatic missions, and negotiating international agreements. In practice, strategic foreign policy is set by the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader) and coordinated through the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council), with the Ministry implementing day-to-day diplomacy. Foreign Ministers since 1981: [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati) (1981–1997), [Kamal Kharrazi](/people/kamal-kharrazi) (1997–2005), [Manouchehr Mottaki](/people/manouchehr-mottaki) (2005–2010), [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) (2010–2013), [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) (2013–2021), [Hossein Amir-Abdollahian](/people/hossein-amir-abdollahian) (2021–2024), [Abbas Araghchi](/people/abbas-araghchi) (2024–present).

The Ministry of Intelligence (Vezarat-e Ettelaat, VAJA / formerly MOIS) is Iran's primary civilian intelligence service, established in 1983 from several post-revolution intelligence bodies. The Minister of Intelligence must be a qualified Islamic jurist (mojtahed). The Supreme Leader has historically exercised direct influence over this appointment. Ministers: Mohammad Reyshahri (1984–1989), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) (1989–1997), [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) (1997–1999), Ali Younessi (1999–2005), [Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei) (2005–2009), [Heydar Moslehi](/people/heydar-moslehi) (2009–2013), [Mahmoud Alavi](/people/mahmoud-alavi) (2013–2021), [Esmaeil Khatib](/people/esmaeil-khatib) (2021–present).

Iran's Ministry of Interior (Vezarat-e Keshvar) oversees provincial governance, domestic security coordination, civil registration, and the administration of elections. As the agency that supervises and certifies election results, it wields enormous political power in conjunction with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council). Key ministers: Ali Akbar Natiq-Nouri (1981–1985), Mohammad Reyshahri briefly (1989), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian) briefly, Abdollah Nouri (1989–1993, 1997–1998), [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (2005–2008), [Sadegh Mahsouli](/people/sadegh-mahsouli) (2008–2009), Mostafa Mohammad-Najjar (2009–2013), Abdolreza Rahmani Fazli (2013–2017), [Ahmad Vahidi](/people/ahmad-vahidi) (2021–2024), [Eskandar Momeni](/people/eskandar-momeni) (2024–present).

Iran's Ministry of Justice (Vezarat-e Dadgostari) manages the administrative interface between the executive and judiciary. The minister does not head the Judiciary — the Chief Justice is separately appointed by the Supreme Leader — but administers courts, prisons, and judicial coordination. Key ministers: [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) (2013–2017, implicated in 1988 massacre as deputy minister), Alireza Avaei (2017–2021, sanctioned for executions), [Amin Hossein Rahimi](/people/amin-hossein-rahimi) (2021–2024), Amin Hossein Rahimi (2024–present, reappointed by Pezeshkian).

Iran's Ministry of Petroleum (Vezarat-e Naft) manages the National Iranian Oil Company (NIOC) and oversees all upstream and downstream oil, gas, and petrochemical activities. Oil revenue has historically funded the majority of the state budget. Key ministers: Gholam Reza Aghazadeh (1985–1997), [Bijan Namdar Zanganeh](/people/bijan-namdar-zanganeh) (1997–2005, 2013–2021), Kazem Vaziri Hamaneh (2005–2008), Gholamhossein Nozari (2008–2009), Masoud Mir-Kazemi (2009–2011), [Rostam Ghasemi](/people/rostam-ghasemi) (2011–2013), [Javad Owji](/people/javad-owji) (2021–2024), [Mohsen Paknejad](/people/mohsen-paknejad) (2024–present).

MintPress News is a US-based independent online outlet founded in 2011 by Mnar Muhawesh (now Adley) in Minneapolis, Minnesota. The site publishes commentary and reporting on US foreign policy, the Middle East, and geopolitics from an anti-establishment, anti-interventionist perspective. BuzzFeed News described its editorial stance in 2013 as aligning with the Islamic Republic of Iran — sympathetic to the Syrian and Iranian governments, hostile to Saudi Arabia, and critical of Western military intervention. The outlet has received NewsGuard's 'red' rating for failing to adhere to basic journalistic standards. MintPress was one of several English-language platforms through which pro-Iran narratives were laundered into Western alternative media. [Catherine Perez-Shakdam](/people/catherine-perez-shakdam) was a regular contributor between 2014 and 2017, writing under her married name Catherine Shakdam.

The Moderation and Development Party is an Iranian centrist political party associated with former President [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani). Founded in 1999, it advocates pragmatist economic and political reform within the framework of the Islamic Republic.

Department 312 is a directorate within Iran's Ministry of Intelligence (MOIS/VAJA) responsible for internal security and the running of MOIS external operations and agents in Europe. The unit gained international attention when senior officer [Assadollah Assadi](/people/assadollah-assadi) was identified as the operational planner of the 2018 Villepinte bomb plot against the NCRI/MEK rally in Paris — the first time an Iranian diplomat-intelligence officer was convicted in Europe for directing a terrorist attack on European soil. The directorate is believed to use Iranian diplomatic missions as cover for its officers abroad.

sources.title: Belgium sentences Iranian diplomat to 20 years for bomb plot

data.description: The Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK), also known as the People's Mojahedin Organization of Iran (PMOI), originated as a splinter group from the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran). It was co-founded in 1965 by [Mohammad Hanifnejad](/people/mohammad-hanifnejad), [Saeed Mohsen](/people/saeed-mohsen), and [Ali-Asghar Badizadegan](/people/ali-asghar-badizadegan), blending progressive Islam with anti-imperialist politics. After the three founders were executed by the Shah in 1972, [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) emerged as leader upon his release in 1979. The MEK participated in the revolution but clashed with [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s regime, organizing the [June 20 uprising](/events/june-20-uprising-1981) and carrying out the 1981 bombing that killed President [Mohammad-Ali Rajai](/people/mohammad-ali-rajai) and Prime Minister [Mohammad-Javad Bahonar](/people/mohammad-javad-bahonar). After a brutal crackdown, the MEK relocated to Iraq under Saddam Hussein's patronage and founded the [National Liberation Army](/orgs/national-liberation-army-of-iran), whose catastrophic [Operation Forough-e Javidan](/events/operation-forough-e-javidan-1988) in 1988 provided a pretext for the [mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988) of thousands of prisoners. The [NCRI](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran), led by [Maryam Rajavi](/people/maryam-rajavi), serves as its political umbrella. The MEK's intelligence network [revealed Iran's secret Natanz nuclear facility](/events/natanz-nuclear-revelation-2002) in 2002. Now based in Albania, it was removed from the US terrorist list in 2012.

nodes.label: Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization

data.name_en: Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization
data.description: The Mojahedin of the Islamic Revolution Organization (OMIIR) is a reformist party reconstituted in 1991 from the left wing of the original 1979 organisation of the same name. It is closely allied with the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front) within the reform movement. Key figures include [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi), [Mohsen Armin](/people/mohsen-armin), and [Mostafa Tajzadeh](/people/mostafa-tajzadeh). The party emphasised the republican dimensions of the Islamic Republic over its theocratic elements. Several members were arrested and imprisoned after the 2009 [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) protests.

Mosharekat (مشارکت, 'Participation') was the newspaper of the Islamic Iran Participation Front — the party of Mohammad Reza Khatami, brother of President Khatami. Closed without stated justification on 27 April 2000, simultaneously with [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), as part of the mass April 2000 crackdown following the reformists' parliamentary election landslide.

The Nation of Iran Party (Hezb-e Mellat-e Iran) is one of the oldest nationalist-democratic political parties in Iran, founded in 1951 during the oil nationalisation movement. Led for decades by [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar), it advocated for secular democracy, free elections, and the rule of law. Forouhar and his wife [Parvaneh Forouhar](/people/parvaneh-forouhar) were assassinated in their home on 22 November 1998 as part of the [chain murders](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). The party has been effectively banned since the early Islamic Republic but maintained an informal presence through the Forouhars' political salon.

The National Consultative Assembly (Majles-e Showrā-ye Mellī) was the unicameral parliament of Iran from 1906 until the Islamic Revolution in 1979. Established as part of the Persian Constitutional Revolution of 1906, it was the central legislative institution of Qajar and Pahlavi Iran, running for 24 sessions over its 73-year history. The assembly was the forum through which nationalist politicians such as [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh), [Hossein Makki](/people/hossein-makki), and [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi) advanced the oil nationalisation movement in the early 1950s and built the [National Front of Iran](/orgs/national-front-iran). It was dissolved in February 1979 during the Islamic Revolution and superseded by the [Islamic Consultative Assembly](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly), established in 1980 under a new constitution.

nodes.label: National Council of Resistance of Iran

data.name_en: National Council of Resistance of Iran
data.description: The NCRI was co-founded by [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) and President [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr) on 21 July 1981 as a broad coalition opposing the Islamic Republic. It functions as a parliament-in-exile comprising approximately 540 members, with the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) as its principal member organization. [Maryam Rajavi](/people/maryam-rajavi) has served as its President-elect since 1993. Banisadr left the coalition in 1984 over disagreements with Rajavi's leadership. The NCRI is headquartered at Auvers-sur-Oise near Paris and advocates for the overthrow of the Islamic Republic and establishment of a secular democratic government.

Coalition of nationalist political parties founded by [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh) in 1949. Led the oil nationalization movement and supported [Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh)'s government (1951–1953). Reconstituted as the Second National Front (1960) and Third National Front (1964). The [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) split from it in 1961. Members including [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi) served in the post-revolution provisional government, but the organization was marginalized by the Islamic Republic.

The National Liberation Army of Iran (NLA) was the armed wing of the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq), founded in 1987 in Iraq with Saddam Hussein's support. It fielded approximately 7,000 fighters equipped with Iraqi-supplied weapons and armored vehicles. Its most significant operation was [Operation Forough-e Javidan](/events/operation-forough-e-javidan-1988) on 26 July 1988, a failed offensive into western Iran that was repelled by Iranian forces with devastating losses. The NLA was effectively disbanded after the 2003 US invasion of Iraq when its members were disarmed by American forces.

The National Trust Party (Hezb-e E'temad-e Melli) was founded in 2005 by [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi) after he came third in the first round of the presidential election that year. It positioned itself as a reformist but pragmatic party advocating social freedoms, economic reform, and political pluralism. Karroubi ran for president again under the party's banner in 2009. After the disputed election result and the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), the party's leadership was suppressed and Karroubi himself was placed under house arrest.

US military graduate school located in Monterey, California, providing advanced education and research to the United States Navy and other services. Institutional home of [Afshon Ostovar](/people/afshon-ostovar), an associate professor specializing in Iranian security, the IRGC, and Middle East military affairs.

Neshat (نشاط, 'Joy/Happiness') was the third iteration of [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin)'s serial chain, built on the dormant *Akhbar* license. Staff from [Tous](/orgs/tous-newspaper) and [Jame'eh](/orgs/jameeh-newspaper) formed the core. In September 1999 Press Court Judge [Saeed Mortazavi](/people/saeed-mortazavi) closed it after it published an open letter urging Khamenei to distance himself from hardliners, and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi)'s article criticising capital punishment. Shamsolvaezin was sentenced to 30 months — but founded [Asr-e Azadegan](/orgs/asr-e-azadegan-newspaper) before he could be imprisoned.

American newspaper of record based in New York City. Former employer of [Nazila Fathi](/people/nazila-fathi), who served as the Times' Tehran bureau correspondent from 1999 until the deteriorating security environment forced her to leave Iran in 2009.

NIAC Action is the 501(c)(4) lobbying arm of the [National Iranian American Council (NIAC)](/orgs/niac), created in June 2015 to engage in direct political lobbying during the push for the [JCPOA nuclear deal](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). Led by [Jamal Abdi](/people/jamal-abdi), it conducts grassroots advocacy, maintains local chapters, and engages political candidates on Iran-related policy. Its creation formalized lobbying activities that NIAC had already been conducting through its 501(c)(3) — activities that were the subject of the Hassan Dai lawsuit controversy.

The National Iranian American Council (NIAC) is a Washington D.C.-based 501(c)(3) nonprofit founded in 2002 by [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi).
