It describes itself as promoting the civic participation of Iranian Americans and diplomacy between the U.S. and Iran. Critics — including the Hoover Institution, members of Congress, and Iranian diaspora activists — allege it functions as a de facto lobby for the Iranian regime. During a 2008 defamation lawsuit filed by NIAC against journalist Hassan Dai, discovery revealed extensive email communications between Parsi and [Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) (then Iran's UN Ambassador), including arranging meetings between Zarif and U.S. Congress members. In August 2013, Zarif told the Iranian parliament that Parsi and NIAC had been his 'main partners' in a campaign to exploit American political divisions on Iran policy. Internal NIAC documents showed employees referring to their work as 'lobbying,' and an employee was found to have altered 130 files changing 'lobbying' to 'legislative direct.' The case was dismissed in 2012, and NIAC was sanctioned $183,480 for discovery abuses in 2015. In 2020, Senators Cotton, Braun, and Cruz wrote to the Attorney General urging a FARA investigation of NIAC. NIAC created a 501(c)(4) sister organization, [NIAC Action](/orgs/niac-action), in 2015 to lobby for the [JCPOA nuclear deal](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015). After leaving NIAC, Parsi co-founded the [Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft](/orgs/quincy-institute).

Norooz (نوروز, 'New Year') was a reformist daily of the Executives of Construction Party (Hezb-e Kargozaran-e Sazandegi), a technocratic reformist party linked to [Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani). Closed 2003.

nodes.label: Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute

data.name_en: Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute
data.description: The Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute (NSTRI) is the principal research arm of the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran), responsible for applied nuclear research, isotope production, and development of reactor and radiation technologies. [Amir Hossein Faghihi](/people/amir-hossein-faghihi), a former AEOI vice president, served as its head before being killed in Israeli airstrikes on 13 June 2025.

sources.title: Nuclear Science and Technology Research Institute

data.description: The Office of Prime Minister was the head of government position in the Islamic Republic from 1979 to 1989. Under the 1979 constitution, executive power was divided between the president and the prime minister. The role was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, which merged its powers into the presidency. [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) was the last holder of the office.

The Presidency of the Islamic Republic of Iran is the second-highest office in the political system, below the Supreme Leader. The president is elected by popular vote for four-year terms, with a two-term consecutive limit. The president chairs the cabinet and is responsible for implementing the constitution. The post of prime minister was abolished in the 1989 constitutional revision, merging its powers into the presidency.

The Office of the Supreme Leader (Daftar-e Maqam-e Mo'azzam-e Rahbari) is the institutional apparatus of the Supreme Leader of Iran. The Supreme Leader holds ultimate authority over all branches of government, the armed forces, the judiciary, and state media under the doctrine of Velayat-e Faqih. The office encompasses numerous subsidiary bodies including the Supreme Leader's Inspection Office, his representatives in the provinces, and his representatives to the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and other military branches.

nodes.label: Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas

data.name_en: Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas
data.description: The Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas (OIPFG), commonly known as Fadaiyan-e Khalq, was a Marxist-Leninist guerrilla organization founded in 1971 from the merger of two underground communist groups: the Jazani-Zarifi group led by [Bijan Jazani](/people/bijan-jazani), and the Ahmadzadeh-Pouyan group. The organization's founding is marked by the attack on the Siahkal gendarmerie post in February 1971, an event celebrated by Iranian dissidents as a catalyst for armed resistance against the Shah's regime. Inspired by Che Guevara, Mao Zedong, and the theory of armed propaganda, the Fadaiyan waged guerrilla warfare against the Pahlavi monarchy throughout the 1970s, suffering heavy losses to [SAVAK](/orgs/savak). After the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), the organization participated in political life but faced internal crises that led to its split in 1980 into the [Fadaiyan-e Khalq (Majority)](/orgs/fadaiyan-e-khalq-majority) and [Fadaiyan-e Khalq (Minority)](/orgs/fadaiyan-e-khalq-minority) factions.

data.name_fa: سازمان پیکار در راه آزادی طبقه کارگر
data.description: The Peykar Organization (Organization of Struggle for the Emancipation of the Working Class), also known as the Marxist Mojahedin, was a Maoist splinter group from the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq). The ideological split originated in 1975 when a faction within the MEK, led by Taghi Shahram, declared that they had abandoned Islam in favour of Marxism-Leninism. This schism triggered violent internal conflict, including the murder of MEK members who refused to convert. The Marxist faction formally renamed itself Peykar in 1978 shortly before the [revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Considered the most radical of Iran's communist groups, Peykar was rapidly suppressed by the Islamic Republic through mass arrests and executions in the early 1980s, and ceased to exist by 1982.

The Phoenix Project of Iran is a think tank launched by [Reza Pahlavi](/people/reza-pahlavi) in Washington, D.C. in February 2019. It focuses on post-regime transition planning for Iran, developing policy frameworks and governance proposals for a future secular democratic Iranian state.

The project has produced an Emergency Phase Booklet covering the first six months of a post-Islamic Republic transition, as well as the Iran Prosperity Project for long-term economic planning.

PJAK (Partiya Jiyana Azad a Kurdistanê) is an Iranian Kurdish militant and political organisation founded in 2004, operating primarily from the Qandil Mountains along the Iran-Iraq border. Ideologically and organisationally linked to the Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK), PJAK follows the democratic confederalism philosophy of Abdullah Öcalan. The group has conducted armed operations against [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) forces in the border regions of Kurdistan and Kermanshah provinces. Iran and Turkey have designated it a terrorist organisation. PJAK advocates for Kurdish self-governance within Iran through a decentralised, non-state model.

The Kurdistan Workers' Party (PKK; Kurdish: Partiya Karkerên Kurdistanê) is a Kurdish militant and political organisation founded in 1978 by Abdullah Öcalan in Turkey. It launched an armed insurgency against the Turkish state in 1984, seeking initially an independent Kurdish state and later evolving toward a demand for Kurdish autonomy and democratic confederalism. The PKK is designated a terrorist organisation by Turkey, the United States, the European Union, and several other states. Based primarily in the Qandil Mountains along the Iraq-Iran border, the PKK serves as the umbrella organisation for affiliated groups in neighbouring countries, including [PJAK](/orgs/pjak) in Iran. Its ideology — democratic confederalism — was developed by Öcalan while imprisoned in Turkey and draws on the political philosophy of Murray Bookchin.

PLASCO (Plāscokār) was [Habibollah Elghanian](/people/habibollah-elghanian)'s flagship industrial conglomerate and the foundation of his business empire. Founded in 1948, it began as Iran's first major plastics manufacturer, operating 270 machines processing 50–60 tons of PVC daily by the mid-1960s and growing into the largest plastics enterprise in the Middle East. The conglomerate diversified into aluminium production, PVC pipes (Louleh Shomal), refrigerators (General Steel), and mining, employing approximately 20,000 workers across all ventures at its peak.

Elghanian also constructed the iconic 17-storey Plasco Building in central Tehran in 1962 — Iran's first private high-rise skyscraper — which housed a shopping mall, fashion industry tenants, and commercial offices. After Elghanian's arrest and execution following the [1979 Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), all PLASCO assets were confiscated by the state. The Plasco Building, heavily associated with the Elghanian name and pre-revolutionary Tehran, became a cultural landmark before collapsing in a catastrophic fire in January 2017, killing 16 firefighters.

The Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF, or Hashd al-Sha'abi) is an Iraqi state-sponsored umbrella organisation of predominantly Shia paramilitary groups, many of which receive training, funding, and direction from Iran's [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force). Its deputy commander Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis was killed alongside [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani) in the January 2020 US drone strike at Baghdad International Airport.

Press TV is [IRIB](/orgs/irib)'s English-language 24-hour news channel, launched in 2007 to project Iran's perspective to international audiences. Under its former head [Mohammad Sarafraz](/people/mohammad-sarafraz), the channel broadcast forced confessions of political prisoners including Newsweek journalist Maziar Bahari, who was detained for 118 days in 2009 and coerced into a televised confession filmed inside Evin Prison. In January 2012 the UK media regulator Ofcom revoked Press TV's broadcasting licence for airing Bahari's confession without his consent. The channel was subsequently dropped from European satellite providers under EU sanctions. Press TV's website domain was among Iranian media domains seized by the US Department of Justice in 2021. The channel continues to broadcast via the internet and non-Western satellites.

The Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft is a Washington D.C.-based think tank co-founded in 2019 by [Trita Parsi](/people/trita-parsi) and Andrew Bacevich, advocating diplomatic engagement and military restraint in U.S. foreign policy. Parsi serves as Executive Vice President. The institute received initial funding from both the Charles Koch Foundation and George Soros's Open Society Foundations. Former [NIAC](/orgs/niac) staff, including Adam Weinstein, have moved to the Quincy Institute. Critics view it as a continuation of Parsi's advocacy for U.S.-Iran engagement under a broader anti-interventionist umbrella.

Radio Farda (Radio Tomorrow) is a Persian-language radio station operated by [Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty](/orgs/radio-free-europe-radio-liberty) (RFE/RL), headquartered in Prague. Launched in 2002, it is funded by the US Congress through the [US Agency for Global Media](/orgs/us-agency-for-global-media) with an annual budget of approximately $7–8 million. Radio Farda broadcasts 24/7 radio programming and maintains a significant online presence, covering Iranian politics, human rights, and cultural affairs. The Iranian regime considers Radio Farda hostile and routinely jams its broadcasts.

Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty (RFE/RL) is a US-funded international broadcaster headquartered in Prague, Czech Republic. Established during the Cold War to provide free press to countries behind the Iron Curtain, RFE/RL today broadcasts in 27 languages across Central Asia, Eastern Europe, the Caucasus, and the Middle East. It is funded by the [US Agency for Global Media](/orgs/us-agency-for-global-media) and operates as a private non-profit. RFE/RL's Persian-language service, [Radio Farda](/orgs/radio-farda), is one of the most prominent diaspora media outlets targeting audiences inside Iran.

Radio Zamaneh is a Persian-language online media outlet founded in 2006 in Amsterdam by journalist [Mehdi Jami](/people/mehdi-jami). Initially funded by the Dutch Foreign Ministry through its MATRA program for democratic transitions, Radio Zamaneh focuses on civil society, human rights, women's issues, and minority rights in Iran. The outlet faced significant funding cuts around 2013–2014 when Dutch government support was reduced, but continues to operate online with diminished capacity.

nodes.label: Organization of Revolutionary Workers of Iran (Rahe Kargar)

data.name_en: Organization of Revolutionary Workers of Iran (Rahe Kargar)
data.description: Rahe Kargar (Worker's Path) was formed during the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979) as a Marxist political organization with close ties to the [Fadaiyan-e Khalq](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas) movement. The organization distinguished itself from the pro-Soviet [Tudeh Party](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) by maintaining an independent socialist line critical of both superpowers. Rahe Kargar published a newspaper of the same name and was active in labour organizing and political education. Like other leftist groups, it was suppressed in the early 1980s crackdown. Many members were executed in the [1988 mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988). The organization continues in exile.

Raja News is a hardline online news outlet linked to principlist political circles, particularly those around [Saeed Jalili](/people/saeed-jalili)'s faction. Founded around 2008, it serves as a platform for ultra-conservative viewpoints and has been used to attack reformist and pragmatist politicians.

Resalat (رسالت, 'Mission') was founded in 1985 as the organ of traditional conservative Islamic politics, closely tied to the Tehran Bazaar and the Islamic Coalition Party (Hezb-e Mo'talefeh). Alongside [Kayhan](/orgs/kayhan-newspaper), it is one of the two leading hardline-conservative dailies but is editorially independent of direct Supreme Leader control.

Rooz Online was an Iranian reformist-to-opposition news website founded in 2004 by a collective of Iranian diaspora journalists and former reform-era editors. Operating from outside Iran, it published political analysis, human rights reporting, and news targeting the reformist and opposition readership. Rooz Online was one of the pioneering diaspora digital outlets of the mid-2000s, providing a platform for Iranian journalists who could not work freely inside the country. The outlet became largely inactive after roughly 2012–2014 as funding dried up and staff dispersed, but it remains historically significant as an early model of diaspora opposition media.

The Royal United Services Institute (RUSI) is the world's oldest independent think tank on international defence and security. Based in London, it conducts research on defence, security, and foreign policy, including analysis of Iran's military capabilities, IRGC operations, sanctions enforcement, and regional security dynamics. [Ali M. Ansari](/people/ali-m-ansari) serves as a Senior Associate Fellow.

Russian state broadcaster funded by the Kremlin and operating internationally in English, Arabic, Spanish, and other languages. RT regularly platforms Iranian regime-aligned commentators such as [Mohammad Marandi](/people/mohammad-marandi) as purportedly independent analysts, amplifying narratives favorable to both Moscow and Tehran. The outlet has been designated a foreign agent in multiple Western jurisdictions.

Salam (سلام, 'Hello/Peace') was a highly influential reformist daily published from 9 February 1991 to 7 July 1999. Its name was given by Ahmad Khomeini, son of the Supreme Leader. Publisher was reformist cleric Mohammad Mousavi Khoeiniha; editor-in-chief was [Abbas Abdi](/people/abbas-abdi). Salam built reformist momentum ahead of Khatami's 1997 election. Its closure on 7 July 1999 — after publishing a leaked MOIS document about plans to restrict the press — directly ignited the [July 1999 student uprising](/events/iran-student-uprising-july-1999), the largest protests in Iran since 1979. At least four students were killed when security forces attacked Tehran University dormitories.

SAVAK (Sāzemān-e Ettelā'āt va Amniyat-e Keshvar — Organization of Intelligence and National Security) was the secret police, domestic security, and intelligence service of Iran under [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi). Established in 1957 with CIA and Mossad assistance, it suppressed political opposition including the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran), the [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran), leftist organizations, and Islamist groups. Known for systematic use of torture, it imprisoned thousands of political dissidents. Dissolved after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979).

SAVAMA (Sāzemān-e Ettelā'āt va Amniyat-e Mellat-e Iran — Organization of Intelligence and National Security of the Iranian Nation) was the post-revolution intelligence and security service that replaced [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) in 1979. Initially headed by Hosein Fardust, a childhood friend of Mohammad Reza Shah who defected to the Islamic Republic, SAVAMA retained much of SAVAK's personnel and institutional knowledge. It carried out the systematic surveillance, arrest, and interrogation of political opponents in the early years of the Islamic Republic, most notably orchestrating the mass arrests of the [Tudeh Party](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) leadership in 1983. SAVAMA was superseded by the [Ministry of Intelligence (MOIS)](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) when that body was formally established in 1984.

Semafor is a global news platform launched in October 2022 by former BuzzFeed News editor-in-chief Ben Smith and former Bloomberg Media CEO Justin B. Smith. In September 2023, Semafor journalist Jay Solomon published a landmark investigation, jointly with [Iran International](/orgs/iran-international), that revealed the [Iran Experts Initiative](/events/iei-email-leak-2023) — a covert Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) influence operation in which Western-based academics were recruited to promote Tehran's nuclear negotiating positions in Western media and policy circles.

nodes.label: Shafaqna Institute for Middle East Studies

data.name_en: Shafaqna Institute for Middle East Studies
data.description: The Shafaqna Institute for Middle East Studies is a UK-based think tank affiliated with the Shafaqna media network, which takes a Shia-oriented and broadly Tehran-sympathetic editorial position on Middle Eastern affairs. The institute produced analysis on the Yemeni civil war, Gulf politics, and Iranian foreign policy, often framing events in alignment with Iranian state narratives. It provided an institutional platform for analysts publishing in pro-Iran media ecosystems during the 2010s.

Shahid Beheshti University (SBU) is a major state research university in Tehran, Iran. Founded in 1959 as the National University of Iran, it was renamed after [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti) following the 1979 revolution. It hosts a prominent nuclear engineering faculty whose staff were disproportionately affected by Israeli airstrikes in June 2025: [Abdolhamid Minouchehr](/people/abdolhamid-minouchehr), [Amir Hossein Faghihi](/people/amir-hossein-faghihi), and [Ahmad Reza Zolfaghari Daryani](/people/ahmad-reza-zolfaghari-daryani) were all killed on 13 June 2025.

Shargh (شرق, 'East') is the most durable reformist daily of the post-Khatami era, founded in 2003. Suspended multiple times — most notably in September 2006 simultaneously with the lifting of the *Iran* newspaper ban, and after the [2009 Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) — yet consistently resumed. Journalists Niloofar Hamedi and Elaheh Mohammadi of Shargh broke the Mahsa Amini story in September 2022 and were imprisoned 17 months for it.

Sobh-e Emroz (صبح امروز, 'This Morning') was founded by [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian), Khatami's chief political strategist and a former deputy Minister of Intelligence. It was the investigative flagship of the reformist press, publishing [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) and [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi)'s landmark series on the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998) using code names ('Excellency Red Garmented' for Rafsanjani, 'Master Key' for [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian)). This reporting forced the government to admit Ministry of Intelligence 'rogue elements' were responsible. On 12 March 2000, Hajjarian was shot in the face outside Tehran City Council by a Basij gunman, leaving him permanently disabled. Sobh-e Emroz was among the 16 papers closed in the 24 April 2000 crackdown.

nodes.label: Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom

data.name_en: Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom
data.description: The Society of Seminary Teachers of Qom (Jame'eh-ye Modarresin-e Howzeh-ye Elmiyyeh-ye Qom) is an influential association of Shia clerics based in the Qom seminary. Founded in 1961, it became a key vehicle for [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s political movement and played a central role in the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). The society's members dominated the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) in 1979 and have maintained significant influence over the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) and the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts). It is considered a conservative-to-hardline clerical body closely aligned with the ruling establishment.

Soura University (دانشگاه سوره) is an arts and communications university in Tehran affiliated with the [Islamic Propaganda Organization](/orgs/islamic-propaganda-organization) (Sazman-e Tabligh-e Eslami), which operates under the Supreme Leader. Founded in 1992, the university focuses on fine arts, cinema, theatre, graphic design, and communications — disciplines through which the regime cultivates culturally and ideologically aligned creative talent. [Mohammad-Ali Zam](/people/mohammad-ali-zam), the reformist cultural official and father of executed journalist Ruhollah Zam, served as the university's president, reflecting the institution's position at the intersection of regime cultural policy and reformist-conservative tensions.

nodes.label: Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research (SPND)

data.name_en: Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research (SPND)
data.description: The Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research (Sazman-e Pazhuhesh-haye Defai-ye Novin, SPND) is a secretive Iranian defence research body established circa 2011 under the [Ministry of Defense](/orgs/ministry-of-defense). It is widely assessed by Western intelligence services and the IAEA as the successor to the Amad Project — the covert nuclear weapons programme led by [Mohsen Fakhrizadeh](/people/mohsen-fakhrizadeh) until its nominal 'dissolution' in 2003. SPND absorbed key personnel and continued research on weaponisation, high-explosives diagnostics, and delivery system integration. The IAEA has sought access to SPND facilities since 2012. The organisation was headed by [Reza Mozaffari Nia](/people/reza-mozaffari-nia) and later [Hossein Jabal Amelian](/people/hossein-jabal-amelian), both of whom were killed in Israeli airstrikes on 28 February 2026.

sources.title: Organisation of Defensive Innovation and Research — Wikipedia
IAEA Director General's Report on Iran
Organization of Defensive Innovation and Research (SPND)
sources.publisher: Wisconsin Project on Nuclear Arms Control

data.description: State University of New York at Stony Brook, a flagship public research university on Long Island. Home to [Said Amir Arjomand](/people/said-amir-arjomand), SUNY Distinguished Service Professor of Sociology, whose comparative-historical sociology of Shia Islam and the Islamic Republic is foundational to the field.

nodes.label: Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution

data.name_en: Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution
data.description: The Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution is an Iranian state body created in December 1984 to succeed the original seven-member Cultural Revolution Committee (also known as the Cultural Revolution Headquarters) that [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) established in June 1980. The original committee was charged with closing universities, purging them of Western and secular influences, and reshaping academic curricula along Islamic lines during the [Iranian Cultural Revolution](/events/iranian-cultural-revolution-1980). The council continues to set cultural and educational policy for the Islamic Republic, including oversight of universities, regulation of the internet, and media policy.

The Supreme Leader's Military Office (دفتر نظامی رهبری, Daftar-e Nezami-ye Rahbari), also referred to as the military section of the beit-e rahbari (Leader's Household), serves as [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei)'s direct military staff. It coordinates military directives from the Supreme Leader to the [General Staff of the Armed Forces](/orgs/general-staff-of-the-armed-forces), the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), and [Artesh](/orgs/artesh), and functions as the principal interface between the Supreme Leader and operational military command. The office was headed by Brigadier General [Mohammad Shirazi](/people/mohammad-shirazi-military) at the time of his death, with [Akbar Ebrahimzadeh](/people/akbar-ebrahimzadeh) as deputy. Both were killed in the [February 28, 2026 decapitation strikes](/events/israeli-strikes-iran-feb-2026), effectively destroying the Supreme Leader's personal military command apparatus.

sources.title: Office of the Supreme Leader (Iran) — Wikipedia

data.description: The Supreme National Security Council (Shora-ye Ali-ye Amniat-e Melli, SNSC) was established by the 1989 constitutional revision to coordinate Iran's defence, intelligence, and foreign policy. The President chairs it; the Secretary — appointed with the Supreme Leader's approval — is effectively Iran's national security adviser and a direct representative of the [Office of the Supreme Leader](/orgs/office-of-the-supreme-leader). The SNSC has also been Iran's primary nuclear negotiating body. Key secretaries include [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) (1989–2005), [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) (2005–2007), and Saeed Jalili (2007–2013).

Tabnak is an online news outlet founded in 2007 by [Mohsen Rezaei](/people/mohsen-rezaei), the former IRGC commander and secretary of the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council). While conservative, Tabnak has occasionally taken independent editorial positions, reflecting Rezaei's pragmatist-conservative political positioning that sometimes diverges from the hardline mainstream.

Tarbiat Modares University (TMU, Daneshgah-e Tarbiat Modares) is a graduate-only public research university in Tehran, established in 1982 to train graduate students and faculty. It is one of Iran's leading research institutions and has produced a significant number of senior military and governmental figures. [Gholam Ali Rashid](/people/gholam-ali-rashid), IRGC senior commander, earned his doctorate in political geography from TMU.

Tasnim News Agency is a hardline news agency closely affiliated with the [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), launched in 2012 as a complement to [Fars News Agency](/orgs/fars-news-agency). Together with Fars and [Mashregh News](/orgs/mashregh-news), Tasnim forms an IRGC-aligned media triangle used for regime messaging, discrediting opposition figures, and publishing intelligence-style content. The agency produces multilingual output and has been sanctioned by the United States.

The Toilers' Party of Iran (Hezb-e Zahmatkeshan-e Mellat-e Iran) was a nationalist political party founded and led by [Mozaffar Baghai](/people/mozaffar-baghai) in 1951 as a constituent member of the [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran). The party was strongly nationalist and anti-communist, drawing on urban lower-middle-class and bazaari support. It was initially a firm backer of Mossadegh's oil nationalisation campaign. However, Baghai broke with Mossadegh in 1953, and accusations later emerged that he had collaborated with the royalist forces backing the [1953 Iranian coup d'état](/events/iranian-coup-1953). The party dissolved after the coup. It should not be confused with the Komala Toilers Party (Zehmetkêshan), which is a separate Kurdish party of the same period.

The Toufan Organization (Organization of Toilers of Iran, Sazman-e Toufan) is Iran's principal Maoist political organization, distinct from the pro-Soviet [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) and from the guerrilla-oriented Fadaiyan currents. Founded in 1964 as a Maoist breakaway from the Tudeh Party, Toufan embraced the Sino-Soviet split and aligned with the People's Republic of China against Soviet revisionism. The organization participated in the pre-revolutionary opposition and remained active after the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), publishing theoretical journals from exile. Toufan has maintained a consistent Maoist ideological line and criticised both the Soviet-aligned left and the more popular worker-communist tendency associated with [Mansoor Hekmat](/people/mansoor-hekmat).

Tous (طوس) was the direct successor to [Jame'eh](/orgs/jameeh-newspaper), relaunched by [Mashallah Shamsolvaezin](/people/mashallah-shamsolvaezin) and [Hamid Reza Jalalipour](/people/hamid-reza-jalalipour) with the same staff under a new masthead and dormant license. Tous was also closed within months, and Shamsolvaezin immediately relaunched as [Neshat](/orgs/neshat-newspaper). The pattern — acquire a dormant license and restart with the same team — became the defining structural feature of Khatami-era reformist journalism.

The Tudeh Party of Iran (Party of the Masses of Iran) is the oldest communist party in Iran, founded on 29 September 1941 by a group of Marxist intellectuals released from prison after the Allied invasion of Iran. Under [Iraj Eskandari](/people/iraj-eskandari)'s early leadership, the party grew into Iran's largest political organization by the mid-1940s, with some 25,000 members and 400,000 affiliated trade unionists. The party was banned in 1949 after an assassination attempt on [Mohammad Reza Shah](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) and driven fully underground after the 1953 coup. Following the [1979 revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), the Tudeh initially supported [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s government under the leadership of [Nureddin Kianouri](/people/nureddin-kianouri), who advocated alliance with the Islamic Republic against imperialism. This strategy proved catastrophic: in 1983 the regime arrested the entire party leadership, tortured members into televised confessions, and executed an estimated 163 cadres. The party's chief ideologue [Ehsan Tabari](/people/ehsan-tabari) died in prison. Surviving leaders fled to Western Europe, where the party continues to operate in exile, publishing its newspaper Nameh-ye Mardom.

The Union of Iranian Communists, known as Sarbedaran (a reference to the medieval Sarbedaran revolt against Mongol rule), was a Maoist organization formed in 1976 from the alliance of several militant communist groups operating inside Iran. The organization is best known for its January 1982 armed uprising in Amol, a city in northern Iran, where Sarbedaran fighters attempted to seize control in an insurrection against the Islamic Republic. The uprising was quickly crushed by government forces and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) units, and most of the organization's members were killed or arrested and subsequently executed. The Sarbedaran's Amol uprising was the only significant armed communist insurrection attempted inside Iran after the revolution. In 2001, remnants reconstituted as the Communist Party of Iran (Marxist-Leninist-Maoist).

The University of Bradford is a public university in Bradford, West Yorkshire, England. Founded in 1966, it is known for its programmes in peace studies, political science, and social sciences. Several Iranian academics and political commentators have studied there.

Public research university in Tampa, Florida. Home to the Center for Strategic and Diplomatic Studies, where [Mohsen Milani](/people/mohsen-milani) has served as professor and executive director for decades, making USF a consistent voice in US academic analysis of Iranian foreign policy.

The University of Tehran is Iran's oldest and most prominent modern university. Founded in 1934, it played a central role in the [Iranian Cultural Revolution](/events/iranian-cultural-revolution-1980) of 1980–1983, during which it was closed along with all other Iranian universities and purged of secular and leftist faculty. It remains Iran's leading academic institution across the sciences, humanities, and social sciences.

The US Agency for Global Media (USAGM) is an independent agency of the United States federal government that funds and oversees US international broadcasting. It is the parent body of [Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty](/orgs/radio-free-europe-radio-liberty), the Voice of America (parent of [VOA Persian](/orgs/voa-persian)), and several other international outlets. USAGM's mandate is to promote democracy and freedom of the press globally through publicly funded journalism. Its Iran-focused services — VOA Persian and [Radio Farda](/orgs/radio-farda) — are major sources of Persian-language news for audiences inside Iran.

Vatan-e Emroz (وطن امروز, 'Today's Homeland') is a hardline daily in the IRGC-linked neo-radical media ecosystem, alongside the [Fars](/orgs/fars-news-agency) and [Tasnim](/orgs/tasnim-news-agency) news agencies and the [Mashregh](/orgs/mashregh-news) and [Raja](/orgs/raja-news) news websites. It was a strong Ahmadinejad supporter.

Voice of America Persian (Sedaye America) is the Persian-language service of Voice of America, the US government's international broadcaster. Broadcasting radio since 1942 and later expanding to television and digital platforms, VOA Persian is funded by the US Congress through the [US Agency for Global Media](/orgs/us-agency-for-global-media) (USAGM). While governed by a statutory editorial firewall, it is perceived inside Iran as US state media. VOA Persian covers Iranian politics, human rights, and US-Iran relations, and has been a target of regime jamming and content blocking.

Voice of America (VOA) is the official external broadcasting service of the United States government, funded and supervised by the [US Agency for Global Media (USAGM)](/orgs/us-agency-for-global-media). Founded in 1942 to counter Axis propaganda during World War II, VOA broadcasts in over 40 languages worldwide. In the Iranian context, VOA's Persian-language service — [VOA Persian](/orgs/voa-persian) — is one of the most-watched diaspora media outlets, reaching millions of Iranians via satellite television, radio, and digital platforms. VOA has played a significant role in providing uncensored news to audiences inside Iran, particularly during protest periods such as 2009 (Green Movement) and 2022 (Woman Life Freedom).

Volant Media is a UK-registered company that was established in 2017 to found and operate [Iran International](/orgs/iran-international), the Persian-language television news network. The ownership and funding of Volant Media has been a subject of investigative reporting; multiple outlets have alleged links to Saudi Arabian government funding, connecting the channel to Riyadh's broader media strategy targeting the Islamic Republic. The Islamic Republic has used these alleged Saudi ties to accuse Iran International of being a foreign-operated influence operation. Volant Media's principal beneficiary has been reported as Goli Ameri, though the full ownership structure has not been publicly disclosed.

nodes.label: Washington Institute for Near East Policy

data.name_en: Washington Institute for Near East Policy
data.description: Washington think tank focused on US policy in the Middle East. Founded in 1985, it hosts scholars and former officials who produce analysis and policy recommendations on Iran, Israel, Arab states, and counterterrorism. Its Iran work includes the Libitzky Family Fellowship, held by [Holly Dagres](/people/holly-dagres), and technical analysis on the IRGC and Iran's missile programs by [Farzin Nadimi](/people/farzin-nadimi).

sources.title: About WINEP
sources.publisher: Washington Institute for Near East Policy

data.description: The Washington Post is a major American daily newspaper based in Washington D.C., founded in 1877. It is one of the most widely read English-language newspapers in the United States, covering U.S. politics, international affairs, and national security. Its journalists have faced detention and harassment in Iran; [Jason Rezaian](/people/jason-rezaian) served as the Post's Tehran bureau chief before his arrest by Iranian authorities in 2014.

We Believe in Israel is a British advocacy organization that campaigns for Israel within the UK political landscape. It engages with parliamentary lobbying, grassroots mobilization, and public communications to counter hostile narratives about Israel in British media and politics. The organization has been led by analysts with backgrounds in Middle Eastern affairs.

The Worker-communist Party of Iran (WPI) was founded in 1991 by [Mansoor Hekmat](/people/mansoor-hekmat) and other members who split from the [Communist Party of Iran](/orgs/communist-party-of-iran). The party rejects both the Soviet model and Third World nationalism, advocating instead for a form of Marxism centred on working-class self-emancipation and secularism. Its primary slogans are 'Liberty, Equality, Workers' Rule' and 'For a Socialist Republic.' Hekmat led the party until his death in 2002, after which a leadership dispute split the WPI into two factions: one under Hamid Taqvaee and another under Koorosh Modarresi, who founded a separate Worker-communist Party (Hekmatist). The WPI has been active in international solidarity campaigns, particularly around women's rights, and opposed the Islamic Republic from exile.

Workers' House (Khaneh Kargar) is Iran's largest state-affiliated labour institution, originally established in 1979 after the revolution to organise the working class under Islamic Republic structures. Over time it developed a reformist orientation, becoming a bridge between organised labour and the reformist political movement in the 1990s and 2000s. Workers' House is the parent organisation of the [Iranian Labour News Agency (ILNA)](/orgs/ilna), which it founded in 2003 to provide independent coverage of labour issues. During the Khatami era, Workers' House aligned with the reformist camp and supported workers' right to organise, placing it in periodic conflict with hardline factions and the Revolutionary Guards' economic interests. It has faced crackdowns and leadership changes as the political winds have shifted.

Zan (زن, 'Woman') was a reformist daily founded by Faezeh Hashemi Rafsanjani, daughter of [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) and a reformist MP. Closed by Islamic Revolutionary Court in April 1999 for publishing a cartoon satirising blood money laws (which valued a woman's life at half a man's), and for reprinting a New Year's greeting from Empress Farah Pahlavi. Demonstrated that even publications with powerful family connections were not immune.

data.name: Albania Expulsions of Iranian Diplomats
data.description: Between 2018 and 2022, Albania expelled multiple Iranian diplomats in a series of escalating actions. The first expulsion came in December 2018 when Ambassador [Gholamhossein Mohammadnia](/people/gholamhossein-mohammadnia) — a senior MOIS deputy — was declared persona non grata for activities 'damaging to national security', including an alleged plot against members of the Mojahedin-e Khalq ([MEK](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq)) based at Camp Ashraf-3 near Manez. Further diplomats were expelled in 2020. The crisis culminated in September 2022 when the Albanian government severed diplomatic relations entirely with Iran and ordered the closure of the Iranian embassy, in connection with a cyberattack on Albanian government infrastructure attributed to Iranian state actors.

sources.title: Albania Expels Iranian Ambassador Over 'National Security' Concerns

data.name: Algiers Accords
data.description: The Algiers Accords were agreements between Iran and the United States, mediated by Algeria, that resolved the Iran hostage crisis and secured the release of 52 American embassy staff held in Tehran for 444 days. [Behzad Nabavi](/people/behzad-nabavi) served as Iran's chief negotiator. The accords were signed on 19 January 1981, and the hostages were released on 20 January, minutes after Ronald Reagan's inauguration as US president.

data.name: Allied Invasion of Iran (1941)
data.description: On 25 August 1941, British and Soviet forces jointly invaded and occupied Iran in Operation Countenance. The stated purpose was to secure the Trans-Iranian Railway as a supply route to the Soviet Union and to remove German influence from Iran. The invasion forced the abdication of Reza Shah Pahlavi, who was exiled to South Africa, and placed his 21-year-old son [Mohammad Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/mohammad-reza-shah-pahlavi) on the throne. The political opening created by the collapse of Reza Shah's authoritarian rule allowed political prisoners to be released and political parties to reorganise. Most significantly, survivors of the Group of Fifty-Three (the proto-Tudeh network arrested in 1937) were freed and founded the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) in September 1941.

data.name: AMIA Bombing
data.description: On 18 July 1994, a car bomb destroyed the Asociación Mutual Israelita Argentina (AMIA), a Jewish community centre in Buenos Aires, killing 85 people and injuring hundreds more. It remains the deadliest terrorist attack in Argentine history and the deadliest attack on a Jewish target since World War II. Argentine prosecutors, led by investigator [Alberto Nisman](/people/alberto-nisman), concluded that the bombing was ordered by the Iranian government and carried out by [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) operatives acting on Iranian direction. The Iranian intelligence network in Buenos Aires — operating through the [Islamic Cultural Institute of Buenos Aires](/orgs/islamic-cultural-institute-buenos-aires) — provided target intelligence and logistics. Iranian suspects identified by Argentine prosecutors include cultural attaché [Mohsen Rabbani](/people/mohsen-rabbani), Ambassador [Hadi Soleimanpour](/people/hadi-soleimanpour), and embassy third secretary [Ahmad Reza Asghari](/people/ahmad-reza-asghari). [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian), then Minister of Intelligence, was also named. Argentina issued international arrest warrants and Interpol red notices for the Iranian suspects. In 2024, an Argentine court concluded a trial-in-absentia proceeding against several Iranian defendants.

sources.title: Blast Hits Jewish Center in Argentina, Killing at Least 48

data.name: Amol Uprising (1982)
data.description: The Amol uprising of January–February 1982 was an armed insurrection launched by the [Union of Iranian Communists (Sarbedaran)](/orgs/union-of-iranian-communists-sarbedaran) against the Islamic Republic — the only significant armed communist insurrection attempted inside Iran after the 1979 revolution. Taking inspiration from their namesake medieval revolt, Sarbedaran fighters descended from bases in the forests of the Alborz mountains on Amol, a city in Mazandaran province in northern Iran, on 25 January 1982. The fighters — numbering in the hundreds — briefly occupied parts of the city, seized the police headquarters, and called on the population to rise against the Islamic Republic. The uprising was swiftly and brutally crushed by [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) forces and Basij militias who responded in strength within days. The majority of the Sarbedaran fighters were killed in combat or captured; those captured were subsequently executed. The organisation was effectively destroyed as an armed force. The failed uprising demonstrated the revolutionary left's inability to mount sustained armed resistance to the Islamic Republic, which by 1982 had consolidated its hold on state power and defeated or suppressed all armed opposition groups.

nodes.label: Arbabsiar Plot to Assassinate Saudi Ambassador (2011)

data.name: Arbabsiar Plot to Assassinate Saudi Ambassador (2011)
data.description: In October 2011, the US Department of Justice announced the indictment of [Manssor Arbabsiar](/people/manssor-arbabsiar) and [Gholam Shakuri](/people/gholam-shakuri) for an [IRGC-Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force)-directed plot to assassinate Saudi Ambassador Adel Al-Jubeir in Washington DC. The scheme involved hiring an alleged Mexican drug cartel hitman — in reality a DEA informant — to carry out the killing on US soil, potentially at a Washington restaurant. Shakuri, a Tehran-based IRGC-QF officer, directed Arbabsiar — his Iranian-American cousin with no intelligence background — and wired $100,000 as a down-payment. The FBI disrupted the plot and arrested Arbabsiar on October 11, 2011. Shakuri was indicted in absentia and remains at large in Iran. Arbabsiar pleaded guilty in 2012 and was sentenced to 25 years in federal prison. The plot is a landmark case demonstrating IRGC-QF willingness to conduct assassination operations on US soil using diaspora proxies.

sources.title: Two Men Charged in Alleged Plot to Assassinate Saudi Arabian Ambassador to the United States

data.name: Assassination of Kazem Rajavi
data.description: On 24 April 1990, [Kazem Rajavi](/people/kazem-rajavi) — professor of political science at the University of Geneva, human rights advocate, and elder brother of MEK leader [Massoud Rajavi](/people/massoud-rajavi) — was assassinated near his home in Coppet, Switzerland. He was shot at close range by multiple [Iranian intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence) agents who had entered Switzerland on diplomatic passports. [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian), then intelligence minister under [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), was charged by a Swiss court with masterminding the operation. In 2020, the Swiss case was reopened to be investigated as a crime against humanity.

data.name: Bid Kaneh Explosion (2011)
data.description: On 12 November 2011 a massive explosion destroyed the Shahid Modarres missile garrison (Alghadir base) at Bid Kaneh, near Shahriar, approximately 48 km west of Tehran. The blast killed 17 IRGC members including Brigadier General [Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam](/people/hassan-tehrani-moghaddam), the principal architect of Iran's ballistic missile programme, and wounded sixteen others. Iranian authorities described it as an accident during the transport and handling of munitions. U.S. defense officials assessed it as likely an industrial accident involving volatile missile propellants. A Western intelligence official told *Time* magazine it was intentional, and Israeli Defence Minister Ehud Barak commented cryptically: 'May there be more like it' — a remark widely interpreted as implicit acknowledgement of Israeli involvement. Satellite imagery revealed extensive structural destruction across the base. The explosion occurred amid a broader pattern of covert operations against Iran's weapons programmes between 2007 and 2012, including Stuxnet, assassinations of nuclear scientists, and prior blasts at missile facilities. No state has officially claimed responsibility.

data.name: Chain Murders of Iranian Intellectuals (1988–1998)
data.description:
The chain murders (قتل‌های زنجیره‌ای) were a decade-long campaign of state-sponsored assassinations carried out by a unit within the [Ministry of Intelligence](/orgs/ministry-of-intelligence), targeting Iranian intellectuals, writers, poets, political activists, and dissidents from 1988 to 1998. The campaign was led by MOIS deputy [Saeed Emami](/people/saeed-emami), who reported to Intelligence Minister [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian). Victims numbered over 80 people. Methods included stabbing, strangulation, staged car accidents, lethal injections of potassium, and poisoning.

The murders came to public attention in late 1998 when five prominent figures were killed in quick succession: [Pirouz Davani](/people/pirouz-davani) (disappeared August 1998), [Hamid Hajizadeh](/people/hamid-hajizadeh) and his 9-year-old son (stabbed September 1998), [Majid Sharif](/people/majid-sharif) (potassium injection, November 1998), [Dariush Forouhar](/people/dariush-forouhar) and [Parvaneh Forouhar](/people/parvaneh-forouhar) (stabbed in their home, 22 November 1998), [Mohammad Mokhtari](/people/mohammad-mokhtari) (strangled, December 1998), and [Mohammad Jafar Pouyandeh](/people/mohammad-jafar-pouyandeh) (strangled, December 1998). Earlier victims included [Kazem Sami](/people/kazem-sami) (axed to death 1988, likely the first victim), [Ali Akbar Saidi Sirjani](/people/ali-akbar-saidi-sirjani) (killed in custody 1994), [Ahmad Tafazzoli](/people/ahmad-tafazzoli) (killed 1997), and [Ebrahim Zalzadeh](/people/ebrahim-zalzadeh) (arrested and killed 1997). [Ahmad Khomeini](/people/ahmad-khomeini), son of the revolution's founder, is widely believed to have been poisoned in 1995 as part of the same campaign. In August 1996, 21 members of the [Iranian Writers' Association](/orgs/iranian-writers-association) narrowly survived when a MOIS operative attempted to drive their bus off a cliff at Heyran Pass.

The MOIS issued an unprecedented admission on 6 January 1999 that its agents were responsible. Intelligence Minister [Qorbanali Dorri-Najafabadi](/people/qorbanali-dorri-najafabadi) resigned. [Saeed Emami](/people/saeed-emami) was arrested but died in custody in June 1999 — officially by suicide, widely believed murdered to protect those who gave the orders. Fifteen operatives were tried in 2000–2001; three were sentenced to death and others to prison terms, but the trial was widely regarded as a sham. Journalist [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) identified Fallahian as the 'Master Key' and was imprisoned for six years. [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian), whose newspaper [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper) broke the story, was shot in the head in March 2000 and left paralysed for life. Lawyer [Nasser Zarafshan](/people/nasser-zarafshan) was imprisoned for representing victims' families. [Shirin Ebadi](/people/shirin-ebadi), who represented the Forouhar family, discovered she herself had been on the murder list.

sources.title: The Chain Murders: Killing Dissidents and Intellectuals, 1988–1998
Q&A: All you need to know about Iran's Chain Murders
Two Decades Later, Still No Justice For Iran Chain Murders

data.name: 1979 Constitutional Assembly Election
data.description: The election for the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) was held on 3–4 August 1979, six months after the [Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979). Voters chose 73 members to draft the new constitution. Of 428 registered candidates, the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) and Khomeini-aligned clerics won approximately 55 seats. Turnout was 51.7% (10.8 million of 20.9 million eligible voters). The [Freedom Movement of Iran](/orgs/freedom-movement-of-iran) and secular nationalists won a small minority of seats. [Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou](/people/abdul-rahman-ghassemlou), leader of the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan), won over 80% of the vote in West Azerbaijan but his credentials were rejected on [Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s orders.

sources.title: The 1979 Assembly of Experts for the Drafting of the Constitution Election

data.name: 1979 Iranian Constitutional Referendum
data.description: The constitutional referendum held on 2–3 December 1979 submitted the draft constitution produced by the [Assembly of Experts for the Constitution](/orgs/assembly-of-experts-for-constitution) to a popular vote. The constitution, which embedded the doctrine of velayat-e faqih and granted sweeping powers to a Supreme Leader, was approved with 99.5% of votes cast. Turnout was 75.2% (15.7 million voters). The referendum was boycotted by leftist parties, secular nationalists, Kurdish autonomy movements including the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan), followers of Ayatollah Shariatmadari, and the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq). Turnout was notably low in Kurdistan, Sistan-Baluchestan, and Azerbaijan provinces.

data.name: 1989 Constitutional Revision
data.description: The 1989 constitutional revision amended the Constitution of the Islamic Republic shortly before [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini)'s death. Key changes included abolishing the position of prime minister and merging its powers into the presidency, establishing the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) and the [Expediency Discernment Council](/orgs/expediency-discernment-council) as constitutional bodies, removing the requirement that the Supreme Leader be a marja (senior religious authority), and expanding the Supreme Leader's powers. The revision was approved by referendum on 28 July 1989, the same day as the presidential election that brought [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) to office.

data.name: Death of Grand Ayatollah Montazeri
data.description: Grand Ayatollah [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri) died on 19 December 2009 at his home in Qom at the age of 87. As the most senior clerical critic of the Islamic Republic and the most authoritative religious voice supporting the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009), his death triggered massive mourning gatherings in Qom, Isfahan, and his hometown Najafabad that became political protests. The regime attempted to suppress the ceremonies but could not prevent them. The fortieth-day mourning coincided with Ashura (27 December 2009), leading to some of the most violent clashes of the Green Movement. His death removed the highest-ranking clerical opponent of [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei)'s leadership.

sources.title: Profile: Grand Ayatollah Hossein Ali Montazeri

data.name: Execution of Ruhollah Zam (2020)
data.description: Dissident journalist [Ruhollah Zam](/people/ruhollah-zam), founder of the Amad News Telegram channel, was executed on 12 December 2020 after being lured from exile in France to Iraq and captured by [IRGC Intelligence](/orgs/irgc-intelligence-organisation) operatives in October 2019. His forced confession was broadcast on [IRIB](/orgs/irib). A revolutionary court convicted him of 'corruption on earth' for his role in covering the December 2017 protests through Amad News, which had over one million followers. His execution was condemned by France, the European Union, and international human rights organisations. He was the son of reformist cleric [Mohammad-Ali Zam](/people/mohammad-ali-zam).

data.name: Assassination of Mohsen Fakhrizadeh
data.description: Mohsen Fakhrizadeh, head of the IRGC's clandestine Amad nuclear weapons research programme and deputy Defence Minister, was assassinated on 27 November 2020 near Absard, east of Tehran. A sophisticated remote-controlled ambush used a weapon mounted on a truck to target his vehicle. Iran and Western intelligence attributed the killing to Israeli intelligence (Mossad), though Israel did not officially claim responsibility. Fakhrizadeh had been identified by the IAEA as the central figure in Iran's military nuclear work. His death was the most high-profile in a series of targeted killings of Iranian nuclear scientists and defence officials attributed to Israel since 2010. The Majlis responded by passing legislation requiring the government to increase enrichment to 20% and restrict IAEA inspector access — accelerating the nuclear programme and hastening the JCPOA's collapse. [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi) at the [Atomic Energy Organisation of Iran](/orgs/atomic-energy-organisation-of-iran) described him as the 'shahid of science'.

data.name: Assassination of Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou
data.description: On 13 July 1989, [Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou](/people/abdul-rahman-ghassemlou), Secretary-General of the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan), was assassinated along with his aide Abdullah Ghaderi-Azar and Kurdish mediator Fadel Rasoul in an apartment in Vienna, Austria, during what he believed were secret peace negotiations with Iranian government envoys. The Iranian delegation included intelligence operative Mohammad Jafar Sahraroudi. Despite strong evidence implicating the Iranian agents, Austrian authorities controversially allowed the suspects to leave the country and the case was never brought to trial. The assassination is widely attributed to Iranian intelligence services and is part of a pattern of extraterritorial killings of Iranian dissidents. Ghassemlou's successor, [Sadegh Sharafkandi](/people/sadegh-sharafkandi), was also assassinated three years later in Berlin.

data.name: Green Movement Protests
data.description: Following the disputed June 2009 presidential election in which incumbent [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) was declared the winner over reformist candidates [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), millions of Iranians took to the streets in the largest protests since the 1979 revolution. The government response was violent; the [Basij](/orgs/basij) and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) killed dozens of protesters. The movement was suppressed by late 2009, and its leaders Mousavi and Karroubi were placed under house arrest in 2011.

data.name: Group of Fifty-Three Arrests (1937)
data.description: In 1937 [Reza Shah Pahlavi](/people/reza-shah-pahlavi)'s security services arrested 53 prominent Marxist intellectuals — predominantly young, university-educated men from middle-class families — in a sweeping crackdown on the Communist Party of Iran. The detainees, led by Dr. Taqi Arani, became known as the Group of Fifty-Three (Panjah-o Se Nafar). The arrests effectively destroyed the Communist Party of Iran as an organised force. Several detainees died under torture or in prison; Arani himself died in Qasr Prison in 1940 under disputed circumstances. After Reza Shah's forced abdication in 1941, the surviving members were released under Soviet-Allied pressure. They subsequently founded the Tudeh Party of Iran in 1941, which became the most significant left-wing political organisation in Iranian history.

sources.title: Brief History of the Tudeh Party of Iran

data.name: Hafte Tir Bombing
data.description: On 28 June 1981 (7 Tir 1360 in the Iranian calendar), a bomb exploded at the headquarters of the [Islamic Republican Party](/orgs/islamic-republican-party) in Tehran during a leadership meeting, killing over 70 people including [Mohammad Beheshti](/people/mohammad-beheshti), four cabinet ministers, 27 members of parliament, and other senior officials. It was one of the deadliest political assassinations in modern Iranian history. The attack is generally attributed to the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) (MEK), though the group has denied responsibility and attribution remains disputed by some analysts.

data.name: Iran Experts Initiative Email Leak
data.description: In September 2023, [Iran International](/orgs/iran-international) and [Semafor](/orgs/semafor) published thousands of leaked Iranian [Foreign Ministry](/orgs/ministry-of-foreign-affairs) emails dating back to 2014, revealing the Iran Experts Initiative (IEI) — a covert influence operation run through the Ministry's think tank, the [Institute for Political and International Studies (IPIS)](/orgs/ipis). The emails showed that [Saeed Khatibzadeh](/people/saeed-khatibzadeh), a Berlin-based diplomat, conceived the initiative in March 2014 and recruited a core group of Western-based Iranian academics — including [Ariane Tabatabai](/people/ariane-tabatabai), [Dina Esfandiary](/people/dina-esfandiary), [Ali Vaez](/people/ali-vaez), [Adnan Tabatabai](/people/adnan-tabatabai), [Ellie Geranmayeh](/people/ellie-geranmayeh), and [Rouzbeh Parsi](/people/rouzbeh-parsi) — to promote Tehran's positions on the nuclear deal in Western media and policy circles. [Mostafa Zahrani](/people/mostafa-zahrani), then head of IPIS, oversaw the operation and served as the main liaison between the network and [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif)'s Foreign Ministry. The leak revealed that members shared article drafts with Iranian officials before publication, received guidance on conference attendance, and that one member offered to ghostwrite op-eds for Tehran. At least three IEI members — Tabatabai, Vaez, and Esfandiary — later worked for or advised [Robert Malley](/people/robert-malley), the Biden administration's special envoy on Iran, who was himself placed on leave and investigated by the FBI in 2023. The revelations triggered a Pentagon security review of Tabatabai and congressional demands for investigation.

data.name: Iran–Argentina Memorandum of Understanding (2013)
data.description: On 27 January 2013, the governments of Argentina and Iran signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) establishing a joint truth commission to reinvestigate the AMIA bombing of 1994. The agreement was signed by Argentine Foreign Minister Héctor Timerman and his Iranian counterpart. [Alberto Nisman](/people/alberto-nisman), the AMIA special prosecutor, argued that the MOU was designed to shield Iranian suspects — including those subject to Interpol red notices — from prosecution in exchange for oil and trade concessions, effectively undermining the criminal proceedings he had built over a decade. In January 2015, Nisman filed a 289-page charging document accusing President [Cristina Fernández de Kirchner](/people/cristina-fernandez-de-kirchner) and Foreign Minister [Jorge Taiana](/people/jorge-taiana) of treason in connection with the MOU. Nisman was found dead on 18 January 2015, the night before his planned congressional testimony. Argentine courts later determined he had been murdered.

sources.title: Argentina and Iran sign deal over 1994 Jewish centre bombing

data.name: Iran-Contra Affair
data.description: The Iran-Contra affair was a covert arrangement in which the United States secretly sold arms to Iran — then under an arms embargo and engaged in the [Iran-Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war) — in exchange for Iran's assistance in securing the release of American hostages held by [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) in Lebanon. Profits from the sales were illegally diverted to fund Contra rebels in Nicaragua. On the Iranian side, [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), then Speaker of the Majlis, was the principal interlocutor whom U.S. officials believed led a 'moderate' faction open to rapprochement. [Manucher Ghorbanifar](/people/manucher-ghorbanifar), an expatriate Iranian arms dealer, served as the initial intermediary, while [Hassan Karoubi](/people/hassan-karoubi) met with Ghorbanifar and U.S. officials in Geneva in October 1985 to propose the missile-for-hostage exchange. [Mohsen Kangarloo](/people/mohsen-kangarloo), security adviser to Prime Minister [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi), negotiated arms transfers in Germany in February 1986. Between August 1985 and October 1986, Iran received over 2,000 TOW anti-tank missiles and Hawk anti-aircraft missile parts through Israeli and then direct U.S. channels. In May 1986, former U.S. National Security Adviser Robert McFarlane led a secret delegation to Tehran but failed to reach agreement with Iranian officials. From August 1986, [Ali Hashemi Bahramani](/people/ali-hashemi-bahramani), Rafsanjani's nephew and an [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) officer, replaced Ghorbanifar as the primary Iranian contact — the 'second channel.' The affair was exposed on 3 November 1986 when the Lebanese magazine Ash-Shiraa published details leaked by [Mehdi Hashemi](/people/mehdi-hashemi), an [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) official aligned with [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri) who opposed the secret dealings. Hashemi was arrested and executed in September 1987. The scandal damaged both the Reagan administration and the credibility of Iranian 'moderates,' and contributed to Montazeri's eventual removal as Khomeini's designated successor.

data.name: Iran Hostage Crisis
data.description: On 4 November 1979, a group of Islamist students calling themselves 'Muslim Student Followers of the Imam's Line,' led by [Mohammad Mousavi Khoeiniha](/people/mohammad-mousavi-khoeiniha), stormed the US Embassy in Tehran and took 52 American diplomats and citizens hostage. The crisis lasted 444 days, ending with the release of the hostages on 20 January 1981 under the [Algiers Accords](/events/algiers-accords-1981). [Abbas Abdi](/people/abbas-abdi) was among the prominent student participants. The crisis cemented the revolutionary regime's anti-American identity and led to the severing of US-Iran diplomatic relations.

data.name: Iran-Iraq War
data.description: The Iran-Iraq War (1980–1988) was an eight-year conflict initiated by Iraq's invasion of Iran in September 1980. It was the longest conventional war of the twentieth century, killing an estimated 500,000 to 1 million people on both sides. The war shaped a generation of Iranian military and political leaders, particularly those who went on to lead the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) served as prime minister throughout the conflict. The war ended with UN-brokered ceasefire Resolution 598, which [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) accepted, comparing it to 'drinking poison'.

data.name: 2010 Iran Nuclear Scientist Assassinations
data.description: On 29 November 2010, two coordinated car-bombing attacks in Tehran targeted Iranian nuclear scientists. [Majid Shahriari](/people/majid-shahriari), a nuclear physicist at Shahid Beheshti University, was killed when a bomb attached to his car by a motorcyclist detonated; his wife survived. In a separate simultaneous attack, [Fereydoun Abbasi-Davani](/people/fereydoun-abbasi-davani) and his wife survived a similar bomb attack. The attacks were part of a broader campaign of targeted killings against Iranian nuclear scientists between 2010 and 2012 that also included the killing of Daryoush Rezaei (July 2011) and Mostafa Ahmadi Roshan (January 2012). Western and Israeli governments did not publicly claim responsibility, but the operations are widely attributed to Israeli intelligence (Mossad), possibly with US knowledge. Iran accused the United States and Israel and mourned Shahriari as a martyr of science.

sources.title: Assassination of Iranian nuclear scientists — Wikipedia
Iran Says Nuclear Scientist Killed in Explosion

data.name: Iran Press Crackdown — April 2000
data.description: On 24 April 2000, the day after Iranian reformists celebrated their landslide victory in February's parliamentary elections (taking three-quarters of seats), Press Court Judge [Saeed Mortazavi](/people/saeed-mortazavi) executed an order closing 16 to 21 reformist newspapers overnight — the single largest assault on the Iranian press in the Islamic Republic's history. The action directly followed a speech by [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) branding reformist newspapers 'enemy bases' that 'appear to be directed from the same centre' to 'make the people pessimistic about the system.' Mortazavi cited a 1960 crime-prevention law to justify mass closures without individual hearings. Papers closed included [Sobh-e Emroz](/orgs/sobh-e-emroz-newspaper), [Mosharekat](/orgs/mosharekat-newspaper), [Ham-Mihan](/orgs/ham-mihan-newspaper), [Asr-e Azadegan](/orgs/asr-e-azadegan-newspaper), and many others. The same week, [Saeed Hajjarian](/people/saeed-hajjarian) was shot in the face and severely disabled. [Akbar Ganji](/people/akbar-ganji) was arrested on 22 April; [Emadeddin Baghi](/people/emadeddin-baghi) on 29 May. When the reformist-dominated Sixth Majlis tried to pass a press freedom bill in August 2000, Khamenei personally ordered the bill removed from the parliamentary agenda. By February 2004 Mortazavi had personally overseen the closure or license revocation of approximately 90–120 publications.

sources.title: Freedom of the press in Iran

data.name: Iran Student Uprising — July 1999
data.description: The July 1999 student uprising was the largest protests in Iran since the 1979 revolution, directly triggered by the closure of the reformist newspaper [Salam](/orgs/salam-newspaper). On 7 July 1999, the Special Court for the Clergy closed Salam after it published a leaked MOIS document suggesting the Ministry of Intelligence had recommended restricting the press and may have been involved in the [chain murders of 1998](/events/chain-murders-iran-1998). That night, riot police and Basij attacked the dormitories at Tehran University where students were protesting the closure, forcing entry at 1am and beating students in their beds. At least four students were killed and hundreds injured over six days of protests that spread to multiple cities. Among those who played central roles in the dormitory attacks were three IRGC commanders: Brigadier General Mohammad Bagher Zolghadr, Brigadier General Mohammad Reza Naghdi (later commander of the Basij), and Brigadier General Hossein Nejat. The protests were suppressed within six days. None of the commanders responsible for the dormitory attacks were prosecuted during the Khatami era, a failure that became a defining symbol of reformist political impotence.

data.name: 1953 Iranian Coup d'état
data.description: The 1953 Iranian coup d'état (Operation Ajax/Boot) was a CIA and MI6-backed operation that overthrew the democratically elected government of Prime Minister [Mohammad Mossadegh](/people/mohammad-mossadegh). On 19 August 1953, royalist officers and mobs organised with covert US and British support seized key installations in Tehran and toppled Mossadegh's cabinet. Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, who had briefly fled the country, returned to power. Mossadegh was arrested and tried by a military tribunal, receiving a three-year prison sentence before being placed under permanent house arrest. [Karim Sanjabi](/people/karim-sanjabi) and other [National Front](/orgs/national-front-iran) leaders were arrested. The coup ended Iran's experiment with democratic governance and the oil nationalisation era, and set the stage for the Shah's authoritarian consolidation, backed by [SAVAK](/orgs/savak), over the following decades.

sources.title: CIA Admits Role in 1953 Iranian Coup
sources.publisher: National Security Archive, George Washington University

data.name: Iranian Cultural Revolution
data.description: In June 1980, Ayatollah Khomeini ordered the closure of all Iranian universities to purge them of Western and secular influences. The Cultural Revolution lasted until approximately 1983, during which thousands of students and faculty were expelled. A Cultural Revolutionary Headquarters was established, later replaced by the Supreme Council of the Cultural Revolution in 1984. The campaign resulted in the expulsion of all Baháʼí students and professors, bans on Western cultural influences, and the emigration of thousands of academics and technocrats.

data.name: Iranian Revolution
data.description: The Islamic Revolution of 1979 overthrew the Pahlavi monarchy and established the Islamic Republic of Iran. Following months of mass protests, Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi fled Iran on 16 January 1979. [Ayatollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) returned from exile on 1 February 1979. The revolutionary government took power on 11 February 1979, the date now commemorated as the Day of Victory of the Islamic Revolution. A constitutional referendum in March confirmed the Islamic Republic, and a new theocratic constitution was approved in December 1979.

data.name: Israeli Strikes on Iran — February 28, 2026
data.description: On 28 February 2026, combined US-Israel precision airstrikes targeted Iran's military and security leadership in Tehran and other locations. The strikes killed [Mohammad Pakpour](/people/mohammad-pakpour) (IRGC Commander-in-Chief), [Abdolrahim Mousavi](/people/abdolrahim-mousavi) (Chief of the General Staff), [Hossein Jabal Amelian](/people/hossein-jabal-amelian) (head of SPND), and [Reza Mozaffari Nia](/people/reza-mozaffari-nia) (former SPND head). Also killed in the February 28 strikes were intelligence and military office figures: [Saleh Asadi](/people/saleh-asadi) (Deputy Head of Armed Forces Intelligence), [Mohammad Shirazi](/people/mohammad-shirazi-military) (Head of the Supreme Leader's Military Office), [Akbar Ebrahimzadeh](/people/akbar-ebrahimzadeh) (Deputy Head of the Supreme Leader's Military Office), [Mohammad Baseri](/people/mohammad-baseri) (senior MOIS official), and [Yahya Hosseini Panjaki](/people/yahya-hosseini-panjaki) (Deputy Minister of Intelligence for Israel Affairs). [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) was also killed on the same day, triggering a succession crisis within the Islamic Republic. The February 28 strikes represented the most devastating decapitation of Iran's military, intelligence, and nuclear leadership since Operation Rising Lion in June 2025.

nodes.label: Israeli Strikes on Iran — Operation Rising Lion (June 2025)

data.name: Israeli Strikes on Iran — Operation Rising Lion (June 2025)
data.description: On 13 June 2025, Israel launched large-scale airstrikes on Iran targeting military command centres, nuclear infrastructure, and senior IRGC leadership — an operation Israel called 'Operation Rising Lion'. The strikes, described as pre-emptive, killed approximately 30 IRGC generals in the opening attack, including [Hossein Salami](/people/hossein-salami) (IRGC Commander-in-Chief), [Amir Ali Hajizadeh](/people/amir-ali-hajizadeh) (Aerospace Force commander), [Esmail Qaani](/people/esmail-qaani) (Quds Force commander), and Mohammad Bagheri (Chief of Staff of the Armed Forces). US airstrikes simultaneously damaged nuclear facilities at Natanz, Isfahan, and Fordow. The strikes initiated what became known as the 'Twelve-Day War'. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) was killed in a subsequent strike on 28 February 2026, triggering the [succession crisis](/events/mojtaba-khamenei-supreme-leader-2026) that resulted in [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) becoming Supreme Leader.

data.name: January 2026 Iran Massacres
data.description: Between 8 and 25 January 2026, Iranian security forces killed thousands of civilians during a crackdown on the 2025-2026 protests. [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) ordered the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council) to crush the protests by any means necessary on 9 January. [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani), secretary of the SNSC, was identified as the chief planner. The [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps), [Basij](/orgs/basij), and [Law Enforcement Command](/orgs/law-enforcement-command) carried out the killings using live ammunition, military-grade weapons, and snipers. Iraqi and Lebanese militias including the [Popular Mobilization Forces](/orgs/popular-mobilization-forces) and [Hezbollah](/orgs/hezbollah) were reported to have participated. Death toll estimates range from HRANA's verified count of over 7,000 to unverified estimates exceeding 30,000. The UN Special Rapporteur stated the killings may constitute crimes against humanity.

sources.title: Iran: Growing Evidence of Countrywide Massacres
Iran: Authorities unleash heavily militarized clampdown to hide protest massacres
At least 6,126 people killed in Iran's crackdown on nationwide protests

data.name: JCPOA Nuclear Deal
data.description: The Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) was finalised in Vienna on 14 July 2015 between Iran and the P5+1 (US, UK, France, Russia, China, Germany) plus the EU. After 20 months of negotiations, Iran agreed to limit uranium enrichment to 3.67%, reduce its centrifuge count to 5,060 at Natanz, repurpose Fordow for non-enrichment research, and redesign the Arak heavy water reactor to eliminate plutonium production — all in exchange for lifting of UN, EU, and US nuclear-related sanctions. [Mohammad Javad Zarif](/people/javad-zarif) led Iran's negotiating team alongside [Abbas Araghchi](/people/abbas-araghchi) and [Ali Akbar Salehi](/people/ali-akbar-salehi). [Hassan Rouhani](/people/hassan-rouhani) championed the deal domestically against fierce hardliner opposition led by [Saeed Jalili](/people/saeed-jalili) and [Mahmoud Nabavian](/people/mahmoud-nabavian). The deal went into effect on 16 January 2016 ('Implementation Day') after IAEA verification. It was publicly opposed by Israel and Saudi Arabia.

data.name: US Withdrawal from JCPOA
data.description: On 8 May 2018, President Donald Trump announced US withdrawal from the JCPOA, reimposing all sanctions and adding new ones under a 'maximum pressure' campaign. The US re-designated Iran's oil, banking, shipping, and petrochemical sectors. The withdrawal triggered a cascade: European signatories (UK, France, Germany) attempted to sustain the deal through the Instex mechanism, but US secondary sanctions deterred European businesses from trading with Iran. Iran responded by gradually stepping down its JCPOA commitments from May 2019 onward, enriching uranium above the 3.67% cap, exceeding the stockpile limit, and eventually restricting IAEA inspector access. By January 2020, following [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani)'s assassination, Iran announced it would no longer observe any JCPOA limits. The deal's collapse significantly accelerated Iran's uranium enrichment programme, which reached 60% purity by 2021 — a short technical step from the 90% weapons-grade level.

data.name: June 20 Uprising (30 Khordad)
data.description: On 20 June 1981, the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq) organized massive anti-government protests in Tehran and other cities in response to the impeachment of President [Abolhassan Banisadr](/people/abolhassan-banisadr). The Tehran demonstration attracted up to 500,000 people. Leading clerics declared demonstrators "enemies of God" subject to immediate execution. The [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) violently suppressed the protests, killing approximately 50 people near Tehran University alone and arresting over 1,000. At least 23 arrested protesters, including teenage girls, were subsequently executed. This date marks the beginning of the MEK's open war with the Islamic Republic and the start of the 1981–1982 mass repressions that drove the organization underground and eventually into exile.

nodes.label: Indictment of Mostafa Kavakebian for false allegations about Perez-Shakdam (2025)
