data.name: Indictment of Mostafa Kavakebian for false allegations about Perez-Shakdam (2025)
data.description: In July 2025, Tehran's prosecutor charged former reformist MP [Mostafa Kavakebian](/people/mostafa-kavakebian) after he appeared on Iranian state television and claimed that [Catherine Perez-Shakdam](/people/catherine-perez-shakdam) had maintained sexual relationships with 120 senior Iranian officials. Iran's state broadcaster publicly dismissed his statements as "obscene and false," and Iran's security and judicial bodies confirmed they held no documents indicating immoral behavior or illegal activity by Perez-Shakdam during her time in Iran. The prosecutor's office charged Kavakebian with making unsupported public claims and disturbing public opinion. He was ultimately convicted, receiving a 14-month prison sentence suspended for four years and a two-year ban on all media activity. The indictment was a notable episode in which the Iranian regime's own judiciary repudiated one of the most sensationalized elements of the domestic propaganda narrative around the Perez-Shakdam case.

sources.title: Iran Charges Former MP Who Claimed 120 Officials Had Sex With French Journalist
Iranian Judiciary Files Charges Against Former MP Over Controversial TV Remarks
Ex and current Iranian MPs sentenced to jail, media bans

data.name: Kurdistan Conflict Post-Revolution (1979–1983)
data.description: Following the [Iranian Revolution of 1979](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), Kurdish political groups — primarily the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) (PDKI) and [Komala](/orgs/komala-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) — demanded autonomy for the Kurdish regions. When negotiations collapsed and Ruhollah Khomeini issued a fatwa declaring jihad against armed Kurdish groups in August 1979, the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) launched a sustained military campaign into Kurdistan Province, Kermanshah, and West Azerbaijan. Heavy fighting erupted in Sanandaj, Mahabad, and Paveh. Sadegh Khalkhali, appointed as 'hanging judge,' conducted summary executions in recaptured towns. Tens of thousands were killed, wounded, or displaced over the course of the campaign. PDKI leader [Abdul Rahman Ghassemlou](/people/abdul-rahman-ghassemlou) led armed resistance, while Komala also mounted guerrilla operations. By 1983, the Islamic Republic had established military control over the major urban centres, pushing Kurdish forces into the mountains and ultimately into exile in Iraqi Kurdistan. The conflict effectively ended organised Kurdish armed resistance inside Iran, though low-level clashes continued throughout the Iran-Iraq War period.

sources.name: Human Rights Watch — Guardians of Thought: Limits on Freedom of Expression in Iran
Jwaideh, Wadie. The Kurdish National Movement. Syracuse University Press, 2006.

data.name: Kurdistan Region Independence Referendum (2017)
data.description:
The Kurdistan Region independence referendum of 25 September 2017 was called by Kurdistan Regional Government (KRG) President [Masoud Barzani](/people/masoud-barzani) and resulted in over 93% voting in favour of independence from Iraq. The referendum was held in the KRG's three governorates and in disputed territories including Kirkuk. It was declared unconstitutional by the Iraqi central government and opposed by nearly all regional and international powers — including the United States, the European Union, Turkey, and Iran. Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei publicly condemned the vote as an act of betrayal toward the region.

The aftermath was swift and strategically catastrophic for the KRG. In October 2017, Iraqi central government forces — including [Popular Mobilization Forces](/orgs/popular-mobilization-forces) whose coordination with Iran had long been established — moved on Kirkuk. The KRG surrendered the oil-rich city and approximately 40% of its territory within days, without meaningful military resistance. Masoud Barzani stepped down as KRG President on 1 November 2017. The episode significantly weakened Iraqi Kurdistan's strategic position, reinforced Iranian influence over disputed territories in northern Iraq, and cast a long shadow over Iranian Kurdish opposition parties' own debates about independence strategy.

sources.name: How the Kurdish independence referendum backfired — Washington Post

data.name: Kurdistan Military Campaign (1979)
data.description:
In August 1979, the Islamic Republic launched a military offensive against Kurdish autonomy movements following months of clashes between Kurdish armed groups and the new revolutionary state. The [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) (PDKI) and [Komala](/orgs/komala-party-of-iranian-kurdistan) had taken control of large parts of Kurdistan province, including the cities of Mahabad, Baneh, Sardasht, and Sanandaj, after the revolution created a power vacuum.

[Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) declared jihad against the Kurdish armed movements on 19 August 1979 following the breakdown of negotiations. [Sadegh Khalkhali](/people/sadegh-khalkhali) was dispatched to the region as a revolutionary judge. In Sanandaj and Paveh, Khalkhali held summary courts and ordered mass executions of captured Kurdish political prisoners and fighters. Estimates of those executed by Khalkhali's courts in Kurdistan range from dozens to over 100 in a matter of days.

The broader military campaign involved IRGC forces, regular army units, and later resulted in the deaths of thousands of Kurdish civilians and fighters. The Kurdish cities were bombarded and occupied. The campaign effectively crushed the initial Kurdish autonomy uprising, though armed resistance by the PDKI and Komala continued for years.

data.name: Assassination of Ali Larijani
data.description: Former [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly) Speaker and senior establishment figure [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) was killed in an Israeli airstrike on Tehran on 17 March 2026. His son Morteza Larijani and office chief Alireza Bayat were also killed in the attack. The killing came weeks after the death of [Supreme Leader Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and amid an ongoing Israeli-Iranian conflict that had severely degraded Iran's military and nuclear infrastructure. Iran officially declared Larijani a martyr. The assassination removed one of the Islamic Republic's most experienced diplomatic and legislative figures at a critical moment of regime transition.

sources.title: Iran's wartime leader isn't a dealmaker

data.name: Mahsa Amini Protests (Woman, Life, Freedom)
data.description: Following the death of 22-year-old Mahsa (Zhina) Amini in morality police custody on 16 September 2022, nationwide protests erupted under the slogan Woman, Life, Freedom (Zan, Zendegi, Azadi). The protests were the most sustained unrest in the history of the Islamic Republic, continuing for months across all provinces. Security forces killed over 500 protesters according to human rights organisations. Over 15,000 people were arrested. The protests were characterised by women publicly removing and burning their headscarves.

nodes.label: March 2026 Strike Wave on IRGC/Basij/SNSC Leadership

data.name: March 2026 Strike Wave on IRGC/Basij/SNSC Leadership
data.description: A sustained US-Israel strike campaign from 2–31 March 2026 (with operations extending to early April) that killed 30+ named officials across the [Basij](/orgs/basij), [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) regional commands, [IRGC Quds Force Lebanon Corps](/orgs/irgc-quds-force-lebanon-corps), Ministry of Intelligence, and the [Supreme National Security Council](/orgs/supreme-national-security-council). The wave followed the February 2026 Israeli strikes that killed [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) and began a systematic campaign to eliminate Iran's reconstituted military leadership. The most strategically significant single strike occurred on 17 March 2026, targeting the SNSC compound: it killed [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani), his son [Morteza Larijani](/people/morteza-larijani), SNSC Deputy Secretary [Ali Bateni](/people/ali-bateni), and SNSC Deputy for Internal Security [Alireza Bayat](/people/alireza-bayat). Other senior casualties included Basij head [Asadollah Badfar](/people/asadollah-badfar), Khatam al-Anbiya Staff Chief [Abolqasem Babaeian](/people/abolqasem-babaeian), and multiple IRGC provincial and Aerospace Force commanders.

sources.title: Dozens of Senior IRGC and SNSC Officials Killed in March 2026 Strikes
Iran's wartime leader isn't a dealmaker

data.name: 1988 Mass Executions of Political Prisoners
data.description: In late July 1988, [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) issued a secret fatwa ordering the execution of political prisoners across Iran who remained loyal to the Mojahedin-e Khalq (MEK/PMOI) or refused to renounce their political beliefs. Three-person 'Death Commissions' were established in prisons throughout Iran, each comprising a Sharia judge, a prosecutor, and an intelligence representative. In Tehran, the commission — chaired by [Hossein-Ali Nayyeri](/people/hossein-ali-nayyeri), with [Morteza Eshraqi](/people/morteza-eshraqi) as prosecutor, [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) as deputy prosecutor, and [Mostafa Pourmohammadi](/people/mostafa-pourmohammadi) as Intelligence Ministry representative — processed prisoners in minutes. Estimated deaths range from 2,800 to 5,000 (UN, HRW, Amnesty) or higher. The victims were buried in secret mass graves. Ayatollah Montazeri, then designated successor to Khomeini, protested and was subsequently dismissed as heir. In 2016 his family released an audio recording of his 15 August 1988 meeting with Death Committee members in which he said: 'The greatest crime committed during the reign of the Islamic Republic... has been committed by you.' The UN Special Rapporteur on Iran concluded in a 2024 report that the executions constituted crimes against humanity and genocide. None of the perpetrators has faced legal accountability.

nodes.label: Election of Mojtaba Khamenei as Supreme Leader

data.name: Election of Mojtaba Khamenei as Supreme Leader
data.description: On 8 March 2026, the [Assembly of Experts](/orgs/assembly-of-experts) elected [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei) — second son of the late [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) — as the third Supreme Leader of the Islamic Republic, eight days after his father's death on 28 February 2026. The election confirmed a dynastic transfer of power. An interim three-member leadership council — [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian), [Gholam-Hossein Mohseni-Ejei](/people/gholam-hossein-mohseni-ejei), and [Alireza Arafi](/people/alireza-arafi) — had governed under Article 111 of the constitution in the intervening period. Mojtaba Khamenei, like his father in 1989, did not hold the marja' credential traditionally associated with the Supreme Leader role.

nodes.label: Dismissal of Montazeri as Supreme Leader Successor

data.name: Dismissal of Montazeri as Supreme Leader Successor
data.description: On 28 March 1989, [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) formally stripped [Hossein-Ali Montazeri](/people/hossein-ali-montazeri) of his position as designated successor to the Supreme Leadership. The dismissal followed Montazeri's sustained opposition to the [1988 mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988) of political prisoners and his public criticisms of the revolutionary establishment. [Ahmad Khomeini](/people/ahmad-khomeini), who controlled access to his father, and [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), then Speaker of Parliament, played key roles in Montazeri's isolation. In his letter of dismissal, Khomeini accused Montazeri of being manipulated by 'hypocrites' — the regime's term for the Mojahedin-e Khalq. With Montazeri removed, the [1989 constitutional revision](/events/constitutional-revision-1989) dropped the requirement that the Supreme Leader be a marja-e taqlid, enabling [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei) — then only a Hojatoleslam — to assume the position after Khomeini's death in June 1989.

data.name: Mykonos Restaurant Assassination
data.description: On 17 September 1992, gunmen entered the Mykonos Greek restaurant in Berlin and shot dead [Sadegh Sharafkandi](/people/sadegh-sharafkandi), Secretary-General of the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan), along with PDKI representatives Fattah Abdoli and Homayoun Ardalan, and their translator Nouri Dehkordi. The assassination was carried out by Kazem Darabi, an Iranian national, and Abbas Rhayel, a Lebanese associate. The subsequent trial (1993–1997) produced a landmark verdict finding that the killings were ordered by the Committee for Special Operations, which included [Ali Khamenei](/people/ali-khamenei), [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani), [Ali Fallahian](/people/ali-fallahian), and [Ali Akbar Velayati](/people/ali-akbar-velayati). This was the first time a European court officially attributed state-sponsored terrorism to the highest levels of the Iranian government, triggering the recall of all EU ambassadors from Tehran.

data.name: Natanz Nuclear Revelation
data.description: On 14 August 2002, [NCRI](/orgs/national-council-of-resistance-of-iran) spokesman Alireza Jafarzadeh held a press conference in Washington revealing the existence of two secret Iranian nuclear sites: the Natanz uranium enrichment facility and the Arak heavy water production facility, based on intelligence from the [Mojahedin-e Khalq](/orgs/mojahedin-e-khalq)'s network inside Iran. This revelation triggered the first IAEA inspections of Iranian nuclear sites in 2003, which confirmed traces of enriched uranium despite Iranian decontamination efforts. The disclosure ultimately led to six UN Security Council resolutions against Iran and set in motion the international confrontation over Iran's nuclear program that culminated in the [JCPOA](/events/jcpoa-nuclear-deal-2015).

data.name: Operation Forough-e Javidan (Eternal Light)
data.description: On 26 July 1988, six days after [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) accepted UN Resolution 598 for a ceasefire in the [Iran–Iraq War](/events/iran-iraq-war), the MEK's [National Liberation Army](/orgs/national-liberation-army-of-iran) launched Operation Forough-e Javidan (Eternal Light) from Iraqi territory with approximately 7,000 fighters. The offensive aimed to capture Kermanshah and march on Tehran. MEK forces initially seized the town of Islamabad-e Gharb but were met with a devastating Iranian counterattack (Operation Mersad). The MEK suffered catastrophic losses — 1,500 to 2,500 killed in three days — and the operation failed completely. This was the last land battle of the Iran–Iraq War and is widely considered to have provided the regime a pretext for the [1988 mass executions](/events/mass-executions-1988) of political prisoners.

data.name: 2000 Iranian Parliamentary Election (6th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2000 parliamentary election produced a landslide victory for the reformist coalition aligned with [President Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami). Reformist parties, led by the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front), won approximately 195 of 290 seats. It was the high-water mark of the reform movement: the Islamic Iran Participation Front's [Mohammad-Reza Khatami](/people/mohammad-reza-khatami) topped the Tehran ballot with nearly 1.8 million votes. The 6th [Majlis](/orgs/islamic-consultative-assembly), led by [Speaker Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), was the most reformist parliament in the Islamic Republic's history and was in constant conflict with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council).

data.name: 2004 Iranian Parliamentary Election (7th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2004 parliamentary election was marked by the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council)'s mass disqualification of approximately 2,400 candidates, including most reformist incumbents and all candidates of the [Islamic Iran Participation Front](/orgs/islamic-iran-participation-front). Approximately 80 sitting MPs were barred from seeking re-election. The result was a sweeping conservative victory, with the [Abadgaran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran) coalition winning over 190 seats. Speaker [Gholam-Ali Haddad-Adel](/people/gholam-ali-haddad-adel) was elected with 226 of 259 votes, becoming the first non-clerical Speaker since the revolution. Turnout collapsed to around 51%.

data.name: 2020 Iranian Parliamentary Election (11th Majlis)
data.description: The February 2020 parliamentary election produced another conservative sweep, with the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) disqualifying over 9,000 candidates including many moderate incumbents. Turnout was a record low of approximately 42% nationally and around 25% in Tehran. The principlist coalition won a large majority, and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps)-aligned figure [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf) was elected Speaker days later. The election represented the full consolidation of hardliner control over the legislature.

data.name: 2024 Iranian Parliamentary Election (12th Majlis)
data.description: The March 2024 parliamentary election produced Iran's lowest-ever turnout at approximately 41% nationally and below 25% in Tehran. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) approved only around 15,200 of 48,847 registered candidates to run. Principlists won 233 of 290 seats. In Tehran, ultraconservatives from the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) outperformed the establishment SHANA coalition backed by Speaker [Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf](/people/mohammad-bagher-ghalibaf). [Mahmoud Nabavian](/people/mahmoud-nabavian) topped the Tehran ballot, followed by [Amir-Hossein Sabeti](/people/amir-hossein-sabeti) and [Hamid Rasaee](/people/hamid-rasaee). [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) won a Tabriz seat but resigned it in July 2024 upon election as President. A second round was held on 10 May 2024.

data.name: Post-Revolution Executions (1979)
data.description:
In the weeks and months following the [1979 Iranian Revolution](/events/iranian-revolution-1979), the Islamic Revolutionary Courts presided over by [Sadegh Khalkhali](/people/sadegh-khalkhali) conducted a wave of summary executions targeting Pahlavi-era military commanders, SAVAK officials, and prominent figures of the ancien régime.

The first executions took place on the night of 15–16 February 1979 on the rooftop of the Alavi School in Tehran, where [Ruhollah Khomeini](/people/ruhollah-khomeini) was headquartered. The four executed were [SAVAK](/orgs/savak) director [Nematollah Nassiri](/people/nematollah-nassiri), Military Governor of Tehran [Mehdi Rahimi](/people/mehdi-rahimi), and generals Mohammad Khosrowdad and Nasser Naji — all tried and shot within hours.

Over the following months, Khalkhali's courts continued to process former officials. On 7 April 1979, former Prime Minister [Amir Abbas Hoveyda](/people/amir-abbas-hoveyda) — the most prominent victim — was executed after a brief trial. On 9 May 1979, industrialist [Habibollah Elghanian](/people/habibollah-elghanian), head of the Iranian Jewish community, was executed on charges of corruption and espionage. On 11 April 1979, the last SAVAK director [Nasser Moghadam](/people/nasser-moghadam) was executed alongside other senior security figures.

Hundreds of military officers, SAVAK agents, former ministers, and officials were executed in the months that followed. Trials were frequently conducted in minutes, defendants were denied lawyers, and appeals were not permitted. International human rights organisations condemned the proceedings. The executions became a defining feature of the Islamic Republic's founding violence and a template for the regime's treatment of perceived enemies.

sources.title: Iran: Religious and Ethnic Minorities — Discrimination in Law and Practice

data.name: 1997 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The 1997 Iranian presidential election resulted in a landmark victory for reformist candidate [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami), who won approximately 70% of the vote against establishment favourite [Ali Akbar Nategh-Nouri](/people/ali-akbar-nategh-nouri). The election shocked the Iranian political establishment and is considered the beginning of the reformist era. It demonstrated the significant gap between popular preferences and the ruling establishment's expectations.

data.name: 2005 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The 2005 presidential election resulted in the surprise victory of Tehran mayor [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) over [Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani](/people/akbar-hashemi-rafsanjani) in a run-off. Ahmadinejad ran as a populist outsider against the establishment, backed by the [Alliance of Builders of Islamic Iran](/orgs/alliance-of-builders-of-islamic-iran) and networks within the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) and [Basij](/orgs/basij). [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi), who initially led in first-round results before official tallies shifted, alleged fraud. The election marked the beginning of a sharp turn toward hardline populism.

data.name: 2009 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The June 2009 presidential election was one of the most consequential and disputed in the Islamic Republic's history. Incumbent [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad) was declared the winner with 63% of the vote over reformist challenger [Mir-Hossein Mousavi](/people/mir-hossein-mousavi) and [Mehdi Karroubi](/people/mehdi-karroubi). The opposition alleged massive fraud, and the disputed results triggered the [Green Movement](/events/green-movement-protests-2009) — the largest protests Iran had seen since the 1979 revolution. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) certified the results after a partial recount.

data.name: 2021 Iranian Presidential Election
data.description: The June 2021 presidential election produced one of the lowest turnouts in the Islamic Republic's history — approximately 48% nationally, and around 26% in Tehran. The [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) disqualified 585 of 592 candidates who registered, approving only seven to run. The disqualifications included former Majlis Speaker [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani), former president [Mahmoud Ahmadinejad](/people/mahmoud-ahmadinejad), and First Vice President Eshaq Jahangiri. [Saeed Jalili](/people/saeed-jalili) withdrew before election day to consolidate the hardliner vote. [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi) won with approximately 62% of votes cast. The disqualification of [Ali Larijani](/people/ali-larijani) prompted his brother [Sadegh Amoli Larijani](/people/sadegh-larijani) to resign from the [Guardian Council](/orgs/guardian-council) in protest.

data.name: 2024 Iranian Snap Presidential Election
data.description: The snap presidential election was triggered by [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi)'s death in a helicopter crash on 19 May 2024. The first round was held on 28 June 2024; with no outright majority, a runoff on 5 July 2024 saw [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) defeat Saeed Jalili with nearly 16.4 million votes to Jalili's approximately 13.5 million. The result surprised many analysts who had expected a Jalili victory. Reformist consolidation behind Pezeshkian — led by endorsements from [Mohammad Khatami](/people/mohammad-khatami) and senior figures — proved decisive. Jalili was backed by the [Front of Islamic Revolution Stability](/orgs/front-of-islamic-revolution-stability) and had close ties to [Mojtaba Khamenei](/people/mojtaba-khamenei).

nodes.label: Death of President Raisi in Helicopter Crash

data.name: Death of President Raisi in Helicopter Crash
data.description: President [Ebrahim Raisi](/people/ebrahim-raisi), Foreign Minister [Hossein Amir-Abdollahian](/people/hossein-amir-abdollahian), and six others died when their Bell 212 helicopter crashed in a fog-covered mountainous area near Jolfa in East Azerbaijan Province on 19 May 2024. The crash triggered a constitutional provision requiring a snap presidential election within fifty days. First Vice President [Mohammad Mokhber](/people/mohammad-mokhber) served as acting president. [Masoud Pezeshkian](/people/masoud-pezeshkian) was elected as successor on 5 July 2024.

data.name: Republic of Mahabad
data.description: The Republic of Mahabad was a short-lived Kurdish state proclaimed on 22 January 1946 in the city of Mahabad in northwestern Iran, under the presidency of [Qazi Muhammad](/people/qazi-muhammad). Established with Soviet backing during the broader Azerbaijan crisis, the republic had its own flag, army, and government institutions and promoted Kurdish language and culture. When the Soviet Union withdrew its forces in May 1946, the republic lost its guarantor. Iranian government forces reoccupied Mahabad in December 1946 without significant resistance. Qazi Muhammad and key leaders were subsequently arrested and executed in March 1947. The Republic of Mahabad remains a foundational symbol in Kurdish nationalist memory and a key reference point for the [Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan](/orgs/democratic-party-of-iranian-kurdistan).

sources.name: Eagleton, William. The Kurdish Republic of 1946. Oxford University Press, 1963.

data.name: Rosewater (2014 film)
data.description: Rosewater is a 2014 biographical drama film written and directed by [Jon Stewart](/people/jon-stewart), based on the memoir 'Then They Came for Me' by [Maziar Bahari](/people/maziar-bahari). The film depicts Bahari's 118-day imprisonment in Evin Prison in 2009 following the disputed Iranian presidential election and the [Green Movement protests](/events/green-movement-protests-2009). Bahari, an Iranian-Canadian journalist working for Newsweek, was arrested and subjected to interrogation and psychological torture. His interrogator — referred to as 'Rosewater' after his cologne — used a clip of Bahari's interview on The Daily Show as evidence of espionage. The film received international attention for humanising the experience of political detention under the Islamic Republic and bringing the issue of press freedom in Iran to a global mainstream audience.

data.name: Sejjil Missile First Test (2008)
data.description: In November 2008, Iran successfully test-fired the Sejjil (سجیل), its first two-stage solid-fuelled ballistic missile, marking a major milestone in the programme developed under [Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam](/people/hassan-tehrani-moghaddam). Unlike earlier liquid-fuelled missiles, the Sejjil's solid propellant allowed for faster launch preparation and greater mobility, reducing vulnerability to pre-emptive strikes. The missile has an estimated range of approximately 2,000–2,500 km, placing Israel and much of the Middle East within reach. Development of the solid-fuel technology was assisted by expertise from North Korea. A second test was conducted in May 2009. The Sejjil programme significantly advanced Iran's deterrence posture and was among Moghaddam's most technically ambitious projects.

data.name: Shahab-3 First Test (1998)
data.description: In July 1998, Iran conducted the first test flight of the Shahab-3 (شهاب ۳), a medium-range ballistic missile developed under [Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam](/people/hassan-tehrani-moghaddam)'s direction. Based on the North Korean Nodong-1 design, the Shahab-3 had an estimated range of 1,300–1,500 km — sufficient to reach Israel, Saudi Arabia, and U.S. military bases in the Gulf region. The test was a major escalation in Iran's deterrence posture and prompted immediate concern from Western governments and Israel. Further tests followed in 2000 and 2003 before the missile entered operational service with the [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force). The Shahab-3 formed the backbone of Iran's strategic deterrent for over a decade and was the platform from which later variants, including the Ghadr-110 and Emad, were derived.

data.name: Siahkal Incident (1971)
data.description: On 8 February 1971, a small armed group affiliated with the nascent [Organization of Iranian People's Fadai Guerrillas](/orgs/organization-of-iranian-peoples-fadai-guerrillas) attacked a gendarmerie post in the village of Siahkal in the forested mountains of Gilan province. The operation, intended partly to free a captured comrade, was militarily modest but symbolically transformative. The Shah's regime responded with a massive manhunt that killed or captured most of the participants, and thirteen guerrillas were subsequently executed. Despite its failure as a military operation, the Siahkal incident became the founding myth of the Iranian armed left, celebrated as proof that organised resistance to the Pahlavi monarchy was possible. [Massoud Ahmadzadeh](/people/massoud-ahmadzadeh) and [Amir Parviz Pouyan](/people/amir-parviz-pouyan) were among the theorists whose ideas animated the operation, while [Hamid Ashraf](/people/hamid-ashraf) was among those who survived the immediate aftermath. The date — 19 Bahman in the Iranian calendar — became an annual commemoration for the Iranian left in exile. The incident directly triggered the formation of the OIPFG as a consolidated organisation.

data.name: Assassination of Qasem Soleimani
data.description: [Qasem Soleimani](/people/qasem-soleimani), commander of the [IRGC Quds Force](/orgs/irgc-quds-force), was killed in a US drone strike on his convoy outside Baghdad International Airport on 3 January 2020, authorised by President Trump. Also killed was Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis, deputy commander of Iraq's [Popular Mobilization Forces](/orgs/popular-mobilization-forces). Iran vowed retaliation and launched ballistic missile strikes on US bases in Iraq days later. [Esmail Qaani](/people/esmail-qaani) was appointed Quds Force commander the same day.

data.name: Tudeh Party Mass Arrests (1983)
data.description: In February 1983, the Islamic Republic carried out a systematic operation to destroy the [Tudeh Party of Iran](/orgs/tudeh-party-of-iran) — then the only significant communist organisation still operating (semi-)openly in Iran. The regime had tolerated and even cultivated the Tudeh's support since 1979, but by early 1983 moved to eliminate it. The SAVAMA (successor to SAVAK) and [IRGC](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps) arrested the entire Tudeh leadership in a coordinated sweep. First Secretary [Nureddin Kianouri](/people/nureddin-kianouri) was arrested on 6 February 1983, followed by hundreds of cadres. Under torture, Kianouri and other leaders were forced to deliver nationally televised confessions in which they denounced their own party, admitted to spying for the Soviet Union, and praised the Islamic Republic. Approximately 163 Tudeh cadres were subsequently executed. Chief party ideologue [Ehsan Tabari](/people/ehsan-tabari) was arrested and broke under interrogation, producing lengthy recantations of Marxism broadcast on state television. The mass arrests effectively destroyed the Tudeh Party's organisation inside Iran. The party was formally banned in April 1983. Surviving members who had evaded arrest fled into exile in Western Europe and the Soviet Union.

sources.title: The Massacre of Political Prisoners in Iran, 1988

data.name: UK Sanctions Against Ali Ansari
data.description: The United Kingdom designated [Ali Ansari](/people/ali-ansari-banker) under the Iran Sanctions Regulations (2023) for his alleged role in financially enabling the [Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps](/orgs/islamic-revolutionary-guard-corps). The sanctions included an asset freeze, travel ban, and director disqualification. Minister Hamish Falconer described Ansari as a 'corrupt Iranian banker and businessman.' This brought the UK's total Iran-related designations to over 500 individuals and entities. The designation came one week after the [Central Bank of Iran](/orgs/central-bank-of-iran) dissolved [Ayandeh Bank](/orgs/ayandeh-bank), which Ansari had founded.

sources.source_title: UK announces sanctions against individual who funds the work of the IRGC

data.name: Ukraine International Airlines Flight 752 Shootdown
data.description: On 8 January 2020, Ukraine International Airlines Flight 752 (PS752) was shot down by two Tor-M1 surface-to-air missiles fired by the IRGC Aerospace Force shortly after takeoff from Tehran Imam Khomeini International Airport. All 176 passengers and crew were killed. The shootdown occurred hours after Iran launched missile strikes on US bases in Iraq in retaliation for the killing of Qasem Soleimani. Iranian authorities initially denied responsibility, claiming a technical failure, before admitting three days later that the aircraft was struck by missiles. The majority of victims were Iranian and Canadian citizens. The incident prompted international demands for accountability and led to the formation of the Association of Families of Flight PS752 Victims.

sources.title: Iran plane crash: Ukraine jet was 'ichot down by missile'

data.name: 2018 Villepinte Bomb Plot
data.description: On 30 June 2018, Belgian, French, and German intelligence services jointly foiled a MOIS plot to bomb the annual Free Iran gathering of the National Council of Resistance of Iran (NCRI/MEK) at the Villepinte exhibition centre near Paris. The attack was planned by [Assadollah Assadi](/people/assadollah-assadi), a MOIS Department 312 officer operating under diplomatic cover as third secretary at the Iranian Embassy in Vienna. Assadi had recruited a Belgian-Iranian couple to plant the explosive device. The couple were arrested near Brussels with 500 grams of TATP and a detonator. The event drew tens of thousands of attendees and numerous Western politicians. Assadi was arrested on the German motorway while travelling on his Iranian diplomatic passport. In February 2021, an Antwerp court convicted Assadi to 20 years in prison — the first conviction of an Iranian diplomat-intelligence officer for directing a terrorist attack on European soil. He was exchanged in a prisoner swap in May 2023 for Belgian aid worker Olivier Vandecasteele.

sources.title: Belgium sentences Iranian diplomat to 20 years for bomb plot
